 CHAPTER I. 

 PENELOPE . 


 People din early forty year ago than they do now . even that salt of the earth , the elect of society , represent by that little great world which lie between the narrow circle bound by Bryanstone Square on the north and by Birdcage Walk on the south , do not consider seven o'clock too early an hour for a dinner party which be to be follow by rout , drum , concert , conversazione , as the case might be . it be seven o'clock on a lovely June evening , and the Park be already desert , and carriage be roll swiftly along all the Westend square , carry rank , fashion , wealth , and beauty , political influence , and intellectual power , to the particular circle in which each be destine to illumine upon that particular evening . 

 stateliest among London square , Grosvenor in some wise a wonder to the universe as newly light with gas grave Grosvenor , with its heavy old georgian house and pompous portico , sparkle and shine , not alone with the novel splendour of gas , but with the light of many wax candle , cluster flower - like in silver branch and girandole , multiply their flame in numerous mirror ; and of all the house in that stately square none have a more impose aspect than Lord Denyer 's dark red brick mansion , with stone dressing , and the massive grandeur of an egyptian mausoleum . 

 Lord Denyer be an important personage in the political and diplomatic world . he have be ambassador at Constantinople and at Paris , and have now retire on his laurel , an influence still , but no long an active power in the machine of government . at his house gather all that be most brilliant in London society . to be see at Lady Denyer 's evening party be the guinea stamp of social distinction ; to dine with Lord Denyer be an opening in life , almost as valuable as University honour , and more difficult of attainment . 

 it be during the quarter of an hour before dinner that a group of person , mostly personage , congregate round Lord Denyer 's chimney - piece , naturally trend towards the social hearth , albeit it be the season for rose and lily rather than of fire , and the hum of the city be float in upon the breath of the warm June evening through the five tall window which open upon Lord Denyer 's balcony . 

 the ten or twelve person assemble seem only a sprinkling in the large lofty room , furnish sparsely with amber satin sofas , a pair of florentine marble table , and half an acre or so of look glass . voluminous amber drapery shroud the window , and deaden the sound of rolling wheel , and the voice and footfall of western London . the drawing room of those day be neither artistic nor picturesque neither early English nor Low Dutch , nor Renaissance , nor anglo - japanese . a stately commonplace distinguish the reception room of the great world . upholstery stagnate at a dead level of fluted leg , gilding , plate glass , and amber satin . 

 Lady Denyer stand a little way in advance of the group on the hearthrug , fan herself , with her eye on the door , while she listen languidly to the remark of a youthful diplomatist , a sprig of a lordly tree , upon the last dťbut at her Majesty 's Theatre . 

 ' my own idea be that she scream , ' say her ladyship . ' but the new Rosinas generally do scream . why do we have a new Rosina every year , whom nobody ever hear of afterwards ? what become of they ? do they die , or do they set up as sing mistress in second - rate watering - place ? ' hazard her ladyship , with her eye always on the door . 

 she be a large woman in amethyst satin , and a gauze turban with a diamond aigrette , a splendid jewel , which would not have misbeseeme the head - gear of an indian prince . Lady Denyer be one of the last woman who wear a turban , and that oriental head - dress become her bold and massive feature . 

 infinitely bore by the whiskerless attachť , who have enter upon a disquisition on the genius of Rossini as compare with this new man Meyerbeer , her ladyship make believe to hear , while she listen intently to the confidential murmur of the group on the hearthrug , the little knot of personage cluster round Lord Denyer . ' indian mail in this morning , ' say one ' nothing else talk of at the club . very flagrant case ! a good deal bad than Warren Hastings . quite clear there must be a public inquiry House of Lords criminal prosecution . ' 

 ' I be tell on very good authority , that he have be recall , and be now on his passage home , ' say another man . 

 Lord Denyer shrug his shoulder , purse up his lip , and look ineffably wise , a way he have when he know very little about the subject under discussion . 

 ' how will she take it , do you think ? ' inquire Colonel Madison , of the Life Guards , a man about town , and an inveterate gossip , who know everybody , and everybody 's family history , down to the peccadillo of people 's great grandmother . 

 ' you will have an opportunity of judging , ' reply his lordship , coolly . ' she be to be here this evening . ' 

 ' but do you think she will show ? ' ask the Colonel . ' the mail must have bring the news to she , as well as to other people suppose she know nothing about it beforehand . she must know that the storm have burst . do you think she will - - ' 

 ' come out in the thunder and lightning ? ' interrupt Lord Denver ; ' I be sure she will . she have the pride of Lucifer and the courage of a lion . five to one in pony that she be here before the clock strike seven ! ' 

 ' I think you be right . I know her mother , Constance Talmash . Pluck be a family characteristic of the Talmashes . wicked as devil , and brave as lion . Old Talmash , the grandfather , shoot his valet in a paroxysm of delirium tremen , ' say Colonel Madison . ' she be a splendid woman , and she will not   flinch . I would rather back she than bet against she . ' 

 ' Lady Maulevrier ! ' announce the groom of the chamber ; and Lady Denyer move at least three pace forward to meet her guest . 

 the lady who enter , with slow and stately movement and proudly balanced head , might have serve for a model as Juno or the Empress Livia . she be still in the bloom of youth , at most seven - and - twenty , but she have all the calm assurance of middle - age . no dowager , harden by the varied experience of a quarter of a century in the great world , could have face society with more perfect coolness and self - possession . she be beautiful , and she let the world see that she be conscious of her beauty , and the power that go along with it . she be clever , and she use her cleverness with unfailing tact and unscrupulous audacity . she have win her place in the world as an acknowledge beauty , and one of the leader of fashion . two year ago she have be the glory and delight of anglo - indian society in the city of Madras , rule that remote and limited kingdom with a despotic power . then all of a sudden she be order , or she order her physician to order she , an immediate departure from that perilous climate , and she come back to England with her three - year - old son , two Ayahs , and four european servant , leave her husband , Lord Maulevrier , Governor of the Madras Presidency , to finish the term of his service in an enforced widowhood . 

 she return to be the delight of London society . she throw open the family mansion in Curzon Street to the very good people , but to those only . she go out a great deal , but she be never see at a second - rate party . she have not a single doubtful acquaintance upon her visit list . she spend half of every year at the family seat in Scotland , be a miracle of goodness to the poor of her parish , and teach her boy his alphabet . 

 Lord Denyer come forward while his wife and Lady Maulevrier be shake hand , and greet she with more than his usual cordiality . Colonel Madison watch for the privilege of a recognise nod from the divinity . Sir Jasper Paulet , a legal luminary of the first brilliancy , likely to be employ for the Crown if there should be an inquiry into Lord Maulevrier 's conduct out yonder , come to press Lady Maulevrier 's hand and murmur a tender welcome . 

 she accept their friendliness as a matter of course , and not by the faint extra quiver of the tremulous star which glitter in a circlet above her raven hair do she betray her consciousness of the cloud that darken her husband 's reputation . never have she appear gayer , or more completely satisfied with herself and the world in which she live . she be ready to talk about anything and everything the newly - wed queen , and the fortunate Prince , whose existence among we have all the charm of novelty of Lord Melbourne 's decline health and Sir Robert Peel 's slide scale mesmerism the Oxford tract the late balloon ascent the opera Macready 's last production at Drury lane Bulwer 's new novel that clever little comic paper , just struggle into popularity what do you call the thing Punch ? yes , Punch , or the London Charivari a much more respectable paper than its parisian prototype . 

 seat next Lord Denyer , who be an excellent listener , Lady Maulevrier 's vivacity never flag throughout the dinner , happily not so long as a modern banquet , albeit more ponderous and not less expensive . from the turtle to the pine and strawberry , Lady Maulevrier hold her host or her right - hand neighbour in interested conversation . she always know the particular subject likely to interest particular people , and be a good listener as well as a good talker . her right - hand neighbour be Sir Jasper Paulet , who have be allot to the pompous wife of a court physician , a lady who have begin her married life in the outer darkness of Guildford Street , Bloomsbury , with a household consist of a maid - of - all - work and a boy in button , with an occasional interregnum of charwoman ; and for whom all the length and breadth of Harley Street be now much too small . 

 Sir Jasper be only decently civil to this haughty matron , who on the strength of a card for a ball or a concert at the palace once in a season affect to be on the most intimate term with Royalty , and know everything that happen , and every fluctuation of opinion in that charm circle . the great lawyer 's left ear be listen greedily for any word of meaning that might fall from the lip of Lady Maulevrier ; but no such word fall . she talk delightfully , with a touch - and - go vivacity which be the high form of dinner - table talk , not dwell with a heavy hand upon any one subject , but glance from theme to theme with airy lightness . but not one word do she say about the governor of Madras ; and at this juncture of affair it would have be the worst possible taste to inquire too closely after his lordship 's welfare . 

 so the dinner wear on to its stately close , and just as the solemn procession of flunkey , long as the shadowy line of the king in ' Macbeth , ' file off with the empty ice - dish , Lady Maulevrier say something which be as if a shell have explode in the middle of the table . 

 ' perhaps you be surprised to see I in such good spirit , ' she say , beam upon her host , and speak in those clear , perfectly finish syllable which be hear far than the loud accent of less polished speaker , ' but you will not wonder when I let you into the secret . Maulevrier be on his way home . ' 

 ' indeed ! ' say Lord Denyer , with the most benignant smile he could command at such short notice . he feel that the muscle round his eye and the corner of his mouth be betray too much of his real sentiment . ' you must be very glad . ' 

 ' I be gladder than I can say , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , gaily . ' that horried climate a sky like molten copper an atmosphere that taste of red - hot sand that flat barren coast never suit he . his term of office would expire in little more than a year , but I hardly think he could have live out the year . however , I be happy to say the mail that come in to - day I suppose you know the mail be in ? '   Lord Denyer bow   ' bring I a letter from his Lordship , tell I that he have send in his resignation , and take his passage by the next big ship that leave Madras . I imagine he will be home in October . ' 

 ' if he have a favourable passage , ' say Lord Denyer . ' favour by your good wish the wind and the wave ought to deal gently with he . ' 

 ' ah , we have do with the old day of greek story , when Neptune be open to feminine influence , ' sigh her ladyship . ' my poor Ulysses have no goddess of wisdom to look after he . ' 

 ' perhaps not , but he have the most charming of Penelopes wait for he at home . ' 

 ' a Penelope who go to dinner , and take life pleasantly in his absence . that be a new order of thing , be it not ? ' say her ladyship , laughingly . ' I hope my poor Ulysses will not come home thoroughly break in health , but that our sutherlandshire breeze will set he up again . ' 

 ' rather an ordeal after India , I should think , ' say Lord Denyer . 

 ' it be his native air . he will revel in it . ' 

 ' Delicious country , no doubt , ' assent his lordship , who be no sportsman , and who detest Scotland , grouse moor , deer forest , salmon river include . 

 his only idea of a winter residence be Florence or Capri , and of the two he prefer Capri . the island be at that time little frequent by Englishmen . it have hardly be fashionable since the time of Tiberius , but Lord Denyer go there , accompany by his french chef , and a dozen other servant , and rough it in a native hotel ; while Lady Denyer winter at the family seat among the hill near Bath , and give herself over to low Church devotion , and work of benevolence . she make herself a terror to the neighbourhood by the strictness of her idea all through the autumn and winter ; and in the spring she go up to London , put on her turban and her diamond , and plunge into the vortex of west - end society , where she revolve among other jewel matron for the season , tell herself and her intimate that this sacrifice of inclination be due to his lordship 's position . Lady Denyer be not the less serious - minded because she be see at every aristocratic resort , and wear low gown with very short sleeve , and a great display of mottled arm and dimpled elbow . 

 now come her ladyship 's smile signal for the withdrawal of that fair half of the assembly which be suppose to be indifferent to Lord Denyer 's famous port and Madeira . she have be throw out her gracious signal unperceive for at least five minute before Lady Maulevrier respond , so entirely be that lady absorb in her conversation with Lord Denyer ; but she catch the look at last , and rise , as if move by the same machinery which impel her hostess , and then , graceful as a swan sail with the current , she drift down the room to the distant door , and head the stately procession of matronly velvet and diamond , herself at once the most regal and the most graceful figure in that bevy of fair woman . 

 in the drawing - room nobody could be gayer than Lady Maulevrier , as she mark the time of Signor Paponizzi 's saltarello , exquisitely perform on the Signor 's famed Amati violin or talk of the late scandal always except that late scandal of all which involve her own husband in subdued murmur with one of her intimate . in the dining - room the man draw close together over their wine , and tear Lord Maulevrier 's character to rag . yea , they rent he with their tooth and gnaw the flesh from his bone , until there be not so much left of he as the dog leave of Jezebel . 

 he have be a scamp from his cradle , a spendthrift at Eton and Oxford , a blackleg in his manhood . false to man , false to woman . clever ? yes , undoubtedly , just as Satan be clever , and as unscrupulous as that very Satan . this be what his friend say of he over their wine . and now he be rumour to have sell the british force in the carnatic province to one of the native Princes . yes , to have take gold , gold to an amount which clive in his most rapacious moment never dream of , for his countryman 's blood . tiding of dark transaction between the Governor and the native Princes have reach the ear of the Government , tiding so vague , so incredible , that the Government might naturally be slow to believe , still slow to act . there be whisper of a woman 's influence , a beautiful ranee , a creature as fascinating and as unscrupulous as Cleopatra . the scandal have be grow for month past , but it be only in the letter receive to - day that the rumour have take a tangible shape , and now it be currently report that Lord Maulevrier have be recall , and would have to answer at the bar of the House of Lords for his misdemeanour , which be of a much dark colour than those act for which Warren Hastings have be call to account fifty year before . 

 yet in the face of all this , Lady Maulevrier bear herself as proudly as if her husband 's name be spotless , and talk of his return with all the ardour of a fond and trust wife . 

 ' one of the fine bit of act I ever see in my life , ' say the court physician . ' Mademoiselle Mars never do anything well . ' 

 ' do you really think it be act ? ' inquire Lord Denyer , affect a youthful candour and trustfulness which at his age , and with his experience , he could hardly be suppose to possess . 

 ' I know it , ' reply the doctor . ' I watch she while she be talk of Maulevrier , and I see just one bead of perspiration break out on her upper lip an unmistakable sign of the mental struggle . ' 

 CHAPTER II . 

 ULYSSES . 


 October be end drearily with north - east wind , dust , drift dead leaf , and a steel - grey sky ; and the Dolphin Hotel at Southampton be glorify by the presence of Lady Maulevrier and suite . her ladyship 's suite be on this occasion limit to three servant her french maid , a footman , and a kind of factotum , a man of no distinct and arbitrary signification in her ladyship 's household , neither butler nor steward , but that privileged being , an old and trusted servant , and a person who be suppose to enjoy more of Lady Maulevrier 's confidence than any other member of her establishment . 

 this James Steadman have be valet to her ladyship 's father , Lord Peverill , during the decline year of that nobleman . the narrow limit of a sick room have bring the master and servant into a close companionship than be common to that relation . Lady Diana Angersthorpe be a devoted daughter , and in her attendance upon the Earl during the last three year of his life a life which close more than a year before her own marriage she see a great deal of James Steadman , and learn to trust he as servant be not often trust . he be not more than twenty year of age at the beginning of his service , but he be a man of extraordinary gravity , much in advance of his year ; a man of shrewd common - sense and clear , sharp intellect . not a read man , or a man in any way superior to his station and belonging , but a man who could think quickly , and understand quickly , and who always seem to think rightly . prompt in action , yet steady as a rock , and to all appearance recognise no earthly interest , no human tie , beyond or above the interest of his master . as a nurse Steadman show himself invaluable . Lord Peverill leave he a hundred pound in acknowledgment of his service , which be something for Lord Peverill , who have very little ready cash wherewith to endow his only daughter . after his death the title and the estate go to a distant cousin ; Lady Diana Angersthorpe be take in hand by her aunt , the Dowager Marchioness of Carrisbrook ; and James Steadman would have have to find employment among stranger , if Lady Diana have not plead so urgently with her aunt as to secure he a somewhat insignificant post in her ladyship 's establishment . 

 ' if ever I have a house of my own , you shall have a well place in it , Steadman , ' say Lady Diana . 

 she keep her word , and on her marriage with Lord Maulevrier , which happen about eighteen month afterwards , Steadman pass into that nobleman 's service . he be a member of her ladyship 's bodyguard , and his employment seem to consist chiefly in poking fire , cut the leaf of book and newspaper , superintend the footman 's attendance upon her ladyship 's household pet , and convey her sentiment to the other servant . he be in a manner Lady Maulevrier 's mouthpiece , and although treat with a respect that verge upon awe , he be not a favourite with the household . 

 and now the house in Mayfair be give over to the charge of caretaker . all the other servant have be despatch by coach to her ladyship 's favourite retreat in Westmoreland , within a few mile of the Laureate 's home at Rydal Mount , and James Steadman be charge with the whole responsibility of her ladyship 's travel arrangement . 

 Penelope have come to Southampton to wait for Ulysses , whose ship have be due for more than a week , and whose white sail might be expect above the horizon at any moment . James Steadman spend a good deal of his time wait about at the dock for the early news of Greene 's ship , the Hypermnestra ; while Lady Maulevrier wait patiently in her sitting - room at the Dolphin , whose three long french window command a full view of the High Street , with all those various distraction afford by the chief thoroughfare of a provincial town . her ladyship be provide with a large box of book , from eber ' in Bond Street , a basket of fancy work , and her favourite Blenheim spaniel , Lalla Rookh ; but even these source of amusement do not prevent the involuntary expression of weariness in occasional yawn , and frequent pacing up and down the room , where the formal hotel furniture have a comfortless and chilly look . 

 Fellside , her ladyship 's place in Westmoreland , be the pleasure house which , among all her possession , she most value ; but it have hitherto be reserve for summer occupation , or for perhaps two or three week at Easter , when the spring be exceptionally fine . the sudden determination to spend the come winter in the house near Grasmere be consider a curious freak of Lady Maulevrier 's , and she be constrain to explain her motif to her friend . 

 ' his lordship be out of health , ' she say , ' and want perfect rest and retirement . now , Fellside be the only place we have in which he be likely to get perfect rest . anywhere else we should have to entertain . Fellside be out of the world . there be no one to be entertain . ' 

 ' except your neighbour , Wordsworth . I suppose you see he sometimes ? ' 

 ' dear simple - minded old soul , he give nobody any trouble , ' say her ladyship . 

 ' but be not Westmoreland very cold in winter ? ' ask her friend . 

 Lady Maulevrier smile benignly , as at an inoffensive ignorance . 

 ' so sheltered , ' she murmur . ' we be at the base of the Fell . Loughrigg rise up like a cyclopean wall between we and the wind . ' 

 ' but when the wind be in the other direction ? ' 

 ' we have Nabb Scar . you do not know how we be girdle and defend by hill . ' 

 ' very pleasant , ' agree the friend ; ' but for my own part I would rather winter in the south . ' 

 those terrible rumour which have first come upon the world of London last June , have be grow dark and more define ever since , but still Lady Maulevrier make believe to ignore they ; and she act her part of unconsciousness with such consummate skill that nobody in her circle could be sure where the acting begin and where the ignorance leave off . the astute Lord Denyer declare that she be a wonderful woman , and know more about the real state of the case than anybody else . 

 meanwhile it be say by those who be suppose to be well - inform that a mass of evidence be accumulate against Lord Maulevrier . the India House , it be rumour , be busy with the secret investigation of his case , prior to that public inquiry which be to come on during the next session . his private fortune would be make answerable for his misdemeanours his life , say the alarmist , might pay the penalty of his treason . on all side it be agree that the case against Lord Maulevrier be black as Erebus ; and still Lady Maulevrier look society in the face with an unshaken courage , and be ready with smile and gracious word for all comer . 

 but now come a hard trial , which be to receive the man who have disgrace she , lower her pride to the dust , degrade the name she bear . she have marry he , not love he nay , pluck another love out of her heart in order that she might give herself to he . she have marry he for position and fortune ; and now by his folly , by his extravagance , and by that greed of gold which be inevitable in the spendthrift and profligate , he have go near to cheat she out of both name and fortune . yet she so command herself as to receive he with a friendly air when he arrive at the dolphin , on a dull grey autumn afternoon , after she have wait for he nearly a fortnight . 

 James Steadman usher in his lordship , a frail attenuate look figure , of middle height , wrap in a furred cloak , yet shiver , a pale sickly face , light auburn whisker , light blue eye , full and large , but with no intellectual power in they . Lady Maulevrier be sit by the fire , in a melancholy attitude , with the Blenheim spaniel on her lap . her son be at Hastings with his nurse . she have nothing near and dearer than the spaniel . 

 she rise and go over to her husband , and let he kiss she . it would have be too much to say that she kiss he ; but she submit her lip unresistingly to his , and then they sit down on opposite side of the hearth . 

 ' a wretched afternoon , ' say his lordship , shivering , and draw his chair close to the fire . Steadman have take away his fur - line cloak . ' I have really underrate the disagreeableness of the english climate . it be abominable ! ' 

 ' to - day be not a fair sample , ' answer her ladyship , try to be cheerful ; ' we have have some pleasant autumn day . ' 

 ' I det autumn ! ' exclaim Lord Maulevrier , ' a season of dead leaf , damp , and dreariness . I should like to get away to Montpellier or Nice as soon as we can . ' 

 her ladyship give he a scathing look , half - scornful , half - incredulous . 

 ' you surely would not dream of leave the country , ' she say , ' under present circumstance . so long as you be here to answer all charge no one will interfere with your liberty ; but if you be to cross the Channel ' 

 ' my slanderer might insinuate that I be run away , ' interrupt Maulevrier , ' although the very fact of my return ought to prove to every one that I be able to meet and face this cabal . ' 

 ' be it a cabal ? ' ask her ladyship , look at he with a gaze that search his soul . ' can you meet their charge ? can you live down this hideous accusation , and hold up your head as a man of honour ? ' 

 the sensualist 's blue eye nervously shun that look of earnest interrogation . his lip answer the wife 's speak question with a lie , a lie make manifest by the expression of his countenance . 

 ' I be not afraid , ' he say . 

 his wife answer not a word . she be assure that the charge be true , and that the battered rake who shiver over the fire have neither courage nor ability to face his accuser . she see the whole fabric of her life in ruin , her son the penniless successor to a tarnished name . there be silence for some minute . Lady Maulevrier sit with lowered eyelid look at the fire , deep in painful thought . two perpendicular wrinkle upon her broad white forehead so calm , so unclouded in society tell of gnawing care . then she steal a look at her husband , as he recline in his arm - chair , his head lie back against the cushion in listless repose , his eye look vacantly at the window , whence he could see only the rain - blur front of opposite house , blank , dull window , grey slat roof , against a leaden sky . 

 he have be a handsome man , and he be handsome still , albeit premature decay , the result of an evil life , be distinctly mark in his faded face . the dull , yellow tint of the complexion , the tarnish dimness of the large blue eye , the discontent droop of the lip , the languor of the attitude , the pallid transparency of the waste hand , all tell of a life wear threadbare , energy exhaust , chance throw away , a mind abandon to despair . 

 ' you look very ill , ' say his wife , after that long blank interval , which mark so unnatural an apathy between husband and wife meeting after so long a severance . 

 ' I be very ill . I have be worried to death surround by rogue and liar the victim of a most infernal conspiracy . ' he speak hurriedly , grow whiter and more tremulous as he go on . 

 ' do not   talk about it . you agitate yourself to no purpose , ' say Lady Maulevrier , with a tranquillity which seem heartless yet which might be the result of suppress feeling . ' if you be to face this scandal firmly and boldly next January , you must try to recover physical strength in the meanwhile . mental energy may come with well health . ' 

 ' I shall never be any well , ' say Lord Maulevrier , testily ; ' that infernal climate have shatter my constitution . ' 

 ' two or three month of perfect rest and good nursing will make a new man of you . I have arrange that we shall go straight from here to Fellside . no one can plague you there with that disguised impertinence call sympathy . you can give all your thought to the ordeal before you , and be ready to meet your accuser . fortunately , you have no Burke against you . ' 

 ' Fellside ? you think of go to Fellside ? ' 

 ' yes . you know how fond I be of that place . I little think when you settle it upon I a cottage in Westmoreland with fifty acre of garden and meadow so utterly insignificant that I should ever like it well than any of your place . ' 

 ' a charming retreat in summer ; but we have never winter there ? what put it into your head to go there at such a season as this ? why , I daresay the snow be on the top of the hill already . ' 

 ' it be the only place I know where you will not be watch and talk about , ' reply Lady Maulevrier . ' you will be out of the eye of the world . I should think that consideration would weigh more with you than two or three degree of the thermometer . ' 

 ' I detest cold , ' say the Earl , ' and in my weak health - - ' 

 ' we will take care of you , ' answer her ladyship ; and in the discussion which follow she bear herself so firmly that her husband be fain to give way . 

 how could a disgraced and ruin man , break in health and spirit , contest the mere detail of life with a high - spirited woman ten year his junior ? 

 the Earl want to go to London , and remain there at least a week , but this her ladyship strenuously opposed . he must see his lawyer , he urge ; there be step to be take which could be take only under legal advice counsel to be retain . if this lie invention of Satan be really destine to take the form of a public trial , he must be prepare to fight his foe on their own ground . 

 ' you can make all your preparation at Fellside , ' answer his wife , resolutely . ' I have see Messrs. Rigby and Rider , and your own particular ally , Rigby , will go to you at Fellside whenever you want he . ' 

 ' that be not like my be on the spot , ' say his lordship , nervously , evidently much disconcert by her ladyship 's firmness , but too feeble in mind and body for a prolonged contest . 

 ' I ought to be on the spot . I be not without influence ; I have friend , man in power . ' 

 ' surely you be not go to appeal to friendship in order to vindicate your honour . these charge be true or false . if they be false your own manhood , your own rectitude , can face they and trample upon they , unaide by back - stair influence . if they be true , no one can help you . ' 

 ' I think you , at least , ought to know that they be as false as hell , ' retort the Earl , with an attempt to maintain his dignity . 

 ' I have act as if I so believe , ' reply his wife . ' I have live as if there be no such slander in the air . I have steadily ignore every report , every insinuation have hold my head as high as if I know you be immaculate . ' 

 ' I expect as much from you , ' answer the Earl , coolly . ' if I have not know you be a woman of sense I should not have marry you . ' 

 this be his utmost expression of gratitude . his next remark have reference solely to his own comfort . where be his room ? at what hour be they to dine ? and hereupon he ring for his valet , a german Swiss , and a servant out of a thousand . 

 CHAPTER III . 

 on the wrong ROAD . 


 Lord and Lady Maulevrier leave Southampton next morning , post . they take two servant in the rumble , Steadman and the footman . Steadman be to valet his lordship , the footman to be useful in all emergency of the journey . the maid and the valet be to travel by heavy coach , with the luggage her ladyship dispense with all personal attendance during the journey . 

 the first day take they to Rugby , whither they travel across country by Wallingford and Oxford . the second day take they to Lichfield . Lord Maulevrier be out of health and feeble , and grumble a good deal about the fatigue of the journey , the badness of the weather , which be dull and cold , east wind all day , and a light frost morning and night . as they progress northward the sky look grayer , the air become more biting . his lordship insist upon the stage be shorten . he lie in bed at his hotel till noon , and be seldom ready to start till two o'clock . he could see no reason for haste ; the winter would be long enough in all conscience at Fellside . he complain of mysterious ache and pain , describe himself in the presence of hotel - keeper and headwaiter as a mass of malady . he be nervous , irritable , intensely disagreeable . Lady Maulevrier bear his humour with unwavering patience , and win golden opinion from all sort of people by her devotion to a husband whose blighted name be the common talk of England . everybody , even in distant provincial town , have hear of the scandal against the Governor of Madras ; and everybody look at the sallow , fade Anglo - indian with morbid curiosity . his lordship , sensitive on all point touch his own ease and comfort , be keenly conscious of this unflattering inquisitiveness . 

 the journey , protract by Lord Maulevrier 's languor and ill - health , drag its slow length along for nearly a fortnight ; until it seem to Lady Maulevrier as if they have be travel upon those dismal , flat , unpicturesque road for month . each day be so horribly like yesterday . the same hedgerow and flat field , and pass glimpse of river or canal . the same absence of all beauty in the landscape the same formal hotel room , and smirk landlady and so on till they come to Lancaster , after which the country become more interesting hill arise in the background . even the smoky manufacturing town through which they pass without stop , be less abominable than the level monotony of the Midland county . 

 but now as they draw near the hill the weather grow cold , snow be speak of , and when they get into Westmoreland the mountain - peak gleam whitely against a lead - colour sky . 

 ' you ought not to have bring I here in such weather , ' complain the Earl , shiver in his sable , as he sit in his corner of the travel chariot , look discontentedly at the gloomy landscape . ' what be to become of we if we be catch in a snowstorm ? ' 

 ' we shall have no snow worth talk about before we be safely house at Fellside , and then we can defy the element , ' say Lady Maulevrier , coolly . 

 they sleep that night at Oxenholme , and start next morning , under a clean , bright sky , intend to take luncheon at Windermere , and to be at home by nightfall . 

 but by the time they get to windermere the sky have change to a dark grey , and the people at the hotel prophesy a heavy fall before night , and urge the Earl and Countess to go no far that day . the latter part of the road to Fellside be rough and hilly . if there should be a snowstorm the horse would never be able to drag the carriage up the steep bit of the way . here , however , Lord Maulevrier 's obstinacy come into play . he would not endure another night at an hotel so near his own house . he be sick to death of travel , and want to be at rest among comfortable surrounding . 

 ' it be murder to bring I here , ' he say to his wife . ' if I have go to Hastings I should have be a new man by this time . as it be I be a great deal bad than when I land . ' 

 everyone at the hotel notice his lordship 's white and haggard look . he have be know there as a young man in the bloom of health and strength , and his decay be particularly obvious to these people . 

 ' I see death in his face , ' the landlord say , afterwards . 

 every one , even her ladyship 's firmness and good sense , give way before the invalid 's impatience . at three in the afternoon they leave the hotel , with four horse , to make the remain nineteen mile of the way in one stage . they have not be on the road half an hour before the snow begin to fall thickly , whiten everything around they , except the lake , which show a dark leaden surface at the bottom of the slope along the edge of which they be travel . too sullen for speech , Lord Maulevrier sit back in his corner , with his sable cloak draw up to his chin , his travel cap cover head and ear , his eye contemplate the whiten world with a weary anger . his wife watch the landscape as long as she could , but the snow soon begin to darken all the air , and she could see nothing save that blank blinding fall . 

 half - way to Fellside there be a point where two road meet , one lead towards Grasmere , the other towards the village of Great Langdale , a cluster of humble habitation in the heart of the hill . when the horse have struggle as far as this point , the snow be six inch deep on the road , and make a thick curtain around they as it fall . by this time the Earl have doze off to sleep . 

 he wake an hour after , let down the window , which let in a snow - laden gust , and try to pierce the gloom without . 

 ' as black as Erebus ! ' he exclaim , ' but we ought to be close at home by this time . yes , thank God , there be the light . ' 

 the carriage draw up a minute afterwards , and Steadman come to the door . 

 ' very sorry , my lord . the horse must have take a wrong turn after we cross the bridge . and now the man say they can not   go back to Fellside unless we can get fresh horse ; and I be afraid there be no chance of that here . ' 

 ' here ! ' exclaim the Earl , ' what do you mean by here ? where the devil be we ? ' 

 ' Great Langdale , my lord . ' 

 a door open and let out a flood of light the red light of a wood fire , the pale flame of a candle upon the snowy darkness , reveal the panel hall of a neat little rustic inn : an eight - day clock tick in the corner , a black and white sheep - dog come out at his master 's heel to investigate the traveller . to the right of the door show the light of a window , shelter by a red curtain , behind which the chief of the village be enjoy their evening . 

 ' have you any post - horse ? ' ask the Earl , discontentedly , as the landlord stand on the threshold , shade the candle with his hand . ' no , sir . we do not   keep post - horse . ' 

 ' of course not . I know as much before I ask , ' say the Earl . 

 ' we be fix in this dismal hole for the night , I suppose . how far be we from Fellside ? ' 

 ' seven mile , ' answer the landlord . ' I beg your pardon , my lord ; I do not   know it be your lordship , ' he add , hurriedly . ' we be in sore trouble , and it make a man daft - like ; but if there be anything we can do - - ' 

 ' be there no hope of get on , Steadman ? ' ask the Earl , cut short these civility . 

 ' not with these horse , my lord . ' 

 ' and you hear we can not   get any other . be there any farmer about here who could lend we a pair of carriage horse ? ' 

 the landlord know of no such person . 

 ' then we must stop here till to - morrow morning . what infernal fool those post - boy must be , ' protest Lord Maulevrier . 

 James Steadman apologise for the postilion , explain that when they come to the critical point of their journey , where the road branch off to the Langdales , the snow be fall so thickly , the whole country be so hidden in all - pervade whiteness , that even he , who know the way so well , could give no help to the driver . he could only trust to the instinct of local postilion and local horse ; and instinct have prove wrong . 

 the traveller alight , and be usher into a not uncomfortable - look parlour ; very low as to the ceiling , very old - fashioned as to the furniture , but spotlessly clean , and enliven by a good fire , to which his lordship draw near , shiver and mutter discontentedly to himself . 

 ' we might be bad off , ' say her ladyship , look round the bright little room , which please she well than many a state apartment in the large hotel at which they have stop . 

 ' hardly , unless we be out on the moor , ' grumble her husband . ' I be sick to death of this ill - advise , unreasonable journey . I be at a loss to imagine your motive in bring I here . you must have have a motive . ' 

 ' I have , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , with a freezing look . ' I want to get you out of the way . I tell you that plainly enough at Southampton . ' 

 ' I do not   see why I should be hurry away and hide , ' say Lord Maulevrier . ' I must face my accuser , soon or later . ' 

 ' of course . the day of reckoning must come . but in the meantime have you no delicacy ? do you want to be point at everywhere ? ' 

 ' all I know be that I be very ill , ' answer her husband , ' and that this wretched journey have make I twenty year old . ' 

 ' we shall be safe at home before noon to - morrow , and you can have Horton to set you right again . you know you always believe in his skill . ' 

 ' Horton be a clever fellow enough , as country doctor go ; but at Hastings I could have have the good physician in London to see I , ' grumble his lordship . 

 the rustic maid - servant come in to lay the table , assist by her ladyship 's footman , who look a good deal too tall for the room . 

 ' I shall not   dine , ' say the Earl . ' I be a great deal too ill and cold . light a fire in my room , girl , and send Steadman to I ' this to the footman , who hasten to obey . ' you can send I up a basin of soup presently . I shall go to bed at once . ' 

 he leave the room without another word to his wife , who sit by the hearth star thoughtfully at the cheery wood fire . presently she look up , and see that the man and maid be go on with their preparation for dinner . 

 ' I do not care about dining alone , ' say her ladyship . ' we lunch at windermere , and I have no appetite . you can clear away those thing , and bring I some tea . ' 

 when the table furniture have be clear , and a neat little tea - tray set upon the white cloth , Lady Maulevrier draw her chair to the table , and take out her pocket - book , from which she produce a letter . this she read more than once , meditate profoundly upon its content . 

 ' I be very sorry he have come home , ' write her correspondent , ' and yet if he have stay in India there must have be an investigation on the spot . a public inquiry be inevitable , and the knowledge of his arrival in the country will precipitate matter . from all I hear I much fear that there be no chance of the result be favourable to he . you have ask I to write the unvarnished truth , to be brutal even , remember . his delinquency be painfully notorious , and I apprehend that the last sixpence he own will be answerable . his landed estate I be tell can also be confiscate , in the event of an impeachment at the bar of the House of Lords , as in the Warren Hastings case . but as yet nobody seem clear as to the form which the investigation will take . in reply to your inquiry as to what would have happen if his lordship have die on the passage home , I believe I be justify in say the scandal would have be allow to die with he . he have contrive to provoke powerful animosity both in the Cabinet and at the India House , and there be , I fear , an intention to pursue the inquiry to the bitter end . ' 

 assurance of the writer 's sympathy follow these harsh truth . but to this polite commonplace her ladyship pay no attention . her mind be intent on hard fact , the dismal probability of the near future . 

 ' if he have die upon the passage home ! ' she repeat . ' would to God that he have so die , and that my son 's name and fortune could be save . ' 

 the innocent child who have never give she an hour 's care ; the one creature she love with all the strength of her proud nature his future be to be blight by his father 's misdoing overshadow by shame and dishonour in the very dawn of life . it be a wicked wish an unnatural wish to find room in a woman 's breast ; but the wish be there . would to God he have die before the ship touch an english port . 

 but he be live , and would have to face his accuser and she , his wife , must give he all the help she could . 

 she sit long by the wan fire . she take nothing but a cup of tea , although the landlady have send in substantial accompaniment to the tea - tray in the shape of broiled ham , new - lay egg , and hot cake , argue that a traveller on such a night must be hungry , albeit disinclined for a ceremonious dinner . she have be sit for nearly an hour in almost the same attitude , when there come a knock at the door , and , on be bid to enter , the landlady come in , with some log in her apron , under pretence of replenish the fire . 

 ' I be afraid your fire must be get low , and that you would be amost starve , my lady , ' she say , as she put on the log , and sweep up the ash on the hearth . ' such a dreadful night . so early in the year , too . I be think we shall have a gay hard winter . ' 

 ' that do not always follow , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' have Steadman come downstairs ? ' 

 ' yes , my lady . he tell I to tell your ladyship that his lordship be pretty comfortable , and hop to pass a good night . ' 

 ' I be glad to hear it . you can give I another room , I suppose . it would be well for his lordship not to be disturb , as he be very much out of health . ' 

 ' there be another room , my lady , but it be very small . ' 

 ' I do not   mind how small , if it be clean and airy . ' 

 ' yes , my lady . I be thankful to say you will not   find dirt or stuffiness anywhere in this house . his lordship do look mortal badly , ' add the landlady , shake her head dolefully ; ' and I remember he such a fine young gentleman , when he use to come down the Rothay with the otter hound , run along the bank joomping in and out of the beck up to his knee in the water and now to see he , so white and mashiate , and break - down like , in the very prime of life , all along of live out in a hot country , among blackamoor , which be use to it poor , ignorant creature and never know no well . it must be a hard trial for you , my lady . ' 

 ' it be a hard trial . ' 

 ' ah ! we all have our trial , rich and poor , ' sigh the woman , who desire nothing well than to be allow to unbosom her woe to the grand look lady in the fur - border cloth pelisse , with beautiful dark hair pile up in clustering mass above a broad white forehead , and slender white hand on which diamond flash and glitter in the firelight , an unaccustomed figure by that rustic hearth . 

 ' we all have our trial high and low . ' 

 ' that remind I , ' say Lady Maulevrier , look up at she , ' your husband say you be in trouble . what do that mean ? ' 

 ' Sickness in the house , my lady . a brother of mine that go to America to make his fortune , and seem to be do so well for the first five or six year , and write home such beautiful letter , and then leave off write all at once , and we make sure as he be dead , and never get a word from he for ten year , and just three week ago he drop in upon we as we be sit over our tea between the light , look as white as a ghost . I give a shriek when I see he , for I be regular scared out of my sens . " Robert 's ghost ! " I cry ; but it be Robert himself , come home to we to die . and he be lie upstairs now , with so little life in he that I expect every breath to be his last . ' 

 ' what be his complaint ? ' 

 ' Apathy , my lady . dear , dear , that be not it . I never do remember the doctor 's foreign name . ' 

 ' Atrophy , ' perhaps . 

 ' yes , my lady , that be it . happen such crack - jaw word come easy to a scholar like your ladyship . ' 

 ' do the doctor give no hope ? ' 

 ' well , no , my lady . he do not   go so far as to say there be no hope , though Robert have be badly so long . it all depend , he say , upon the rallying power of the constitution . the lung be not go , and the heart be not disease . if there be rallying power , Robert will come round , and if there be not   he will sink . but the doctor say nature will have to make an effort . but I have my own idea about the case , ' add the landlady , with a sigh . 

 ' what be your idea ? ' 

 ' that our Robert be mark for death when he come into this house , and that he mean what he say when he speak of come home to die . thing have go against he for the last ten year in America . he marry and take his wife out to a farm in the Bush , and think to make a good thing out of farming with the bit of brass he have save at heeam . but America be not   Gert Langdale , you see , my lady , and his knowledge stand he in no stead in the Bush ; and first he lose his money , and he fashe himself terrible about that , and then he lose a child or two , and then he lose his wife , and he come back to we a break - hearted man , with no wish to live . the doctor may call it atrophy , but I will call it what the scripture call it , a broken and a wound spirit . ' 

 ' who be your doctor ? ' 

 ' Mr. Evans , of Ambleside . ' 

 ' that little half - blind old man ! ' exclaim her ladyship . ' surely you have no confidence in he ? ' 

 ' not much , my lady . but I do not   believe all the doctor in London could do anything for Robert . good nursing will bring he round if anything can ; and he get that , I can assure your ladyship . he be my only brother , the only kith and kin that be leave to I , and he and I be gay fond of each other when he be young . you may be sure I do not   spare any trouble , and my good man think the good of his larder or his celler hardly good enough for Robert . ' 

 ' I be sure you be kind good people , ' reply her ladyship gently ; ' but I should have think Mr. Horton , of Grasmere , could have do more than old Evans . however , you know well . I hope his lordship be not go to add to your care by be lay up here , but he look very ill this evening . ' 

 ' he do , my lady , mortal bad . ' 

 ' however , we must hope for the good . Steadman be a splendid servant in illness . he nurse my father for year . will you tell he to come to I , if you please ? I want to hear what he think of his lordship , and to discuss the chance of our get home early to - morrow . ' 

 the landlady retire , and summon Mr. Steadman , who be enjoy his modest glass of grog in front of the kitchen fire . he have teach himself to dispense with the consolation of tobacco , lest he should at any time make himself obnoxious to her ladyship . 

 Steadman be closet with Lady Maulevrier for the next half - hour , during which his lordship 's condition be gravely discuss . when he leave the sitting - room he tell the landlord to be sure and feed the post - horse well , and make they comfortable for the night , so that they might be ready for the drive to Fellside early next morning . 

 ' do you think his lordship will be well enough to travel ? ' ask the landlord . 

 ' he have make up his mind to get home ill or well , ' answer Steadman . ' he have waste about a week by his dawdling way on the road ; and now he be in a fever to get to Fellside . ' 

 CHAPTER IV . 

 the last stage . 


 the post - horse which have be well feed , but accommodate somewhat poorly in stable and barn be quite ready to go on next morning ; but Lord Maulevrier be not able to leave his room , where her ladyship remain in close attendance upon he . the hill and valley be white with snow , but there be none fall , and Mr. Evans , the elderly surgeon from Ambleside , ride over to Great Langdale on his elderly cob to look at Robert Haswell , and be call in to see Lord Maulevrier . her ladyship have speak lightly of his skill on the previous evening , but any doctor be well than none , so this feeble little personage be allow to feel his lordship 's pulse , and look at his lordship 's tongue . 

 his opinion , never too decidedly give , be a little more hazy than usual on this occasion , perhaps because of a certain awfulness , to unaccustomed eye , in Lady Maulevrier 's proud bearing . he say that his lordship be low , very low , and that the pulse be more irregular than he like , but he commit himself no far than this , and go away , promise to send such pill and potion as be appropriate to the patient 's condition . 

 a boy ride the same pony over to langdale later in the afternoon with the promise medicine . 

 throughout the short winter day , which seem terribly long in the stillness and solitude of Great Langdale , Lady Maulevrier keep watch in the sick - room , Steadman go in and out in constant attendance upon his master save for one half - hour only , which her ladyship pass in the parlour below , in conversation with the landlady , a very serious conversation , as indicate by Mrs. Smithson 's grave and somewhat troubled look when she leave her ladyship ; but a good deal of her trouble may have be cause by her anxiety about her brother , who be pronounce by the doctor to be ' much the same . ' 

 at eleven o'clock that night a mount messenger be send off to Ambleside in hot haste to fetch Mr. Evans , who come to the inn to find Lady Maulevrier kneeling beside her husband 's bed , while Steadman stand with a troubled countenance at a respectful distance . 

 the room be dimly light by a pair of candle burn on a table near the window , and at some distance from the old four - post bedstead , shade by dark moreen curtain . the surgeon look round the room , and then fumble in his pocket for his spectacle , without the aid of which the outside world present itself to he under a blurred and uncertain aspect . 

 he put on his spectacle , and move towards the bed ; but the first glance in that direction show he what have happen . the outline of the rigid figure under the coverlet look like a sculptured effigy upon a tomb . a sheet be draw over the face of death . 

 ' you be too late to be of any use , Mr. Evans , ' murmur Steadman , lay his hand upon the doctor 's sleeve and draw he away towards the door . 

 they go softly on to the landing , off which open the door of that other sick - room where the landlady 's brother be lie . 

 ' when do this happen ? ' 

 ' a quarter of an hour after the messenger ride off to fetch you , ' answer Steadman . ' his lordship lie all the afternoon in a heavy sleep , and we think he be go on well ; but after dark there be a difficulty in his breathing which alarm her ladyship , and she insist upon you be send for . the messenger have hardly be go a quarter of an hour when his lordship wake suddenly , mutter to himself in a curious way , give just one long draw sigh , and and all be over . it be a terrible shock for her ladyship . ' 

 ' indeed it must have be , ' murmur the village doctor . ' it be a great surprise to I . I know Lord Maulevrier be low , very low , the pulse feeble and intermittent ; but I have no fear of anything of this kind . it be very sudden . ' 

 ' yes , it be awfully sudden , ' say Steadman , and then he murmur in the doctor 's ear , ' you will give the necessary certificate , I hope , with as little trouble to her ladyship as possible . this be a dreadful blow , and she - - ' 

 ' she shall not be trouble . the body will be remove to - morrow , I suppose . ' 

 ' yes . he must be bury from his own house . I send a second messenger to Ambleside for the undertaker . he will be here very soon , no doubt , and if the shell be ready by noon to - morrow , the body can be remove then . I have arrange to get her ladyship away to - night . ' 

 ' so late ? after midnight ? ' 

 ' why not ? she can not stay in this small house so near the dead . there be a moon , and there be no snow fall , and we be within seven mile of Fellside . ' 

 the doctor have nothing furth to say against the arrangement , although such a drive seem to he a somewhat wild and reckless proceeding . Mr. Steadman 's grave , self - possess manner answer all doubt . Mr. Evans fill in the certificate for the undertaker , drink a glass of hot brandy and water , and remount his nag , in nowise relish his midnight ride , but console himself with the reflection that he would be handsomely pay for his trouble . 

 an hour later Lady Maulevrier 's travel carriage stand ready in the stable yard , in the deep shadow of wall and gable . it be at Steadman 's order that the carriage wait for her ladyship at an obscure side door , rather than in front of the inn . an east wind be blow keenly along the mountain road , and the careful Steadman be anxious his mistress should not be expose to that chilly blast . 

 there be some delay , and the four horse jingle their bit impatiently , and then the door of the inn open , a feeble light gleam in the narrow passage within , Steadman stand ready to assist her ladyship , there be a bustle , a confusion of dark figure on the threshold , a huddled mass of cloak and fur wrap be lift into the carriage , the door be clap to , the horse go clatter out of the yard , turn sharply into the snowy road , and start at a swing pace towards the dark sullen bulk of Loughrigg Fell . 

 the moon be shin upon Elterwater in the valley yonder the mountain ridge , the deep gorge below those sullen height , look back where the shadow of night enfold they , but all along the snow - white road the silver light shine full and clear , and the mountain way look like a path through fairyland . 

 CHAPTER V. 

 forty year after . 


 ' what a horrid day ! ' say Lady Mary , throw down her book with a yawn , and look out of the deep bay window into a world of mountain and lake which be cloud over by a dense veil of rain and dull grey mist ; such rain as one see only in a lake district , a curtain of gloom which shut off sky and distance , and narrow the world to one solitary dwelling , suspend amidst cloud and water , like another ark in a new deluge . 

 rain such rain as make out - of - door exercise impossible be always an affliction to Lady Mary Haselden . her delight be in open air and sunshine fish in the lake and river sit in some shelter hollow of the hill more fitting for an eagle 's nest than for the occupation of a young lady , try to paint those ever - vary , unpaintable mountain peak , which change their hue with every change of the sky swimming , rid , rove far and wide over hill and heather pleasure all more or less masculine in their nature , and which be a subject of regret with Lady Maulevrier . 

 Lady Lesbia be of a different temper . she love ease and elegance , the gracious luxury of life . she love art and music , but not to labour hard at either . she play and sing a little excellently within that narrow compass which she have allot to herself play Mendelssohn 's ' Lieder ' with finished touch and faultless phrasing , sing Heine 's ballad with consummate expression . she paint not at all . why should anyone draw or paint indifferently , she ask , when Providence have furnish the world with so many great painter in the past and present ? she could not understand Mary 's ardent desire to do the thing herself , to be able with her own pencil and her own brush to reproduce the lake and valley , the wild brown hill she love so passionately . Lesbia do not care two straw for the lovely lake district amidst which she have be rear , every pike and force , every beck and gill whereof be distinctly dear to her young sister . she think it a very hard thing to have spend so much of her life at Fellside , a trial that would have hardly be endurable if it be not for grandmother . Grandmother and Lesbia adore each other . Lesbia be the one person for whom Lady Maulevrier 's stateliness be subjugate by perfect love . to all the rest of the world the Countess be marble , but to Lesbia she be wax . Lesbia could mould she as she please ; but happily Lesbia be not the kind of young person to take advantage of this privilege ; she be thoroughly ductile or docile , and have no desire , at present , which run counter to her grandmother . 

 Lesbia be a beauty . in her nineteenth year she be a curious reproduction in face and figure , expression and carriage , of that Lady Diana Angersthorpe who five and forty year ago flutter the dove - cot of St. James 's and Mayfair by her brilliant beauty and her keen intelligence . there in the panel drawing - room at Fellside hang Harlow 's portrait of Lady Diana in her zenith , in a short - waiste , white satin frock , with large puff gauze sleeve , through which the perfect arm show dimly . stand under that picture Lady Lesbia look as if she have step out of the canvas . she be to be paint by Millais next year . Lady Maulevrier say , when she have be introduce , and society be begin to talk about she : for Lady Maulevrier make up her mind five or six year ago that Lesbia should be the reign beauty of her season . to this end she have educate and train she , furnish she with all those grace well calculate to please and astonish society . she be too clever a woman not to discover Lesbia 's shallowness and lack of all great gift , save that one peerless dower of perfect beauty . she know exactly what Lesbia could be train to do ; and to this end Lesbia have be educate ; and to this end Lady Maulevrier bring down to Fellside the most accomplished of hanoverian governess , who have learn French in Paris , and have toil in the educational mill with profit to herself and her pupil for a quarter of a century . to this lady the Countess entrust the education of her granddaughter ' mind , while for their physical training she provide another teacher in the person of a clever little parisian dancing mistress , who have set up at the West - end of London as a teacher of dancing and calisthenic , and have utterly fail to find pupil enough to pay her rent and keep her modest pot - au - feu going . Mademoiselle Thiebart be very glad to exchange the uncertainty of a first floor in North Audley Street for the comfort and security of Fellside Manor , with a salary of one hundred and fifty pound a year . 

 both Fršulein and Mademoiselle have be quick to discover that Lady Lesbia be the apple of her grandmother 's eye , while Lady Mary be comparatively an outsider . 

 so it come about that Mary 's education be in somewise a mere picking - up of the crumb which fall from Lesbia 's table , and that she be allow in a general way to run wild . she be much quick at any intellectual exercise than Lesbia . she learn the lesson that be give she at railroad speed , and rattle off her exercise with a slap - dash penmanship which horrify the neat and niggle Fršulein , and then rush off to the lake or mountain , and by this mean grow browner and browner , and more indelibly freckle day by day , thus widen the gulf between herself and her beauty sister . 

 but it be not to be suppose that because Lesbia be beautiful , Mary be plain . this be very far from the truth . Mary have splendid hazel eye , with a dancing light in they when she smile , ruddy auburn hair , white tooth , a deeply - dimple chin , and a vivacity and archness of expression , which serve only in her present state of tutelage for the subjugation of old woman and shepherd boy . Mary have be teach to believe that her chance of future promotion be of the small ; that nobody would ever talk of she , or think of she by - and - by when she in her turn would make her appearance in London society , and that it would be a very happy thing for she if she be so fortunate as to attract the attention of a fashionable physician , a Canon of Westminster or St. Paul 's , or a barrister in good practice . 

 Mary turn up her pert little nose at this humdrum lot . 

 ' I would much rather spend all my life among these dear hill than marry a nobody in London , ' she say , fearless of that grand old lady at whose frown so many people shiver . ' if you do not   think people will like I and admire I a little you have well save yourself the trouble of take I to London . I do not   want to play second fiddle to my sister . ' 

 ' you be a very impertinent person , and deserve to be take at your word , ' reply my lady , scowl at she ; ' but I have no doubt before you be twenty you will tell another story . ' 

 ' oh ! ' say Mary , now just turn seventeen , ' then I be not to come out till I be twenty . ' 

 ' that will be soon enough , ' answer the Countess . ' it will take you as long to get rid of those odious freckle . and no doubt by that time Lesbia will have make a brilliant marriage . ' 

 and now on this rainy July morning these two girl , neither of whom have any serious employment for her life , or any serious purpose in living , waste the hour , each in her own fashion . 

 Lesbia recline upon a cushion seat in the deep embrasure of a Tudor window , her pose perfection it be one of many such attitude which Mademoiselle have teach she , and which by assiduous training have become a second nature . poor Mademoiselle , have finish her mission and teach Lesbia all she could teach , have now depart to a new and far less luxurious situation in a finishing school at Passy ; but Fršulein M ller be still retain , as watch - dog and duenna . 

 Lesbia 's pale blue morning gown harmonise exquisitely with a complexion of lily and rose , violet eye , and golden - brown hair . her feature be distinguish by that perfect chiselling which give such a haughty grace to her grandmother 's countenance , even at sixty - seven year of age a loveliness which , like the sculptured marble it resemble , be unalterable by time . Lesbia be read Keats . it be her habit to read the poet , carefully and deliberately , take up one at a time , and duly lay a volume aside when she find herself mistress of its content . she have no passion for poetry , but it be an elegant leisurely kind of reading which suit her languid temperament . moreover , her grandmother have tell she that an easy familiarity with the great poet be of all knowledge that which good qualify a woman to shine in conversation , without offend the superior sex by any assumption of scholarship . 

 Mary be a very different class of reader ; capricious , omniverous , tear out the heart of book , roam from flower to flower in the field of literature , love old and new , romance and reality , novel , travel , play , poetry , and never dwell long on any one theme . perhaps if Mary have live in the bosom of a particularly sympathetic family she might have be reckon almost a genius , so much of poetry and originality be there in her free unconventional character ; but hitherto it have be Mary 's mission in life to be snub , whereby she have acquire a very poor opinion of her own talent . 

 ' oh , ' she cry with a desperate yawn , while Lesbia smile her languid smile over endymion , ' how I wish something would happen anything to stir we out of this statuesque , sleep - beauty state of be . I verily believe the spider be all asleep in the ivy , and the mouse behind the wainscot , and the horse in the stable . ' 

 ' what could happen ? ' ask Lesbia , with a gentle elevation of pencil brow . ' be not these lovely line 

 " and coverlid gold - tinted like the peach , 
 or ripe October 's fade marigold , 
 fall sleek about he in a thousand fold . " 
 Faded marigold ! be not that intensely sweet ? ' 

 ' very well for your sleepy Keats , but I do not   suppose you would have notice the passage if marigold be not in fashion , ' say Mary , with a touch of scorn . ' what could happen ? why a hundred thing an earthquake , flood , or fire . what could happen , do you say , Lesbia ? why Maulevrier might come home unexpectedly , and charm we out of this death - in - life . ' 

 ' he would occasion a good deal of unpleasantness if he do , ' answer Lesbia , coldly . ' you know how angry he have make grandmother . '

 ' not at all . that would make hardly any difference , except that he would not ruin himself quite so quickly . Grandmother say that a young man who go on the turf be sure to be ruin soon or later . and then Maulevrier 's habit be altogether wild and foolish . it be very hard upon grandmother , who have such noble ambition for all of we . '

' her view about I be very humble . she consider that I shall be most fortunate if a doctor or a lawyer condescend to like I well enough to make I an offer . he might make I the offer without like I , for the sake of hear himself and his wife announce as Mr. and Lady Mary Snooks at dinner party . that would be too horrid ! but I daresay such thing have happen .
