 ' do not   talk nonsense , Mary , ' say Lesbia , loftily . ' there be no reason why you should not make a really good marriage , if you follow grandmother 's advice and do not   affect eccentricity . ' 

 ' I do not   affect eccentricity , but I be afraid I really be eccentric , ' murmur Mary , meekly , ' for I like so many thing I ought not to like , and detest so many thing which I ought to admire . ' 

 ' I daresay you will have tame down a little before you be present , ' say Lesbia , carelessly . 

 she could not even affect a profound interest in anyone but herself . she have a narrowness of mental vision which prevent she look beyond the limited circle of her own pleasure , her own desire , her own dream and hope . she be one of those strictly correct young woman who be not likely to do much harm in the world but who be just as unlikely to do any good . Mary sigh , and go back to her book , a bulky volume of travel , and try to lose herself in the sandy waste of Africa , and to be deeply interested in the source of the Congo , not , in her heart of heart , care a straw whether that far - away river come from the mountain of the moon , or from the moon itself . to - day she could not pin her mind to page which might have interest she at another time . her thought be with Lord Maulevrier , that fondly - love only brother , just seven year her senior , who have take to race - horse and bad way , and seem to be try his hard to dissipate the splendid fortune which his grandmother , the dowager Countess , have nurse so judiciously during his long minority . Maulevrier and Mary have always be what the young man call ' no end of chum . ' 

 he call she his own brown - eyed Molly , much to the annoyance of Lady Maulevrier and Lesbia ; and Mary 's life be all gladness when Maulevrier be at Fellside . she devote herself wholly to his amusement , ride and drive with he , follow on her pony when he go otter hunting , and very often abandon the pony to the care of some stray mountain youth in order to join the hunter , and go leap from stone to stone on the margin of the stream , and occasionally , in moment of wild excitement , when the hound be in full cry , splash in and out of the water , like a naiad in a neat little hunting - habit . 

 Mary look after Maulevrier 's stable when he be away , and have supreme command of a kennel of fox - terrier which cost her brother more money than the Countess would have care to know ; for in the wide area of Lady Maulevrier 's ambition there be no room for two hundred guinea fox - terrier , be they never so perfect . 

 Altogether Mary 's life be a different life when her brother be at home ; and in his absence the good part of her day be spend in think about he and fulfil the duty of her position as his representative in stable and kennel , and among certain rustic in the district , chiefly of the sporting type , who be Maulevrier 's choose ally or protťgťs . 

 never , perhaps , have two girl of patrician lineage live a more secluded life than Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter . they have know no pleasure beyond the narrow sphere of home and home friend . they have never travel they have see hardly anything of the outside world . they have never be to London or Paris , or to any city large than York ; and their visit to that centre of dissipation have be of the brief , a mere flash of mild gaiety , a horticultural show or an oratorio , and back by express train , closely guard by governess and footman , to Fellside . in the autumn , when the leaf be fall in the woode ground of Fellside , the young lady be send , still under guardianship of governess and footman , to some quiet seaside resort between Alnwick and Edinburgh , where Mary live the wild free life she love , roam about the beach , boat , shrimping , seaweed - gathering , make hard work for the governess and footman who have be send in charge of she . 

 Lady Maulevrier never accompany her granddaughter on these occasion . she be a vigorous old woman , straight as a dart , slim as a girl , active in her degree as any young athlete among those hill , and she declare that she never feel the need of change of air . the sodden shrubbery , the fall leaf , do she no harm . never within the memory of this generation have she leave Fellside . her love of this mountain retreat be a kind of culte . she have come here break spirit , perhaps break hearted , bring her dead husband from the little inn at Great Langdale forty year ago , and she have hardly leave the spot since that day . 

 in those day Fellside House be a very different kind of dwelling from the gracious modern Tudor mansion which now crown and beautify the hill - side above Grasmere Lake . it be then an old ramble stone house , with queer little room and inconvenient passage , low ceiling , thatched gable , and all manner of strange nook and corner . Lady Maulevrier be of too strictly conservative a temper to think of pull down an old house which have be in her husband 's family for generation . she leave the original cottage undisturbed , and build her new house at right angle with it , connect the two with a wide passage below and a handsome corridor above , so that access should be perfect in the event of she require the accommodation of the old quaint , low ceile room for her family or her guest . during forty year no such necessity have ever arise ; but the old house , know as the south wing , be still leave intact , the original furniture undisturbed , although the only occupant of the building be her ladyship 's faithful old house - steward , James Steadman , and his elderly wife . 

 the house which Lady Maulevrier have build for herself and her grandchild have not be create all at once , though the nucleus date forty year back be a handsome building . she have add more room as necessity or fancy dictate , now a library with bedroom over it , now a music room for Lady Lesbia and her grand piano anon a billiard - room , as an agreeable surprise for Maulevrier when he come home after a tour in America . thus the house have grow into a long low pile of Tudor masonry steep gable , heavily mullione casement , grey stone wall , curtain with the rich growth of passion - flower , magnolia , clematis , myrtle and rose and all those flower which thrive and flourish in that mild and sheltered spot . 

 the view from those mullione casement be perfect . Switzerland could give hardly any more exquisite picture than that lake shut in by hill , grand and bold in their varied outline , so rich in their colouring that the eye , dazzle with beauty , forget to calculate the actual height of those craggy peak and headland , the mind forget to despise they because they be not so lofty as Mont Blanc or the Matterhorn . the velvet sward of the hill slop steeply downward from Lady Maulevrier 's drawing - room window to the road beside the lake , and this road be so hide by the woode screen which bound her ladyship 's ground that the lake seem to lie in the green heart of her garden , a lovely , placid lake on summer day , reflect the emerald hue of the surround hill , and look like a smooth green meadow , which invite the foot passenger to cross it . 

 the house be approach by a wind carriage drive that lead up and up and up from the road beside the lake , so screened and shelter by shrubbery and pine wood , that the stranger know not whither he be go , till he come upon an opening in the wood , and the stately italian garden in front of a massive stone porch , through which he enter a spacious oak - panel hall , and anon , descend a step or two , he find himself in Lady Maulevrier 's drawing - room , and face to face with that divine view of the everlaste hill , the lake shin below he , bath in sunlight . 

 or if it be the stranger 's evil fate to come in wet weather , he see only a rain - blot landscape the blurred outline of grey mountain peak , scowl at he from the other side of a grey pool . but if the picture without be depressing , the picture within be always good to look upon , for those oak - panel or tapestrie room , communicate by richly - curtain doorway from draw room to library , from library to billiard room , be as perfect as wealth and taste could make they . Lady Maulevrier argue that as there be but one house among all the possession of her race which she care to inhabit , she have a right to make that house beautiful , and she have spar nothing upon the beautification of Fellside ; and yet she have spend much less than would have be squander by any pleasure - love dowager , restlessly rove from Piccadilly to the Engadine , from Pontresina to Nice or Monaco , wind up with Easter in Paris , and then back to Piccadilly . her ladyship 's friend wonder that she could care to bury herself alive in Westmoreland , and expatiate on the eccentricity of such a life ; nay , those who have never see Fellside argue that Lady Maulevrier have take in her old age to hoarding , and that she pig at a cottage in the Lake district , in order to swell a fortune which young Maulevrier would set about squandering as soon as she be in her coffin . but here they be wrong . it be not in Lady Maulevrier 's nature to lead a sordid life in order to save money . yet in these quiet year that be go start with that golden nucleus which her husband be suppose to have bring home from India , obtain no one know how , the Countess have amass one of the large fortune possess by any dowager in the peerage . she have it , and she hold it , with a grasp that nothing but death could loosen ; nay , that all - foresee mind of hers might contrive to cheat grim death itself , and to scheme a way for protect this wealth , even when she who have gather and garner it should be moulder in her grave . the entail estate belong to Maulevrier , be he never such a fool or spendthrift ; but this fortune of the dowager 's be her own , to dispose of as she please , and not a penny of it be likely to go to the young Earl . 

 Lady Maulevrier 's pride and hope be concentrate upon her granddaughter Lesbia . she should be the inheritress of this noble fortune she should spread and widen the power of the Maulevrier race . Lesbia 's son should link the family name with the name of his father ; and if by any hazard of fate the present Earl should die young and childless , the old Countess 's interest should be strain to the uttermost to obtain the title for Lesbia 's offspring . why should she not be Countess of Maulevrier in her own right ? but in order to make this future possible the most important factor in the sum be yet to be find in the person of a husband for Lady Lesbia a husband worthy of peerless beauty and exceptional wealth , a husband whose own fortune should be so important as to make he above suspicion . that be Lady Maulevrier 's scheme to we d wealth to wealth to double or quadruple the fortune she have build up in the long slow year of her widowhood , and thus to make her granddaughter one of the great lady in the land ; for it need hardly be say that the man who be to we d Lady Lesbia must be she equal in wealth and lineage , if not her superior . 

 Lady Maulevrier be not a miser . she be liberal and benevolent to all who come within the circle of her life . wealth for its own sake she value not a jot . but she live in an age in which wealth be power , and ambition be her rule passion . as she have be ambitious for her husband in the day that be go , she be now ambitious for her granddaughter . Time have intensify the keen eagerness of her mind . she have be disappoint , cruelly , bitterly , in the ambition of her youth . she have be make to drink the cup of shame and humiliation . but to this ambition of her old age she hold with even great tenacity . God help she if she should be disappoint here ! 

 it be not to be suppose that so astute a schemer as Lady Maulevrier have not survey the marriage market in order to discover that fortunate youth who should be deem worthy to become the winner of Lesbia 's hand . year ago , when Lesbia be still in the nursery , the dowager have make herself informed of the age , weight , and colour of every likely runner in the matrimonial stake ; or , in plainer word , have keep herself , by her correspondence with a few intimate friend , and her close study of the fashionable newspaper , thoroughly acquaint with the character and exploit , the disposition and antecedent , of those half - dozen eld son , among whom she hop to find Lesbia 's lord and master . she know her peerage by heart , and she know the family history of every house record therein ; the sin and weakness , the folly and loss of bygone year ; the taint , mental and physical ; the lateral branch and intermarriage ; the runaway wife and unfaithful husband ; idiot son or scrofulous daughter . she know everything that be to be know about that aristocratic world into which she have be bear sixty - seven year ago ; and the sum - total of her knowledge be that there be one man whom she desire for her granddaughter 's husband one man , and one only , and into whose hand , when earth and sky should fade from her glaze eye , she could be content to resign the sceptre of power . 

 there be no doubt half - a - dozen , or more , in the list of eld son , who be fairly eligible . but this young man be the Achilles in the rank and file of chivalry , and her soul yearn to have he and no other for her darling . 

 her soul yearn to he with a tenderness which be not all on Lesbia 's account . forty - nine year ago she have fondly love his father love he and have be fain to renounce he ; for Ronald Hollister , afterwards Earl of Hartfield , be then a young son , and the two family have agree that marriage between pauper be an impudent fly in the face of Providence , which must be put down with an iron hand . Lord Hartfield send his son to Turkey in the diplomatic service ; and the old dowager Lady Carrisbrook whisk her niece off to London , and keep she there , under watch and ward , till Lord Maulevrier propose and be accept by she . there should be no foolishness , no clandestine correspondence . the iron hand crush two young heart , and secure a brilliant future for the body which survive . 

 fifteen year later Ronald 's eld brother die unmarried . he abandon that career of vagrant diplomacy which have take he all over Europe , and as far as Washington , and re - appear in London , the most elegant man of his era , but thoroughly blasť . there be rumour of an unhappy attachment in the Faubourg Saint Germain ; of a tragedy at Petersburg . society protest that Lord Hartfield would die a bachelor , as his brother die before he . the Hollisters be not a marry family , say society . but six or seven year after his return to England Lord Hartfield marry Lady Florence Ilmington , a beauty in her first season , and a very sweet but somewhat prudish young person . the marriage result in the birth of an heir , whose appearance upon this mortal stage be follow within a year by his father 's exit . hence the Hartfield property , always a fine estate , have be nurse and fatten during a long minority , and the present Lord Hartfield be repute one of the rich young man of his time . he be also speak of as a superior person , inherit all his father 's intellectual gift , and have the reputation of be singularly free from the vice of profligate youth . he be neither prig nor pedant , and he be very popular in the good society ; but he be not ashamed to let it be see that his ambition soar high than the fashionable world of turf and stable , card and pigeon match . 

 though not of the gay world , nor in it , Lady Maulevrier have contrive to keep herself thoroughly en rapport with society . her few choose friend , with whom she correspond on term of perfect confidence , be among the good people in London not the circulator of club - house canard , the picker - up of second - hand gossip from the society paper , but actor in the comedy of high life , arbiter of fashion and taste , bear and breed in the purple . 

 last season Lord Hartfield 's absence have cast a cloud over the matrimonial horizon . he have be a traveller for more than a year Patagonia , Peru , the Pyramids , Japan , the North Pole society care not where the fact that he be go be all - sufficient . bachelor a shade less eligible come to the front in his absence and become first favourite . Lady Maulevrier , well inform in advance , have defer Lesbia 's presentation till next season , when she be tell Lord Hartfield would certainly re - appear . his plan have be make for return before Christmas ; and it would seem that his scheme of life be lay down with as much precision as if he have be a prince of the blood royal . thus it happen , to Lesbia 's intense disgust , that her dťbut be defer till the verge of her twentieth birthday . it would never do , Lady Maulevrier tell herself , for the edge to be take off the effect which Lesbia 's beauty be to make on society during Lord Hartfield 's absence . he must be there , on the spot , to see this star rise gently and slowly above society 's horizon , and to mark how everybody bow down and worship the new light . 

 ' I shall be an old woman before I appear in society , ' say Lesbia , petulantly ; ' and I shall be like a wild woman of the wood ; for I have see nothing , and know nothing of the civilised world . ' 

 ' you will be ever so much more attractive than the young woman I hear of , who have see and know a great deal too much , ' answer the dowager ; and as her granddaughter know that Lady Maulevrier 's word be a law that alter not , there be no more idle repining . 

 her ladyship give no reason for the postponement of Lesbia 's presentation . she be far too diplomatic to breathe a word of her idea with regard to Lord Hartfield . anything like a matrimonial scheme would have be revolt to Lesbia , who have grand , but not sordid view about matrimony . she think it her mission to appear and to conquer . a crowd of suitor would sigh around she , like the love and grace round that fair Belinda whose story she have read so often ; and it would be her part to choose the most worthy . the day be go when a girl would so much as look at such a fribble as Sir Plume . her virgin fancy demand the tennysonian ideal , the grave and knightly Arthur . 

 but when Lesbia think of the most worthy , it be always of the worthy in her own particular sphere ; and he of course would be title and wealthy , and altogether fit to be her husband . he would take she by the hand and lead she to a high seat on the dai , and place upon her head , or at least upon her letter - paper and the panel of her carriage , a coronet in which the strawberry leaf should stand out more prominently than in her brother 's emblazonment . Lesbia 's mind could not conceive an ignoble marriage , or the possibility of the most worthy happen to be find in a low circle than her own . 

 and now it be the end of July , and the season which should have be glorify by Lady Lesbia 's dťbut be over and do with . she have read in the society paper of all the ball , and birthday , and race meeting , and regatta , and cricket match , and gown , and parasol , and bonnet what this beauty wear on such an occasion , and how that other beauty look on another occasion and she feel as she read like a spell - bind princess in a fairy tale , mew up in a battlemented tower , and deprive of her legitimate share in all the pleasure of earth . she have no patience with Mary that wild , unkempt , ungraceful creature , who could be as happy as summer day be long , race about the hill with her bamboo alpenstock , riot with a pack of fox - terrier , practise long loser , row on the lake , do all thing unbecoming Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter . 

 that long rainy day drag its slow length to a close ; and then come fine day , in which molly and her fox - terrier go wander over the sunlit hill , skip and dance across the mountain streamlet gill , as they be call in this particular world almost as gaily as the shadow of fleecy cloudlet dance up yonder in the windy sky . molly spend half her day among the hill , steal off from governess and grandmother and the stately beauty sister , and sometimes hardly be miss by they , so ill do her young exuberance harmonise with their calm life . 

 ' one can tell when Mary be at home by a perpetual banging of door , ' say Lesbia , which be a sisterly exaggeration found upon fact , for Molly be give to impetuous rushing in and out of room when that eager spirit of hers impel the light lithe body upon some new expedition . nor be the society of fox - terrier conducive to repose or stateliness of movement ; and Maulevrier 's terrier , although strictly forbid the house , be for ever break bond and leap in upon Molly 's retirement at all unreasonable hour . she and they be enchant to get away from the beautiful luxurious room , and to go rove by hill - side and force , away to Easedale Tarn , to bask for hour on the grassy margin of the deep still water , or to row round and round the mountain lake in a rotten boat . it be here , or in some kindre spot , that Molly get through most of her reading here that she read Shakespeare , Byron , and Shelley , and Wordsworth dwelling lingeringly and lovingly upon every line in which that good old man speak of her native land . sometimes she climb to high ground , and feel herself ever so much near heaven upon the crest of Silver Howe , or upon the rugged stony steep of Dolly Waggon pike , half way up the dark brow of Helvellyn ; sometimes she disappear for hour , and climb to the summit of the hill , and wander in perilous pathway on Striding Edge , or by the dark still water of the Red Tarn . this have be her life ever since she have be old enough to have an independent existence ; and the hill and the lake , and the book of her own choosing , have do a great deal more in ripen her mind than Fršulein M ller and that admirable series of educational work which have be provide for the tuition of modern youth . grammar and geography , primer and elementary work of all kind , be Mary 's detestation ; but she love book that touch her heart and fill her mind with thought wide and deep enough to reach into the infinite of time and space , the mystery of mind and matter , life and death . 

 nothing occur to break the placid monotony of life at Fellside for three long day after that rainy morning ; and then come an event which , although commonplace enough in itself , mark the beginning of a new era in the existence of Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter . 

 it be evening , and the two girl be dawdle about on the slop lawn before the drawing - room window , where Lady Maulevrier read the newspaper in her own particular chair by one of those broad Tudor window , accord to her infallible custom . remote as her life bad be from the busy world , her ladyship have never allow her knowledge of public life and the bent of modern thought to fall into arrear . she take a keen interest in politic , in progress of all kind . she be a staunch conservative , and look upon every liberal politician as her personal enemy ; but she take care to keep herself informed of everything that be be say or do in the enemy 's camp . she have an intense respect for Lord Bacon 's maxim : Knowledge be power . it be a kind of power secondary to the power of wealth , perhaps ; but wealth unprotecte by wisdom would soon dwindle into poverty . 

 Lady Lesbia saunter about the lawn , look very elegant in her cream - colour indian silk gown , very listless , very tired of her lovely surrounding . neither lake nor mountain possess any charm for she . she have have too much of they . Mary roam about with a swifter footstep , look at the rose , pluck off a dead leaf , or a canker bud here and there . presently she tear across the lawn to the shrubbery which screen the lawn and flower garden from the wind carriage drive sink many foot below , and disappear in a thicket of arbutus and irish yew . 

 ' what terribly hoydenish manner ! ' murmur Lesbia , with a languid shrug of her shoulder , as she stroll back to the drawing - room . 

 she care very little for the newspaper , for politic not at all ; but anything be well than everlasting contemplation of the blue still water , and the rugged crest of Helm Crag . 

 ' what be the matter with Mary that she rush off like a mad woman ? ' inquire Lady Maulevrier , look up from the Times . 

 ' I have not   the least idea . Mary 's movement be quite beyond the limit of my comprehension . perhaps she have go after a bird's - nest . ' 

 Mary be intent upon no bird' - nest . her quick ear have catch the sound of manly voice in the wind drive under the pine wood ; and surely , yes , surely one be a clear and familiar voice , which herald the coming of happiness . in such a moment she seem to have wing . she become unconscious that she touch the earth ; she go skim bird - like over the lawn , and in and out , with flutter muslin frock , among arbutus and bay , yew and laurel , till she stand poise lightly on the top of the woode bank which border the steep ascent to Lady Maulevrier 's gate , look down at two figure which be saunter up the drive . 

 they be both young man , both tall , broad - shoulder , manly , walk with the easy swing movement of man accustom to active exercise . one , the handsomer of the two in Mary 's eye , since she think he simply perfection , be fair - haired , blue - eye , the typical Saxon . this be Lord Maulevrier . the other be dark , bronze by foreign travel , perhaps , with black hair , cut very close to an intelligent - looking head , bar to the evening breeze . 

 ' Hulloa ! ' cry Maulevrier . ' there be Molly . how d'ye do , old girl ? ' 

 the two man look up , and molly look down . delight at her brother 's return so fill her heart and mind that there be no room leave for embarrassment at the appearance of a stranger . 

 ' O , Maulevrier , I be so glad ! I have be pin for you . why do not   you write to say you be come ? it would have be something to look forward to . ' 

 ' could not . never know from day to day what I be go to be up to ; besides , I know I should find you at home . ' 

 ' of course . we be always at home , ' say Mary ; ' go up to the house as fast as ever you can . I will go and tell grandmother . ' 

 ' and tell they to get we some dinner , ' say Maulevrier . 

 Mary 's flutter figure dip and be go , vanish in the dark labyrinth of shrub . the two young man saunter up to the house . 

 ' we need not   hurry , ' say Maulevrier to his companion , whom he have not take the trouble to introduce to his sister . ' we shall have to wait for our dinner . ' 

 ' and we shall have to change our dusty clothe , ' add the other ; ' I hope that man will bring our portmanteaux in time . ' 

 ' oh , we need not   dress . we can spend the evening in my den , if you like ! ' 

 Mary fly across the lawn again , and bound up the step of the verandah a picturesque swiss verandah which make a cover promenade in front of the house . 

 ' Mary , may I ask the meaning of this excitement , ' inquire her ladyship , as the breathless girl stand before she . 

 ' Maulevrier have come home . ' 

 ' at last ? ' 

 ' and he have bring a friend . ' 

 ' indeed ! he might have do I the honour to inquire if his friend 's visit would be agreeable . what kind of person ? ' 

 ' I have no idea . I do not   look at he . Maulevrier be look so well . they will be here in a minute . may I order dinner for they ? ' 

 ' of course , they must have dinner , ' say her ladyship , resignedly , as if the whole thing be an infliction ; and Mary run out and interview the butler , beg that all thing might be make particularly comfortable for the traveller . it be nine o'clock , and the servant be enjoy their eventide repose . 

 have give her order , Mary go back to the drawing - room , impatiently expectant of her brother 's arrival , for which event Lesbia and her grandmother wait with perfect tranquillity , the dowager calmly continue the perusal of her Times , while Lesbia sit at her piano in a shadowy corner , and play one of Mendelssohn 's soft Lieder . to these dreamy strain Maulevrier and his friend presently enter . 

 ' how d'ye do , grandmother ? how do , Lesbia ? this be my very good friend and canadian travel companion , Jack Hammond Lady Maulevrier , Lady Lesbia . ' 

 ' very glad to see you , Mr. Hammond , ' say the dowager , in a tone so purely conventional that it might mean anything . ' Hammond ? I ought to remember your family the Hammonds of - - ' 

 ' of nowhere , ' answer the stranger in the easy tone ; ' I spring from a race of nobody , of whose existence your ladyship be not likely to have hear . ' 

 CHAPTER VI . 

 MAULEVRIER 's HUMBLE FRIEND . 


 that faint interest which Lady Lesbia have feel in the advent of a stranger dwindle to nothing after Mr. Hammond 's frank avowal of his insignificance . at the very beginning of her career , with the world wait to be conquer by she , a high - bear beauty could not be expect to feel any interest in nobody . Lesbia shake hand with her brother , honour the stranger with a stately bend of her beautiful throat , and then withdraw herself from their society altogether as it be , and begin to explore her basket of crewel , at a distant table , by the soft light of a shaded lamp , while Maulevrier answer his grandmother 's question , and Mary stand watch he , hang on his word , as if unconscious of any other presence . 

 Mr. Hammond go over to the window and look out at the view . the moon be rise above the amphitheatre of hill , and her ray be silver the placid bosom of the lake . light be dot here and there about the valley , telling of village life . the Prince of Wales 's hotel yonder sparkle with its many light , like a castle in a fairy tale . the stranger have look upon many a grander scene , but on none more lovely . here be lake and mountain in little , without the snow - peak and awful inaccessible region of solitude and peril ; homely hill that one might climb , placid english vale in which english poet have live and die . 

 ' Hammond and I mean to spend a month or six week with you , if you can make we comfortable , ' say Maulevrier . 

 ' I be delighted to hear that you can contemplate stay a month anywhere , ' reply her ladyship . ' your usual habit be as restless as if your life be a disease . it shall not be my fault if you and Mr. Hammond be uncomfortable at Fellside . ' 

 there be courtesy , but no cordiality in the reply . if Mr. Hammond be a sensitive man , touchily conscious of his own obscurity , he must have feel that he be not want at Fellside that he be an excrescence , matter in the wrong place . 

 nobody have present the stranger to Lady Mary . it never enter into Maulevrier 's mind to be ceremonious about his sister molly . she be so much a part of himself that it seem as if anyone who know he must need know she . molly sit a little way from the window by which Mr. Hammond be stand , and look at he doubtfully , wonderingly , with not altogether a friendly eye , as he stand with his profile turn to she , and his eye upon the landscape . she be inclined to be jealous of her brother 's friend , who would most likely deprive she of much of that beloved society . Hitherto she have be Maulevrier 's choose companion , at Fellside indeed , his sole companion after the dismissal of his tutor . now this brown , bearded stranger would usurp her privilege those two young man would go roam over the hill , fishing , otter - hunting , go to distant wrestling match and leave she at home . it be a hard thing , and she be prepared to detest the interloper . even to - night she would be a loser by his presence . under ordinary circumstance she would have go to the dining - room with Maulevrier , and sit by he and wait upon he as he eat . but she dare not intrude herself upon a meal that be to be share with a stranger . 

 she look at John Hammond critically , eager to find fault with his appearence ; but unluckily for her present humour there be not much room for fault - finding . 

 he be tall , broad - shoulder , well - build . his enemy would hardly deny that he be good - look nay , even handsome . the massive regular feature be irreproachable . he be more sunburnt than a gentleman ought to be , Mary think . she tell herself that his good look be of a vulgar quality , like those of Charles Ford , the champion wrestler , whom she see at the sport the other day . why do Maulevrier pick up a companion who be evidently not of his own sphere ? hoydenish , plain - speak , frank and affectionate as Mary Haselden be , she know that she belong to a race apart , that there be circle beneath circle , below her own world , circle which hers could never touch , and she suppose Mr. Hammond to be some waif from one of those nethermost world a village doctor 's son , perhaps , or even a tradesman 's send to the University by some benevolent busybody , and place at a disadvantage ever afterwards , an unfortunate anomaly , suspend between two world like Mahomet 's coffin . 

 the butler announce that his lordship 's dinner be serve . 

 ' come along , molly , ' say Maulevrier ; ' come and tell I about the terrier , while I eat my dinner . ' 

 Mary hesitate , glance doubtfully at her grandmother , who make no sign , and then slip out of the room , hang fondly on her brother 's arm , and almost forget that there be any such person as Mr. Hammond in existence . 

 when these three be go Lady Lesbia express herself strongly upon Maulevrier 's folly in bring such a person as Mr. Hammond to Fellside . 

 ' what be we to do with he , grandmother ? ' she say , pettishly . ' be he to live with we , and be one of we , a person of whose belonging we know positively nothing , who own that his people be common ? ' 

 ' my dear , he be your brother 's friend , and we have the right to suppose he be a gentleman . ' 

 ' not on that account , ' say Lesbia , more sharply than she will not . ' do not   he make a friend , or almost a friend of Jack Howell , the huntsman , and of Ford , the wrestler . I have no confidence in Maulevrier 's idea of fitness . ' 

 ' we shall find out all about this Mr. Hamleigh no Hammond in a day or two , ' reply her ladyship , placidly ; ' and in the meantime we must tolerate he , and be grateful to he if he reconcile Maulevrier to remain at Fellside for the next six week . ' 

 Lesbia be silent . she do not consider Maulevrier 's presence at Fellside an unmitigated advantage , or , indeed , his presence anywhere . those two be not sympathetic . Maulevrier make fun of his eld sister 's perfection , chaff she intolerably about the great man she be go to captivate , in her first season , the great house in which she be go to reign . Lesbia despise he for that neglect of all his opportunity of culture which have leave he , after the most orthodox and costly curriculum , almost as ignorant as a ploughboy . she despise a man whose only delight be in horse and hound , gun and fishing - tackle . molly would have care very little for the gun or the fishing - tackle perhaps in the abstract ; but she care for everything that interested Maulevrier , even to the bagful of rat which be let loose in the stable - yard sometimes , for the education of a particularly game fox - terrier . 

 there be plenty of talk and laughter at the dinner - table , while the Countess and Lady Lesbia converse gravely and languidly in the dimly - light drawing - room . the dinner be excellent , and both traveller be ravenous . they have eat nothing since breakfast , and have drive from windermere on the top of the coach in the keen evening air . when the sharp edge of the appetite be blunt , Maulevrier begin to talk of his adventure since he and Molly have last meet . he have not be dissipate in London all the time or , indeed , any great part of the time of his absence from Fellside ; but Molly have be leave in Cimmerian , darkness as to his proceeding . he never write a letter if he could possibly avoid do so . if it become a vital necessity to he to communicate with anyone he telegraph , or , in his own language , ' wire ' to that person ; but to sit down at a desk and labour with pen and ink be not within his capacity or his view of his mission in life . 

 ' if a fellow be to write letter he might as well be a clerk in an office , ' he say , ' and sit on a high stool . ' 

 thus it happen that when Maulevrier be away from Fellside , no fair ch telaine of the Middle Ages could be more ignorant of the movement or whereabout of her crusader knight than Mary be of her brother 's going on . she could but pray for he with fond and faithful prayer , and wait and hope for his return . and now he tell she that thing have go badly with he at Epsom , and bad at Ascot , that he have be , as he express it , ' up a tree , ' and that he have go off to the Black Forest directly the Ascot week be over , and at Rippoldsau he have meet his old friend and fellow traveller , Hammond , and they have go for a walk tour together among the homely village , the watchmaker , the timber cutter , the pretty peasant girl . they have dance at fair and shoot at village sport and have altogether enjoy the thing . Hammond , who be something of an artist , have sketch a good deal . Maulevrier have do nothing but smoke his german pipe and enjoy himself . 

 ' I be glad to find myself in a world where a horse be an exception and not the rule , ' he say .

 ' of course , ' answer Mr. Hammond ; ' Mephistopheles be our valet de place , and we go up among a company of witch rid on broomstick . ' and then quote , 

 ' Seh ' die Bšume hinter Bšumen , 
 Wie sie schnell vor berr cken , 
 Und die Klippen , die sich b cken , 
 Und die langen Felsennasen , 
 Wie sie schnarchen , wie sie blasen ! ' 
 this be the first time he have address himself directly to Mary , who sit close to her brother 's side , and never take her eye from his face , ready to pour out his wine or to change his plate , for the serve - man have be dismiss at the beginning of this unceremonious meal . 

 Mary look at the stranger almost as superciliously as Lesbia might have do . she be not inclined to be friendly to her brother 's friend . 

 ' do you read German ? ' she inquire , with a touch of surprise . 

 ' you have well ask he what language he do not read or speak , ' say her brother . ' Hammond be an admirable Crichton , my dear by - the - by , who be admirable Crichton ? know everything , can twist your little head the right way upon any subject . ' 

 ' oh , ' think Mary , ' highly cultivate , be he ? very proper in a man who be educate on charity to have work his hard at the University . ' 

 she be not prepared to think very kindly of young man who have be successful in their college career , since poor Maulevrier have make such a dismal failure of his , have be gate and send down , and plough , and have everything ignominious do to he that could be do , which ignominy have involve an expenditure of money that Lady Maulevrier bemoan and lament until this day . because her brother have not be virtuous , Mary grudge virtuous young man their triumph and their honour . great , raw - boned fellow , who have take their degree at Scotch Universities , come to Oxford and Cambridge and sweep the board , Maulevrier have tell she , when his own failure demand explanation . perhaps this Mr. Hammond have graduate north of the Tweed , and have come southward to rob the native . Mary be not any more inclined to be civil to he because he be a linguist . he have a pleasant manner , frank and easy , a good voice , a cheery laugh . but she have not yet make up her mind that he be a gentleman . 

 ' if some benevolent old person be to take a fancy to Charles Ford , the wrestler , and send he to a Scotch University , I daresay he would turn out just as fine a fellow , ' she think , Ford be somewhat of a favourite as a local hero . 

 the two young man go off to the billiard - room after they have din . it be half - past ten by this time , and , of course , Mary do not go with they . she bid her brother good - night at the dining - room door . 

 ' good - night , molly ; be sure you be up early to show I the dog , ' say Maulevrier , after an affectionate kiss . 

 ' good - night , Lady Mary , ' say Mr. Hammond , hold out his hand , albeit she have no idea of shake hand with he . 

 she allow her hand to rest for an instant in that strong , friendly grasp . she have not rise to give a couple of finger to a person whom she consider her inferior ; but she be inclined to snub Mr. Hammond as rather a presume young man . 

 ' well , Jack , what do you think of my beauty sister ? ' ask his lordship , as he choose his cue from the well - fill rack . 

 the lamp be light , the table uncover and ready , Carambole in his place , albeit it be month since any player have enter the room . everything which concern Maulevrier 's comfort or pleasure be do as if by magic at Fellside ; and Mary be the household fairy whose influence secure this happy state of thing . 

 ' what can any man think except that she be as lovely as the fine of Reynold 's portrait , as that Lady Diana Beauclerk of Colonel Aldridge 's , or the Kitty Fisher , or any example you please to name of womanly loveliness ? '

' shall I break ? ' and he begin with the usual miss in baulk .

 ' perfectly . she have be bury alive here ; except parson and a few decent people whom she be allow to meet now and then at the house about here , she have see nothing of the world . my grandmother have keep Lesbia as close as a nun . she be not so fond of Molly , and that young person have wild way of her own , and give everybody the slip . by - the - by , how do you like my little Moll ? ' 

 the adjective be hardly accurate about a young lady who measure five foot six , but Maulevrier have not yet grow out of the idea belong to that period when Mary be really his little sister , a girl of twelve , with long hair and short petticoat .
