' 

 John Hammond be use to his lordship 's style of conversation , and understand his friend at all time . Maulevrier be not an intellectual companion , and the distance be wide between the two man ; but his lordship 's gaiety , good - nature , and acuteness make amend for all shortcoming in culture . and then Mr. Hammond may have be one of those good conservative who do not expect very much intellectual power in an hereditary legislator . 

 CHAPTER VII . 

 in the SUMMER morning . 


 John Hammond love the wild freshness of morning , and be always eager to explore a new locality ; so he be up at five o'clock next morning , and out of door before six . he leave the sophisticated beauty of the Fellside garden below he , and climb higher and high up the Fell , till he be able to command a bird's - eye view of the lake and village , and just under his foot , as it be , Lady Maulevrier 's favourite abode . he be provide with a landscape glass which he always carry in his ramble , and with the aid of this he could see every stone of the building . 

 the house , add to at her ladyship 's pleasure , and without regard to cost , cover a considerable extent of ground . the new part consist of a straight range of about a hundred and twenty foot , face the lake , and commandingly place on the crest of a steepish slope ; the old building , at right angle with the new , make a quadrangle , the third and fourth side of which be form by the dead wall of servant ' room and coach - house , which have no window upon this inner enclose side . the old building be low and irregular , one portion of the roof thatch , another tile . in the quadrangle there be an old - fashioned garden , with geometrical flower - bed , a yew tree hedge , and a stone sun - dial in the centre . a peacock stalk about in the morning light , and greet the newly rise sun with a discordant scream . presently a man come out of a half glass door under a verandah which shade one side of the quadrangle , and stroll about the garden , stop here and there to cut a dead rose , or trim a geranium , a stoutly - build broad shoulder man , with gray hair and beard , the image of well - feed respectability . 

 Mr. Hammond wonder a little at the man 's leisurely movement as he saunter about , whistle to the peacock . it be not the manner of a servant who have duty to perform rather that of a gentleman live at ease , and hardly know how to get rid of his time . 

 " some superior functionary , I suppose , " think Hammond , " the house - steward , perhaps . " 

 he ramble a long way over the hill , and come back to Fellside by a path of his own discovering , which bring he to a wooden gate lead into the stable - yard , just in time to meet Maulevrier and Lady Mary emerge from the kennel , where his lordship have be inspect the terrier . 

 ' Angelina be bully about the muzzle , ' say Maulevrier ; ' we shall have to give she away . ' 

 ' oh , do not , ' cry Mary . ' she be a most perfect darling , and laugh so deliciously whenever she see I . ' 

 Angelina be in Lady Mary 's arm at this moment ; a beautifully mark little creature , all thew and sinew , palpitate with suppress emotion , and grin to her heart 's content . 

 Lady Mary look very fresh and bright in her neat tailor gown , kilte kirtle , and tight - fitting bodice , with neat little brass button . it be a gown of Maulevrier 's ordering , make at his own tailor 's . her splendid chestnut hair be uncover , the short crisp curl about her forehead dance in the morning air . her large , bright , brown eye be dance , too , with delight at have her brother home again . 

 she shake hand with Mr. Hammond more graciously than last night ; but still with a carelessness which be not complimentary , look at he absently , as if she hardly know that he be there , and hug Angelina all the time . 

 Hammond tell his friend about his ramble over the hill , yonder , up above that homely bench call ' rest , and be thankful , ' on the crest of Loughrigg Fell . he be begin to learn the name of the hill already . Yonder darkle brow , rugged , gloomy looking , be Nab Scar ; yonder green slope of sunny pasture , stretch wide its two arm as if to enfold the valley , be Fairfield ; and here , close on the left , as he face the lake , be Silver Howe and Helm Crag , with that stony excrescence on the summit of the latter know as the ' Lion and the Lamb . ' Lady Maulevrier 's house stand within a circle of mountain peak and long fell , which wall in the deep , placid , fertile valley . 

 ' if you be not too tired to see the garden , we might show they to you before breakfast , ' say Maulevrier . ' we have three - quarter of an hour to the good . ' 

 ' half an hour for a stroll , and a quarter to make myself presentable after my long walk , ' say Hammond , who do not wish to face the dowager and Lady Lesbia in disorder apparel . Lady Mary be such an obvious Tomboy that he might be pardon for leave she out of the question . 

 they set out upon an exploration of the garden , Mary cling to her brother 's arm , as if she want to make sure of he , and still carry Angelina . 

 the garden be as other garden , but pass beautiful . the slop lawn and richly - timbere bank , wind shrubbery , broad terrace cut on the side of the hill , give infinite variety . all that wealth and taste and labour could do to make those ground beautiful have be do the rare conifer , the lovely flower shrub grow and flourish there , and the flower bloom as they bloom only in Lakeland , where every cottage garden can show a wealth of luxurious bloom , unknown in more exposed and arid district . Mary be very proud of those garden . she have love they and work in they from her babyhood , trot about on chubby leg after some choose old gardener , carry a few weed or withered leaf in her pinafore , and fancy herself useful . 

 ' I help ' oo , do not   I , Teeven ? ' she use to say to the gray - head old gardener , who first teach she to distinguish flower from weed . 

 ' I shall never learn as much out of these horrid book as poor old Stevens teach I , ' she say afterwards , when the gray head be at rest under the sod , and governess , botany manual , and hard word from the Greek be the order of the day . 

 nine o'clock be the breakfast hour at Fellside . there be no family prayer . Lady Maulevrier do not pretend to be pious , and she put no restraint of piety upon other people . she go to Church on Sunday morning for the sake of example ; but she read all the new scientific book , subscribe to the Anthropological Society , and think as the new scientific people think . she rarely communicate her opinion among her own sex ; but now and then , in strictly masculine and superior society , she have be hear to express herself freely upon the nebular hypothesis and the doctrine of evolution . 

 ' after all , what do it matter ? ' she say , finally , with her grand air ; ' I have only to marry my granddaughter creditably , and prevent my grandson go to the dog , and then my mission on this insignificant planet will be accomplish . what new form that particular modification of molecule which you call Lady Maulevrier may take afterwards be hide in the great mystery of material life . ' 

 there be no family prayer , therefore , at Fellside . the sister have be properly educate in their religious duty , have be teach the anglican faith carefully and well by their governess , Fršulein M ller , who have become a staunch anglican before enter the family of the english nobility , and by the kind Vicar of Grasmere , who take a warm interest in the orphan girl . their grandmother have give they to understand that they might be as religious as they like . she would be no let or hindrance to their piety ; but they must ask she no awkward question . 

 ' I have read a great deal and think a great deal , and my idea be still in a state of transition , ' she tell Lesbia ; and Lesbia , who be somewhat automatic in her piety , have no desire to know more . 

 Lady Maulevrier seldom appear in the forenoon . she be an early riser , be too vivid and highly string a creature , even at sixty - seven year of age , to give way to sloth . she rise at seven , summer and winter , but she spend the early part of the day in her own room , reading , writing , give order to her housekeeper , and occasionally interview Steadman , who , without any onerous duty , be certainly the most influential person in the house . People in the village talk of he , and envy he so good a berth . he have a gentleman 's house to live in , and to all appearance live as a gentleman . this tranquil retirement , free from care or labour , be a rich reward for the faithful service of his youth . and it be know by the well informed among the Grasmere people that Mr. Steadman be save money , and have share in the north - Western Railway . these fact have ooze out , of themselves , as it be . he be not a communicative man , and rarely waste half an hour at the snug little inn near St. Oswald 's Church , amidst the cluster of habitation that be once call Kirktown . he be an unsociable man , people say , and think himself well than Grasmere folk , the lodge - house keeper , and guide , and wrestler , and the honest friendly soul who be the outcome of that band of norwegian exile which find a home in these peaceful vale . 

 Miss M ller , more commonly know as Fršulein , officiate at breakfast . she never appear at the board when Lady Maulevrier be present , but in her ladyship 's absence Miss M ller be guardian of the propriety . she be a stout , kindly creature , and by no mean a formidable dragon . when the gong sound , John Hammond go into the dining - room , where he find Miss M ller seat alone in front of the urn . 

 he bow , quick to read ' governess ' or ' companion ' in the lady 's appearance ; and she bow . 

 ' I hope you have have a nice walk , ' she say . ' I see you from my bedroom window . ' 

 ' do you ? then I suppose yours be one of the few window which look into that curious old quadrangle ? ' 

 ' no , there be no window look into the quadrangle . those that be in the original plan of the house be wall up at her ladyship 's order , to keep out the cold wind which sweep down from the hill in winter and early spring , when the edge of Loughrigg Fell be white with snow . my window look into the garden , and I see you there with his lordship and Lady Mary . ' 

 Lady Lesbia come in at this moment , and salute Mr. Hammond with a haughty inclination of her beautiful head . she look lovelier in her simple morning gown of pale blue cambric than in her more elaborate toilette of last evening ; such purity of complexion , such lustrous eye ; the untarnished beauty of youth , breathe the delicate freshness of a newly - open flower . she might be as scornful as she please , yet John Hammond could not withhold his admiration . he be inclined to admire a woman who keep he at a distance ; for the general bent of young woman now - a - day be otherwise . 

 Maulevrier and Mary come in , and everyone sit down to breakfast . Lady Lesbia unbent a little presently , and smile upon the stranger . there be a relief in a stranger 's presence . he talk of new thing , place and people she have never see . she brighten and become quite friendly , deign to invite the expression of Mr. Hammond 's opinion upon music and art , and after breakfast allow he to follow she into the drawing - room , and to linger there fascinate for half an hour , look over her new book , and her last batch of music , but look most of all at she , while Maulevrier and Mary be loaf on the lawn outside . 

 ' what be you go to do with yourself this morning ? ' ask Maulevrier , appear suddenly at the window . 

 ' anything you like , ' answer Hammond . ' stay , there be one pilgrimage I be eager to make . I must see Wordsworth 's grave , and Wordsworth 's house . ' 

 ' you shall see they both , but they be in opposite direction one at your elbow , the other a four mile walk . which will you see first ? we will toss for it , ' take a shilling from a pocketful of loose cash , always ready for moment of hesitation . ' head , house ; tail , grave . tail it be . come and have a smoke , and see the poet 's grave . the splendour of the monument , the exquisite neatness with which it be keep , will astound you , consider that we live in a period of Wordsworth worship . ' 

 Hammond hesitate , and look at Lady Lesbia . 

 ' be not   you come ? ' call Maulevrier from the lawn . ' it be a fair offer . I have   get my cigarette case . ' 

 ' yes , I be come , ' answer the other , with a disappointed air . 

 he have hop that Lesbia would offer to show he the poet 's grave . he could not abandon that hope without a struggle . 

 ' will you come with we , Lady Lesbia ? we will suppress the cigarette ! ' 

 ' thank , no , ' she say , become suddenly frigid . ' I be go to practice . ' 

 ' do you never walk in the morning on such a lovely morning as this ? ' 

 ' not very often . ' 

 she have re - enter those frozen region from which his attention have lure he for a little while . she have remind herself of the inferior social position of this person , in whose conversation she have allow herself to be interested . 

 ' Filons ! ' cry Maulevrier from below , and they go . 

 Mary would have very much like to go with they , but she do not want to be intrusive ; so she go off to the kennel to see the terrier eat their morning and only meal of dog biscuit . 

 CHAPTER VIII . 

 there be always a skeleton . 


 the two young man stroll through the village , Maulevrier pause to exchange greeting with almost everyone he meet , and so to the rustic churchyard , above the beck . 

 the beck be swollen with late rain , and be brawl merrily over its stony bed ; the churchyard grass be deep and cool and shadowy under the cluster branch . the poet 's tomb be disappointing in its unlovely simplicity , its stern , slatey hue . the plain granite cross would have satisfy Mr. Hammond , or a cross in pure white marble , with a sculptured lamb at the base . surely the lamb , emblem at once pastoral and sacred , ought to enter into any monument to Wordsworth ; but that gray headstone , with its catalogue of date , those stern iron railing be these fit memorial of one whose soul so love nature 's loveliness ? 

 after Mr. Hammond have see the little old , old church , and the medallion portrait inside , have see all that Maulevrier could show he , in fact , the two young man go back to the place of graf , and sit on the low parapet above the beck , smoke their cigarette , and talk with that perfect unreserve which can only obtain between man who be old and try friend . they talk , as it be only natural they should talk , of that household at Fellside , where all thing be new to John Hammond . 

 ' you like my sister Lesbia ? ' say Maulevrier . 

 ' like she ! well , yes . the difficulty with most man must be not to worship she . ' 

 ' ah , she be not my style . and she be beastly proud . ' 

 ' a little hauteur give piquancy to her beauty ; I admire a grand woman . ' 

 ' so do I in a picture . Titian 's Queen of Cyprus , or any party of that kind ; but for flesh and blood I like humility a woman who know she be human , and not infallible , and only just a little well than you or I . when I choose a wife , she will be no such example of cultivate perfection as my sister Lesbia . I want no goddess , but a nice little womanly woman , to jog along the rough and tumble road of life with I . ' 

 ' Lady Maulevrier 's influence , no doubt , have in a great measure determine the bent of your sister 's character : and from what you have tell I about her ladyship , I should think a fix idea of her own superiority would be inevitable in any girl train by she . ' 

 ' yes , she be a proud woman a proud , hard woman and she have steep Lesbia 's mind in all her own pet idea and prejudice . yet , God know , we have little reason to hold our head high , ' say Maulevrier , with a gloomy look . 

 John Hammond do not reply to this remark : perhaps there be some difficulty for a man situate as he be in find a fit reply . he smoke in silence , look down at the pure swift water of the Rotha tumble over the crag and boulder below . 

 ' do not   somebody say there be always a skeleton in the cupboard , and the nobler and more ancient the race the big the skeleton ? ' say Maulevrier , with a philosophical air . 

 ' yes , your family secret be an attribute of a fine old race . the Pelopida , for instance in their case it be not a single skeleton , but a whole charnel house . I do not   think your skeleton need trouble you , Maulevrier . it belong to the remote past . ' 

 ' those thing never belong to the past , ' say the young man . ' if it be any other kind of taint profligacy madness , even the story of a duel that go very near murder a runaway wife a rebellious son a cruel husband . I have hear such story hint at in the record of family . but our story mean disgrace . I seldom see stranger put their head together at the club without fancy they be tell each other about my grandfather , and point I out as the grandson and heir of a thief . ' 

 ' why use unduly hard word ? ' 

 ' why should I stoop to sophistication with you , my friend . Dishonesty be dishonesty all the world over ; and to plunder Rajahs on a large scale be no less vile than to pick a pocket on Ludgate Hill . ' 

 ' nothing be ever prove against your grandfather . ' 

 ' no , he die in the nick of time , and the inquiry be squash , thank to the Angersthorpe interest , and my grandmother 's cleverness . but if he have live a few week long England would have ring with the story of his profligacy and dishonour . some people say he commit suicide in order to escape the inquiry ; but I have hear my mother emphatically deny this . my father tell she that he have often talk with the people who keep the little inn where his father die , and they be clear enough in their assertion that the death be a natural death the sudden collapse of an exhausted constitution . ' 

 ' be it on account of this scandal that your father spend the good part of his life away from England ? ' Hammond ask , feel that it be a relief to Maulevrier to talk about this secret burden of his . 

 the young Earl be light - hearted and frivolous by nature , yet even he have his graver moment ; and upon this subject of the old Maulevrier scandal he be peculiarly sensitive , perhaps all the more so because his grandmother have never allow he to speak to she about it , have never satisfy his curiosity upon any detail of that painful story . 

 ' I have very little doubt it be so though I be not   old enough when he die to hear as much from his own lip . my father go straight from the University to Vienna , where he begin his career in the diplomatic service , and where he soon afterwards marry a dowerless english girl of good family . he go to Rio as first secretary , and die of fever within seven year of his marriage , leave a widow and three baby , the young in long clothe . mother and baby all come over to England , and be at once establish at Fellside . I can remember the voyage and I can remember my poor mother who never recover the blow of my father 's death , and who die in yonder house , after five year of broken health and broken spirit . we have no one but the dowager to look to as child hardly another friend in the world . she do what she like with we ; she keep the girl as close as nun , so they have never hear a hint of the old history ; no breach of scandal have reach their ear . but she could not shut I up in a country house for ever , though she do succeed in keep I away from a public school . the time come when I have to go to the University , and there I hear all that have be say about Lord Maulevrier . the man who tell I about the old scandal in a friendly way pretend not to believe it ; but one night , when I have get into a row at a wine - party with a tailor 's son , he tell I that if his father be a snip my grandfather be a thief , and so he think himself the well breed of the two . I smash his nose for he , but as it be a decided pug before the row begin , that hardly square the matter . ' 

 ' do you ever hear the exact story ? ' 

 ' I have hear a dozen story ; and if only a quarter of they be true my grandfather be a scoundrel . it seem that he be immensely popular for the first year or so of his government , give more splendid entertainment than have be give at Madras for half a century before his time , lavish his wealth upon his favourite . then arise a rumour that the governor be insolvent and harass by his creditor , and then a new source of wealth seem to be at his command ; he be more reckless , more princely than ever ; and then , little by little , there arise the suspicion that he be traffic in english interest , sell his influence to petty prince , wink at those mysterious crime by which rightful heir be push aside to make room for usurper . lastly it become notorious that he be the slave of a wicked woman , false wife , suspect murderess , whose husband , a native prince , disappear from the scene just when his existence become perilous to the governor 's reputation . accord to one version of the story , the scandal of this Rajah 's mysterious disappearance , follow not long after by the ranee 's equally mysterious death , be the immediate cause of my grandfather 's recall . how much , or how little of this story or other dark story of the same kind be true , whether my grandfather be a consummate scoundrel , or the victim of a baseless slander , whether he leave India a rich man or a poor man , be know to no mortal except Lady Maulevrier , and compare with she the Theban Sphinx be a communicative individual . ' 

 ' let the dead bury their dead , ' say Hammond . ' neither you nor your sister can be the bad for this ancient slander . no doubt every part of the story have be distort and exaggerate in the telling ; and a great deal of it may be pure invention , evolve from the inner consciousness of the slanderer . God forbid that any whisper of scandal should ever reach Lady Lesbia 's ear . ' 

 he ignore poor Mary . it be to he as if there be no such person . her feeble light be extinguish by the radiance of her sister 's beauty ; her very individuality be annihilate . 

 ' as for you , dear old fellow , ' he say , with warm affection , ' no one will ever think the bad of you on account of your grandfather 's peccadillo . ' 

 ' yes , they will . hereditary genius be one of our modern craze . when a man 's grandfather be a rogue , there must be a taint in his blood . People do not   believe in spontaneous generation , moral or physical , now - a - day . typhoid breed typhoid , and typhus breed typhus , just as dog breed dog ; and who will believe that a cheat and a liar can be the father of honest man ? ' 

 ' in that case , know what kind of man the grandson be , I will never believe that the grandfather be a rogue , ' say Hammond , heartily . 

 Maulevrier put out his hand without a word , and it be warmly grasp by his friend . 

 ' as for her ladyship , I respect and honour she as a woman who have lead a life of self - sacrifice , and have wear her pride as an armour , ' continue Hammond . 

 ' yes , I believe the dowager 's character be rather fine , ' say Maulevrier ; ' but she and I have never hit our horse very well together . she would have like such a fellow as you for a grandson , Jack a man who take high honour at Oxford , and could hold his own against all comer . such a grandson would have gratify her pride , and would have repay she for the trouble she have take in nursing the Maulevrier estate ; for however poor a property it be when her husband go to India there be no doubt that it be a very fine estate now , and that the dowager have be the making of it . ' 

 the two young man stroll up to Easedale Tarn before they go back to Fellside , where Lady Maulevrier receive they with a stately graciousness , and where Lady Lesbia unbent considerably at luncheon , and condescend to an animate conversation with her brother 's friend . it be such a new thing to have a stranger at the family board , a man whose information be well abreast with the march of progress , who could talk eloquently upon every subject which people care to talk about . in this new and animate society Lesbia seem like an enchant princess suddenly awaken from a spell - bind slumber . molly look at her sister with absolute astonishment . never have she see she so bright , so beautiful no long a picture or a statue , but a woman warm with the glow of life . 

 ' no wonder Mr. Hammond admire she , ' think poor Molly , who be quite acute enough to see the stranger 's keen appreciation of her sister 's charm , and positive indifference towards herself . 

 there be some thing which woman find out by instinct , just as the needle turn towards the magnet . shut a girl up in a tower till she be eighteen year old , and on the day of her release introduce she to the first man her eye have ever look upon , and she will know at a glance whether he admire she . 

 after luncheon the four young people start for Rydal Mount ; with Fršulein as chaperon and watch - dog . the girl be both good walker . Lady Lesbia even , though she look like a hot - house flower , have be train to active habit , could walk and ride , and play tennis , and climb a hill as become a mountain - breed damsel . molly , feel that her conversational power be not appreciate by her brother 's friend , take half a dozen dog for company , and with three fox - terrier , a little Yorkshire dog , a colley and an otter - hound , be at no loss for society on the road , more especially as Maulevrier give she most of his company , and entertain she with an account of his Black Forest adventure , and all the fine thing he have say to the fair - haired , blue - eyed Baden girl , who have sell he photograph or wild strawberry , or have awaken the echo of the hill with the music of their rustic flute . 

 Fršulein be perfectly aware that her mission upon this particular afternoon be not to let Lady Lesbia out of her sight for an instant , to hear every word the young lady say , and every word Mr. Hammond address to she . she have receive no specific instruction from Lady Maulevrier . they be not necessary , for the Fršulein know her ladyship 's intention with regard to her elder granddaughter , know they , at least , so far as that Lesbia be intend to make a brilliant marriage ; and she know , therefore , that the presence of this handsome and altogether attractive young man be to the last degree obnoxious to the dowager . she be oblige to be civil to he for her nephew 's sake , and she be too wise to let Lesbia imagine he dangerous : but the fact that he be dangerous be obvious , and it be Fršulein 's duty to protect her employer 's interest . 

 everybody know Lord Maulevrier , so there be no difficulty about get admission to Wordsworth 's garden and Wordsworth 's house , and after Mr. Hammond and his companion have explore these , they go back to the shore of the little lake , and climb that rocky eminence upon which the poet use to sit , above the placid water of silvery Rydal . it be a lovely spot , and that narrow lake , so poor a thing be magnitude the gauge of beauty , have a soft and pensive loveliness in the clear afternoon light . 

 ' Poor Wordsworth ' sigh Lesbia , as she stand on the grassy crag look down on the shin water , break in the foreground by fringe of rush , and the rich luxuriance of water - lily . ' be it not pitiable to think of the year he spend in this monotonous place , without any society worth speak of , with only the shabby collection of book , with hardly any interest in life except the sky , and the hill , and the peasantry ? ' 

 ' I think Wordsworth 's be an essentially happy life , in spite of his narrow range , ' answer Hammond . ' you , with your ardent youth and vivid desire for a life of action , can not imagine the calm bliss of reverie and constant communion with nature . Wordsworth have a thousand companion you and I would never dream of ; for he every flower that grow be an individual existence almost a soul . ' 

 ' it be a mild kind of lunacy , an everlasting opium dream without the opium ; but I be grateful to he for live such a life , since it have bequeath we some exquisite poetry , ' say Lesbia , who have be too carefully cultured to fleer or flout at Wordsworth . 

 ' I do believe there be an otter just under that bank , ' cry Molly , who have be watch the obvious excitement of her bandy - legged hound ; and she rush down to the brink of the water , leap lightly from stone to stone , and incite the hound to business . 

 ' let he alone , can not   you ? ' roar Maulevrier ; ' leave he in peace till he be want . if you disturb he now he will desert his holt , and we may have a blank day . the hound be to be out to - morrow . ' 

 ' I may go with you ? ' ask Mary , eagerly . 

 ' well , yes , I suppose you will want to be in it . ' molly and her brother go on an exploring ramble along the edge of the water towards Ambleside , leave John Hammond in Lesbia 's company , but closely guard by Miss M ller . these three go to look at Nab Cottage , where poor Hartley Coleridge end his brief and clouded day ; and they have go some way upon their homeward walk before they be rejoin by Maulevrier and Mary , the damsel 's kilte skirt considerably the bad for mud and mire . 

 ' what would grandmother say if she be to see you ! ' exclaim Lesbia , look contemptuously at the muddy petticoat . 

 ' I be not go to let she see I , so she will say nothing , ' cry Mary , and then she call to the dog , ' Ammon , Agag , Angelina ; ' and the three fox terrier fly along the road , fall over themselves in the swiftness of their flight , darting , and leaping , and scramble over each other , and offer the spectator the most intense example of joyous animal life . 

 the colley be far up on the hill - side , and the otter - hound be still hunt the water , but the terrier never go out of Mary 's sight . they look to she to take the initiative in all their sport . 

 they be back at Fellside in time for a very late tea . Lady Maulevrier be wait for they in the drawing - room . 

 ' oh , grandmother , why do you not take your tea ! ' exclaim Lesbia , look really distressed . ' it be six o'clock . ' 

 ' I be use to have you at home to hand I my cup , ' reply the dowager , with a touch of reproachfulness . 

 ' I be so sorry , ' say Lesbia , sit down before the tea - table , and begin her accustomed duty . ' indeed , dear grandmother , I have no idea it be so late ; but it be such a lovely afternoon , and Mr. Hammond be so interested in everything connect with Wordsworth ' 

 she be look her lovely at this moment , all that be soft in her nature call forth by her desire to please her grandmother , whom she really love . she hang over Lady Maulevrier 's chair , attend to her small want , and seem scarcely to remember the existence of anyone else . in this phase of her character she seem to Mr. Hammond the perfection of womanly grace . 

 Mary have rush off to her room to change her muddy gown , and come in presently , dress for dinner , look the picture of innocence . 

 John Hammond receive his tea - cup from Lesbia 's hand , and linger in the drawing - room talk to the dowager and her granddaughter till it be time to dress . Lady Maulevrier find herself favourably impress by he in spite of her prejudice . it be very provoking of Maulevrier to have bring such a man to Fellside . his very merit be objectionable . she try with exquisite art to draw he into some revealment as to his family and antecedent : but he evade every attempt of that kind . it be too evident that he be a self - make man , whose intellect and good look be his only fortune . it be criminal in Maulevrier to have bring such a person to Fellside . her ladyship begin to think seriously of send the two girl to St. Bees or Tynemouth for change of air , in charge of Fršulein . but any sudden proceeding of that kind would inevitably awaken Lesbia 's suspicion ; and there be nothing so fatal to a woman 's peace as this idea of danger . no , the peril must be face . she could only hope that Maulevrier would soon tire of Fellside . a week 's Westmoreland weather gray sky and long rainy day , would send these young man away . 

 CHAPTER IX . 

 a CRY in the darkness . 


 the peril have to be face , for the weather do not favour Lady Maulevrier 's hope . Westmoreland sky forget to shed their accustomed shower . Westmoreland hill seem to have lose their power of draw down the rain . that August be a lovely month , and the young people at Fellside revel in ideal weather . Maulevrier take his friend everywhere by hill and stream and force and gill to all those choose spot which make the glory of the Lake country on Windermere and Thirlmere , away through the bleak pass of Kirkstone to Ullswater on drive excursion , and on boat excursion , and pedestrian ramble , which latter the homely - minded Hammond seem to like good of all , for he be a splendid walker , and love the freedom of a mountain ramble , the liberty to pause and loiter and waste an hour at will , without be accountable to anybody 's coachman , or responsible for the well - being of anybody 's horse . 

 on some occasion the two girl and Miss M ller be of the party , and then it seem to John Hammond as if nothing be need to complete the glory of earth and sky . there be other day rougher journey when the man go alone , and there be day when Lady Mary steal away from her book and music , and all those study which she be suppose still to be pursue no long closely supervise by her governess , but on parole , as it be and go with her brother and his friend across the hill and far away . those be happy day for Mary , for it be always delight to she to be with Maulevrier ; yet she have a profound conviction of John Hammond 's indifference , kind and courteous as he be in all his dealing with she , and a sense of her own inferiority , of her own humble charm and little power to please , which be so acute as to be almost pain . one day this keen sense of humiliation break from her unaware in her talk with her brother , as they two sit on a broad heathy slope face to face with one of the Langdale pike , and with a deep valley at their foot , while John Hammond be climb from rock to rock in the gorge on their right , explore the beauty of Dungeon Ghyll . 

 ' I wonder whether he think I very ugly ? ' say Mary , with her hand clasp upon her knee , her eye fix on Wetherlam , upon whose steep brow a craggy mass of brown rock clothe with crimson heather stand out from the velvety green of the hill - side . 

 ' who think you ugly ? ' 

 ' Mr. Hammond . I be sure he do . I be so sunburnt and so horrid ! ' 

 ' but you be not ugly . why , Molly , what be you dream about ? ' 

 ' oh , yes , I be ugly . I may not seem so to you , perhaps , because you be use to I , but I know he must think I very plain compare with Lesbia , whom he admire so much . ' 

 ' yes , he admire Lesbia . there be no doubt of that . ' 

 ' and I know he think I plain , ' say Molly , contemplate Wetherlam with sorrowful eye , as if the sequence be inevitable . 

 ' my dear girl , what nonsense ! plain , forsooth ? ugly , quotha ? why , there be not a fine pair of eye in Westmoreland than my Molly 's , or a pretty smile , or whiter tooth . ' 

 ' but all the rest be horrid , ' say Mary , intensely in earnest . ' I be sunburnt , freckle , and altogether odious like a haymaker or a market woman . Grandmother have say so often enough , and I know it be the truth . I can see it in Mr. Hammond 's manner . ' 

 ' what ! freckle and sunburn , and the haymaker , and all that ? ' cry Maulevrier , laugh . ' what an expressive manner Jack 's must be , if it can convey all that like Lord Burleigh 's nod , by Jove . why , what a goose you be , Mary . Jack think you a very nice girl , and a very pretty girl , I will be bind ; but be not   you clever enough to understand that when a man be over head and ear in love with one woman , he be apt to seem just a little indifferent to all the other woman in the world ? and there be no doubt Jack be desperately in love with Lesbia . ' 

 ' you ought not to let he be in love with she , ' protest Mary . ' you know it can only lead to his unhappiness . you must know what grandmother be , and how she have make up her mind that Lesbia be to marry some great person . you ought not to have bring Mr. Hammond here . it be like let he into a trap . ' 

 ' do you think it be wrong ? ' ask her brother , smile at her earnestness . ' I should be very sorry if poor Jack should come to grief . but still , if Lesbia like he which I think she do we ought to be able to talk over the dowager . ' 

 ' never , ' cry Mary . ' Grandmother would never give way . you have no idea how ambitious she be . why , once when Lesbia be in a poetical mood , and say she would marry the man she like well in the world , if he be a pauper , her ladyship fly into a terrible passion , and tell she she would renounce she , that she would curse she , if she be to marry beneath she , or marry without her grandmother 's consent . ' 

 ' hard line for Hammond , ' say Maulevrier , rather lightly . ' then I suppose we must give up the idea of a match between he and Lesbia . ' 

 ' you ought not to have bring he here , ' retort Mary . ' you have well invent some plan for send he away . if he stay it will be only to break his heart . ' 

 ' dear child , man 's heart do not break so easily . I have fancy that mine be break more than once in my life , yet it be sound enough , I assure you . ' 

 ' oh ! ' sigh Mary , ' but you be not like he ; wound do not go so deep with you . ' 

 the subject of their conversation come out of the rocky cleft in the hill as Mary speak . she see his hat appear out of the gorge , and then the man himself emerge , a tall well - build figure , clothe in brown tweed , come towards they , with sketch - book and colour - box in his pocket . he have be make what he call memoranda of the waterfall , a stone or two here , a cluster of fern there , or a tree tear up by the root , and yet green and living , hang across the torrent , a rude natural bridge . 

 this round by the Langdale Pikes and Dungeon Ghyll be one of their good day ; or , at least , Molly and her brother think so ; for to those two the presence of Lesbia and her chaperon be always a restraint . 

 Mary could walk twice as far as her eld sister , and revel in hill - side path and all manner of rough place . they order their luncheon at the inn below the waterfall , and have it carry up on to the furzy slope in front of Wetherlam , where they could eat and drink and be merry to the music of the force as it come down from the hill behind they , while the light and shadow come and go upon yonder rugge brow , now gray in the shadow , now ruddy in the sunshine . 

 Mary be as gay as a bird during that rough and ready luncheon . no one would have suspect her uneasiness about John Hammond 's peril or her own plainness . she might let her real self appear to her brother , who have be her trust friend and father confessor from her babyhood ; but she be too thorough a woman to let Mr. Hammond discover the depth of her sympathy , the tenderness of her compassion for his woe . later , as they be walk home across the hill , by Great Langdale and Little Langdale , and Fox Howe and Loughrigg Fell , she fall behind a few pace with Maulevrier , and say to he very earnestly 

 ' you will not   tell , will you , dear ? ' 

 ' tell what ? ' he ask , star at she . 

 ' do not   tell Mr. Hammond what I say about his think I ugly . he might want to apologise to I , and that would be too humiliating . I be very childish to say such a silly thing . ' 

 ' undoubtedly you be . ' 

 ' and you will not   tell he ? ' 

 ' tell he anything that would degrade my Mary ? assail her dignity by so much as a breath ? soon would I have this tongue tear out with red - hot pincer . ' 

 on the next day , and the next , sunshine and summer sky still prevail ; but Mr. Hammond do not seem to care for ramble far afield . he prefer loitering about in the village , row on the lake , read in the garden , and play lawn tennis . he have only inclination for those amusement which keep he within a stone 's throw of Fellside : and Mary know that this disposition have arise in his mind since Lesbia have withdraw herself from all share in their excursion . Lesbia have not be rude to her brother or her brother 's friend ; she have decline their invitation with smile and sweetness ; but there be always some reason a new song to be practise , a new book to be read , a letter to be write why she should not go for drive or walk or steamboat trip with Maulevrier and his friend . 

 so Mr. Hammond suddenly find out that he have see all that be worth see in the Lake country , and that there be nothing so enjoyable as the placid idleness of Fellside ; and at Fellside Lady Lesbia could not always avoid he without a too - mark intention , so he taste the sweetness of her society to a much great extent than be good for his peace , if the case be indeed as hopeless as Lady Mary declare . he stroll about the ground with she ; he drink the sweet melody of her voice in Heine 's tender ballad ; he read to she on the sunlit lawn in the lazy afternoon hour ; he play billiard with she ; he be her faithful attendant at afternoon tea ; he give himself up to the study of her character , which , to his charmed eye , seem the perfection of pure and placid womanhood . there might , perhaps , be some lack of passion and of force in this nature , a marked absence of that impulsive feeling which be a charm in some woman : but this want be atone for by sweetness of character , and Mr. Hammond argue that in these calm nature there be often an unsuspected depth , a latent force , a grandeur of soul , which only reveal itself in the great ordeal of life . 

 so John Hammond hang about the luxurious drawing - room at Fellside in a manner which his friend Maulevrier ridicule as unmanly . 

 ' I have no idea you be such a tame cat , ' he say : ' if when we be salmon fishing in Canada anybody have tell I you could loll about a drawing - room all day listen to a girl squall and read novel , I should not   have believe a word of it . ' 

 ' we have plenty of rough on the shore of the St. Lawrence , ' answer Hammond . ' summer idleness in a drawing - room be an agreeable variety . ' 

 it be not to be suppose that John Hammond 's state of mind could long remain unperceived by the keen eye of the dowager . she see the gradual dawning of his love , she see the glow of its meridian . she be pleased to behold this proof of Lesbia 's power over the heart of man . so would she conquer the man foredoom to be her husband when the come time should bring they together . but agreeable as the fact of this first conquest might be , as an evidence of Lesbia 's supremacy among woman , the situation be not without its peril ; and Lady Maulevrier feel that she could no long defer the duty of warn her granddaughter . she have wish , if possible , to treat the thing lightly to the very last , so that Lesbia should never know there have be danger . she have tell she , a few day ago , that those drive , and walk with the two young man be undignifie , even although guard by the Fršulein 's substantial presence . 

 ' you be make yourself too much a companion to Maulevrier and his friend , ' say the dowager . ' if you do not take care you will grow like Mary . ' 

 ' I would do anything in the world to avoid that , ' reply Lesbia . ' our walk and drive have be very pleasant . Mr. Hammond be extremely clever , and can talk about everything . ' 

 her colour heighten ever so little as she speak of he , an indication duly observe by Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' no doubt the man be clever ; all adventurer be clever ; and you have sense enough to see that this man be an adventurer a mere sponge and toady of Maulevrier 's . ' 

 ' there be nothing of the sponge or the toady in his manner , ' protest Lady Lesbia , with a still deep blush , the warm glow of angry feeling . 

 ' my dear child , what do you know of such people or of the atmosphere in which they be generate ? the sponge and toady of to - day be not the clumsy fawn wretch you have read about in old - fashioned novel . he can flatter adroitly , and feed upon his friend , and yet maintain a show of manhood and independence . I will wager Mr. Hammond 's trip to Canada do not cost he sixpence , and that he hardly open his purse all the time he be in Germany . ' 

 ' if my brother want the company of a friend who be much poor than himself , he must pay for it , ' argue Lesbia . ' I think Maulevrier be lucky to have such a companion as Mr. Hammond . ' 

 yet , even while she so argue , Lady Lesbia feel in some manner humiliate by the idea that this man who so palpably worship she be too poor to pay his own travelling expense . 

 poet and philosopher may say what they will about the grandeur of plain living and high thinking ; but a young woman think well of the plain liver who be not compel to plainness by want of cash . the idea of narrow mean , of dependence upon the capricious generosity of a wealthy friend be not without its humiliating influence . Lesbia be barely civil to Mr. Hammond that evening when he praise her singing ; and she refuse to join in a four game propose by Maulevrier , albeit she and Mr. Hammond have beat Mary and Maulevrier the evening before , with much exultant hilarity . 

 Hammond have be at Fellside nearly a month , and Maulevrier be begin to talk about a move far northward . there be a grouse moor in Argyleshire which the two young man talk about as belong to some unnamed friend of the Earl 's , which they have think of shoot over before the grouse season be end . 

 ' Lord Hartfield have property in Argyleshire , ' say the dowager , when they talk of these shooting . ' do you know his estate , Mr. Hammond ? ' 

 ' Hammond know that there be such a place , I daresay , ' reply Maulevrier , reply for his friend . 

 ' but you do not know Lord Hartfield , perhaps , ' say her ladyship , not arrogantly , but still in a tone which imply her conviction that John Hammond would not be hand - in - glove with earl , in Scotland or elsewhere . 

 ' oh , yes ! I know he by sight every one in Argyleshire know he by sight . ' 

 ' naturally . a young man in his position must be widely know . be he popular ? ' 

 ' fairly so . ' 

 ' his father and I be friend many year ago , ' say Lady Maulevrier , with a faint sigh . ' have you ever hear if he resemble his father ? ' 

 ' I believe not . I be tell he be like his mother 's family . ' 

 ' then he ought to be handsome . Lady Florence Ilmington be a famous beauty . ' 

 they be sit in the drawing - room after dinner , the room dimly light by darkly - shade lamp , the window wide open to the summer sky and moonlit lake . in that subdue light Lady Maulevrier look a woman in the prime of life . the classical modelling of her feature and the delicacy of her complexion be unimpaire by time , while those trace of thought and care which give age to her face in the broad light of day be invisible at night . John Hammond contemplate that refined and placid countenance with profound admiration . he remember how her ladyship 's grandson have compare she with the Sphinx ; and it seem to he to - night , as he study her proud and tranquil beauty , that there be indeed something of the mysterious , the unreadable in that countenance , and that beneath its heroic calm there might be the ash of tragic passion , the trace of a life - long struggle with fate . that such a woman , so beautiful , so gifted , so well fit to shine and govern in the great world , should have be content to live a long life of absolute seclusion in this remote valley be in itself a social mystery which must need set an observant young man wonder . it be all very well to say that Lady Maulevrier love a country life , that she have make Fellside her earthly Paradise , and have no desire beyond it . the fact remain that it be not in Lady Maulevrier 's temperament to be satisfied with such an existence ; that falcon eye be never mean to gaze for ever upon one narrow range of mountain and lake ; that lip be make to speak among the great one of the world . 

 Lady Maulevrier be particularly gracious to her grandson 's friend this evening . Maulevrier speak so decisively about a speedy migration northward , seem so inclined to regret the time waste since the twelfth of the month , that she think the danger be past , and she could afford to be civil . she really like the young man , have no doubt in her own mind that he be a gentleman in the high and broad sense of the word , but not in the sense which make he an eligible husband for either of her granddaughter . 

 Lesbia be in a pensive mood this evening . she sit in the verandah , look dreamily at the lake , and at Fairfield yonder , a broad green slope , silver with moonlight , and seem to stretch far away into unfathomable distance . 

 if one could but take one 's lover by the hand and go wander over those mystic moonlit slope into some new unreal world where it would not matter whether a man be rich or poor , high - bear or low - bear , where there should be no such thing as rank and state to be win or lose ! Lesbia feel to - night as if she would like to live out her life in dreamland . reality be too hard , too much set round by difficulty and sacrifice . 

 while Lesbia be lose herself in that dream - world , Lady Maulevrier unbent considerably to John Hammond , and talk to he with more appearance of interest in his actual self , and in his own affair , than she have manifest , hitherto although she have be uniformly courteous . 

 she ask he his plan for the future have he choose a profession ? 

 he tell she that he have not . he mean to devote himself to literature and politic . 

 ' be not that rather vague ? ' inquire her ladyship . 

 ' everything be vague at first . ' 

 ' but literature now as an amusement , no doubt , it be delightful but as a profession do literature ever pay ? ' 

 ' there have be such case . ' 

 ' yes , I suppose so . Walter Scott , Gibbon , Macaulay , Froude , those make money no doubt . but there be a suspicion of hopelessness in the idea of a young man start in life intend to earn his bread by literature . one remember Chatterton . I should have think that in your case the law or the church would have be well . in the latter Maulevrier might have be useful to you . he be patron of three or four living . ' 

 ' you be too good even to think of such a thing , ' say Hammond ; ' but I have set my heart upon a political career . I must swim or sink in that sea . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier look at he with a compassionate smile poor young man ! no doubt he think himself a genius , and that door which have remain shut to everybody else would turn on their hinge directly he knock at they . she be sincerely sorry for he . young , clever , enthusiastic , and doom to bitter disappointment .

 ' I believe it have . ' 

 ' well , I hope Providence will smile upon your path . '

 he take up the beautiful hand , white to transparency , show the delicate tracing of blue vein , and press his lip upon it in chivalrous worship of age and womanly dignity .

 there be no music that evening . Maulevrier and Mary be play billiard ; Fršulein M ller be sit in her corner work at a high - art counterpane .  they play a fifty game , and smoke and talk till the stroke of midnight , by which time it seem as if there be not another creature awake in the house . Maulevrier put out the lamp in the billiard - room , and then they go softly up the shadowy staircase , and part in the gallery , the Earl go one way , and his friend the other . 

 the house be large and roomy , spread over a good deal of ground , Lady Maulevrier have insist upon there be only two story . the servant ' room be all in a side wing , correspond with those old building which have be give over to Steadman and his wife , and among the villager of Grasmere enjoy the reputation of be haunt . a wide panel corridor extend from one end of the house to the other . it be light from the roof , and serve as a gallery for the display of a small and choice collection of modern art , which her ladyship have acquire during her long residence at Fellside . here , too , in Sheraton cabinet , be those treasure of old English china which Lady Maulevrier have inherit from past generation . 

 her ladyship 's room be situate at the southern end of this corridor , her bed - chamber be at the extreme end of the house , with window command two magnificent view , one across the lake and the village of Grasmere to the green slope of Fairfield , the other along the valley towards Rydal Water . this and the adjoining boudoir be the prettiest room in the house , and no one wonder that her ladyship should spend so much of her life in the luxurious seclusion of her own apartment . 

 John Hammond go to his room , which be on the same side of the house as her ladyship 's ; but he be in no disposition for sleep . he open the casement , and stand look out upon the moonlit lake and the quiet village , where one solitary light shine like a faint star in a cottage window , amidst that little cluster of house by the old church , once know as Kirktown . beyond the village rise gentle slope , crown with foliage , and above those woode crest appear the grand outline of the hill , surround and guard Easedale 's lovely valley , as the hill surround Jerusalem of old . 

 he look at that delicious landscape with eye that hardly see its beauty . the image of a lovely face come between he and all the glory of earth and sky . 

 ' I think she like I , ' he be say to himself . ' there be a look in her eye to - night that tell I the time be come when - - ' 

 the thought die unfinished in his brain . through the silent house , across the placid lake , there ring a wild , shrill cry that freeze the blood in his vein , or seem so to freeze it a shriek of agony , and in a woman 's voice . it ring out from an open window near his own . the sound seem close to his ear . 

 CHAPTER X. 

 ' o bitterness of thing too sweet . ' 


 only for an instant do John Hammond stand motionless after hear that unearthly shriek . in the next moment he rush into the corridor , expect to hear the sound repeat , to find himself face to face with some midnight robber , whose presence have cause that wild cry of alarm . but in the corridor all be silent as the grave . no open door suggest the entrance of an intruder . the dimly - burn lamp show only the long empty gallery . he stand still for a few moment listen for voice , footstep , the rustle of garment : but there be nothing . 

 nothing ? yes , a groan , a long - draw moaning sound , as of infinite pain . this time there be no doubt as to the direction from which the sound come . it come from Lady Maulevrier 's room . the door be ajar , and he could see the faint light of the night - lamp within . that fearful cry have come from her ladyship 's room . she be in peril or pain of some kind . 

 convince of this one fact , Mr. Hammond have not an instant 's hesitation . he push open the door without compunction , and enter the room , prepare to behold some terrible scene . 

 but all be quiet as death itself . no midnight burglar have violate the sanctity of Lady Maulevrier 's apartment . the soft , steady light of the night - lamp shone on the face of the sleeper . yes , all be quiet in the room , but not in that sleeper 's soul . the broad white brow be painfully contract , the lip draw down and distort , the delicate hand , half hide by the deep Valenciennes ruffle , clutch the coverlet with convulsive force . Sigh after sigh burst from the agitated breast . John Hammond gaze upon the sleeper in an agony of apprehension , uncertain what to do . be this dream only ; or be it some kind of seizure which call for medical aid ? at her ladyship 's age the idea of paralysis be not too improbable for belief . if this be a dream , then indeed the vision of Lady Maulevrier 's head upon her bed be more terrible than the dream of common mortal . 

 in any case Mr. Hammond feel that it be his duty to send some attendant to Lady Maulevrier , some member of the household who be familiar with her ladyship 's habit , her own maid if that person could be unearth easily . he know that the servant sleep in a separate wing ; but he think it more than likely that her ladyship 's personal attendant occupy a room near her mistress . 

 he go back to the corridor and look round he in doubt , for a moment or two . 

 close against her ladyship 's door there be a swing door , cover with red cloth , which seem to communicate with the old part of the house . John Hammond push this door , and it yield to his hand , reveal a lamp - light passage , narrow , old - fashioned , and low . he think it likely that Lady Maulevrier 's maid might occupy a room in this half - desert wing . as he push open the door he see an elderly man come towards he , with a candle in his hand , and with the appearance of have huddle on his clothe hastily .

 ' it be very dreadful . her whole countenance be convulse just now , when I go into her room to see what be wrong . I be almost afraid of a fit of some kind . ought not she maid to go to she ? '

 he go to her ladyship 's door , push it open a little way , and look in . ' yes , she be sleep as quietly as an infant , ' he say , shut the door softly as he speak . 

 ' I be very glad ; but surely she ought to have she maid near she at night , if she be subject to those attack . '

 he have hear of this old servant Steadman , who be suppose to enjoy more of her ladyship 's confidence than any one else in the household ; but he have never speak to the man before that night . 

 ' yes , I come . it be my duty to come , know her ladyship 's habit . I be a light sleeper , and that scream wake I instantly . if her ladyship 's maid be want I should call she . I be a kind of watch - dog , you see , sir . '

 Steadman go back to his own quarter . Mr. Hammond hear he draw the bolt of the swing door , thus cut off all communication with the corridor . 

 the eight - day clock on the staircase strike two as Mr. Hammond return to his room , even less inclined for sleep than when he leave it . strange , that nocturnal disturbance of a mind which seem so tranquil in the day . or be that tranquillity only a mask which her ladyship wear before the world : and be the bitter memory of event which happen forty year ago still a source of anguish to that highly strung nature ? 

 ' there be some mind which can not forget , ' John Hammond say to himself , as he meditate upon her ladyship 's character and history . ' the story of her husband 's crime may still be fresh in her memory , though it be only a tradition for the outside world . his crime may have involve some deep wrong do to herself , some outrage against her love and faith as a wife . one of the story Maulevrier speak of the other day be of a wicked woman 's influence upon the governor a much more likely story than that of any traffic in british interest or british honour , which would have be almost impossible for a man in Lord Maulevrier 's position . if the scandal be of a dark kind a guilty wife the mysterious disappearance of a husband the horror of the thing may have make a deep impression on Lady Maulevrier than even her nearest and dear dream of : and that superb calm which she wear like a royal mantle may be maintain at the cost of struggle which tear her heart - string . and then at night , when the will be dormant , when the nervous system be no long rule by the power of wake intelligence , the old familiar agony return , the hat image flash back upon the brain , and in proportion to the fineness of the temperament be the intensity of the dreamer 's pain . ' 

 and then he go on to reflect upon the long monotonous year spend in that lonely house , shut in from the world by those everlaste hill . albeit the house be an ideal house , set in a landscape of infinite beauty , the monotony must be none the less oppressive for a mind burden with dark memory , weigh down by sorrow which could seek no relief from sympathy , which could never become familiarise by discussion .

 Mr. Hammond inquire after her ladyship 's health next morning , and be tell she be perfectly well .

' it be quite a heroic adventure . you must know our fox - hunting here be rather a peculiar institution , very good in its way , but strictly local . no horse could live among our hill , so we hunt on foot , and as the pace be good , and the work hard , nobody who start with the hound be likely to be in at the death , except the huntsman . we be all mad for the sport , and off we go , over the hill and far away , pick up a fresh field as we go . the ploughman leave his plough , and the shepherd leave his flock , and the farmer leave his thrashing , to follow we ; in every field we cross we get fresh blood , while those who join we at the start fall off by degree . well , it happen one day late in October , when there be long ridge of snow on Helvellyn , and patch of white on Fairfield , Mistress Mary here must need take her bamboo staff and start for the Striding Edge . it be just the day upon which she might have meet her death easily on that perilous point , but happily something occur to divert her juvenile fancy , for scarcely have she get to the bottom of Dolly Waggon pike you know dolly - - '

 in the kennel there be riot and confusion . Ahab be demolish Angelina , Absalom have Agamemnon in a deadly grip . Dog - whip in hand , Mary rush to the rescue , and lay about she , like the knight of old , utterly forgetful of her frock . she soon succeed in restore order , but the Madras muslin , the Breton lace have perish in the conflict . she leave the kennel panting , and in rag and tatter , some of the muslin and lace hang about she in strip a yard long , but the great part remain in the possession of the terrier , who have maul and munch her finery to their heart ' content , while she be read the Riot Act .

 Mr. Hammond follow the two lady to their bower .

'

 Mr. Hammond come back with a shabby little book in his hand and establish himself comfortably in one of the two Beaconsfield chair . 

 he open his book at that group of short poem call Heimkehr , and read here and there , as fancy lead he . sometimes the strain be a love - song , brief , passionate as the cry of a soul in pain ; sometimes the verse be bitter and cynical ; sometimes full of tender simplicity , telling of childhood , and youth and purity ; sometimes dark with hidden meaning , grim , awful , cold with the chill breath of the charnel - house . sometimes Lesbia 's heart beat a little fast as Mr. Hammond read , for it seem as if it be he who be speak to she , and not the dead poet .

 ' Lesbia , this be my last day at Fellside , and you and I may never have a minute alone together again while I be here . will you come for a little walk with I on the Fell ? there be something I must say to you before I go . ' 

 Lesbia 's delicate cheek grow a shade more pale . Instinct tell she what be come , though never mortal man have speak to she of love . nor until now have Mr. Hammond ever address she by her christian name without the ceremonious prefix . there be a deep tone in his voice , a graver look in his eye , than she have ever notice before .

 ' Lesbia , you know that I love you , ' he begin , in his low , grave voice , tremulous with feeling . ' no word I can say to - day can tell you of my love more plainly than my heart have be tell you in every hour of this happy , happy time that you and I have spend together . I love you as I never hop to love , fervently , completely , believe that the perfection of earthly bliss will be mine if I can but win you . Dearest , be there such a sweet hope for I ; be you indeed my own , as I be your , heart and soul , and mind and be , till the last throb of life in this poor clay ? '
