 ' she would like to marry you to a man of rank and fortune a man who will choose you , perhaps , because other people admire you , rather than because he himself love you as you ought to be love ; who will choose you because you be altogether the good and most perfect thing of your year ; just as he would buy a yearling at Newmarket or Doncaster . her ladyship mean you to make a great alliance coronet , not heart , be the counter for her game ; but , Lesbia , would you , in the bloom and freshness of youth you with the pulse of youth throb at your heart lend yourself to the calculation of age which have live its life and forget the very meaning of love ? would you submit to be play as a card in the game of a dowager 's ambition ? trust I , dearest , in the crisis of a woman 's life there be one only counsellor she should listen to , and that counsellor be her own heart . if you love I as I dare to hope you do trust in I , hold by I , and leave the rest to Heaven . I know that I can make your life happy . ' 

 ' you frighten I by your impetuosity , ' say Lesbia . ' surely you forget how short a time we have know each other . ' 

 ' an age . all my life before the day I see you be a dead , dull blank as compare with the magical hour I have spend with you . ' 

 ' I do not even know who and what you be . ' 

 ' first , I be a gentleman , or I should not be your brother 's friend . a poor gentleman , if you like , with only my own right arm to hew my pathway through the wood of life to the temple of fortune ; but trust I , only trust I , Lesbia , and I will so hew my path as to reach that temple . look at I , love . do I look like a man bear to fail ? ' 

 she look up at he shyly , with eye that be dim with tear . he look like a demi - god , tall , straight as the pine trunk amongst which he be stand , a frame form for strength and activity , a face instinct with mental power , dark eye that glow with the fire of intellect and passion . the sunlight give an almost unearthly radiance to the clear dark of his complexion , the curly brown hair cut close to the finely shape head , the broad brow and boldly model feature . 

 Lesbia feel in her heart that such a man must be destine for success , bear to be a conqueror in all strife , a victor upon every field . 

 ' have I the thews and sinew of a man doom to be beat in the battle ? ' he ask she . ' no , dearest ; Heaven mean I to succeed ; and with you to fight for I shall not be beat by adverse fortune . can you not trust Providence and I ? ' 

 ' I can not disobey my grandmother . if she will consent - - ' 

 ' she will not consent . you must defy Lady Maulevrier , Lesbia , if you mean to reward my love . but I will promise you this much , darling , that if you will be my wife with your brother 's consent which I be sure of before I ask for it , within one year of our marriage I will find mean of reconcile her ladyship to the match , and win her entire forgiveness for you and I . ' 

 ' you be talk of impossibility , ' say Lesbia , frown . ' why do you talk to I as if I be a child ? I know hardly anything of the world , but I do know the woman who have rear and educate I . my grandmother would never forgive I if I marry a poor man . I should be an outcast . ' 

 ' we would be outcast together happy outcast . besides , we should not always be poor . I tell you I be predestine to conquer fate . ' 

 ' but we should have to begin from the beginning . ' 

 ' yes , we should have to begin from the beginning , as Adam and Eve do when they leave Paradise . ' 

 ' we be not tell in the Bible that they have any happiness after that . it seem to have be all trouble and weariness , and toil and death , after the angel with the flaming sword drive they out of Eden . ' 

 ' they be together , and they must have be happy . oh , Lesbia , if you do not feel that you can face poverty and the world 's contempt by my side , and for my sake , you do not love I . love never calculate so nicely ; love never fear the future ; and yet you do love I , Lesbia , ' he say , try to fold she in his arm ; but again she draw herself away from he this time with a look almost of horror and stand face he , cling to one of the pine trunk , like a scared wood - nymph . 

 ' you have no right to say that , ' she say . 

 ' I have the divine right of my own deep love of heart which cry out to heart . do you think there be no magnetic power in true love which can divine the answer love in another ? Lesbia , call I an insolent coxcomb if you like , but I know you love I , and that you and I may be utterly happy together . oh , why why do you shrink from I , my beloved ; why withhold yourself from my arm ! oh , love , let I hold you to my heart let I seal our betrothal with a kiss ! ' 

 ' betrothal no , no ; not for the world , ' cry Lesbia . ' Lady Maulevrier would cast I off for ever ; she would curse I . ' 

 ' what would the curse of an ambitious woman weigh against my love ? and I tell you that her anger would be only a pass tempest . she would forgive you . ' 

 ' never you do not   know she . ' 

 ' I tell you she would forgive you , and all would be well with we before we have be marry a year . why can not you believe I , Lesbia ? ' 

 ' because I can not believe impossibility , even from your lip , ' she answer sullenly . 

 she stand before he with downcast eye , the tear stream down her pale cheek , exquisitively lovely in her agitation and sorrow . yes , she do love he ; her heart be beat passionately ; she be long to throw herself on his breast , to be fold upon that manly heart , in trust in that brave , bright look which seem to defy fortune . yes , he be a man bear to conquer ; he be handsome , intellectual , powerful in all mental and physical gift . a man of man . but he be , by his own admission , a very obscure and insignificant person , and he have no money . life with he mean a long fight with adverse circumstance ; life for his wife must mean patience , submission , long wait upon destiny , and perhaps with old age and grey hair the tardy turning of Fortune 's wheel . and be she for this to resign the kingdom that have be promise to she , the giddy height which she be bear to scale , the triumph and delight and victory of the great world ? yes , Lesbia love this fortuneless knight ; but she love herself and her prospect of promotion still well . 

 ' oh , Lesbia , can you not be brave for my sake trustful for my sake ? God will be good to we if we be true to each other . ' 

 ' God will not be good to I if I disobey my grandmother . I owe she too much ; ingratitude in I would be doubly base . I will speak to she . I will tell she all you have say , and if she give I the faint encouragement - - ' 

 ' she will not ; that be a foregone conclusion . tell she all , if you like ; but let we be prepare for the answer . when she deny the right of your heart to choose its own mate , then rise up in the might of your womanhood and defy she . tell she , " I love he , and be he rich or poor , I will share his fate ; " tell she boldly , bravely , nobly , as a true woman should ; and if she be adamant still , proclaim your right to disobey her worldly wisdom rather than the voice of your own heart . and then come to I , darling , and be my own , and the world which you and I will face together shall not be a bad world . I will answer for that . no trouble shall come near you . no humiliation shall ever touch you . only believe in I . ' 

 ' I can believe in you , but not in the impossible , ' answer Lesbia , with measured accent . 

 the voice be silver - sweet , but pass cold . just then there be a rustling among the pine branch , and Lesbia look round with a startled air . 

 ' be there any one listen ? ' she exclaim . ' what be that ? ' 

 ' only the breath of heaven . oh , Lesbia , if you be but a little less wise , a little more trustful . do not be a dumb idol . say that you love I , or do not love I . if you can look I in the face and say the last , I will leave you without another word . I will take my sentence and go . ' 

 but this be just what Lesbia could not do . she could not deny her love ; and yet she could not sacrifice all thing for her love . she lift the heavy lid which veil those lovely eye , and look up at he imploringly . 

 ' give I time to breathe , time to think , ' she say . 

 ' and then will you answer I plainly , truthfully , without a shadow of reserve , remember that the fate of two life hang on your word . ' 

 ' I will . ' 

 ' let it be so , then . I will go for a ramble over the hill , and return in time for afternoon tea . I shall look for you on the tennis lawn at half - past four . ' 

 he take she in his arm , and this time she yield herself to he , and the beautiful head rest for a few moment upon his breast , and the soft eye look up at he in confide fondness . he bend and kiss she once only , but a kiss that mean for life and death . in the next moment he be go , leave she alone among the pine tree . 

 CHAPTER XI . 

 ' if I be to do as ISEULT do . ' 


 Lady Maulevrier rarely appear at luncheon . she take some slight refection in her morning - room , among her book and paper , and in the society of her canine favourite , whose company suit she well at certain hour than the noisier companionship of her grandchild . she be a studious woman , love the silent life of book well than the inane chatter of everyday humanity . she be a woman who think much and read much , and who live more in the past than the present . she live also in the future , count much upon the splendid career of her beautiful granddaughter , which should be in a manner a lengthen out , a renewal of her own life . she look forward to the day when Lesbia should reign supreme in the great world , a famous beauty and leader of fashion , her every act and word inspire and direct by her grandmother , who would be the shadow behind the throne . it be possible nay , probable that in those day Lady Maulevrier would herself re - appear in society , establish her salon , and draw around her closing year all that be wittiest , good , and wisest in the great world . 

 her ladyship be repose in her low reading - chair , with a volume of Tyndall on the book - stand before she , when the door be open softly and Lesbia come glide in , and seat herself without a word on the hassock at her grandmother 's foot . Lady Maulevrier pass her hand caressingly over the girl 's soft brown hair , without look up from her book . 

 ' you be a late visitor , ' she say ; ' why do you not come to I after breakfast ? ' 

 ' it be such a lovely morning , we go straight from the breakfast table to the garden ; I do not think you want I . ' 

 ' I do not want you ; but I be always glad to see my pet . what be you do in the garden all the morning ? I do not hear you play tennis . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier have already interrogate the german governess upon this very subject , but she have her own reason for wish to hear Lesbia 's account . 

 ' no , it be too warm for tennis . Fršulein and I sit and work , and Mr. Hammond read to we . ' 

 ' what do he read ? ' 

 ' Heine 's ballad . he read german beautifully . 

 ' indeed ! I daresay he be at school in Germany . there be cheap school there to which middle - class people send their boy . ' 

 this be like a thrust from a rusty knife . 

 ' Mr. Hammond be at Oxford , ' Lesbia say , reproachfully ; and then , after a longish pause , she clasp her hand upon the arm of Lady Maulevrier 's chair , and say , in a plead voice , ' Grandmother , Mr. Hammond have ask I to marry he . ' 

 ' indeed ! only that ? and pray , do he tell you what be his mean of maintain Lord Maulevrier 's sister in the position to which her birth entitle she ? ' inquire the dowager , with crush calmness . 

 ' he be not rich ; indeed , I believe , he be poor ; but he be brave and clever , and he be full of confidence in his power to conquer fortune . ' 

 ' no doubt ; that be your true adventurer 's style . he confide implicitly in his own talent , and in somebody else 's banker . Mr. Hammond would make a tremendous figure in the world , I daresay , and while he be make it your brother would have to keep he . well , my dear Lesbia , I hope you give this gentleman the answer his insolence deserve ; or that you do well , and refer he to I . I should be glad to give he my opinion of his conduct a person admit to this house as your brother 's hanger - on tolerate only on your brother 's account ; such a person , nameless , penniless , friendless   except for Maulevrier 's too facile patronage , to dare to lift his eye to my granddaughter ! it be ineffable insolence ! ' 

 Lesbia crouch by her grandmother 's chair , her face hide from Lady Maulevrier 's falcon eye . every word utter by her ladyship sting like the knot cord of a knout . she know not whether to be most ashamed of her lover or of herself of her lover for his obscure position , his hopeless poverty ; of herself for her folly in love such a man . and she do love he , and would fain have plead his cause , have she not be cow by the authority that have rule she all her life . 

 ' Lesbia , if I think you have be silly enough , degrade enough , to give this young man encouragement , to have justify his audacity of to - day by any act or word of your , I should despise , I should detest you , ' say Lady Maulevrier , sternly . ' what could be more contemptible , more hateful in a girl rear as you have be than to give encouragement to the first comer to listen greedily to the first adventurer who have the insolence to make love to you , to be eager to throw yourself into the arm of the first man who ask you . that my granddaughter , a girl rear and teach and watch and guard by I , should have no more dignity , no more modesty , or womanly feeling , than a barmaid at an inn ! ' 

 Lesbia begin to cry . 

 ' I do not   see why a barmaid should not be a good woman , or why it should be a crime to fall in love , ' she say , in a voice break by sob . ' you need not speak to I so unkindly . I be not go to marry Mr. Hammond . ' 

 ' oh , you be not ? that be very good of you . I be deeply grateful for such an assurance . ' 

 ' but I like he well than anyone I ever see in my life before . ' 

 ' you have see so many people . you have have such a wide area for choice . ' 

 ' no ; I know I have be keep like a nun in a convent : but I do not   think when I go into the world I shall ever see anyone I should like well than Mr. Hammond . ' 

 ' wait till you have see the world before you make up your mind about that . and now , Lesbia , leave off talk and think like a child ; look I in the face and listen to I , for I be go to speak seriously ; and with I , when I be in earnest , what be say once be say for ever . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier grasp her granddaughter 's arm with long slender finger which hold it as tightly as the grasp of a vice . she draw the girl 's slim figure round till they be face to face , look into each other 's eye , the dowager 's eagle countenance light up with impassioned feeling , severe , awful as the face of one of the fatal sister , the avenger of blood , the harbinger of doom . 

 ' Lesbia , I think I have be good to you , and kind to you , ' she say . 

 ' you have be all that be kind and dear , ' falter Lesbia . 

 ' then give I measure for measure . my life have be a hard one , child ; hard and lonely , and loveless and joyless . my son , to whom I devote myself in the vigour of youth and in the prime of life , never love I , never repay I for my love . he spend his day far away from I , when his presence would have gladden my difficult life . he die in a strange land . of his three child , you be the one I take into my heart . I do my duty to the other ; I lavish my love upon you . do not give I curse instead of blessing . do not give I a stone instead of bread . I have build every hope of happiness or pleasure in this world upon you and your obedience . obey I , be true to I , and I will make you a queen , and I will sit in the shadow of your throne . I will toil for you , and be wise for you . you shall have only to shine , and dazzle , and enjoy the glory of life . my beautiful darling , for pity 's sake do not give yourself over to folly . ' 

 ' do not you marry for love , grandmother ? ' 

 ' no , Lesbia . Lord Maulevrier and I get on very well together , but ours be no love - match . ' 

 ' do nobody in our rank ever marry for love ? be all marriage a mere exchange and barter ? ' 

 ' no , there be love - match now and then , which often turn out badly . but , my darling , I be not ask you to marry for rank or for money . I be only ask you to wait till you find your mate among the noblest in the land . he may be the handsome and most accomplish of man , a man bear to win woman 's heart ; and you may love he as fervently as ever a village girl love her first lover . I be not go to sacrifice you , or to barter you , dearest . I mean to marry you to the good and noble young man of his day . you shall never be ask to stoop to the unworthy , not even if worthlessness wear strawberry leaf in his cap , and own the great estate in the land . ' 

 ' and if instead of wait for this King Arthur of yours I be to do as Iseult do as Guinevere do choose for myself - - ' 

 ' Iseult and Guinevere be wanton . I wonder that you can name they in comparison with yourself . ' 

 ' if I be to marry a good and honourable man who have his place to make in the world , would you never forgive I ? ' 

 ' you mean Mr. Hammond ? you may just as well speak plainly , ' say Lady Maulevrier , freezingly . ' if you be capable of such idiocy as that , Lesbia , I would pluck you out of my heart like a foul weed . I would never look upon you , or hear your name speak , or think of you again as long as I live . my life would not last very long after that blow . old age can not bear such shock . oh , Lesbia , I have be father and mother to you ; do not bring my grey hair in sorrow to the grave . ' 

 Lesbia give a deep sigh , and brush the tear from her cheek . yes , the very idea of such a marriage be foolishness . just now , in the pine wood , carry away by the force of her lover 's passion , by her own soft feeling , it have seem to she as if she could count the world well lose for his sake ; but now , at Lady Maulevrier 's foot , she become again true to her training , and the world be too much to lose . 

 ' what can I do , grandmother ? ' she ask , submissively , despairingly . ' he love I , and I love he . how can I tell he that he and I can never be anything to each other in this world ? ' 

 ' refer he to I . I will give he his answer . ' 

 ' no , no ; that will not do . I have promise to answer he myself . he have go for a walk on the hill , and will come back at four o'clock for my answer . ' 

 ' sit down at that table , and write as I dictate . ' 

 ' but a letter will be so formal . ' 

 ' it be the only way in which you can answer he . when he come back from his walk you will have leave Fellside . I shall send you off to St. Bees with Fršulein . you must never look upon that man 's face again . ' 

 Lesbia brush away a few more tear , and obey . she have be too well train to attempt resistance . Defiance be out of the question . 

 CHAPTER XII . 

 ' the GREATER CANTLE of the world be lose . ' 


 the sky be still cloudless when John Hammond stroll slowly up the leafy avenue at Fellside . he have be across the valley and up the hill to Easedale Tarn , and then by rough untrodden way , across a chaos of rock and heather , into a second valley , long , narrow , and sterile , know as Far Easedale , a desolate gorge , a rugged cleft in the heart of the mountain . the walk have be long and laborious ; but only in such clambering and toiling , such expenditure of muscular force and latent heat , could the man 's restless soul endure those long hour of suspense . 

 ' how will she answer I ? oh , my God ! how will she answer ? ' he say within himself , as he walk up the romantic winding road , which make so picturesque an approach to Lady Maulevrier 's domain , ' be my idol gold or clay ? how will she come through the crucible ? oh , dearest , sweetest , loveliest , only be true to the instinct of your womanhood , and my cup will be full of bliss , and all my day will flow as sweetly as the burden of a song . but if you prove heartless , if you love the world 's   wealth well than you love I ah ! then all be over , and you and I be lose to each other for ever . I have make up my mind . ' 

 his face settle into an expression of indomitable determination , as of a man who would die rather than be false to his own purpose . there be no glow of hope in his heart . he have no deep faith in the girl he love ; indeed in his heart of heart he know that this be to whom he have trust his hope of bliss be no heroine . she be a lovely , loveable girl , nothing more . how would she greet he when they meet presently on the tennis lawn ? with tear and entreaty , and pretty little deprecate speech , irresolution , timidity , vacillation , perhaps ; hardly with heroic resolve to act and dare for his sake . 

 there be no one on the tennis lawn when he go there , though the hour be close at hand at which Lesbia have promise to give he his answer . he sit down in one of the low chair , glad to rest after his long ramble have have no refreshment but a bottle of soda - water and a biscuit at the cottage by Easedale Tarn . he wait , calmly as to outward seeming , but with a heavy heart . 

 ' if it be Mary now whom I love , I should have little fear of the issue , ' he think , weigh his sweetheart 's character , as he weigh his chance of success . ' that young termagant would defy the world for her lover . ' 

 he sit in the summer silence for nearly half - an - hour , and still there be no sign of Lady Lesbia . her satin - line workbasket , with the work throw carelessly across it , be still on the rustic table , just as she have leave it when they go to the pine wood . wait be weary work when the bliss of a lifetime tremble in the balance ; and yet he do not want to be impatient . she might find it difficult to get away from her family , perhaps . she be closely watch and guard , as the most precious thing at Fellside . 

 at last the clock strike five , and Hammond could endure delay no long . he go round by the flower garden to the terrace before the drawing - room window , and through an open window to the drawing - room . 

 Lady Maulevrier be in her accustomed seat , with her own particular little table , magazine , book , newspaper at her side . Lady Mary be pour out the tea , a most unusual thing ; and Maulevrier be sit on a stool at her foot , with his knee up to his chin , very warm and dusty , eat pound cake . 

 ' where the mischief have you be hide yourself all day , Jack ? ' he call out as Hammond appear , look round the room as he enter , with eager , interrogate eye , for that one figure which be absent . 

 ' I have be for a walk . ' 

 ' you might have have the civility to announce your design , and Molly and I would have share your peregrination . ' 

 ' I be sorry that I lose the privilege of your company . ' 

 ' I suppose you lose your luncheon , which be of more importance , ' say Maulevrier . 

 ' will you have some tea ? ' ask Mary , who look more womanly than usual in a cream - colour surah gown one of her Sunday gown . 

 she have a faint hope that by this essentially feminine apparel she might lessen the prejudicial effect of Maulevrier 's cruel story about the fox - hunt . 

 Mr. Hammond answer absently , hardly look at Mary , and quite unconscious of her pretty gown . 

 ' thank , yes , ' he say , take the cup and saucer , and look at the door by which he momently expect Lady Lesbia 's entrance , and then , as the door do not open , he look down at Mary , very busy with china teapot and a brass kettle which hiss and throb over a spirit lamp . 

 ' will not   you have some cake , ' she ask , look up at he gently , grieve at the distress and disappointment in his face . ' I be sure you must be dreadfully hungry . ' 

 ' not in the least , thank . how come you to be entrust with those sacred vessel , Lady Mary ? what have become of Fršulein and your sister ? ' 

 ' they have rush off to St. Bees . Grandmother think Lesbia look pale and out of spirit , and pack she off to the seaside at a minute 's notice . ' 

 ' what ! she have leave Fellside ? ' ask Hammond , pal suddenly , as if a man have strike he . ' Lady Maulevrier , do I understand that Lady Lesbia have go away ? ' 

 he ask the question in an authoritative tone , with the air of a man who have a right to be answer . the dowager wonder at his surpass insolence . 

 ' my granddaughter have go to the seaside with her governess , ' she say , haughtily . 

 ' at a minute 's notice ? ' 

 ' at a minute 's notice . I be not in the habit of hesitate about any step which I consider necessary for my grandchild 's welfare . ' 

 she look he full in the face , with those falcon eye of her ; and he give she back a look as resolute , and every whit as full of courage and of pride . 

 ' well , ' he say , after a very perceptible pause , ' no doubt your ladyship have do wisely , and I must submit to your jurisdiction . but I have ask Lady Lesbia a question , and I have be promise an answer . ' 

 ' your question have be answer by Lady Lesbia . she leave a note for you , ' reply Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' thank , ' answer Mr. Hammond , briefly , and he hurry from the room without another word . 

 the letter be on the table in his bedroom . he have little hope of any good wait for he in a letter so write . the dowager and the world have triumph over a girl 's dawn love , no doubt . 

 this be Lesbia 's letter : 

 ' dear Mr. Hammond , Lady Maulevrier desire I to say that the 
 proposal which you honour I by make this morning be one which I 
 can not possibly accept , and that any idea of an engagement between 
 you and I could result only in misery and humiliation to both . she 
 think it well , under these circumstance , that we should not again 
 meet , and I shall therefore have leave Fellside before you receive 
 this letter . 
 ' with all good wish , very faithfully your , 
 ' LESBIA HASELDEN . ' 
 ' very faithfully mine faithful to her false training , to the worldly mind that rule she ; faithful to the god of this world Belial and Mammon , and the Moloch Fashion . poor cowardly soul ! she love I , and own as much , yet weakly fly from I , afraid to trust the strong arm and the brave heart of the man who love she , prefer the glitter sham of the world to the reality of true and honest love . well , child , I have weigh you in the balance and find you want . would to God it have be otherwise ! if you have be brave and bold for love 's sake , where be that pure and perfect chrysolite for which I would have barter you ? ' 

 he fling himself into a chair , and sit with his head bow upon his fold arm , and his eye not innocent of tear . what would he not have give to find truth and courage and scorn of the world 's wealth in that heart which he have try to win . do he think she altogether heartless because she so glibly renounce he ? no , he be too just for that . he call she only half - hearted . she be like the cat in the adage , ' let I dare not , wait upon I would . ' but he tell himself with one deep sigh of resignation that she be lose to he for ever . 

 ' I have try she , and find she not worth the winning , ' he say . 

 the house , even the lovely landscape smile under his window , the pastoral valley , smooth lake and willowy island , seem hateful to he . he feel himself hem round by those green hill , by yonder brown and rugged wall of Nabb Scar , stifle for want of breathe space . the landscape be lovely enough , but it be like a beautiful grave . he long to get away from it . 

 ' another man would follow she to St. Bees , ' he say . ' I will not . ' 

 he fling a few thing into a Gladstone bag , sit down , and write a brief note to Maulevrier , ask he to make his excuse to her ladyship . he have make up his mind to go to Keswick that afternoon , and would rejoin his friend to - morrow , at Carlisle . this do , he ring for Maulevrier 's valet , and ask that person to look after his luggage and bring it on to Scotland with his master 's thing ; and then , without a word of adieu to anyone , John Hammond go out of the house , with the Gladstone bag in his hand , and shake the dust of Fellside off his foot . 

 he order a fly at the Prince of Wales 's Hotel , and drive to Keswick , whence he go on to the Lodore . the gloom and spaciousness of Derwentwater , grey in the gathering dusk , suit his humour well than the emerald prettiness of Grasmere the roar of the waterfall make music in his ear . he din in a private room , and spend the evening roam on the shore of the lake , and at eleven o'clock go back to his hotel and sit late into the night read Heine , and think of the girl who have refuse he . 

 Mr. Hammond 's letter be deliver to Lord Maulevrier five minute before dinner , as he sit in the drawing - room with her ladyship and Mary . Poor Mary have put on another pretty gown for dinner , still bend upon efface Mr. Hammond 's image of she as a tousled , frantic creature in tear and muddy raiment . she sit watch the door , just as Hammond have watch it three hour ago . 

 ' so , ' say Maulevrier , ' your ladyship have succeed in drive my friend away . Hammond have leave Fellside , and beg I to convey to you his compliment and his grateful acknowledgment of all your kindness . ' 

 ' I hope I have not be uncivil to he , ' answer Lady Maulevrier coldly . ' as you have both make up your mind to go to - morrow , it can matter very little that he should go to - day . ' 

 Mary look down at the ribbon and lace on her prettiest frock , and think that it matter a great deal to she . yet , if he have stay , would he have see her frock or she ? with his bodily eye , perhaps , but not with the eye of his mind . those eye see only Lesbia . 

 ' no , perhaps it hardly matter , ' answer Maulevrier , with suppressed anger . ' the man be not worth talk about or think about . what be he ? only the good , true , brave fellow I ever know . ' 

 ' there be shepherd and guide in Grasmere of whom we could say almost as much , ' say Lady Maulevrier , ' yet you would scarcely expect I to encourage one of they to pay his address to your sister ? pray spare we all nonsense - talk , Maulevrier . this business be very well end . you ought never to have bring Mr. Hammond here . ' 

 ' I be sure of that now . I be very sorry I do bring he . ' 

 ' oh , the man will not die for love . a disappointment of that kind be good for a young man in his position . it will preserve he from more vulgar entanglement , and perhaps from the folly of a too early marriage . ' 

 ' that be a mighty philosophical way of look at the matter . ' 

 ' it be the only true way . I hope when you be my age you will have learn to look at everything in a philosophical spirit . ' 

 ' well , Lady Maulevrier , you have have it all your own way , ' say the young man , walk up and down the room in an angry mood . ' I hope you will never be sorry for have come between two people who love each other , and might have make each other happy . ' 

 ' I shall never be sorry for have save my granddaughter from an imprudent marriage . give I your arm , Maulevrier , and let I hear no more about Mr. Hammond . we have all have quite enough of he , ' say her ladyship , as the butler announce dinner . 

 CHAPTER XIII . 

 ' since PAINTED or not paint all shall fade . ' 


 Fršulein M ller and her charge return from St. Bees after a sojourn of about three week upon that quiet shore : but Lady Lesbia do not appear to be improve in health or spirit by the revivify breeze of the ocean . 

 ' it be a dull , horrid place , and I be bored to death there ! ' she say , when Mary ask how she have enjoy herself . ' there be no question of enjoyment . Grandmother take it into her head that I be look ill , and send I to the sea ; but I should have be just as well at Fellside . ' 

 this mean that between Lesbia and that distinctly inferior being , her young sister , there be to be no confidence . Mary have watch the life - drama act under her eye too closely not to know all about it , and be not inclined to be so put off . 

 that pale perturb countenance of John Hammond 's , those eager inquire eye look to the door which open not , have haunt Mary 's wake thought , have even mingle with the tangled web of her dream . oh , how could any woman scorn such love ? to be so loved , and by such a man , seem to Mary the perfection of earthly bliss . she have never be educate up to those wide and lofty view of life , which teach a woman that house and land , place and power , be the supreme good . 

 ' I can not   understand how you could treat that noble - minded man so badly , ' she exclaim one day , when she and Lesbia be alone in the library , and after she have sit for ever so long , star out of the window , meditate upon her sister 's cruelty . 

 ' of whom be you speak , pray ? ' 

 ' as if you do not   know ! of Mr. Hammond . ' 

 ' and pray , how do you know that he be noble - minded , or that I treat he badly ? ' 

 ' well , as to his be noble - minded , that jump to the eye , as french book say . as for your treatment of he , I be look on all the time , and I know how unkind you be , and I hear he talk to you in the fir - copse that day . ' 

 ' you be listen ' cry Lesbia indignantly . 

 ' I be not listen ! I be pass by . and if people choose to carry on their love affair out of door they must expect to be overhear . I think she must have take out your heart when you be little , and put a stone in its place . '

 ' why grateful ? ' 

 ' because it leave you a chance of get he for yourself ; and everybody can see that you be over head and ear in love with he . that jump to the eye , as you say . ' 

 Mary turn crimson , tremble with rage , look at her sister as if she would kill she , for a moment or so , and finally burst into tear . 

 ' that be not true , and it be shameful for you to say such a thing , ' she cry . 

 ' why , what a virago you be , Mary . well , I be very glad it be not true . Mr. Hammond be yes , I will be quite candid with you he be the only man I be ever likely to admire for his own sake . he be good , brave , clever , all that you think he . but you and I do not live in a world in which girl be free to follow their own inclination . I should break Lady Maulevrier 's heart if I be to make a foolish marriage ; and I owe she too much to set her wish at naught , or to make her decline year unhappy . I must obey she , at any cost to my own feeling . please never mention Mr. Hammond 's name . I be sure I have   have quite enough unhappiness about he . ' 

 ' I see , ' say Mary , bitterly . ' it be your own pain you think of , not his . he may suffer , so long as you be not worried . ' 

 ' you be an impertinent chit , ' retort Lesbia , ' and you know nothing about it . ' 

 after this there be no more say about Mr. Hammond ; but Mary do not forget he . she write long letter to her brother , who be still in Scotland , shooting , deer - stalk , fishing , kill something or other daily , in the most approved fashion of an Englishman take his pleasure . Maulevrier occasionally repay she with a telegram ; but he be not a good correspondent . he declare that life be too short for letter - writing . 

 Summer be go ; the lake be no long a shin emerald floor , dot with the reflection of the flock upon the verdant slope above it , but dull and grey of hue , and break by white - edge wavelet . patch of snow gleam on the misty height of Helvellyn , and the autumn wind howl and shriek around Fellside in the evening , when all the shutter be shut , and the outside world seem little more than an idea : that mystic hour when the sheep be slumber under the starry sky , and when , as the Westmoreland peasant believe , the fairy help the housewife at her spinning - wheel . 

 those October evening be very long and weary for Lesbia and her sister . Lady Maulevrier read and muse in her low chair beside the fire , with her book pile upon her own particular table , and light by her own particular lamp . she talk very little , but she be always gracious to her granddaughter and their governess , and she like they to be with she in the evening . Lesbia play or sing , or sit at work at her basket - table , which occupy the other side of the fireplace ; and Fršulein and Mary have the rest of the room to themselves , as it be , those two place by the hearth be sacred , as if dedicate to household god . Mary read immensely in those long evening , devouring volume after volume , feed her imagination with every kind of nutriment , good , bad , and indifferent . Fršulein M ller knit a woollen shawl , which seem to have neither begin , middle , nor end , and be always ready for conversation , but there be time when silence brood over the scene for long interval , and when every sound of the light wood - ash drop on the tile hearth be distinctly audible . 

 this state of thing go on for about three week after Lesbia 's return from St. Bees , Lady Maulevrier watchful of her granddaughter all the time , though say nothing . she see that Lesbia be not happy , not as she have be in the time before the coming of John Hammond . she have never be particularly gay or light - hearted , never gift with the wild spirit and buoyancy which make girlhood so lovely a season to some nature , a time of dance and song and joyousness , a morning of life steep in the beauty and gladness of the universe . she have never be gay as young lamb and foal and fawn and kitten and puppy dog be gay , by reason of the well - spring of delight within they , need no stimulus from the outside world . she have be just a little inclined to murmur at the dulness of her life at Fellside ; yet she have bear herself with a placid sweetness which have be Lady Maulevrier 's delight . but now there be a marked change in her manner . she be not the less submissive and dutiful in her bear to her grandmother , whom she both love and fear ; but there be moment of fretfulness and impatience which she could not conceal . she be captious and sullen in her manner to Mary and the Fršulein . she would not walk or drive with they , or share in any of their amusement . sometimes of an evening that studious silence of the drawing - room be suddenly break by Lesbia 's weary sigh , breathe unawares as she bent over her work . 

 Lady Maulevrier see , too , that Lesbia 's cheek be pale than of old , her eye less bright . there be a heavy look that tell of broken slumber , there be a pinched look in that oval check . good heaven ! if her beauty be to pale and wane , before society have bow down and worship it ; if this fair flower be to fade untimely ; if this prize rise in the garden of beauty be to wither and decay before it win the prize . 

 her ladyship be a woman of action , and no soon do this fear shape itself in her mind than she take step to prevent the evil her thought foreshadow . 

 among those friend of her youth and ally of her house with whom she have always maintain an affectionate correspondence be Lady Kirkbank , the fashionable wife of a sporting baronet , owner of a castle in Scotland , a place in Yorkshire , a villa at Cannes , and a fine house in Arlington Street , with an income large enough for their enjoyment . when Lady Diana Angersthorpe shine forth in the West End world as the acknowledge belle of the season , the star of Georgina Lorimer be begin to wane . she be the eld daughter of Colonel Lorimer , a man of good old family , and a fine soldier , who have fight shoulder to shoulder with Gough and Lawrence , and who have contrive to make a figure in society with very small mean . Georgina 's sister have all marry well . it be a case of necessity , the Colonel tell they ; they must either marry or gravitate ultimately to the workhouse . so the Miss Lorimers make the good use of their youth and freshness , and ' no good offer refuse ' be the guide rule of their young life . Lucy marry an East India merchant , and set up a fine house in Porchester Terrace . Maud marry wealth personify in the person of a lead member of the Tallow Chandlers ' company , and have her town house and country house , and as fine a set of diamond as a duchess . 

 but Georgina , the eld , trifle with her chance , and her twenty - seventh birthday beheld she pour out her father 's tea in a small furnished house in a street off Portland Place , which the Colonel have hire on his return from India , and which he declare himself unable to maintain another year . 

 ' directly the season be over I shall give up housekeeping and take a lodging at bath , ' say Colonel Lorimer . ' if you do not   like Bath all the year round you can stay with your sister . ' 

 ' that be the last thing I be likely to do , ' answer Georgina ; ' my sister be barely endurable when they be single and poor . they be quite intolerable now they be married and rich . I would soon live in the monkey - house at the Zoological than stay with either Lucy or Maud . ' 

 ' that be rank envy , ' retort her father ' you can not   forgive they for have do so much well than you . ' 

 ' I can not   forgive they for have married snob . when I marry I shall marry a gentleman . ' 

 ' when ! ' echo the parent , with a sneer laugh . ' had not   you well say " if " ? ' 

 at this period Georgina 's wan good look be in some measure counterbalance by the cumulative effect of half a dozen season in good society , which have give style to her person , ease to her manner , and sharpness to her tongue . nobody in society say sharp or more unpleasant thing than Miss Lorimer , and by virtue of this gift she get invite about a great deal more than she might have do have she be distinguish for sweetness of speech and manner . Georgie Lorimer 's presence at a dinner table give just that pungent flavour which be like the faint suspicion of garlic in a fricassee or of tarragon in a salad . 

 now in this very season , when Colonel Lorimer be inclined to speak of his daughter , as Sainte Beuve write of Musset , as a young woman with a very brilliant past , a lucky turn of event give Georgina a fresh start in life , which may be call a new departure . Lady Diana Angersthorpe , the belle of the season , take a fancy to she , be charm with her sharp tongue and acute sense of the ridiculous . the two become fast friend , and be see everywhere together . the good man all flock round the beauty , and all talk to the beauty 's companion : and before the season be over , Sir George Kirkbank , who have half make up his mind to propose to Lady Diana , find himself engage to that uncommonly jolly girl , Lady Diana 's friend . Georgina spend August and September with Lady Di , at the Marchioness of Carisbroke 's delightful villa in the Isle of Wight , and Sir George keep his yacht at Cowes all the time , and be in constant attendance upon his fiancťe . it be George and Georgie everywhere . in October Colonel Lorimer have the profound pleasure of give away his daughter , before the altar in St. George 's , Hanover Square , and it may be say of he that nothing in his relation with that young lady become he well than his manner of part with she . 

 so the needy Colonel 's daughter become Lady Kirkbank , and in the following spring Diana Angersthorpe be marry at the same St. George 's to the Earl of Maulevrier . the friend be divide by distance and by circumstance as the year roll on ; but friendship be steadily maintain ; and a regular correspondence with Lady Kirkbank , whose pen be as sharp as her tongue , be one of the mean by which Lady Maulevrier have keep herself thoroughly post in all those small event , unrecorde by newspaper , which make up the secret history of society . 

 it be of her old friend Georgie that her ladyship think in her present anxiety . Lady Kirkbank have more than once suggest that Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter should vary the monotony of Fellside by a visit to her place near Doncaster , or her castle north of Aberdeen ; but her ladyship have evade these friendly suggestion , be very jealous of any strange influence upon Lesbia 's life . now , however , there have come a time when Lesbia must have a complete change of scenery and surrounding , lest she should pine and dwindle in sullen submission to fate , or else defy the world and elope with John Hammond . 

 now , therefore , Lady Maulevrier decide to accept Lady Kirkbank 's hospitality . she tell her friend the whole story with perfect frankness , and her letter be immediately answer by a telegram . 

 ' I start for Scotland to - morrow , will break my journey by stay a night at Fellside , and will take Lady Lesbia on to Kirkbank with I next day , if she can be ready to go . ' 

 ' she shall be ready , ' say Lady Maulevrier . 

 she tell Lesbia that she have accept an invitation for she , and that she be to go to Kirkbank Castle the day after to - morrow . she be prepare for unwillingness , resistance even ; but Lesbia receive the news with evident pleasure . 

 ' I shall be very glad to go , ' she say , ' this place be so dull . of course I shall be sorry to leave you , grandmother , and I wish you would go with I ; but any change will be a relief . I think if I have to stay here all the winter , count the day and the hour , I should go out of my mind . ' 

 the tear come into her eye , but she wipe they away hurriedly , ashamed of her emotion .

 a visitor from the great London world be so rare an event that there be naturally a little excitement in the idea of Lady Kirkbank 's arrival . the handsome and most spacious of the spare bedroom be prepare for the occasion . the housekeeper be tell that the dinner must be perfect . there must be nothing old - fashioned or ponderous ; there must be mind as well as matter in everything . rarely do Lady Maulevrier look at a bill of fare ; but on this particular morning she go carefully through the menu , and correct it with her own hand . 

 a pair of post - horse bring Lady Kirkbank and she maid from Windermere station , in time for afternoon tea , and the friend who have only meet twice within the last forty year , embrace each other on the threshold of Lady Maulevrier 's morning - room . 

 ' my dear Di , ' cry Lady Kirkbank , ' what a delight to see you again after such age ; and what a too lovely spot you have choose for your retreat from the world , the flesh , and the devil . if I could be a recluse anywhere , it would be amongst just such delicious surrounding . ' 

 without , twilight shade be gather ; within , there be only the light of a fire and a shade lamp upon the tea table ; there be just light enough for the two woman to see each other 's face , and the change which time have work there . 

 never do womanhood in advanced year offer a more striking contrast than that present by the woman of fashion and the recluse . Lady Maulevrier be almost as handsome in the winter of her day as she have be when life be in its spring . the tall , slim figure , erect as a dart , the delicately chisel feature and alabaster complexion , the soft silvery hair , the perfect hand , whiter and more transparent than the hand of girlhood , the stately movement and bearing , all combine to make Lady Maulevrier a queen among woman . her brocade gown of a deep shade of red , with a border of dark sable on cuff and collar , suggest a portrait by Velasquez . she wear no ornament except the fine old brazilian diamond which flash and sparkle upon her slender finger . 

 if Lady Maulevrier look like a picture in the Escurial , Lady Kirkbank resemble a caricature in La Vie Parisienne . everything she wear be in the very late fashion of the parisian demi - monde , that exaggerated elegance of a fashion plate which only the most exquisite of woman could redeem from vulgarity . plush , brocade , peacock 's feather , golden bangle , mousquetaire glove , a bonnet of purple plumage set off by ornament of filagree gold , an infantine little muff of lace and wild flower , buttercup and daisy ; and hair , eyebrow and complexion as artificial as the flower on the muff . 

 all that art could do to obliterate the trace of age have be do for Georgina Kirkbank . but seventy year be not to be obliterate easily , and the crow 's foot show through the bloom de Ninon , and the eye under the paint arch be glassy and haggard , the carnation lip have a withered look . Age be make all the more palpable by the artifice which would have disguise it . 

 Lady Maulevrier suffer an absolute shock at behold the friend of her youth . she have not accustom herself to the idea that woman in society could raddle their cheek , stain their lip , and play trick before high heaven with their eyebrow and eyelash . in her own youth paint face have be the ghastly privilege of a class of womankind of which the woman of society be suppose to know nothing . person who show their ankle and rouge their cheek be to be see of an afternoon in Bond Street ; but Lady Diana Angersthorpe have be teach to pass they by as if she see they not , to behold without see these creature outside the pale . and now she see her own dearest friend , a person distinctly within the pale , plaster with bismuth and stain with carmine , and wear hair of a colour so obviously false and inharmonious , that child - like faith could hardly accept it as reality . forty year ago Lady Kirkbank 's long ringlet have be darkest glossy brown , to - day she wear a tousled fringe of bright yellow , piquantly contrast with Vandyke brown eyebrow .

 for world Lady Maulevrier could not have deny this truth . Happily Lady Kirkbank do not wait for an answer .

'

' 

 their close intimacy of the past warrant freedom of speech in the present .

 ' it be that horrid and I daresay unfortunate scandal about his lordship ; and then the sad shock of his death , ' murmur Lady Kirkbank , sympathetically . '

 indeed , the whole room be poetic , Lady Kirkbank declare , and there be many highly praise scene which less deserve the epithet . the dark red wall and cedar dado , the stamp velvet curtain , of an indescribable shade between silver - grey and olive , the Sheraton furniture , the parqueterie floor and persian prayer - rug , the deep yet brilliant hue of crackle porcelain and chinese cloisonnť enamel , the artistic fireplace , with dog - stove , low brass fender , and ingle - nook recess under the high mantelpiece , all combine to form a luxurious and harmonious whole . 

 Lady Kirkbank admire the tout ensemble in the fitful light of the fire , the dim grey of deepen twilight . 

 ' there never be a more delicious cell ! ' she exclaim , ' but still I should feel it a prison , if I have to spend six week in the year in it . I never stay more than six week anywhere out of London ; and I always find six week more than enough . the first fortnight be rapture , the third and fourth week be calm content , the fifth be weariness , the sixth a fever to be go . I once try a seventh week at Pontresina , and I hat the place so intensely that I dare not go back there for the next three year . but now tell I . Diana , have you really perform suttee , have you bury yourself alive in this sweet spot deliberately , or have the love of retirement grow upon you , and have you become a kind of lotus - eater ? '

 ' I be glad to hear that ; for I really think the bad possible use a woman can make of her life be in waste it on lamentation for a dead and go husband . life be odiously short at the good , and it be mere imbecility to fritter away any of our scanty portion upon the dead , who can never be any the well for our tear . '

 ' ah , there be a subject in which you have reason to be interested . I have not forget the romance of your youth that first season in which Ronald Hollister use to haunt every place at which you appear . do you remember that wet afternoon at the Chiswick flower - show , when you and he and I take shelter in the orange house , and you two make love to each other most audaciously in an atmosphere of orange - blossom that almost stifle I ? yes , those be glorious day ! '

' 

 ' but though they may be sometimes lose to society , they be all the more worthy of society 's esteem when they do appear , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' I think there must be an ennoble influence in Alpine travel , or in the vast solitude of the Dark Continent . a man find himself face to face with unsophisticated nature , and with the grand force of the universe . Professor Tyndall write delightfully of his Alpine experience ; his mind seem to have ripen in the solitude and untainted air of the Alps . and I believe Lord Hartfield be a young man of very high character and of considerable cultivation , be he not ? '

 she be not go to show her card , even to an old friend .

 ' the little fortune I have save be much too small for division . Lesbia will therefore have all I can leave she . Mary have the usual provision as a daughter of the Maulevrier house . ' 

 ' and I suppose Lesbia have that provision also ? ' 

 ' of course . ' 

 ' Lucky Lesbia . I only wish Hartfield be come to we for the shooting . I would engage he should fall in love with she . Kirkbank be a splendid place for match - making . but the fact be I be not very intimate with he . he be almost always travel , and when he be at home he be not in our set . and now , my dear Diana , tell I more about yourself , and your own life in this delicious place . ' 

 ' there be so little to tell . the book I have read , the theory of literature and art and science which I have adopt and dismiss , learn and forget those be the history of my life . the idea of the outside world reach I here only in book and newspaper ; but you who have be live in the world must have so much to say . let I be the listener . ' 

 Lady Kirkbank desire nothing well . she rattle on for three - quarter of an hour about her doing in the great world , her social triumph , the wonderful thing she have do for Sir George , who seem to be as a puppet in her hand , the prince and princeling she have entertain , the song she have compose , the comedy she have write , for private representation only , albeit the Haymarket manager be die to produce it , the scathing witticism with which she have wither her social enemy . she would have go on much long , but for the gong , which remind she that it be time to dress for dinner . 

 half - an - hour later Lady Kirkbank be in the drawing - room , where Mary have retire to the most shadowy corner , anxious to escape the gaze of the fashionable visitor . 

 but Lady Kirkbank be not inclined to take much notice of Mary . Lesbia 's brilliant beauty , the exquisite greek head , the faultless complexion , the deep , violet eye , catch Georgina Kirkbank 's eye the moment she have enter the room , and she see that this girl and no other must be the beauty , the beloved and choose grandchild . 

 ' how do you do , my dear ? ' she say , take Lesbia 's hand , and then , as if with a gush of warm feeling , suddenly draw the girl towards she and kiss she on both cheek . ' I be go to be desperately fond of you , and I hope you will soon contrive to like I just a little . ' 

 ' I feel sure that I shall like you very much , ' Lesbia answer sweetly . ' I be prepared to love you as grandmother 's old friend . ' 

 ' oh , my dear , to think that I should ever be the old friend of anybody 's grandmother ! ' sigh Lady Kirkbank , with unaffected regret . ' when I be your age I use to think all old people odious . it never occur to I that I should live to be one of they . '

 ' no , my love , I have no grandparent . I have a father , and he be all - sufficient anything beyond he in the ancestral line would have be a burden lay upon I great than I could bear , as the poet say . ' 

 Dinner be announce , and Mary come shyly out of her corner , blush deeply .

 ' I be very glad , ' falter Mary . ' I mean I be glad that Lesbia should enjoy herself . ' 

 ' and some fine day , when Lesbia be married and a great lady , I shall ask you to come to Scotland , ' say Lady Kirkbank , condescendingly , and then she murmur in her friend 's ear , as they go to the dining - room , ' quite an english girl . very fresh and frank and nice , ' which be great praise for such a second - rate young person as Lady Mary . 

 ' what do you think of Lesbia ? ' ask Lady Maulevrier , in the same undertone . 

 ' she be simply adorable . your letter prepare I to expect beauty , but not such beauty . my dear , I think the progress of the human race be all in a downward line since our time ; but your granddaughter be as handsome as you be in your first season , and that be go very far . ' 

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 ' not yet . ' 


 Lady Kirkbank carry off Lesbia early next day , the girl radiant at the idea of see life under new condition . she have a few minute ' serious talk with her grandmother before she go . 

 ' Lesbia , you be go into the world , ' say Lady Maulevrier ; ' yes , even a country house be the world in little . you will have many admirer instead of one ; but I think , I believe , that you will be true to I and to yourself . ' 

 ' you need not fear , grandmother . I have be an idiot ; but but it be only a pass folly , and I shall never be so weak again . ' 

 Lesbia 's scornful lip and kindle eye give intensity to her speech . it be evident that she despise herself for that one touch of womanly softness which have make she as ready to fall in love with her first wooer as any peasant girl in Grasmere Vale . 

 ' I be delighted to hear you speak thus , dearest , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' and if Mr. Hamilton Hammond , I mean should have the audacity to follow you to Kirkbank , and to intrude himself upon you there perhaps to persecute you with clandestine address - - ' 

 ' I do not believe he would do anything clandestine , ' say Lesbia , draw herself up . ' he be quite above that . ' 

 ' my dear child , we know absolutely nothing about he . he have his way to make in the world unaide by family or connection . he be clever daring . such a man can not help be an adventurer ; and an adventurer be capable of anything . I warn you to beware of he . ' 

 ' I do not   suppose I shall ever see his face again , ' retort Lesbia , irritably . 

 she have make up her mind that her life be not to be spoil , her brilliant future sacrifice , for the sake of John Hammond ; but the wound which she have suffer in renounce he be still fresh , her feeling be still sore . any contemptuous mention of he sting she to the quick . 

 ' I hope not . and you will beware of other adventurer , Lesbia , man of a bad stamp than Mr. Hammond , more experienced in ruse and iniquity , man steep to the lip in worldly knowledge , man who look upon woman as mere counter in the game of life . the world think that I be rich , and you will no doubt take rank as an heiress . you will therefore be a mark for every spendthrift , noble or otherwise , who want to restore his broken fortune by a wealthy marriage . and now , my dearest , good - bye . half my heart go with you . nothing could induce I to part with you , even for a few week , except the conviction that it be for your good . ' 

 ' but we shall not be part next year , I hope , grandmother , ' say Lesbia , affectionately . ' you say something about present I , and then leave I in Lady Kirkbank 's care for the season . I should not like that at all . I want you to go everywhere with I , to teach I all the mystery of the great world . you have always promise I that it should be so . ' 

 ' and I have always intend that it should be so . I hope that it will be so , ' answer her grandmother , with a sigh ; ' but I be an old woman , Lesbia , and I be root to this place . ' 

 ' but why should you be root here ? what charm can keep you here , when you be so fit to shine in society ? you be old in nothing but year , and not even old in year in comparison with woman whom we hear of , go everywhere and mix in every fashionable amusement . you be full of fire and energy , and as active as a girl . why should you not enjoy a London season , grandmother ? ' plead Lesbia , nestle her head lovingly against Lady Maulevrier 's shoulder . 

 ' I should enjoy it , dearest , with you . it would be a renewal of my youth to see you shine and conquer . I should be as proud as if the glory be all my own . yes , dear , I hope that I shall be a spectator of your triumph . but do not let we plan the future . life be so full of change . remember what Horace say - - ' 

 ' Horace be a bore , ' say Lesbia . ' I hate a poet who be always harp upon change and death . ' 

 the carriage , which be to take the traveller to Windermere Station , be announce at this moment , and Lesbia and her grandmother give each other the farewell embrace . 

 ' you like Lady Kirkbank , I hope ? ' say Lady Maulevrier , as they go towards the hall , where that lady be wait for they , with Lady Mary and Fršulein M ller in attendance upon she . 

 ' she seem very kind , but I should like she well if she do not paint or if she paint well . ' 

 ' my dear child I be afraid it be the fashion of the day , just as it be in Pope 's time , and we ought to think nothing about it . ' 

 ' well , I suppose I shall get harden in time . ' 

 ' my dear Lesbia , ' shriek Lady Kirkbank from below , ' remember we have to catch a train . ' 

 Lesbia hurry downstairs , follow by Lady Maulevrier , who have to bid her friend adieu . the luggage have be send on in a cart , Lesbia 's trunk and dress basket form no small item . she be so well furnish with pretty gown of all kind that there have be no difficulty in get she ready for this sudden visit . she maid be on the box beside the coachman . Lady Kirkbank 's attendant , a Frenchwoman of five - and - thirty , who look as if she have graduate at Mabille , be to occupy the back seat of the landau . 

 Lady Mary look after her sister longingly , as the carriage drive down the hill . she be go into a new world , to see all kind of people clever people distinguish people musical , artistic , political people hunt and shoot people while Mary be to stay at home all the winter among the old familiar face . dearly as she love these hill and vale her heart sink a little at the thought of those long lonely month , day and evening that would be all alike , and which must be spend without sympathetic companionship . and there would be dreary day on which the weather would keep she a prisoner in her luxurious gaol , when the mountain , and the rugged path beside the mountain stream , would be inaccessible , when she would be restrict to Fršulein 's phlegmatic society , that lady be stout and lazy , fond of her meal , and give to afternoon slumber . Lesbia and Mary be not by any mean sympathetic ; yet , after all , blood be thick than water ; and Lesbia be intelligent , and could talk of the thing Mary love , which be well than total dumbness , even if she generally take an antagonistic view of they . 

 ' I shall miss she dreadfully , ' think Mary , as she stroll listlessly in the garden , where the leaf where fall and the flower fade . 

 ' I wonder if she will see Mr. Hammond at Lady Kirkbank 's ? ' muse Mary . ' if he be anything like a lover he would find out all about her visit , and seize the opportunity of she be away from grandmother . but then if he have be much of a lover he would have follow she to St. Bees . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier sorely miss her favourite grandchild . in a life spend in such profound seclusion , so remote from the busy interest of the world , this beloved companionship have become a necessity to she . she have concentrate her affection upon Lesbia , and the girl 's absence make a fearful blank . but her ladyship 's dignity be not compromise by any outward sign of trouble or loss . 

 she spend her morning in her own room , read and writing and musing at her leisure ; she drive or walk every fine afternoon , sometimes alone , sometimes attend by Mary , who hat these stately drive and walk . she din tete - a - tete with Mary , except on those rare occasion when there be visitor the Vicar and his wife , or some wandering star from other world . Mary live in profound awe of her grandmother , but be of far too frank a nature to be able to adapt her speech or her manner to her ladyship 's idea of feminine perfection . she be silent and shy under those falcon eye ; but she be still the same Mary , the girl to whom pretence or simulation of any kind be impossible . 

 letter come almost every day from Kirkbank Castle , letter from Lesbia describe the bright gay life she be live at that hospitable abode , the excursion , the ride , the picnic luncheon after the morning 's sport , the dinner party , the dance . 

 ' it be the most delightful house you can imagine , ' write Lesbia ; ' and Lady Kirkbank be an admirable hostess . I have quite forgive she for wear false eyebrow ; for after all , you know , one must have eyebrow ; they be a necessity ; but why do she not have the two arch alike ? they be never a pair , and I really think that French maid of hers do it on purpose . 

 ' by - the - bye , Lady Kirkbank be go to write to you to beseech you to let I go to Cannes and Monte Carlo with she . Sir George insist upon it . he say they both like young society , and will be horribly vex if I refuse to go with they . and Lady Kirkbank think my chest be just a little weak I almost break down the other night in that lovely little song of Jensen 's and that a winter in the south be just what I want . but , of course , dear grandmother , I will not   ask you to let I be away so long if you think you will miss I . ' 

 ' if I think I shall miss she ! ' repeat Lady Maulevrier . ' have the girl no heart , that she can ask such a question ? but can I wonder at that ? of what account be I or my love to her father , although I sacrifice myself for his good name ? can I expect that she should be of a different clay ? ' 

 and then , meditate upon the event of the summer that be go , Lady Maulevrier think 

 she renounce her first lover at my bidding ; she renounce her love for I at the bidding of the world . or be it not rather self - interest , the fear of make a bad marriage , which influence she in her renunciation of Mr. Hammond . it be not obedience to I , it be not love for I which make she give he up . it be the selfishness engraine in her race . well , I have heap my love upon she , because she be fair and sweet , and remind I of my own youth . I must let she go , and try to be happy in the knowledge that she be enjoy her life far away from I . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier write her consent to the extension of Lesbia 's visit , and by return of post come a letter from Lesbia which seem brim over with love , and which comfort the grandmother 's wound heart . 

 ' Lady Kirkbank and I be both agree , dearest , that you must join we at Cannes , ' write Lesbia . ' at your age it be very wrong of you to spend a winter in our horrible climate . you can travel with Steadman and your maid . Lady Kirkbank will secure you a charming suite of room at the hotel , or she would like it still well if you would stay at her own villa . do consent to this plan , dear grandmother , and then we shall not be part for a long winter . of course Mary would be quite happy at home run wild . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier sigh as she read this letter , sigh again , and heavily , as she put it back into the envelope . alas , how many and many a year have go , long , monotonous , colourless year , since she have see that bright southern world which she be now urge to revisit . in fancy she see it again to - day , the tideless sea of deep sapphire blue , the little wavelet break on a yellow beach , the white triangular sail , the wood full of asphodel and great purple and white lily , the atmosphere steep in warmth and light and perfume , the glare of white house in the sun , the red and yellow blind , the pot of green and orange and crimson clay , with oleander abloom , the wonderful glow of colour everywhere and upon all thing . and then as the eye of the mind recall these vivid image her bodily eye look out upon the rain - blot scene , the mountain rise in a dark and dismal circle round that sombre pool below , wall she in from the outer world . 

 ' I be at the bottom of a grave , ' she say to herself . ' I be in a live tomb , from which there be no escape . forty year ! forty year of patience and hope , for what ? for dream which may never be realise ; for descendant who may never give I the price of my labour . yes , I should like to go to my dear one . I should like to revisit the South of France , to go on to Italy . I should feel young again amidst that eternal , unchangeable loveliness . I should forget all I have suffer . but it can not be . not yet , not yet ! ' 

 presently with a smile of concentrated bitterness she repeat the word ' not yet ! ' 

 ' surely at my age it must be folly to dream of the future ; and yet I feel as if there be half a century of life in I , as if I have lose nothing in either mental or bodily vigour since I come here forty year ago . ' 

 she rise as she say these word , and begin to pace the room , with quiet , firm step , erect , stately , beautiful in her advanced year as she have be in her bloom and freshness , only with another kind of beauty an empress among woman . the boast that she have make to herself be no idle boast . at sixty - seven year of age her physical power show no sign of decay , her mental quality be at their good and bright . long year of thought and study have ripen and widen her mind . she be a woman fit to be the friend and counsellor of statesman , the companion and confidant of her sovereign : and yet fate willed that she should be bury alive in a Westmoreland valley , see the same hill and stream , the same rustic face , from year 's end to year 's end . surely it be a hard fate , a heavy penance , albeit self - impose . 

 Lesbia go straight from Scotland to Paris with Sir George and Lady Kirkbank . here they stay at the Bristol for just two day , during which her hostess go all over the fashionable quarter buy clothe for the Cannes campaign , and assist Lesbia to spend the hundred pound which her grandmother have send she for the replenishment of her well - provide wardrobe . it be astonish how little way a hundred pound go among the dressmaker , corset - maker , and shoemaker of Lutetia . 

 ' I have no notion that clothe be so dear , ' say Lesbia , when she see how little she have get for her money . 

 ' my dear , you have two gown which be absolutely chien , ' reply Lady Kirkbank , ' and you have a corset which give you a figure , which you must forgive I for say you never have before . ' 

 Lady Kirkbank have to explain that chien as apply to a gown or bonnet be the same thing as chic , only a little more so . 

 ' I hope my gown will always be chien , ' say Lesbia meekly . 

 next evening they be din at Cannes , with the blue sea in front of their window , din at a table all abloom with orange flower , tea rose , mignonette , waxen camellia , and pale Parma violet , while Lady Maulevrier and Mary din tete - a - tete at Fellside , with the feathery snow flake fall outside , and the world whiten all around they . 

 next day the world be all white , and Mary 's beloved hill be inaccessible . 

 who could tell how long they might be cover ; the winding track hide ; the narrow force look like black water or molten iron against that glitter whiteness ? Mary could only walk along the road by Loughrigg to the bench call ' rest and be thankful , ' from which she look with long eye across towards the Langdale Pikes , and to the sharp cone - shape peak , know as Coniston Old Man , just visible above the nearer hill . Fršulein M ller suggest that it be in just such weather as this that a well bring up young lady , a young lady with Vernunft and Anstand , should devote herself to the improvement of her mind . 

 ' let we read German this abscheulich afternoon , ' say the Fršulein . ' suppose we go on with the " sorrow of Werther . " ' 

 ' Werther be a fool , ' cry Mary ; ' any book but that . ' 

 ' will you choose your own book ? ' 

 ' let I read Heine . ' 

 Fršulein look doubtful . there be thing in Heine an all - pervade tone which render he hardly an appropriate poet for ' the young person . ' but Fršulein compromise the matter by let Mary read Atta Troll , the exact bearing of which neither of they understand . 

 ' how beautifully Mr. Hammond read Heine that morning ! ' say Mary , break off suddenly from a perfectly automatic reading . 

 ' you do not hear he , do you ? you be not there , ' say the Fršulein . 

 ' I be not there , but I hear he . I I be sit on the bank among the pine tree . ' 

 ' why do you not come and sit with we ? it would have be more ladylike than to hide yourself behind the tree . ' 

 Mary blush crimson . 

 ' I have be in the kennel with Maulevrier ; I be not fit to be see , ' she say . 

 ' hardly a ladylike admission , ' reply the Fršulein , who feel that with Lady Mary her chief duty be to reprove . 

 CHAPTER XV . 

 ' of all MEN else I have AVOIDED THEE . ' 


 it be afternoon . the white hill yonder and all the length of the valley be touch here and there with gleam of wintry sunlight , and Lady Maulevrier be take her solitary walk on the terrace in front of her house , a stately figure wrap in a furred mantle , tall , erect , move with measured pace up and down the smooth gravel path . now and then at the end of the walk the dowager stop for a minute or so , and stand as if in deep thought , with her eye dreamily contemplate the landscape . an intense melancholy shadow her face , as she thus gaze with brood eye on the naked monotony of those wintry hill . so have she look in many and many a winter , and it seem to she that her life be of a piece with those bleak hill , where in the dismal winter time nothing living trod . she stand gaze at the sink sun , a fiery ball shin at the end of a long gallery of crag and rock , like a lamp at the end of a corridor ; and as she gaze the red round orb drop suddenly behind the edge of a crag , as if she have be an enchantress and have dismiss it with a wave of her wand . 

 ' o Lord , how long , how long ? ' she say . ' how many time have I see that sun go down from this spot , in winter and summer , in spring and autumn ! and now that the one be I love and care for be far away , I feel all the weariness and emptiness of my life . ' 

 as she turn to resume her walk she hear the muffle sound of wheel in the road below , that road which be completely hide by foliage in summer , but which be now visible here and there between the leafless tree . a carriage with a pair of horse be come along the road from Ambleside . 

 Lady Maulevrier stand and watch until the carriage draw up at the lodge gate , and then , when the gate have be open , slowly ascend the wind drive to the house . 

 she expect no visitor ; indeed , there be no one likely to come to she from the direction of Ambleside . her heart begin to beat heavily , with the apprehension of come evil . what kind of evil she know not . bad news about her granddaughter , perhaps , or about Maulevrier . and yet that could hardly be . evil tiding of that kind would have reach she by telegram . 

 perhaps it be Maulevrier himself . his movement be generally erratic . 

 Lady Maulevrier hurry back to the house . she go through the conservatory , where the warm whiteness of azalia , and spirea , and arum lily contrast curiously with the cold white snow out of door , to the hall , where a stranger be stand talk to the butler . 

 he be a man of foreign appearance , wear a cloak line with sable , and a sable cap , which he remove as Lady Maulevrier approach . he be thin and small , with a clear olive complexion , olive incline to pale bronze , sleek raven hair , and black almond - shape eye . at the first glance Lady Maulevrier know that he be an oriental . her heart sink within she , and seem to grow chill as death at sight of he . anything associate with India be horrible to she . 

 the stranger come forward to meet she , bow deferentially . he have those lithe , gliding movement which she remember of old , when she have see prince and dignitary of the East creeping shoeless to her husband 's foot . 

 ' will your ladyship do I the honour to grant I an interview ? ' he say in very good English . ' I have travel from London expressly for that privilege . ' 

 ' then I fear you have waste your time , sir , whatever your mission may be , ' the dowager answer , haughtily . ' however , I be willing to hear anything you may have to say , if you will be good enough to come this way . ' 

 she move towards the library , the butler precede she to open the door , and the stranger follow she into the spacious room , where coal and log be heap high upon the wide dog stove , deeply recess beneath the old English mantelpiece . 

 it be one of the handsome room of the house , furnish with oak bookcase about seven foot high , above which vas of oriental ware and varied colouring stand boldly out against the dark oak wall . richly bind book in infinite variety testify to the wealth and taste of the owner ; while one side of the room be absorb by a wide gothic window , beyond which appear the panorama of lake and mountain , beautiful in every season . a tawny velvet curtain divide this room from the drawing - room ; but there be also a strong oak door behind the curtain , which be generally close in cold weather . 

 Lady Maulevrier go over to this door , and take the precaution to draw the bolt , before she seat herself in her arm - chair by the hearth . she have her own particular chair in all the room she occupy a chair which be sacred as a throne . 

 she draw off her sealskin glove , and motion with a slender white hand to the stranger to be seat . 

 ' to whom have I the honour of speak ? ' she ask , look ; he through and through with an unflinche gaze , as she would have look at death himself , have the grim skeleton figure come to beckon she . 

 he hand she a visit card on which be engrave 

 ' Louis Asoph , Rajah of Bisnagar . ' 

 ' if my memory do not deceive I as to the history of modern India , the territory from which you take your title have be absorb into the english dominion ? ' say Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' it be traffic away forty - three year ago , steal , filch from my father ! but so long as I have power to think and to act I will maintain my claim to that land ; yes , if only by the empty mockery of a name on a visit card . it be a duty I owe to myself as a man , which I owe still more to my murder father . ' 

 ' have you come all the way from London , and in such weather , only to tell I this story ? ' 

 she have twist his card between her finger as she listen to he , and now , with an action at once careless and contemptuous , she fling it upon the burn log . slight as the action be it be eloquent of scorn for the man . 

 ' no , Lady Maulevrier , my mention of this story , with which you be no doubt perfectly familiar , be only a preliminary . I have come to claim my own , and to appeal to you as a woman of honour to do I justice .
