Nay , I will say as a woman of common honesty ; since there be no nice point of honour in question , only the plain law of mine and thine , which I believe be the same among all nation and creed . I come to you , Lady Maulevrier , to ask you to restore to I the wealth which your husband steal from my father . ' 

 ' you come to my house , to I , an old woman , helpless , defenceless , in the absence of my grandson , the present Earl , to insult I , and insult the dead , ' say Lady Maulevrier , white as statuary marble , and as cold and calm . ' you come to rake up old lie , and to fle they in the face of a solitary woman , old enough to be your mother . do you think that be a noble thing to do ? even in your barbarous eastern code of moral and manner be that the act of a gentleman ? ' 

 ' we be no barbarian in the East , Lady Maulevrier . I come from the cradle of civilisation , the original fount of learning . we be scholar and gentleman , priest and soldier , two thousand year before your british ancestor run wild in their wood , and sacrifice to their unknown god or rocky altar reeking with human blood ! I know the errand upon which I have come be not a pleasant one , either for you or for I ; but I come to you strong in the right of a son to claim the heritage which be steal from he by an infamous mother and her more infamous paramour - - ' 

 ' I will not hear another word ! ' cry Lady Maulevrier , start to her foot , livid with passion . ' do not dare to pronounce that name in my hearing the name of that abominable woman who bring disgrace and dishonour upon my husband and his race . ' 

 ' and who bring your husband the wealth of my murder father , ' answer the Indian , defiantly . ' do not ignore that fact , Lady Maulevrier . what have become of that fortune two hundred thousand pound in money and jewel . it be know to have pass into Lord Maulevrier 's possession after my father be put away by his pay instrument . 

 ' how dare you bring that vile charge against the dead ? ' 

 ' there be man live in India who know the truth of that charge : man who be at Bisnagar when my father , sick and heartbroken , be shut up in his deserted harem , hem in by spy and traitor , man with murder in their face . there be those who know tint he be strangle by one of those wretch , that a grave be dig for he under the marble floor of his zenana , a grave in which his bone be find less than a year ago , in my presence , and fitly entomb at my bidding . he be say to have disappear of his own free will to have leave his palace under cover of night , and seek refuge from possible treachery in another province ; but there be those , and not a few , who know the real history of his disappearance who know , and at the time be ready to testify in any court of justice , that he have be get rid of by the ranee 's agent , and at Lord Maulevrier 's instigation , and that his possession in money and jewel have be convey in the palankin that carry the ranee and her woman to his lordship 's summer retreat near Madras . the ranee die at that retreat six month after her husband 's murder , not without suspicion of poison , and the wealth which she carry with she when she leave Bisnagar pass into his lordship 's possession . have your husband live , Lady Maulevrier , this story must have be bring to light . there be too many people in Madras interested in sift the fact . there must have be a public inquiry . it be a happy thing for you and your race that Lord Maulevrier die before that inquiry have be institute , and that many animosity die with he . lucky too for you that I be a helpless infant at the time , and that the Mahratta adventurer to whom my father 's territory have be transfer in the shuffling of card at the end of the war be deeply concerned in hush up the story . ' 

 ' and pray , why have you nurse your wrath in all these year ? why do you intrude on I after nearly half a century , with this legend of rapine and murder ? ' 

 ' because for nearly half a century I have be keep in profound ignorance of my father 's fate in ignorance of my race . Lord Maulevrier 's jealousy banish I from my mother 's arm shortly after my father 's death . I be send to the South of France under the care of an ayah . my first memory be of a monastery near Marseilles , where I be rear and educate by a Jesuit community , where I be baptise and bring up in the roman catholic faith . by the influence of the Jesuit Fathers I be place in a house of commerce at Marseilles . fund to provide for my education and establishment in life , under very modest condition , be send periodically by an agent at Madras . it be know that I be of East indian birth , but little more be know about I . it be only when year have go by and I be a merchant on my own account and could afford to go to India on a voyage of discovery yes , as much a voyage of discovery as that of Vasco de Gama or of Drake that I get from the Madras agent the clue which enable I , at the cost of infinite patience and infinite labour , to unravel the mystery of my birth . there be no need to enter now upon the detail of that story . I have overwhelming documentary evidence a cloud of witness to convince the most sceptical as to who and what I be . the document be some of they in my valise , at your ladyship 's service . other be at my hotel in London , ready for the inspection of your ladyship 's lawyer . I do not think you will desire to invite a public inquiry , or force I to recover my birthright in a court of justice . I believe that you will take a broad and nobler view of the case , and that you will restore to the wronged and abandon son the fortune steal from his murder father . ' 

 ' how dare you come to I with this tissue of lie ? how dare you look I in the face and charge my dead husband with treachery and dishonour ? I believe neither in your story nor in you , and I defy you to the proof of this vile charge against the dead ! ' 

 ' in other word you mean that you will keep the money and jewel which Lord Maulevrier steal from my father ? ' 

 ' I deny the fact that any such jewel or money ever pass into his lordship 's possession . that vile woman , your mother , whose infamy cast a dark cloud over Lord Maulevrier 's honour , may have rob her husband , may have empty the public treasury . but not a rupee or a jewel belong to she ever come into my possession . I will not bear the burden of her crime . her existence spoil my life banish I from India , a widow in all but the name , and more desolate than many widow . ' 

 ' Lord Maulevrier be know to leave India carry with he two large chest suppose to contain book but actually contain treasure . a man who be in the Governor 's confidence , and who have be the go - between in his intrigue , confess on his death - bed that he have assist in remove the treasure . now , Lady Maulevrier , since your husband die immediately after his arrival in England , and before he could have have any opportunity of convert or make away with the valuable so appropriate , it stand to reason that those valuable must have pass into your possession , and it be from your honour and good feeling that I claim their restitution . if you deny the claim so advanced , there remain but one course open to I , and that be to make my wrong public , and claim my right from the law of the land . ' 

 ' and do you suppose that any english judge or english jury would believe so wild a story or countenance so vile an accusation against the defenceless ? ' demand Lady Maulevrier , stand up before he , tall , stately , with flash eye and scornful lip , the image of proud defiance . ' bring forward your claim , produce your document , your witness , your death - bed confession . I defy you to injure my dead husband or I by your wild lie , your foul charge ! go to an english lawyer , and see what an english law court will do for you and your claim . I will hear no more of either . ' 

 she ring the bell once , twice , thrice , with passionate hand , and a servant fly to answer that impatient summon . 

 ' show this gentleman to his carriage , ' she say , imperiously . 

 the gentleman who call himself Louis Asoph bow , and retire without another word . 

 as the door close upon he , Lady Maulevrier stand , with clench hand and frown brow , star into vacancy . her right arm be outstretche , as if she would have wave the intruder away . suddenly , a strange numbness creep over that uplifted arm , and it fall to her side . from her shoulder down to her foot , that proud form grow cold and feelingless and dead , and she , who have so long carry herself as a queen among woman , sink in a senseless heap upon the floor . 

 CHAPTER XVI . 

 ' her face resigned to BLISS or BALE . ' 


 Lady Mary and the Fršulein have be sit in the drawing - room all this time wait for Lady Maulevrier to come to tea . they hear she come in from the garden ; and then the footman tell they that she be in the library with a stranger . not even the muffled sound of voice penetrate the heavy velvet curtain and the thick oak door . it be only by the loud ringing of the bell and the sound of footstep in the hall that Lady Mary know of the guest 's departure . she go to the door between the two room , and be surprised to find it bolt . 

 ' Grandmamma , will not   you come to tea ? ' she ask timidly , knock on the oaken panel , but there be no reply . 

 she knock again , and louder . still no reply . 

 ' perhaps her ladyship be go to take tea in her own room , ' she say , afraid to be officious . 

 Attendance upon her grandmother at afternoon tea have be one of Lesbia 's particular duty ; but Mary feel that she be an unwelcome substitute for Lesbia . she want to get a little near her grandmother 's heart if she could ; but she know that her attention be endure rather than like . 

 she go into the hall , where the footman on duty be star at the light snowflake dance past the window , perhaps wish he be a snowflake himself , and enjoy himself in that white whirligig . 

 ' be her ladyship have tea in the morning - room ? ' ask Mary . 

 the footman give a little start , as if awaken out of a kind of trance . the sheer vacuity of his mind might naturally slide into mesmeric sleep . 

 he tell Lady Mary that her ladyship have not leave the library , and Mary go in timidly , wonder why her grandmother have not join they in the drawing - room when the stranger be go . 

 the sky be dark outside the wide window , white hill and valley shroud in the shade of night . the library be only light by the glow of the log on the hearth , and in that ruddy light the spacious room look empty . Mary be turn to go away , think the footman have be mistake , when her eye suddenly light upon a dark figure lie on the ground . and then she hear an awful stertorous breathing , and know that her grandmother be lie there , stricken and helpless . 

 Mary shriek aloud , with a cry that pierce curtain and door , and bring Fršulein and half - a - dozen servant to her help . one of the man bring a lamp , and among they they lift the smite figure . oh , God ! how ghastly the face look in the lamplight ! - the feature draw to one side , the skin livid . 

 ' her ladyship have have a stroke , ' say the butler . 

 ' be she die ? ' falter Mary , white as ash . ' oh , grandmother , dear grandmother , do not   look at we like that ! ' 

 one of the servant rush off to the stable to send for the doctor . of course , be an indoor man , he no more thought of go out himself into the snowy night on such an errand than Noah think of go out of the ark to explore the face of the water in person . 

 they carry Lady Maulevrier to her bed and lay she there , like a figure carve out of stone . she be not unconscious . her eye be open , and she moan every now and then as if in bodily or mental pain . once she try to speak , but have no power to shape a syllable aright , and end with a shudder sigh . once she lift her left arm and wave it in the air , as if wave some one off in fear or anger . the right arm , indeed the whole of the right side , be lifeless , motionless as a stone . it be a piteous sight to see the beautiful feature draw and distort , the lip so accustomed to command mouth the broken syllable of an unknown tongue . Lady Mary sit beside the bed with clasped hand , pray dumbly , with her eye fix on her grandmother 's altered face . 

 Mr. Horton come , as soon as his stout mountain pony could bring he . he do not seem surprised at her ladyship 's condition , and accept the situation with professional calmness . 

 ' a marked case of hemiplegia , ' he say , when he have observe the symptom . 

 ' will she die ? ' ask Mary . 

 ' oh , dear , no ! she will want great care for a little while , but we shall bring she round easily . a splendid constitution , a noble frame ; but I think she have overwork her brain a little , read Huxley and Darwin , and the german physiologist upon whom Huxley and Darwin have build themselves . Metaphysics too . Schopenhauer , and the rest of they . a wonderful woman ! very few brain could hold what hers have have pour into it in the last thirty year . the conduct nerve between the brain and the spinal marrow have be overwork : too much activity , too constant a strain . even the rail and sleeper on the railroad wear out , do not   you know , if there be excessive traffic . ' 

 Mr. Horton have know Mary from her childhood , have give her Gregory 's powder , and see she safely through measle and other infantine ailment , so he be quite at home with she , and at Fellside generally . Lady Maulevrier have give he a good deal of her confidence during those thirty year in which he have practise as his father 's partner and successor at Grasmere . he use to tell people that he owe the good part of his education to her ladyship , who condescend to talk to he of the new book she read , and generally give he a volume to put in his pocket when he be leave she . 

 ' do not   be downhearte , Lady Mary , ' he say ; ' I shall come in two or three time a day and see how thing be go on , and if I see the slight difficulty in the case I will telegraph for Jenner . ' 

 Mary and the Fršulein sit up with the invalid all that night . Lady Maulevrier 's maid be also in attendance , and one of the manservant sleep in his clothe on a couch in the corridor , ready for any emergency . but the night pass peacefully , the patient sleep a good deal , and next day there be evident improvement . the stroke which have prostrate the body , which reduce the vigorous , active frame to an awful statue like stillness a quietude as of death of itself have not overcloud the intellect . Lady Maulevrier lie on her bed in her luxurious room , with wide Tudor window command half the circle of the hill , and be still the rule spirit of the house , albeit powerless to move that slender hand , the light wave of which have be as potent to command in her little world as royal sign - manual or sceptre in the great world outside . 

 now there remain only one thing unimpaire by that awful shock which have lay the stately frame low , and that be the will and sovereign force of the woman 's nature . voice be alter , speech be confused and difficult ; but the strength of will , the supreme power of mind , seem undiminished . 

 when Lady Maulevrier be ask if Lesbia should be telegraph for , she reply no , not unless she be in danger of sudden death . 

 ' I should like to see she before I go , ' she say , labour to pronounce the word . 

 ' dear grandmother , ' say Mary , tenderly , ' Mr. Horton say there be no danger . ' 

 ' then do not send for she ; do not even tell she what have happen ; not yet . ' 

 ' but she will miss your letter . ' 

 ' true . you must write twice a week at my dictation . you must tell she that I have hurt my hand , that I be well but can not use a pen . I would not spoil her pleasure for the world . ' 

 ' dear grandmother , how unselfish you be ! and Maulevrier , shall he be send for ? he be not so far away , ' say Mary , hop her grandmother would say yes . 

 what a relief , what an unspeakable solace Maulevrier 's presence would be in that dreary house , smite to a sudden and awful stillness , as if by the Angel of Death ! 

 ' no , I do not want Maulevrier ! ' answer her ladyship impatiently . 

 ' may I sit here and read to you , grandmother ? ' Mary ask , timidly . ' Mr. Horton say you be to be keep very quiet , and that we be not to let you talk , or talk much to you , but that we might read to you if you like . ' 

 ' I do not wish to be read to . I have my thought for company , ' say Lady Maulevrier . 

 Mary feel that this imply a wish to be alone . she bent over the invalid 's pillow and kiss the pale cheek , feel as if she be take a liberty in venture so much . she would hardly have do it have Lesbia be at home ; but she bad a feeling that in Lesbia 's absence Lady Maulevrier must want somebody 's love even her . and then she creep away , leave Halcott the maid in attendance , sit at her work at the window furthest from the bed . 

 ' alone with my thought , ' muse Lady Maulevrier , look out at the panorama of wintry hill , white , ghost - like against an iron sky . ' pleasant thought , truly ! wall in by the hill wall in and hem round for ever . this place have always feel like a grave : and now I know that it be my grave . ' 

 Fršulein , and Lady Mary , and the maid Halcott , a sedate personage of forty summer , have all be instruct by the doctor that Lady Maulevrier be to be keep profoundly quiet . she must not talk much , since speech be likely to be a painful effort with she for some little time ; she must not be talk to much by anyone , least of all must she be speak to upon any agitate topic . life must be make as smooth and easy for she as for a new - bear infant . no rough breath from the outer world must come near she . she be to see no one but she maid and her granddaughter . Mr. Horton , a plain family man , take it for grant that the granddaughter be dear to her heart , and likely to exercise a soothing influence . thus it happen that although Lady Maulevrier ask repeatedly that James Steadman should be bring to she , she be not allow to see he . she whose will have be paramount in that house , whose word have be law , be now treat as a little child , while the will be still as strong , the mind as keen as ever . 

 ' she would talk to he of business , ' say Mr. Horton , when he be tell of her ladyship 's desire to see Steadman , ' and that can not be allow , not for some little time at least . ' 

 ' she be very angry with we for refuse to obey she , ' say Lady Mary . 

 ' naturally , but it be for her own welfare she be disobey . she can have nothing to say to Steadman which will not keep till she be well . this establishment go by clockwork . ' 

 Mary wish it be a little less like clockwork . since Lady Maulevrier have be lie upstairs the voice which have once rule over the house muffle almost to dumbness the monotony of life at Fellside have seem all the more oppressive . the servant creep about with stealthier tread . Mary dare not touch either piano or billiard ball , and be naturally seize with a longing to touch both . the house have a darkened look , as if the shadow of doom overhung it . 

 during this regimen of perfect quiet Lady Maulevrier be not allow to see the newspaper ; and Mary be warn that in reading to her grandmother she be to avoid all exciting topic . thus it happen that the account of a terrible collision between the Scotch express and a luggage train , a little way beyond Preston , an accident in which seven people be kill and about thirty seriously hurt , be not make known to her ladyship ; and yet that fact would have be of intense interest and significance to she , since one of those passenger whose injury be fatal bore the name of Louis Asoph . 

 CHAPTER XVII . 

 ' and the spring come SLOWLY up this way . ' 


 the wintry week glide smoothly by in a dull monotony , and now Lady Maulevrier , still helpless , still compel to lie on her bed or her invalid couch , motionless as marble , have at least recover her power of speech , be allow to read and to talk , and to hear what be go on in that metropolitan world which she seem unlikely ever to behold again . 

 Lady Lesbia be still at Cannes , whence she write of her pleasure and her triumph , of flower and sapphire sea , and azure sky , of all thing which be not in the grey bleak mountain world that hem in Fellside . she be meet many of the people whom she be to meet again next season in the London world . she have make an informal dťbut in a very select circle , a circle in which everybody be more or less chic , or chien , or zinc , and she be taste all the sweet of success . but in none of her letter be there any mention of Lord Hartfield . he be not in the little great world by the blue tideless sea . 

 there be no talk of Lesbia 's return . she be to stay till the carnival ; she be to stay till the week before Easter . Lady Kirkbank insist upon it ; and both Lesbia and Lady Kirkbank upbraid Lady Maulevrier for her cruelty in not join they at Cannes . 

 so Lady Maulevrier have to resign herself to that solitude which have become almost the habit of her life , and to the society of Mary and the Fršulein . Mary be eager to be of use , to sit with her grandmother , to read to she , to write for she . the warm young heart be deeply move by the spectacle of this stately woman stricken into helplessness , chain to her couch , immure within four wall . to Mary , who so love the hill and the stream , the sun and the wind , this imprisonment seem unspeakable woe . in her pity for such a martyrdom she would have do anything to give pleasure or solace to her grandmother . unhappily there be very little Mary could do to increase the invalid 's sum of pleasure . Lady Maulevrier be a woman of strong feeling , not capable of love many people . she have concentrate her affection upon Lesbia : and she could not open her heart to Mary all at once because Lesbia be out of the way . 

 ' if I have a dog I love , and he be to die , I would never have another in his place , ' Lady Maulevrier say once ; and that speech be the keynote of her character . 

 she be very courteous to Mary , and seem grateful for her attention ; but she do not cultivate the girl 's society . Mary write all her letter in a fine bold hand , and with a rapid pen ; but when the letter - writing be over Lady Maulevrier always dismiss she . 

 ' my dear , you want to be out in the air , rid your pony , or scamper about with your dog , ' she say , kindly . ' it would be a cruelty to keep you indoor . ' 

 ' no , indeed , dear grandmother , I should like to stay . may I stop and read to you ? ' 

 ' no , thank you , Mary . I hate be read to . I like to devour a book . read aloud be such slow work . 

 ' but I be afraid you must sometimes feel lonely , ' falter Mary . 

 ' lonely , ' echo the dowager , with a sigh . ' I have be lonely for the last forty year I have be lonely all my life . those I love never give I back love for love never not even your sister . see how lightly she cut the link that bind she to I . how happy she be among stranger ! yes , there be one who love I truly , and fate part we . do fate part all true lover , I wonder ? ' 

 ' you part Lesbia and Mr. Hammond , ' say Mary , impetuously . ' I be sure they love each other truly . ' 

 ' the old and the worldly - wise be Fate , Mary , ' answer the dowager , not angry at this daring reproach . ' I know your sister ; and I know she be not the kind of woman to be happy in an ignoble life to bear poverty and deprivation . if it have be you , now , whom Mr. Hammond have choose , I might have take the subject into my consideration . ' 

 Mary flame crimson . 

 ' Mr. Hammond never give I a thought , ' she say , ' unless it be to think I contemptible . he be world too good for such a Tomboy . Maulevrier tell he about the fox - hunt , and they both laugh at I at least I have no doubt Mr. Hammond laugh , though I be too much ashamed to look at he . ' 

 ' Poor Mary , you be begin to find out that a young lady ought to be ladylike , ' say Lady Maulevrier ; ' and now , my dear , you may go . I be only joke with you . Mr. Hammond would be no match for any granddaughter of mine . he be nobody , and have neither friend nor interest . if he have go into the church Maulevrier could have help he ; but I daresay his idea be too broad for the church ; and he will have to starve at the bar , where nobody can help he . I hope you will bear this in mind , Mary , if Maulevrier should ever bring he here again . ' 

 ' he be never likely to come back again . he suffer too much ; he be treat too badly in this house . ' 

 ' Lady Mary , be good enough to remember to whom you be speak , ' say her ladyship , with a frown . ' and now please go , and tell some one to send Steadman to I . ' 

 Mary retire without a word , give Lady Maulevrier 's message to a footman in the corridor , slip off to her room , put on her sealskin hat and jacket , take her staff and go out for a long ramble . the hill and valley be still white . it have be a long , cold winter , and spring be still far off February have only just begin . 

 Lady Maulevrier 's couch have be wheel into the morning - room that luxurious room which be furnish with all thing needful to her quiet life , her book , her favourite colour , her favourite flower , every detail studiously arrange for her pleasure and comfort . she be wheel into this room every day at noon . when the day be bright and sunny her couch be place near the window : and when the day be dull and grey the couch be draw close to the low hearth , which flash and glitter with brightly coloured tile and artistic brass . 

 to - day the sky be dull , and the velvet couch stand beside the hearth . Halcott sit at work in the adjoining bed - chamber , and come in every now and then to replenish the fire : a footman be always on duty in the corridor . a spring bell stand among the elegant trifle upon her ladyship 's table ; and the light touch of her left hand upon the bell bring her attendant to her side . she resolutely refuse to have any one sit with she all day long . Solitude be a necessity of her be , she tell Mr. Horton , when he recommend that she should have some one always in attendance upon she . 

 as the week wear on her feature have be restore to their proud , calm beauty , her articulation be almost as clear as of old : yet , now and then , there would be a sudden faltering , the tongue and lip would refuse their office , or she would forget a word , or use a wrong word unconsciously . but there be no recovery of power or movement on that side of the body which have be stricken . the paralysed limb be still motionless , lifeless as marble ; and it be clear that Mr. Horton have begin to lose heart about his patient . there be nothing obscure in the case , but the patient 's importance make the treatment a serious matter , and the surgeon beg to be allow to summon Sir William Jenner . 

 this , however , Lady Maulevrier refuse . 

 ' I do not   want any fuss make about I , ' she say . ' I be content to trust myself to your skill , and I beg that no other doctor may be summon . ' 

 Mr. Horton understand his patient 's feeling on this point . she have a sense of humiliation in her helplessness , and , like some wound animal that crawl to its covert to die , she would fain have hide her misery from the eye of stranger . she have allow no one , not even Maulevrier , to be inform of the nature of her illness . 

 ' it will be time enough for he to know all about I when he come here , ' she say . ' I shall be oblige to see he whenever he do come . ' 

 Maulevrier have spend Christmas and New Year in Paris , Mr. Hammond still his companion . her ladyship comment upon this with a touch of scorn . 

 ' Mr. Hammond be like the Umbra you be read about the other day in Lord Lytton 's " last day of pompeii , " ' she say to Mary . ' it must be very nice for he to go about the world with a friend who frank he everywhere . ' 

 ' but we do not   know that Maulevrier frank he , ' protest Mary , blushing . ' we have no right to suppose that Mr. Hammond do not pay his own expense . ' 

 ' my dear child , be it possible for a young man who have no private mean to go gad about the world on equal term with a spendthrift like Maulevrier to pay for Moors in Scotland and apartment at the Bristol ? ' 

 ' but they be not stay at the Bristol , ' exclaim Mary . ' they be stay at an old - establish french hotel on the left side of the Seine . they be go about amongst the student and the workman , din at popular restaurant , hear people talk . Maulevrier say it be delightfully amusing ever so much well than the beat track of life in anglo - American Paris . ' 

 ' I daresay they be lead a bohemian life , and will get into trouble before they have do , ' say her ladyship , gloomily . ' Maulevrier be as wild as a hawk . ' 

 ' he be the dear boy in the world , ' exclaim Mary . 

 she be deeply grateful for her brother 's condescension in write she a letter of two page long , let she into the secret of his life . she feel as if Mr. Hammond be ever so much near to she now she know where he be , and how he be amusing himself . 

 ' Hammond be such a queer fellow , ' write Maulevrier , ' the strange thing interest he . he sit and talk to the workman for hour ; he poke his nose into all sort of place hospital , workshop , poverty - stricken den and people be always civil to he . he be what Lesbia call sympatico . ah ! what a mistake Lesbia and my grandmother make when they reject Hammond ! what a pearl above price they throw away ! but , you see , neither my lady nor Lesbia could appreciate a gem , unless it be richly set . ' 

 and now Lady Maulevrier lie on her couch by the fire , wait for James Steadman . she have see he several time since the day of her seizure , but never alone . there be an idea that Steadman must necessarily talk to she of business matter , or cause her mind to trouble itself about business matter ; so there have be a well - intentione conspiracy in the house to keep he out of her way ; but now she be much well , and her desire to see Steadman need no long be thwart . 

 he come at her bidding , and stand a little way within the door , tall , erect , square - shoulder , resolute - look , with a quiet force of character express in every feature . he be very much the same man that he have be forty year ago , when he go with her ladyship to Southampton , and accompany his master and mistress on that tedious journey which be destine to be Lord Maulevrier 's last earthly pilgrimage . time have do little to Steadman in those forty year , except to whiten his hair and beard , and imprint some thoughtful line upon his sagacious forehead . Time have do something for he mentally , insomuch as he have read a great many book and cultivate his mind in the monotonous quiet of Fellside . altogether he be a superior man for the passage of those forty year . 

 he have marry within the time , choose for himself the buxom daughter of a lodgekeeper , whose wife have long be lay at rest in Grasmere churchyard . the buxom girl have grow into a bulky matron , but she be a colourless personage , and her existence make hardly any difference in James Steadman 's life . she have bring he no child , and their fireside be lonely ; but Steadman seem to be one of those self - contain personage to whom a solitary life be no affliction . 

 ' I hope I see you in well health , my lady , ' he say , stand straight and square , like a soldier on parade . 

 ' I be well , thank you , Steadman ; well , but a poor lifeless log chain to this sofa . I send for you because the time have come when I must talk to you upon a matter of business . you hear , I suppose , that a stranger call upon I just before I have my attack ? ' 

 ' yes , my lady . ' 

 ' do you hear who and what he be ? ' 

 ' only that he be a foreigner , my lady . ' 

 ' he be of indian birth . he claim to be the son of the Ranee of Bisnagar . ' 

 ' he could do you no harm , my lady , if he be twenty time her son . ' 

 ' I hope not . now , I want to ask you a question . among those trunk and case and package of Lord Maulevrier 's which be send here by heavy coach , after they be land at Southampton , do you remember two case of book ? ' 

 ' there be two large case among the luggage , my lady ; very heavy case , iron clamp . I should not be surprised if they be full of book . ' 

 ' have they never be open ? ' 

 ' not to my knowledge . ' 

 ' be they lock ? ' 

 ' yes , my lady . there be two padlock on each chest . ' 

 ' and be the key in your possession ? ' 

 ' no , my lady . ' 

 ' where be the case ? ' 

 ' in the Oak Room , with the rest of the indian luggage . ' 

 ' let they remain there . no doubt those case contain the book of which I have be tell . you have not hear that the person call himself Rajah of Bisnagar have be here since my illness , have you ? ' 

 ' no , my lady ; I be sure he have not be here . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier give he a scrutinise look . 

 ' he might have come , and my people might have keep the knowledge from I , out of consideration for my infirmity , ' she say . ' I should be very angry if it be so . I should hate to be treat like a child . ' 

 ' you shall not be so treat , my lady , while I be in this house ; but I know there be no member of the household who would presume so to treat you . ' 

 ' they might do it out of kindness ; but I should loathe such kindness , ' say Lady Maulevrier , impatiently . ' though I have be smite down , though I lie here like a log , I have a mind to think and to plan ; and I be not afraid to meet danger , face to face . be you tell I the truth , Steadman ? have there be no visit conceal from I , no letter keep from I since I have be ill ? ' 

 ' I be tell you nothing but the truth , my lady . no letter have be keep from you ; no visitor have be to this house whose come you have not be tell of . ' 

 ' then I be content , ' say her ladyship , with a sigh of relief . 

 after this there follow some conversation upon business matter . James Steadman be trust with the entire management of the dowager 's income , the investment of her saving . his honesty be above all suspicion . he be a man of simple habit , his want few . he have save money in every year of his service ; and for a man of his station be rich enough to be unassailable by the tempter . 

 he have reconcile his mind to the monotonous course of life at Fellside in the beginning of thing ; and , as the year glide smoothly by , his character and want and inclination have , as it be , mould themselves to fit that life . he have easy duty , a comfortable home , supreme authority in the household . he be look up to and make much of in the village whenever he condescend to appear there ; and by the rareness of his visit to the Inn or the reading - room , and his unwillingness to accept hospitality from the tradesman of Grasmere and Ambleside , he maintain his dignity and exaggerate his importance . he have his book and his newspaper , his evening leisure , which no one ever dare to disturb . he have the old wing of the house for his exclusive occupation ; and no one venture to intrude upon he in his privacy . there be a bell in the corridor which communicate with his room , and by this bell he be always summon . there be servant who have be ten year at Fellside , and who have never cross the threshold of the red cloth door which be the only communication between the new house and the old one . Steadman 's wife perform all household duty of cooking and clean in the south wing , where she and her husband take all their meal , and live entirely apart from the other servant , an exclusiveness which be secretly resent by the establishment . 

 ' Mr. Steadman may be a very superior man , ' say the butler ' and I know that in his own estimation the Premier be not   in it compare with he ; but I never be fond of people who set themselves upon pinnacle , and I be not fond of the Steadmans . ' 

 ' Mrs. Steadman 's plain and homely enough , ' reply the housekeeper , ' and I know she would like to be more sociable , and drop into my room for a cup of tea now and then ; but Steadman do so keep she under his thumb : and because he be a misanthrope she be oblige to sit and mope alone . ' 

 if Steadman want to drive , there be a dogcart and horse at his disposal ; but he do not often leave Fellside . he seem in his humble way to model his life upon Lady Maulevrier 's seclude habit . it be grow dusk when Steadman leave his mistress , and she lie for some time look at the landscape over which twilight shadow be steal , and think of her own life . over that life , too , the shadow of evening be creep . she have begin to realise the fact that she be an old woman ; that for her all personal interest in life be nearly over . she have never feel her age while her activity be unimpaired . she have be oblige to remind herself very often that the afternoon and evening of life have slip away unawares in that tranquil retirement , and that the night be at hand . 

 for she the close of earthly life mean actual night . no new dawn , no mysterious after - life shine upon she with magical gleam of an unknown light upon the other side of the dark river . she have accept the Materialist 's bitter and barren creed , and have teach herself that this little life be all . she have learn to scorn the idea of a great Artificer outside the universe , a mighty spirit rid amidst the cloud , and rule the course of nature and the fate of man . she have school herself to think that the idea of a blind , unconscious nature , work automatically through infinite time and space , be ever so much grander than the old - world notion of a personal God , a being of infinite power and inexhaustible beneficence , mighty to devise and direct the universe , with knowledge reach to the farth confine of space , with ear to listen to the prayer of his low creature . her belief stop short even of the Deist 's faith in an Almighty Will . she see in creation nothing but the inevitable development of material law ; and it seem to she that there be quite as much hope of a heavenly world after death for the infusoria in the pool as for man in his pride and power . 

 she read she Bible as diligently as she read her Shakespeare , and the word of the Royal Preacher in some measure embody her own dreary creed . and now , in the darkening winter day , she watch the gloomy shadow creep over the rugged breast of Nabb Scar , and she think how there be a time for all thing , and that her day of hope and ambition be past . 

 of late year she have live for Lesbia , look forward to the day when she be to introduce this beloved grandchild to the great world of London ; and now that hope be go for ever . 

 what could a helpless cripple do for a fashionable beauty ? what good would it be for she to be convey to London , and to lie on a couch in Mayfair , while Lesbia ride in the row and go to three or four party every night with a more active chaperon ? 

 she have hop to go everywhere with her darling , to glory in all her success , to shield she from all possibility of failure . and now Lesbia must stand or fall alone . 

 it be a hard thing ; but perhaps the hard part of it be that Lesbia seem so very well able to get on without she . the girl write in the high spirit ; and although her letter be most affectionately word , they be all about self . that note be dominant in every strain . her triumph , her admirer , her bonnet , her gown . she have have more money from her grandmother , and more gown from Paris . 

 ' you have no idea how the people dress in this place , ' she write . ' I should have be quite out in the cold without my three new frock from Worth . the little Princess bonnet I wear be the rage . worth recommend I to adopt special flower and colour ; so I have wear nothing but primrose since I have be here , and my little primrose bonnet be to be see everywhere , sometimes on hideous old woman . Lady Kirkbank hop you will be able to go to London directly after Easter . she say I must be present at the May drawing - room that be imperative . People have begin to talk about I ; and unless I make my dťbut while their interest be fresh I shall be a failure . there be an american beauty here , and I believe she and I be consider rival , and young man lie wager about we , as to which will look well at a ball , or a regatta , what colour we shall wear , and so on . it be immense fun . I only wish you be here to enjoy it . the american girl be a most insolent person , but I have have the pleasure of crush she on several occasion in the calm way . in the description of the concert in last week 's newspaper I be call l'Anglais de marbre . I certainly have the decency to hold my tongue while Faure be sing . Miss Bolsover 's voice be hear ever so many time above the music . accord to our english idea she have most revolt manner , and the money she spend on her clothe would make your hair stand on end . now do , dearest grandmother , make all your arrangement for begin the campaign directly after Easter . you must take a house in the very choice quarter Lady Kirkbank suggest Grosvenor - place and it must be a large house , for of course you will give a ball . Lady K. say we might have Lord Porlock 's house poor Lady Porlock and her baby die a few week ago , and he have go to Sweden quite break - hearted . it be one of the new house , exquisitely furnish , and Lady K. think you might have it for a song . will you get Steadman to write to his lordship 's steward , and see what can be do ? 

 ' I hope the dear hand be well . you have never tell I how you hurt it . it be very sweet of Mary to write I such long letter , and quite a pleasant surprise to find she can spell ; but I want to see your own dear hand once more . ' 

 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 ' and COME agen be it by NIGHT or DAY . ' 


 those winter month be unutterably dreary for Lady Mary Haselden . she feel weigh down by a sense of death and woe near at hand . the horror of that dreadful moment in which she find her grandmother lie senseless on the ground , the terror of that distorted countenance , those start eye , that stertorous breathing , be not easily banish from a vivid girlish imagination ; see how few distraction there be to divert Mary 's thought , and how the sun sink and rise again upon the same inevitable surrounding , to the same monotonous routine . 

 her grandmother be kind than she have be in day go by , less inclined to find fault ; but Mary know that her society give Lady Maulevrier very little pleasure , that she could do hardly anything towards fill the gap make by Lesbia 's absence . there be no one to scold she , no one to quarrel with she . Fršulein M ller lecture she mildly from time to time ; but that stout German be too lazy to put any force or fire into her lecture . her reproof be like the fall of waterdrop on a stone , and infinite age would have be need to cause any positive impression . 

 February come to an end without sign or token from the outer world to disturb the even tenor of life at Fellside . Mary read , and read , and read , till she feel she be make up of the content of book , cram with other people 's idea . she read history , or natural science , or travel , or german poetry in the morning , and novel or english poetry in the evening . she have pledge herself to devote her morning indoor hour to instructive literature , and to accomplish some portion of study in every day . she be carry on her education on parole , as before state ; and she be too honourable to do less than be expect from she . 

 March come in with its most leonine aspect , howl and blustering ; north - east wind shriek along the gorge and wail from height to height . 

 ' I wonder the lion and the lamb be not blow into the lake , ' say Mary , look at Helm Crag from the library window . 

 she scamper about the garden in the very tooth of those bitter blast , and take her shiver terrier for run on the green slope of the Fell . the snow have gradually melt from the tide of the lowermost range of hill , but the mountain peak be still white and ghostly , the ground be still hard and slippery in the early morning . Mary have to take her walk alone in this bleak weather . Fršulein have a convenient bronchial affection which forbid she to venture so much as the point of her nose outside the house in an east wind , and which justify she in occasionally take her breakfast in bed . she spend her day for the most part in her arm - chair , draw close to the fireplace , which she still insist upon call the oven , knit diligently , or read the Rundschau . even music , which have once be her strong point , be neglect in this try weather . it be such a cold journey from the oven to the piano . 

 Mary play a good deal in her desultory manner , now that she have the drawing - room all to herself , and no fear of Lady Maulevrier 's critical ear or Lesbia 's superior smile . the Fršulein be pleased to hear her pupil ramble on with her favourite bit from Raff , and Hensel , and Schubert , and Mendelssohn , and Mozart , and be very well content to let she play just what she like , and to escape the trouble of train she to that exquisite perfection into which Lady Lesbia have be drill . Lesbia be not a genius , and the training process have be quite as hard for the governess as for the pupil . 

 thus the slow day wear on till the first week in March , and on one bleak bitter afternoon , when Fršulein M ller stick to the oven even a little close than usual , Mary feel she must go out , in the face of the east wind , which be toss the leafless branch in the valley below until the tree look like an angry crowd , hurl its arm in the air , fight , struggle , writhe . she must leave that dreary house for a little while , be it even to be lash and bruise and break by that fierce wind . so she tell Fršulein that she really must have her constitutional ; and after a feeble remonstrance Fršulein let she go , and subside luxuriously into the pillowed depth of her arm - chair . 

 there have be a hard frost , and all the mountain way be perilous , so Mary set out upon a steady tramp along the road lead towards the Langdales . the wind seem to assail she from every side , but she have accustom herself to defy the element , and she only hug her sealskin jacket close to she , and quicken her pace , chirrup and whistle to Ahab and Ariadne , the two fox - terrier which she have select for the privilege of a walk . 

 the terrier race along the road , and Mary , see that she have the road all to herself , race after they . a light snow - shower , large feathery flake fly wide apart , fall from the steel - grey sky ; but Mary mind the snow no more than she mind the wind . she race on , the terrier scamper , rush , fly before she , until , just where the road take a curve , she almost run into a horse , which be step along at a tremendous pace , with a light , high dogcart behind he . 

 ' hi ! ' cry the driver , ' where be you come , young woman ? have you never see a horse till to - day ? ' 

 some one beside the driver leapt out , and run to see if Mary be hurt . the horse have swerve to one side , rear a little , and then spin on for a few yard , leave she standing in the middle of the road . 

 ' why , it be molly ! ' cry the driver , who be no less distinguished a whip than Lord Maulevrier , and who have recognise the terrier . 

 ' I hope you be not hurt , ' say the gentleman who have alight , Maulevrier 's friend and shadow , John Hammond . 

 Mary be cover with confusion by her exploit , and could hardly answer Mr. Hammond 's very simple question . 

 she look up at he piteously , try to speak , and he take alarm at her scared expression . 

 ' I be sure you be hurt , ' he say earnestly , ' the horse must have strike you , or the shaft perhaps , which be bad . be it your shoulder that be hurt , or your chest ? lean on I , if you feel faint or giddy . Maulevrier , you have well drive your sister home , and get she look after . ' 

 ' indeed , I be not hurt ; not the least little bit , ' gasp Mary , who have recover her sens by this time . ' I be only frightened , and it be such a surprise to see you and Maulevrier . ' 

 a surprise yes a surprise which have set her heart throb so violently as to render she speechless . have horse or shaft - point strike she ever so , she would have hardly be more tremulous than she feel at this moment . never have she hop to see he again . he have set his all upon one cast love , woo , and lose her sister . why should he ever come again ? what be there at Fellside worth come for ? and then she remember what her grandmother think of he . he be a hanger - on , a sponge , a lead captain . he be Maulevrier 's Umbra , and must go where his patron go . it be a hard thing so to think of he , and Mary 's heart sink at the thought that Lady Maulevrier 's worldly wisdom might have reckon aright . 

 ' it be very foolish of I to run into the horse , ' say Mary , while Mr. Hammond stand wait for she to recover herself . 

 ' it be very foolish of Maulevrier to run into you . if he do not   drive at such a break - neck pace it would not   have happen . ' 

 Umbra be very plain - speak , at any rate . 

 ' there be rank ingratitude , ' cry Maulevrier , who have turn back , and be look down at they from his elevated perch . ' after my come all the way round by langdale to oblige you with a view of Elterwater . Molly 's all safe and sound . she would not   have mind if I would run over she . come along , child , get up beside I , Hammond will take the back seat . ' 

 this be easy say than do , for the back of the dogcart be pile with Gladstone bag , fishing rod , and hat - box ; but Umbra be ready to oblige . he hand Mary up to the seat by the driver , and clamber up at the back , when he hook himself on somehow among the luggage . 

 ' Dear Maulevrier , how delicious of you to come ! ' say Mary , when they be rattle on towards Fellside ; ' I hope you be go to stay for age . ' 

 ' well , I dare say , if you make yourself very agreeable , I may stay till after Easter . ' 

 Mary 's countenance fall . 

 ' Easter be in three week , ' she say , despondingly . 

 ' and be not   three week an age , at such a place as Fellside ? I do not   know that I should have come at all on this side of the August sport , only as the grandmother be ill , I think it a duty to come and see she . a fellow may not   care much for ancestor when they be well , you know ; but when a poor old lady be down on her luck , her people ought to look after she . so , here I be ; and as I know I should be mop to death here - - ' 

 ' thank you for the compliment , ' say Mary . 

 ' I bring Hammond along with I . of course , I know Lesbia be safe out of the way , ' add Maulevrier in an undertone . 

 ' it be very obliging of Mr. Hammond always to go where you wish , ' return Mary , who could not help a bitter feeling when she remember her grandmother 's cruel suggestion . ' has he no taste or inclination of his own ? ' 

 ' yes , he have , plenty of they , and much loftier taste than mine , I can tell you . but he be kind enough to let I hang on to he , and to put up with my frivolity . there never be two man more different than he and I be ; and I suppose that be why we get on so well together . when we be in Paris he be always up to his eye in serious work lecture , public library , workman 's syndicate , Mary Anne , the International heaven know what , make himself master of the political situation in France ; while I be rigolant and chaloupant at the Bal Bullier . ' 

 it be generous of Maulevrier to speak of his hanger - on thus ; and no doubt the society of a well - inform earnest young man be a great good for Maulevrier , a good far above the price of those pound , shilling , and penny which the Earl might spend for his dependent 's benefit ; but when a girl of Mary 's ardent temper have make a hero of a man , it gall she to think that her hero 's dignity should be sacrifice , his honour impeach , be it by the mere tittle . 

 Maulevrier make a good many inquiry about his grandmother , and seem really full of kindness and sympathy ; but it be with a feeling of profound awe , nay , of involuntary reluctance and shrinking , that he presently enter her ladyship 's sitting - room , usher in by Mary , who have be to her grandmother beforehand to announce the grandson 's arrival . 

 the young man have hardly ever be in a sick room before . he half expect to see Lady Maulevrier in bed , with a crowd of medicine bottle and a cut orange on a table by her side , and a sick nurse of the ancient - crone specie cower over the fire . it be an infinite relief to he to find his grandmother lie on a sofa by the fire in her pretty morning room . a little tea - table be draw close up to her sofa , and she be take her afternoon tea . it be rather painful to see she lift her tea - cup slowly and carefully with her left hand , but that be all . the dark eye still flash with the old eagle glance , the line of the lip be as proud and firm as ever . all sign of contraction or distortion have pass away . in hour of calm her ladyship 's beauty be unimpaire ; but with any strong emotion there come a convulsive work of the feature , and the face be momentarily draw and distort , as it have be at the time of the seizure . 

 Maulevrier 's presence have not an unduly agitate effect on her ladyship . she receive he with tranquil graciousness , and thank he for his coming . 

 ' I hope you have spend your winter profitably in Paris , ' she say . ' there be a great deal to be learn there if you go into the right circle . ' 

 Maulevrier tell she that he have find much to learn , and that he have go into circle where almost everything be new to he . whereupon his grandmother question he about certain noble family in the Faubourg Saint Germain who have be know to she in her own day of power , and whose movement she have observe from a distance since that time ; but here she find her grandson dark . he have not happen to meet any of the people she speak about : the plain truth be that he have live altogether as a Bohemian , and have not use one of the letter of introduction that have be give to he . 

 ' your friend Mr. Hammond be with you , I be tell , ' say Lady Maulevrier , not altogether with delight . 

 ' yes , I make he come ; but he be quite safe . he will bolt like a shot at the least hint of Lesbia 's return . he do not   want to meet that young lady again , I can assure you . ' 

 ' pray do not   talk in that injure tone . Mr. Hammond be a gentlemanlike person , very well inform , very agreeable . I have never deny that . but you could not expect I to allow my granddaughter to throw herself away upon the first adventurer who make she an offer . ' 

 ' Hammond be not an adventurer . ' 

 ' very well , I will not call he so , if the term offend you . but Mr. Hammond be Mr. Hammond , and I can not allow Lesbia to marry Mr. Hammond or Mr. Anybody , and I be very sorry you have bring he here again . there be Mary , a silly , romantic girl . I be very much afraid he have make an impression upon she . she colour absurdly when she talk of he , and fly into a passion with I the other day when I venture to hint that he be not a Rothschild , and that his society must be expensive to you . ' 

 ' his society do not cost I anything . Hammond be the soul of independence . he work as a blacksmith in Canada for three month , just to see what life be like in a wild district . there never be such a fellow to rough it . and as for Molly , well , now , really , if he happen to take a fancy to she , and if she happen to like he , I would not   bosh the business , if I be you , grandmother . take my word for it , molly might do bad . ' 

 ' of course . she might marry a chimney sweep . there be no answering for a girl of her erratic nature . she be silly enough and romantic enough for anything ; but I shall not countenance she if she want to throw herself away on a person without prospect or connection ; and I look to you , Maulevrier , to take care of she , now that I be a wretched log chain to this room . ' 

 ' you may rely upon I , grandmother , Molly shall come to no harm , if I can help it . ' 

 ' thank you , ' say her ladyship , touch her bell twice . 

 the two clear silvery stroke be a summon for Halcott , the maid , who appear immediately . 

 ' tell Mrs. Power to get his lordship 's room ready immediately , and to give Mr. Hammond the room he have last summer , ' say Lady Maulevrier , with a sigh of resignation . 

 while Maulevrier be with his grandmother John Hammond be smoke a solitary cigar on the terrace , contemplate the mountain landscape in its cold March greyness , and wonder very much to find himself again at Fellside . he have go forth from that house full of passionate indignation , shake off the dust from his foot , sternly resolve never again to cross the threshold of that fateful cave , where he have meet his cold - hearted Circe . and now , because Circe be safe out of the way , he have come back to the cavern ; and he be feel all the pain that a man feel who behold again the scene of a great past sorrow . 

 be this the old love and the old pain again , he wonder , or be it only the sharp thrust of a bitter memory ? he have believe himself cure of his useless love a great and noble love , waste on a small nature than his own . he have think that because his eye be open , and he understand the character of the girl he love , his cure must need be complete . yet now , face to face with the well - remember landscape , look down upon that dull grey lake which he have see smile in the sunshine , he begin to doubt the completeness of his cure . he recall the lovely face , the graceful form , the sweet , low voice the perfection of gracious womanhood , manner , dress , movement , tone , smile , all faultless ; and in the absence of that one figure , it seem to he as if he have come back to a tenantless , dismantled house , where there be nothing that make life worth live . 

 the red sun go down a fierce and lurid face that seem to scowl through the grey and Mr. Hammond feel that it be time to arouse himself from gloomy meditation and go in and dress for dinner . Maulevrier 's valet be to arrive by the coach with the heavy part of the luggage , and Maulevrier 's valet do that very small portion of valet which be ever require by Mr. Hammond . a man who have work at a forge in the backwood be not likely to be finicke in his way , or dependent upon servant for look after his raiment . 

 despite Mr. Hammond 's gloomy memory of past joy and disillusion , he contrive to make himself very agreeable , by - and - by , at dinner , and in the drawing - room after dinner , and the evening be altogether gay and sprightly . Maulevrier be in high spirit , full of his parisian experience , and talk slang as glibly as a student of the Quartier Latin . he would talk nothing but French , protest that he have almost forget his native tongue , and his French be the language of Larchey 's Dictionary of Argot , in which nothing be call by its right name . Mary be enchant with this new vocabulary , and want to have every word explain to she ; but Maulevrier confess that there be a good deal that be unexplainable . 

 the evening be much lively than those summer evening when the dowager and Lady Lesbia be present . there be something less of refinement , perhaps , and Fršulein remonstrate now and then about some small violation of the unwritten law of ' Anstand , ' but there be more mirth . Maulevrier feel for the first time as if he be master at Fellside . they all go to the billiard room soon after dinner , and Fršulein and Mary sit by the fire look on , while the two young man play . in such an evening there be no time for bitter memory : and John Hammond be surprised to find how little he have miss that enchantress whose absence have make the house seem desolate to he when he re - enter it . 

 he be tired with his journey and the vary emotion of the day , for it be not without strong emotion that he have consent to return to Fellside and he sleep soundly for the early part of the night . but he have train himself long ago to do with a very moderate portion of sleep , and he be up and dress while the dawn be still slowly creep along the edge of the hill . he go quietly down to the hall , take one of the bamboo from a collection of cane and mountain stick , and set out upon a morning ramble over the snowy slope . the snow shower of yesterday have only sprinkle the greensward upon the low ground , but in the upper region the winter snow still linger , give an Alpine character to the landscape . 

 John Hammond be too experience a mountaineer to be deter by a little snow . he go up Silver Howe , and from the rugged breast of the mountain see the sun leap up from amidst a chaos of hill and crag , in all his majesty , while the grey mist of night slowly float up from the valley that have lie hide below they , and Grasmere Lake sparkle and flash in the light of the newly - rise sun . 

 the church clock be strike eight as Hammond come at a brisk pace down to the valley . there be still an hour before breakfast , so he take a circuitous path to Fellside , and descend upon the house from the Fell , as he have do that summer morning when he see James Steadman saunter about in his garden . 

 within about a quarter of a mile of Lady Maulevrier 's shrubbery Mr. Hammond encounter a pedestrian , who , like himself , be evidently take a constitutional ramble in the morning air , but on a much less extended scale , for this person do not look capable of go far afield . 

 he be an old man , something under middle height , but look as if he have once be tall ; for his shoulder be much bent , and his head be sink on his chest . his whole form look waste and shrink , and John Hammond think he have never see so old a man or at any rate any man who be so deeply mark with all the sign of extreme age ; and yet in the backwood of America he have meet ancient settler who remember Franklin , and who have be boy when the battle of Bunker 's Hill be fresh in the memory of their father and mother . 

 the little old man be clothe in a thick grey overcoat of some shaggy kind of cloth which look like homespun . he wear a feel hat , and carry a thick oak stick , and there be nothing in his appearance to indicate that he belong to any high grade than that of the shepherd and guide with whom Hammond have make himself familiar during his previous visit . and yet there be something distinctive about the man , Hammond thought , something wild and uncanny , which make he unlike any of those hale and hearty - look dalesman on whom old age sate so lightly . no , John Hammond could not fancy this man , with his pallid countenance and pale crafty eye , to be of the same race as those rugged and honest - look descendant of the norseman . 

 perhaps it be the man 's exceeding age , for John Hammond make up his mind that he must be a centenarian , which give he so strange and unholy an air . he have the aspect of a man who have be bury and bring back to life again . 

 so might look one of those Indian Fakirs who have the power to suspend life by some mysterious process , and to lie in the darkness of the grave for a give period , and then at their own will to resume the function of the living . his long white hair fall upon the collar of his grey coat , and would have give he a patriarchal appearance have the face possess the dignity of age : but it be a countenance without dignity , a face deeply score with the line of evil passion and guilty memory the face of the vulture , with a touch of the ferret altogether a most unpleasant face , Mr. Hammond think . 

 and yet there be a kind of fascination about that bent and shrunken figure , those feeble movement , and shuffle gait . John Hammond turn to look after the old man when he have pass he , and stand to watch he as he go slowly up the Fell , plant his crutch stick upon the ground before every footstep , as if it be a third leg , and more serviceable than either of the other two . 

 Mr. Hammond watch he for two or three minute , but , as the old man 's movement have an automatic regularity , the occupation soon pal , and he turn and walk toward Fellside . a few yard near the ground he meet James Steadman , walk briskly , and smoke his morning pipe . 

 ' you be out early this morning , ' say Hammond , by way of civility . 

 ' I be always pretty early , sir . I like a mouthful of morning air . ' 

 ' so do I. by - the - bye , can you tell I anything about a queer - look old man I pass just now a little high up the Fell ? such an old , old man , with long white hair . ' 

 ' yes , sir . I believe I know he . ' 

 ' who be he ? do he live in Grasmere ? ' 

 Steadman look puzzle . 

 ' well , you see , sir , your description might apply to a good many ; but if it be the man I think you mean he live in one of the cottage behind the church . Old Barlow , they call he . ' 

 ' there can not   be two such man he must be at least a century old . if any one tell I he be a hundred and twenty I should not   be incline to doubt the fact . I never see such a shrivel , wrinkle visage , bloodless , too , as if the poor old wretch never feel your fresh mountain air upon his hollow cheek . a dreadful face . it will haunt I for a month . ' 

 ' it must be old Barlow , ' reply Steadman . ' good day , sir . ' 

 he walk on with his swing step , and at such a pace that he be up the side of the Fell and close upon old Barlow 's heel when Hammond turn to look after he five minute later . 

 ' there be a man who show few trace of age , at any rate , ' think Hammond . ' yet her ladyship tell I that he be over seventy . ' 

 CHAPTER XIX . 

 the OLD MAN on the FELL . 


 have make up his mind to stay at Fellside until after Easter , Maulevrier settle down very quietly for he . he ride a good deal , fish a little , look after his dog , play billiard , make a devout appearance in the big square pew at St. Oswald 's on Sunday morning , and behave altogether as a reform character . even his grandmother be fain to admit that Maulevrier be improve , and that Mr. Hammond 's influence upon he must be exercise for good and not for evil . 

 ' I plunge awfully last year , and the year before that , ' say Maulevrier , sit at tea in her ladyship 's morning room one afternoon about a week after his return , when she have express her gracious desire that the two young man should take tea with she . 

 Mary be in charge of the tea - pot and brass kettle , and look as radiant and as fresh as a summer morning . a regular Gainsborough girl , Hammond call she , when he praise she to her brother ; a true english beauty , unsophisticated , a little rustic , but full of youthful sweetness . 

 ' you see , I do not   know what a race stable meant , ' continue Maulevrier , mildly apologetic ' in fact , I think it be an easy way for a nobleman to make as good a living as your City swell , with their soft good or their Brummagem ware , a respectable trade for a gentleman to engage in . and it be only when I be half ruin that I begin to understand the business ; and as soon as I do understand it I make up my mind to get out of it ; and I be happy to say that I sell the very last of my stud in February , and Tony Lumpkin be his own man again . so you may welcome the prodigal grandson , and order the fat calf to be slain , grandmother ! ' 

 Lady Maulevrier stretch out her left hand to he , and the young man bend over it and kiss it affectionately . he feel really touch by her misfortune , and be fonder of she than he have ever be before . she have be somewhat hard with he in his boyhood , but she have always care for his dignity and protect his interest : and , after all , she be a noble old woman , a grandmother of whom a man might be justly proud . he think of the paint harridan , the bare - shoulder skeleton , whom some of his young friend be oblige to own in the same capacity , and he be thankful that he could reverence his father 's mother . 

 ' that be the good news I have hear for a long time , Maulevrier , ' say her ladyship graciously ; ' well medicine for my nerve than any of Mr. Horton 's preparation . if Mr. Hammond 's advice have influence you to get rid of your stable I be deeply grateful to Mr. Hammond . ' 

 Hammond smile as he sip his tea , sit close to Mary 's tray , ready to fly to her assistance on the instant should the brazen kettle become troublesome . it have a threatening way of hiss and bubble over its spirit lamp . 

 ' oh , you have no idea what a fellow Hammond be to lecture , ' answer Maulevrier . ' he be a tremendous Radical , and he think that every young man in my position ought to be a reformer , and devote the great part of his time and trouble to turn out the dirty corner of the world , upset those poor dear family who like to pig together in one room , order all the child off to school , marry the father and mother , thrust himself between free labour and free beer , and interfere with the liberty of the subject in every direction . ' 

 ' all that may sound like Radicalism , but I think it be the true Conservatism , and that every young man ought to do as much , if he want this timeworn old country to maintain its power and prosperity , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , with an approve glance at John Hammond 's thoughtful face . 

 ' right you be , grandmother , ' return Maulevrier , ' and I believe Hammond call himself a Conservative , and mean to vote with the Conservatives . ' 

 mean to vote ! an idle phrase , surely , think her ladyship , where the young man 's chance of get into Parliament be so remote . 

 that afternoon tea in Lady Maulevrier 's room be almost as cheerful as the tea - drinking in the drawing - room , unrestraine by her ladyship 's presence . she be pleased with her grandson 's conduct , and be therefore inclined to be friendly to his friend . she could see an improvement in Mary , too . the girl be more feminine , more subdued , graver , sweet ; more like that ideal woman of Wordsworth 's , whose image embody all that be pure and fairest in womanhood . 

 Mary have not forget that unlucky story about the fox - hunt , and ever since Hammond 's return she have be as it be on her good behaviour , refrain from her race with the terrier , and hold herself aloof from Maulevrier 's masculine pursuit . she shelter herself a good deal under the Fršulein 's substantial wing , and take care never to intrude herself upon the amusement of her brother and his friend . she be not one of those young woman who think a brother 's presence an excuse for a perpetual tete - a - tete with a young man . yet when Maulevrier come in quest of she , and entreat she to join they in a ramble , she be not too prudish to refuse the pleasure she so thoroughly enjoy . but afternoon tea be her privileged hour the time at which she wear her prettiest frock , and forget to regret her inferiority to Lesbia in all the grace of womanhood . 

 one afternoon , when they have all three walk to Easedale Tarn , and be come back by the side of the force , pick their way among the grey stone and the narrow thread of silvery water , it suddenly occur to Hammond to ask Mary about that queer old man he have see on the Fell nearly a fortnight before . he have often think of make the inquiry when he be away from Mary , but have always forget the thing when he be with she . indeed , Mary have a wonderful knack of make he forget everything but herself . 

 ' you seem to know every creature in Grasmere , down to the two - year - old baby , ' say Hammond , Mary have just stop to converse with an infantine group , straggle and struggle over the boulder . ' pray , do you happen to know a man call Barlow , a very old man ? ' 

 ' Old Sam Barlow , ' exclaim Mary ; ' why , of course I know he . ' 

 she say it as if he be a near relative , and the question palpably absurd . 

 ' he be an old man , a hundred , at least , I should think , ' say Hammond . 

 ' poor old Sam , not much on the wrong side of eighty . I go to see he every week , and take he his week 's tobacco , poor old dear . it be his only comfort . ' 

 ' be it ? ' ask Hammond . ' I should have doubt his have so humanise a taste as tobacco . he look too evil a creature ever to have yield to the soften influence of a pipe . ' 

 ' an evil creature ! what , old Sam ? why he be the most genial old thing , and as cheery love to hear the newspaper read to he the murder and railway accident . he do not   care for politic . everybody like old Sam Barlow . ' 

 ' I fancy the grasmere idea of reverend and amiable age must be strictly local . I can only say that I never see a more unholy countenance . ' 

 ' you must have be dream when you see he , ' say Mary . ' where do you meet he ? ' 

 ' on the Fell , about a quarter of a mile from the shrubbery gate . ' 

 ' do you ? I should not   have think he could have get so far . I have   a good mind to take you to see he , this very afternoon , before we go home . ' 

 ' do , ' exclaim Hammond , ' I should like it immensely . I think he a hateful - look old person ; but there be something so thoroughly uncanny about he that he exercise an absolute fascination upon I : he magnetise I , I think , as the green - eyed cat magnetise the bird . I have be positively long to see he again . he be a kind of human monster , and I hope some one will have a big bottle make ready for he and preserve he in spirit when he die . ' 

 ' what a horrid idea ! no , sir , dear old Barlow shall lie beside the Rotha , under the tree Wordsworth plant . he be such a man as Wordsworth would have love . ' 

 Mr. Hammond shrug his shoulder , and say no more . Mary 's little vehement way , her enthusiasm , her love of that valley , which might be call her native place , albeit her eye have open upon heaven 's light far away , her humility , be all very delightful in their way . she be not a perfect beauty , like Lesbia ; but she be a fresh , pure - minded english girl , frank as the day , and if he have have a brother he would have recommend that brother to choose just such a girl for his wife . 

 Mr. Samuel Barlow occupy a little old cottage , which seem to consist chiefly of a gable end and a chimney stack , in that cluster of dwelling behind St. Oswald 's church , which be once know as the Kirk Town . visitor go downstairs to get to Mr. Barlow 's ground - floor , for the influence of time and advance civilisation have raise the pathway in front of Mr. Barlow 's cottage until his parlour have become of a cellar - like aspect . yet it be a very nice little parlour when one get down to it , and it enjoy winter and summer a perpetual twilight , since the light that creep through the leaded casement be temper by a screen of flowerpot , which be old Barlow 's particular care . there be no fine geranium in all Grasmere than Barlow 's , no big carnation or picotee , aster or arum . 

 it be about five o'clock in the March afternoon , when Mary usher John Hammond into Mr. Barlow 's dwelling , and , in the dim glow of a cheery little fire and the faint light that filter through the screen of geranium leaf , the visitor look for a moment or so doubtfully at the owner of the cottage . but only for a moment . those bright blue eye and apple cheek , that benevolent expression , bear no likeness to the strange old man he have see on the Fell . Mr. Barlow be toothless and nut - cracker like of outline ; he be thin and shrunken , and bent with the burden of long year , but his healthy visage have none of those deep line , those cross marking and hollow which make the pallid countenance of that other old man as ghastly as would be the abstract idea of life 's last stage embody by the bitter pencil of a Hogarth . 

 ' I have bring a gentleman from London to see you , Sam , ' say Mary . ' he fancy he meet you on the Fell the other morning . ' 

 barlow rise and quaver a cheery welcome , but protest against the idea of his have get so far as the Fell . 

 ' with my blessed rheumatic , you know it be not   in I , Lady Mary . I shall never get no furth than the churchyard ; but I like to sit on the wall hard by Wordsworth 's tomb in a warm afternoon , and to see the folk pass over the bridge ; and I can potter about look after my flower , I can . but it would be a dull life , now the poor old missus be go and the bairn all out at service , if it be not   for some one drop in to have a chat , or read I a bit of the news sometimes . and there be not   anybody in Grasmere , gentle or simple , that be kind to I than you , Lady Mary . Lord bless you , I do look forward to my newspaper . any more of they dreadful smash ? ' 

 ' no , Sam , thank Heaven , there have be no railway accident . ' 

 ' ah , we shall have them in August and September , ' say the old man , cheerily . ' they be bind to come then . there be a time for all thing , as Solomon say . when the season come t'smashe all coom . and no more of these mysterious murder , I suppose , which baffle t'police and keep I awake o ' night thinking of them . ' 

 ' surely you do not take delight in murder , Mr. Barlow ? ' say Hammond . 

 ' no , sir , I do not wish my fellow - creature to mak ' awa ' wi ' each other ; but if there be a murder go in the paper I like to get the benefit of it . I like to sit in front of my fire of an evening and wonder about it while I smoke my pipe , and fancy I can see the murderer hide in a garret in an out - of - the - way alley , or as a stowaway on board a gert ship , or as a miner deep down in a coalpit , and never think that even there t'police can track he . do you ever hear tell o ' Mr. de Quincey , sir ? ' 

 ' I believe I have read every line he ever write . ' 

 ' ah , you should have hear he talk about murder . it would have make you dream queer dream , just as he do . he live for year in the white cottage that Wordsworth once live in , just behind the street yonder a nice , neat , lile gentleman , in a houseful of book . I have   have many a talk with he when I be a young man . ' 

 ' and how old may you be now , Mr. Barlow ? ' 

 ' get on for eighty four , sir . ' 

 ' but you be not the old man in Grasmere , I should say , by twenty year ? ' 

 ' I do not   think there be many much old than I , sir . ' 

 ' the man I see on the Fell look at least a hundred . I wish you could tell I who he be ; I feel a morbid curiosity about he . ' 

 he go on to describe the old man in the grey coat , as minutely as he could , dwell on every characteristic of that singular - look old person ; but Samuel Barlow could not identify the description with any one in Grasmere . yet a man of that age , see walk on the hill - side at eight in the morning , could hardly have come from far afield . 

 CHAPTER XX . 

 LADY MAULEVRIER 's LETTER - BAG .
