 although Maulevrier have assure his grandmother that John Hammond would take flight at the first warning of Lesbia 's return , Lady Maulevrier 's dread of any meeting between her granddaughter and that ineligible lover determine she in make such arrangement as should banish Lesbia from Fellside , so long as there seem the slight danger of such a meeting . she know that Lesbia have love her fortuneless suitor ; and she do not know that the wound be cure , even by a season in the little - great world of Cannes . now that she , the ruler of that household , be a helpless captive in her own apartment , she feel that Lesbia at Fellside would be her own mistress , and hem round with the danger that beset richly - dower beauty and inexperienced youth . 

 John Hammond might be play a very deep game , perhaps assist by Maulevrier . he might ostensibly leave Fellside before Lesbia 's return , yet lurk in the neighbourhood , and contrive to meet she every day . if Maulevrier encourage this folly , they might be marry and over the border , before her ladyship fetter , impotent as she be could interfere . 

 Lady Maulevrier feel that Georgie Kirkbank be her strong rock . so long as Lesbia be under that astute veteran 's wing there could be no danger . in that embody essence of worldliness and diplomacy , there be an ever - present defence from all temptation that spring from romance and youthful impulse . it be a bitter thing , perhaps , to steep a young and pure soul in such an atmosphere , to harden a fresh young nature in the fiery crucible of fashionable life ; but Lady Maulevrier believe that the end would sanctify the mean . Lesbia , once married to a worthy man , such a man as Lord Hartfield , for instance , would soon rise to a high level than that Belgravian swamp over which the malarian vapour of falsehood , and slander , and self - seeking , and prurient imagining hang dense and thick . she would rise to the loftier table - land of that really great world which govern and admonish the ruck of mankind by example of noble deed and noble thought ; the world of statesman , and soldier , and thinker , and reformer ; the salt wherewithal society be salt . 

 but while Lesbia be tread the tortuous maze of fashion , it be well for she to be guide and guard by such an old campaigner as Lady Kirkbank , a woman who , in the language of her friend , ' know the rope . ' 

 Lesbia 's last letter have be to the effect that she be to go back to London with the Kirkbanks directly after Easter , and that directly they arrive she would set off with she maid for Fellside , to spend a week or a fortnight with her dear grandmother , before go back to Arlington Street for the May campaign . 

 ' and then , dearest , I hope you will make up your mind to spend the season in London , ' write Lesbia . ' I shall expect to hear that you have secure Lord Porlock 's house . how dreadfully slow your poor dear hand be to recover ! I be afraid Horton be not treat the case cleverly . why do you not send for Mr. Erichsen ? it be a shock to my nerve every time I receive a letter in Mary 's masculine hand , instead of in your lovely italian penmanship . strange be not   it ? how much well the woman of your time write than the girl of the present day ! Lady Kirkbank receive letter from stylish girl in a hand that would disgrace a housemaid . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier allow a post to go by before she answer this letter , while she deliberate upon the good and wise manner of arrange her granddaughter 's future . it be an agony to she not to be able to write with her own hand , to be oblige to so shape every sentence that Mary might learn nothing which she ought not to know . it be impossible with such an amanuensis to write confidentially to Lady Kirkbank . the letter to Lesbia be of less consequence ; for Lesbia , albeit so intensely beloved , be not in her grandmother 's confidence , least of all about those scheme and dream which concern her own fate . 

 however , the letter have to be write , so Mary be tell to open her desk and begin . 

 the letter to Lesbia run thus : 

 ' my dear child , 
 ' this be a world in which our bright day - dream generally end in 
 mere dreaming . for year past I have cherish the hope of 
 present you to your sovereign , to whom I be present six and 
 forty year ago , when she be so fair and girlish a creature that 
 she seem to I more like a queen in a fairy tale than the actual 
 ruler of a great country . I have beguile my monotonous day with 
 thought of the time when I should return to the great world , full 
 of pride and delight in show old friend what a sweet flower I 
 have rear in my mountain home ; but , alas , Lesbia , it may not be . 
 ' Fate have will otherwise . the maimed hand do not recover , 
 although Horton be very clever , and thoroughly understand my case . 
 I be not ill , I be not in danger ; so you need feel no anxiety about 
 I ; but I be a cripple ; and I be likely to remain a cripple for 
 month ; so the idea of a London season this year be hopeless . 
 ' now , as you have in a manner make your dťbut at Cannes , it would 
 never do to bury you here for another year . you complain of the 
 dullness last summer ; but you would find Fellside much duller now 
 that you have taste the elixir of life . no , my dear love , it will 
 be well for you to be present , as Lady Kirkbank propose , at the 
 first drawing - room after Easter ; and Lady Kirkbank will have to 
 present you . she will be pleased to do this , I know , for her letter 
 be full of enthusiasm about you . and , after all , I do not think you 
 will lose by the exchange . clever as I think myself , I fear I should 
 find myself sorely at fault in the society of to - day . all thing be 
 change : opinion , manner , creed , moral even . act that be 
 crime in my day be now venial error opinion that be 
 scandalous be now the mark of " advanced thought . " I should be too 
 formal for this easy - go age , should be ridicule as old - fashioned 
 and narrow - minded , should put you to the blush a dozen time a day 
 by my prejudice and opinion . 
 ' it be very good of you to think of travel so long a distance to 
 see I ; and I should love to look at your sweet face , and hear you 
 describe your new experience ; but I could not allow you to travel 
 with only the protection of a maid ; and there be many reason why I 
 think it well to defer the meeting till the end of the season , 
 when Lady Kirkbank will bring my treasure back to I , eager to tell 
 I the history of all the heart she have break . ' 
 the dowager 's letter to Lady Kirkbank be brief and business - like . she could only hope that her old friend Georgie , whose acuteness she know of old , would divine her feeling and her wish , without be explicitly tell what they be . 

 ' my dear Georgie , 
 ' I be too ill to leave this house ; indeed I doubt if I shall ever 
 leave it till I be take away in my coffin ; but please say nothing 
 to alarm Lesbia . indeed , there be no ground for fear , as I be not 
 dangerously ill , and may drag out an imprisonment of long year 
 before the coffin come to fetch I . there be reason , which you 
 will understand , why Lesbia should not come here till after the 
 season ; so please keep she in Arlington Street , and occupy her mind 
 as much as you can with the preparation for her first campaign . I 
 give you carte blanche . if Carson be still in business I should 
 like she to make my girl 's gown ; but you must please yourself in 
 this matter , as it be quite possible that Carson be a little behind 
 the time . 
 ' I must ask you to present my darling , and to deal with she exactly 
 as if she be a daughter of your own . I think you know all my view 
 and hope about she ; and I feel that I can trust to your friendship 
 in this my day of need . the dream of my life have be to launch she 
 myself , and direct she every step in the maze of town life ; but 
 that dream be over . I have keep age and infirmity at a distance , 
 have even forget that the year be go by ; and now I find 
 myself an old woman all at once , and my golden dream have vanish . ' 
 Lady Kirkbank 's reply come by return of post , and happily this gush epistle have not to be submit to Mary 's eye . 

 ' my dear Di , 
 ' my heart positively bleed for you . what be the matter with your 
 hand , that you talk of be a life - long prisoner to your room ? pray 
 send for Paget or Erichsen , and have yourself put right at once . no 
 doubt that local simpleton be make a mess of your case . perhaps 
 while he be dab with lint and lotion the real remedy be the 
 knife . I be sure amputation would be less melancholy than the 
 despondent state of feeling which you be now suffer . if any limb 
 of mine go wrong , I should say to the surgeon , " cut it off , and 
 patch up the stump in your good style ; I give you a fortnight , and 
 at the end of that time I expect to be go to party again . " life 
 be not long enough for dawdle surgery . 
 ' as regard Lesbia , I can only say that I adore she , and I be 
 enchant at the idea that I be to run she myself . I intend she to 
 be the beauty of the season not one of the lovely 
 debutante , or any rot of that kind but just the girl whom 
 everybody will be crazy about . there shall be a mob wherever she 
 appear , Di , I promise you that . there be no one in London who can 
 work a thing of that kind well than your humble servant . and when 
 once the girl be the talk of the town , all the rest be easy . she can 
 choose for herself among the very good man in society . offer will 
 pour in as thickly as circular from undertaker and mourning 
 warehouse after a death . 
 ' Lesbia be so cool - headed and sensible that I have not the least 
 doubt of her success . with an impulsive or romantic girl there be 
 always the fear of a fiasco . but this sweet child of yours have 
 be well bring up , and know her own value . she behave like a 
 queen here , where I need not tell you society be just a little 
 mixed ; though , of course , we only cultivate our own set . your heart 
 would swell with pride if you could see the way she put down man 
 who be not quite good style ; and the ease with which she crush 
 those odious american girl , with their fine complexion and loud 
 manner . 
 ' be assure that I shall guard she as the apple of my eye , and that 
 the detrimental who circumvent I will be a very Satan of schemer . 
 ' I can but smile at your mention of Carson , whose gown use to fit 
 we so well in our girlish day , and whose bill seem moderate 
 compare with the exorbitant account I get now . 
 ' Carson have long be forget , my dear soul , go with the snow 
 of last year . a long procession of fashionable french dressmaker 
 have pass across the stage since her time , like the phantom king 
 in Macbeth ; and now the last rage be to have our gown make by an 
 Englishman who work for the Princess , and who give himself most 
 insufferable air , or an Irishwoman who be employ by all the good 
 actress . it be to the latter , Kate Kearney , I shall entrust our 
 sweet Lesbia 's toilette . ' 
 the same post bring a love letter from Lesbia , full of regret at not be allow to go down to Fellside , and yet full of delight at the prospect of her first season . 

 ' Lady Kirkbank and I have be discuss my court dress , ' she write , 
 ' and we have decide upon a white cut - velvet train , with a border of 
 ostrich feather , over a satin petticoat embroider with seed 
 pearl . it will be expensive , but we know you will not mind that . 
 Lady Kirkbank take the idea from the costume Buckingham wear at the 
 Louvre the first time he meet Anne of Austria . be not   that clever of 
 she ? she be not a deep thinker like you ; be horribly ignorant of 
 science , metaphysic , poetry even . she ask I one day who Plato 
 be , and whether he take his name from the battle of Platoea ; and 
 she say she never could understand why people make a fuss about 
 Shakespeare ; but she have read all the secret history and memoir 
 that ever be write , and know all the in and out of court life 
 and high life for the last three hundred year ; and there be not a 
 person in the peerage whose family history she have not at her 
 finger ' end , except my grandfather . when I ask she to tell I 
 all about Lord Maulevrier and his achievement as Governor of 
 Madras , she have not a word to say . so , perhaps , she draw upon her 
 invention a little in talk about other people , and feel herself 
 restrain when she come to speak of my grandfather . ' 
 this passage in Lesbia 's letter affect Lady Maulevrier as if a scorpion have wriggle from underneath the sheet of paper . she fold the letter , and lay it in the satin - line box on her table , with a deep sigh . 

 ' yes , she be in the world now , and she will ask question . I have never warn she against pronounce her grandfather 's name . there be some who will not be so kind as Georgie Kirkbank ; some , perhaps , who will delight in humiliate she , and who will tell she the bad that can be tell . my only hope be that she will make a great marriage , and speedily . once the wife of a man with a high place in the world , worldling will be too wise to wound she by tell she that her grandfather be an unconvicted felon . ' 

 the die be cast . Lady Maulevrier might dread the hazard of evil tongue , of slanderous memory ; but she could not recall her consent to Lesbia 's dťbut . the girl be already launch ; she have be see and admire . the next stage in her career must be to be woo and win by a worthy wooer . 

 CHAPTER XXI . 

 on the DARK BROW of HELVELLYN . 


 while these plan be be settle , and while Lesbia 's future be the all - absorbing subject of Lady Maulevrier 's thought , Mary contrive to be happy than she have ever be in her life before . it be happiness that grow and strengthen with every day ; and yet there be no obvious reason for this deep joy . her life run in the same familiar groove . she walk and ride on the old pathway ; she row on the lake she have know from babyhood ; she visit her cottager , and teach in the village school , just the same as of old . the change be only that she be no long alone ; and of late the solitude of her life , the ever - present consciousness that nobody share her pleasure or sympathise with she upon any point , have weigh upon she like an actual burden . now she have Maulevrier , who be always kind , who understand and share almost all her taste , and Maulevrier 's friend , who , although not give to say smooth thing , seem warmly interested in her pursuit and opinion . he encourage she to talk , although he generally take the opposite side in every argument ; and she no long feel oppress or irritate by the idea that he despise she . 

 indeed , although he never flatter or even praise she , Mr. Hammond let she see that he like her society . she have go out of her way to avoid he , very fearful lest he should think her bold or masculine ; but he have take pain to frustrate all her effort in that direction ; he have refuse to go upon excursion which she could not share . ' Lady Mary must come with we , ' he say , when they be plan a morning 's ramble . thus it happen that Mary be his guide and companion in all his walk , and roam with he bamboo in hand , over every one of those mountainous path she know and love so well . distance be as nothing to they sometimes a boat help they , and they go over wintry windermere to climb the picturesque height above Bowness . sometimes they take pony , and a groom , and leave their steed to perform the wilder part of the way on foot . in this wise John Hammond see all that be to be see within a day 's journey of Grasmere , except the top of Helvellyn . Maulevrier have shirk the expedition , have always put off Mary and Mr. Hammond when they propose it . the season be not advanced enough the rugged pathway by the Tongue Ghyll would be as slippery as glass no pony could get up there in such weather . 

 ' we have not have any frost to speak of for the last fortnight , ' plead Mary , who be particularly anxious to do the honour of Helvellyn , as the real lion of the neighbourhood . 

 ' what a simpleton you be , molly ! ' cry Maulevrier . ' do you suppose because there be no frost in your grandmother 's garden and if you be to ask Staples about his peach he would tell you a very different story that there be a tropical atmosphere on Dolly Waggon Pike ? why , I would wager the ice on Grisdale Tarn be thick enough for skate . Helvellyn will not   run away , child . you and Hammond can dance the Highland Schottische on Striding Edge in June , if you like . ' 

 ' Mr. Hammond will not   be here in June , ' say Mary . 

 ' who know ? the train service be pretty fair between London and Windermere . Hammond and I would think nothing of put ourselves in the mail on a Friday night , and come down to spend Saturday and Sunday with you if you be good . ' 

 there come a sunny morning soon after Easter which seem mild enough for June ; and when Hammond suggest that this be the very day for Helvellyn , Maulevrier have not a word to say against the truth of that proposition . the weather have be exceptionally warm for the last week , and they have play tennis and sit in the garden just as if it have be actually summer . patch of snow might still linger on the crest of the hill but the approach to those bleak height could hardly be glacial . 

 Mary clasp her hand delightedly . 

 ' dear old Maulevrier ! ' she exclaim , ' you be always good to I . and now I shall be able to show you the Red Tarn , the high pool of water in England , ' she say , turn to Hammond . ' and you will see windermere wind like a silvery serpent between the hill , and grasmere shin like a jewel in the depth of the valley , and the sea glitter like a line of white light between the edge of earth and heaven , and the dark Scotch hill like couchant lion far away to the north . ' 

 ' that be to say these thing be all suppose to be on view from the top of the mountain ; but as a peculiar and altogether exceptional state of the atmosphere be essential to their be see , I need not tell you that they be rarely visible , ' say Maulevrier . ' you be talk to old mountaineer , Molly . Hammond have do Cotapaxi and have his little clamber on the equatorial Andes , and I well , child , I have do my Righi , and I have always find the boasted panorama envelop in dense fog . ' 

 ' it will not   be foggy to - day , ' say Mary . ' shall we do the whole thing on foot , or shall I order the pony ? ' 

 Mr. Hammond inquire the distance up and down , and be tell that it involve only a matter of eight mile , decide upon walk . 

 ' I will walk , and lead your pony , ' he say to Mary , but Mary declare herself quite capable of go on foot , so the pony be dispense with as a possible encumbrance . 

 this be plan and discuss in the garden before breakfast . Fršulein be tell that Mary be go for a long walk with her brother and Mr. Hammond ; a walk which might last over the usual luncheon hour ; so Fršulein be not to wait luncheon . Mary go to her grandmother 's room to pay her duty visit . there be no letter for she to write that morning , so she be perfectly free . 

 the three pedestrian start an hour after breakfast , in light marching order . the two young man wear their argyleshire shoot clothe homespun knickerbocker and jacket , thick - rib hose knit by Highland lass in Inverness . they carry a couple of hunting flask fill with claret , and a couple of sandwich box , and that be all . Mary wear her substantial tailor - gown of olive tweed , and a little toque to match , with a silver mount grouse - claw for her only ornament . 

 it be a delicious morning , the air fresh and sweet , the sun comfortably warm , a little too warm , perhaps , presently , when they have tread the narrow path by the Tongue Ghyll , and be begin to wind slowly upwards over rough boulder and last year 's bracken , tough and brown and tangle , towards that rugged wall of earth and stone tufte with rank grass , which call itself dolly Waggon Pike . here they all come to a stand - still , and wipe the dew of honest labour from their forehead ; and here Maulevrier fling himself down upon a big boulder , with the sol of his stout shoot boot in run water , and take out his cigar case . 

 ' how do you like it ? ' he ask his friend , when he have light his cigarette . ' I hope you be enjoy yourself . ' 

 ' I never be happy in my life , ' answer Hammond . 

 he be stand on high ground , with Mary at his elbow , point out and expatiate upon the detail of the prospect . there be the lake grasmere , a disk of shin blue ; Rydal , a patch of silver ; and windermere wind amidst a labyrinth of woode hill . 

 ' be not   you tired ? ' ask Maulevrier . 

 ' not a whit . ' 

 ' oh , I forget you have do Cotapaxi , or as much of Cotapaxi as live mortal ever have do . that make a difference . I be go home . ' 

 ' oh , Maulevrier ! ' exclaim Mary , piteously . 

 ' I be go home . you two can go to the top . you be both harden mountaineer , and I be not in it with either of you . when I rashly consent to a pedestrian ascent of Helvellyn I have forget what the gentleman be like ; and as to Dolly Waggon I have actually forget her existence . but now I see the lady as steep as the side of a house , and as stony no , naught but herself can be her parallel in stoniness . no , Molly , I will go no far . ' 

 ' but we shall go down on the other side , ' urge Mary . ' it be a little steeper on the Cumberland side , but not nearly so far . ' 

 ' a little steeper ! I can anything be steep than Dolly Waggon ? yes , you be right . it be steep on the Cumberland side . I remember come down a sheer descent , like an exaggerated sugar - loaf ; but I be on a pony , and it be the brute 's look - out . I will not go down the Cumberland side on my own leg . no , Molly , not even for you . but if you and Hammond want to go to the top , there be nothing to prevent you . he be a skilled mountaineer . I will trust you with he . ' 

 Mary blush , and make no reply . of all thing in the world she least want to abandon the expedition . yet to climb Helvellyn alone with her brother 's friend would no doubt be a terrible violation of those law of maidenly propriety which Fršulein be always expound . if Mary be to do this thing , which she long to do , she must hazard a lecture from her governess , and probably a biting reproof from her grandmother . 

 ' will you trust yourself with I , Lady Mary ? ' ask Hammond , look at she with a gaze so earnest so much more earnest than the occasion require that her blush deepen and her eyelid fall . ' I have do a good deal of climb in my day , and I be not afraid of anything Helvellyn can do to I . I promise to take great care of you if you will come . ' 

 how could she refuse ? how could she for one moment pretend that she do not trust he , that her heart do not yearn to go with he . she would have climb the shingly steep of Cotapaxi with he or cross the great Sahara with he and fear nothing . her trust in he be infinite as infinite as her reverence and love . 

 ' I be afraid Fršulein would make a fuss , ' she falter , after a pause . 

 ' Hang Fršulein , ' cry Maulevrier , puff at his cigarette , and kick about the stone in the clear running water . ' I will square it with Fršulein . I will give she a pint of fiz with her lunch , and make she see everything in a rosy hue . the good soul be fond of her Heidseck . you will be back by afternoon tea . why should there be any fuss about the matter ? Hammond want to see the Red Tarn , and you be die to show he the way . go , and joy go with you both . climb a stony hill be a form of pleasure to which I have not yet risen . I shall stroll home at my leisure , and spend the afternoon on the billiard - room sofa read Mudie 's last contribution to the comfort of home . ' 

 ' what a sybarite , ' say Hammond . ' come , Lady Mary , we must not   loiter , if we be to be back at Fellside by five o'clock . ' 

 Mary look at her brother doubtfully , and he give she a little nod which seem to say , ' go , by all mean ; ' so she dig the end of her staff into Dolly 's rugged breast , and mount cheerily , step lightly from boulder to boulder . 

 the sun be not so warm as it have be ten minute ago , when Maulevrier fling himself down to rest . the sky have cloud over a little , and a cool wind be blow across the breast of the hill . Fairfield yonder , that long smooth slope of verdure which a little while ago look emerald green in the sunlight , now wear a soft and shadowy hue . all the world be greyer and dimmer in a moment , as it be , and Coniston Lake in its distant valley disappear beneath a veil of mist , while the shimmering sea - line upon the verge of the horizon melt and vanish among the cloud that overhang it . the weather change very quickly in this part of the world . sharp drop of rain come spit at Hammond and Mary as they climb the crest of the pike , and stop , somewhat breathless , to look back at Maulevrier . he be trudge blithely down the wind way , and seem to have do wonder while they have be do very little . 

 ' how fast he be go ! ' say Mary . 

 ' Easy be the descent of Avernus . he be go down - hill , and we be go upwards . that make all the difference in life , you see , ' answer Hammond . 

 Mary look at he with divine compassion . she think that for he the hill of life would be hard than Helvellyn . he be brave , honest , clever ; but her grandmother have impress upon she that modern civilisation hardly have room for a young man who want to get on in the world , without either fortune or powerful connexion . he have well go to Australia and keep sheep , than attempt the impossible at home . 

 the rain be a pass shower , hardly worth speak of , but the glory of the day be over . the sky be grey , and there be dark cloud creep up from the sea - line . Silvery Windermere have take a leaden hue ; and now they turn their last fond look upon the Westmoreland valley , and set their face steadily towards Cumberland , and the fine grassy plateau on the top of the hill . 

 all this be not do in a flash . it take they some time to scale Dolly 's stubborn breast , and it take they another hour to reach Seat Sandal ; and by the time they come to the iron gate in the fence , which at this point divide the two county , the atmosphere have thicken ominously , and dark wreath of fog be float about and around they , whirl here and there by a boisterous wind which shriek and roar at they with savage fury , as if it be the voice of some Titan monarch of the mountain protest against this intrusion upon his domain . 

 ' I be afraid you will not   see the scottish hill , ' shout Mary , hold on her little cloth hat . 

 she be oblige to shout at the top of her voice , though she be close to Mr. Hammond 's elbow , for that shrill scream wind would have drown the voice of a stentor . 

 ' never mind the view , ' reply Hammond in the same fortissimo , ' but I really wish I have not   bring you up here . if this fog should get any bad , it may be dangerous . ' 

 ' the fog be sure to get bad , ' say Mary , in a brief lull of the hurly - burly , ' but there be no danger . I know every inch of the hill , and I be not a bit afraid . I can guide you , if you will trust I . ' 

 ' my brave of girl , ' he exclaim , look down at she . ' trust you ! yes , I would trust my life to you my soul my honour secure in your purity and good faith . ' 

 never have eye of live man or woman look down upon she with such tenderness , such fervent love . she look up at he ; look with eye which , at first bewilder , then grow bold , and lose themselves , as it be , in the dark grey depth of the eye they meet . the savage wind , hustling and howling , blow she nearer to he , as a reed be blow against a rock . dark grey mist be rise round they like a sea ; but have that ever - thickening , ever - darken vapour be the sea itself , and death inevitable , Mary Haselden would have hardly care . for in this moment the one precious gift for which her soul have long be yearn have be freely give to she . she know all at once , that she be fondly love by that one man whom she have choose for her idol and her hero . 

 what matter that he be fortuneless , a nobody , with but the poor chance of success in the world ? what if he must need , only to win the bare mean of existence , go to Australia and keep sheep , or to the Bed River valley and grow corn ? what if he must labour , as the peasant labour on the side of this rude hill ? gladly would she go with he to a strange country , and keep his log cabin , and work for he , and share his toilsome life , rough or smooth . no loss of social rank could lessen her pride in he , her belief in he . 

 they be stand side by side a little way from the edge of the sheer descent , below which the Bed Tarn show black in a basin scoop out of the naked hill , like water hold in the hollow of a giant 's hand . 

 ' look , ' cry Mary , point downward , ' you must see the Red Tarn , the high water in England ? ' 

 but just at this moment there come a blast which shake even Hammond 's strong frame , and with a cry of fear he snatch Mary in his arm and carry she away from the edge of the hill . he fold she in his arm and hold she there , thirty yard away from the precipice , safely shelter against his breast , while the wind rave round they , blow her hair from the broad , white brow , and show he that noble forehead in all its power and beauty ; while the darkness deepen round they so that they could see hardly anything except each other 's eye . 

 ' my love , my own dear love , ' he murmur fondly ; ' I will trust you with my life . will you accept the trust ? I be hardly worthy ; for less than a year ago I offer myself to your sister , and I think she be the only woman in this wide world who could make I happy . and when she refuse I I be in despair , Mary ; and I leave Fellside in the full belief that I have do with life and happiness . and then I come back , only to oblige Maulevrier , and determine to be utterly miserable at Fellside . I be miserable for the first two hour . memory of dead and go joy and disappointed hope be very bitter . and I try honestly to keep up my feeling of wretchedness for the first few day . but it be no use , molly . there be a genial spirit in the place , a laugh fairy who would not let I be sad ; and I find myself become most unromantically happy , eat my breakfast with a hearty appetite , think my cup of afternoon tea nectar for love of the dear hand that give it . and so , and so , till the new love , the purer and well love , grow and grow into a mighty tree , which be as an oak to an orchid , compare with that passion flower of early growth . Mary , will you trust your life to I , as I trust mine to you . I say to you almost in the word I speak last year to Lesbia , ' and here his tone grow grave almost to solemnity , ' trust I , and I will make your life free from the shadow of care trust I , for I have a brave spirit and a strong arm to fight the battle of life trust I , and I will win for you the position you have a right to occupy trust I , and you shall never repent your trust . ' 

 she look up at he with eye which tell of infinite faith , child - like , unquestione faith . 

 ' I will trust you in all thing , and for ever , ' she say . ' I be not afraid to face evil fortune . I do not care how poor you be how hard our life may be if if you be sure you love I . ' 

 ' sure ! there be not a beat of my heart or a thought of my mind that do not belong to you . I be yours to the very depth of my soul . my innocent love , my clear - eye , clear - soule angel ! I have study you and watch you and think of you , and sound the depth of your lovely nature , and the result be that you be for I earth 's one woman . I will have no other , Mary , no other love , no other wife . ' 

 ' Lady Maulevrier will be dreadfully angry , ' falter Mary . 

 ' be you afraid of her anger ? ' 

 ' no ; I be afraid of nothing , for your sake . ' 

 he lift her hand to his lip , and kiss it reverently , and there be a touch of chivalry in that reverential kiss . his eye cloud with tear as he look down into the trustful face . the fog have darken to a denser blackness , and it be almost as if they be engulf in sudden night . 

 ' if we be never to find our way down the hill ; if this be to be the last hour of our life , Mary , would you be content ? ' 

 ' quite content , ' she answer , simply . ' I think I have live long enough , if you really love I if you be not make fun . ' 

 ' what , molly , do you still doubt ? be it strange that I love you ? ' 

 ' very strange . I be so different from Lesbia . ' 

 ' yes , very different , and the difference be your high charm . and now , love , we have well go down whichever side of the hill be easy , for this fog be rather appalling . I forgive the wind , because it blow you against my heart just now , and that be where I want you to dwell for ever ! ' 

 ' do not   be frightened , ' say Mary . ' I know every step of the way . ' 

 so , lean on her lover , and yet guide he , slowly , step by step , grope their way through the darkness , Lady Mary lead Mr. Hammond down the wind track along which the pony and the guide travel so often in the summer season . and soon they begin to descend out of that canopy of fog which envelop the brow of Helvellyn , and to see the whole world smile beneath they , a world of green pasture and sheepfold , with a white homestead here and there amidst the field , look so human and so comfortable after that gloomy mountain top , round which the temp howl so outrageously . beyond those pasture stretch the dark water of Thirlmere , look like a broad river . 

 the descent be pass steep , but Hammond 's strong arm and steady step make Mary 's progress very easy , while she have in no wise exaggerate her familiarity with the winding and twisting of the track . yet as they have need to travel very slowly so long as the fog still surround they , the journey downward last a considerable time , and it be past five when they arrive at the little roadside inn at the foot of the hill . 

 here Mr. Hammond insist that Mary should rest at least long enough to take a cup of tea . she be very white and tired . she have be profoundly agitated , and look on the point of fainting , although she protest that she be quite ready to walk on . 

 ' you be not go to walk another step , ' say Hammond . ' while you be take your tea I will get you a carriage . ' 

 ' indeed , I have rather hurry on at once , ' urge Mary . ' we be so late already . ' 

 ' you will get home all the soon if you obey I . it be your duty to obey I now , ' say Hammond , in a lowered voice . 

 she smile at he , but it be a weak , wan little smile , for that descent in the wind and the fog have quite exhaust she . Mr. Hammond take she into a snug little parlour where there be a cheerful fire , and see she comfortably seat in an arm chair by the hearth , before he go to look after a carriage . 

 there be no such thing as a conveyance to be have , but the windermere coach would pass in about half an hour , and for this they must wait . it would take they back to Grasmere soon than they could get there on foot , in Mary 's exhausted condition . 

 the tea - tray be bring in presently , and Hammond pour out the tea and wait upon Lady Mary . it be a reversal of the usual formula but it be very pleasant to Mary to sit with her foot on the low brass fender and be wait upon by her lover . that fog on the brow of Helvellyn that pierce wind have chill she to the bone , and there be unspeakable comfort in the glow and warmth of the fire , in the refreshment of a good cup of tea . 

 ' Mary , you be my own property now , remember , ' say Hammond , watch she tenderly as she sip her tea . 

 she glance up at he shyly , now and then , with eye full of innocent wonder . it be so strange to she , as strange as sweet , to know that he love she ; such a marvellous thing that she have pledge herself to be his wife . 

 ' you be my very own mine to guard and cherish , mine to think and work for , ' he go on , ' and you will have to trust I , sweet one , even if the beginning of thing be not altogether free from trouble . ' 

 ' I be not afraid of trouble . ' 

 ' bravely speak ! first and foremost , then , you will have to announce your engagement to Lady Maulevrier . she will take it ill , no doubt ; will do she utmost to persuade you to give I up . have you courage and resolution , do you think , to stand against her argument ? can you hold to your purpose bravely , and cry , no surrender ? '

' 

 ' so much the well , my wayside flower ! when you have say all that be sweet and dutiful to she , and have let she know at the same time that you mean to be my wife , come weal come woe , I will see she , and will have my say . I will not promise she a grand career for my darling : but I will pledge myself that nothing of that kind which the world call evil no penury , or shabbiness of surrounding shall ever touch Mary Haselden after she be Mary Hammond . I can promise at least so much as that . ' 

 ' it be more than enough , ' say Mary . ' I have tell you that I would gladly share poverty with you . ' 

 ' sweet ! it be good of you to say as much , but I would not take you at your word . you do not   know what poverty be . ' 

 ' do you think I be a coward , or self - indulgent ? you be wrong , Jack . may I call you Jack , as Maulevrier do ? ' 

 ' may you ? ' 

 the question evoke such a gush of tenderness that he be fain to kneel beside her chair and kiss the little hand hold the cup , before he consider he have answer properly . 

 ' you be wrong , Jack . I do know what poverty mean . I have study the way of the poor , try to console they , and help they a little in their trouble ; and I know there be no pain that want of money can bring which I would not share willingly with you . do you suppose my happiness be dependent on a fine house and powdered footman ? I should like to go to the Red River with you , and wear cotton gown , and tuck up my sleeve and clean our cottage . ' 

 ' very pretty sport , dear , for a summer day ; but my Mary shall have a sweet life , and shall occasionally walk in silk attire . ' 

 that tea - drinking by the fireside in the inn parlour be the most delicious thing within John Hammond 's experience . Mary be a bewitching compound of earnestness and simplicity , so humble , so confiding , so perplexed and astound at her own bliss . 

 ' Confess , now , in the summer , when you be in love with Lesbia , you think I a horrid kind of girl , ' she say , presently , when they be stand side by side at the window , wait for the coach . 

 ' never , Mary . my crime be to have think very little about you in those day . I be so dazzle by Lesbia 's beauty , so charm by her accomplishment and girlish grace , that I forgot to take notice of anything else in the world . if I think of you at all it be as another Maulevrier a young Maulevrier in petticoat , very gay , and good - humour , and nice . ' 

 ' but when you see I rush about with the terrier I must have seem utterly horrid . ' 

 ' why , dearest ? there be nothing essentially horrible in terrier , or in a bright lively girl run race with they . you make a very pretty picture in the sunlight , with your hat hang on your shoulder , and your curly brown hair and dance hazel eye . if I have not be deep in love with Lesbia 's peerless complexion and grecian feature , I should have look below the surface of that Gainsborough picture , and discover what treasure of goodness , and courage , and truth and purity those frank brown eye and that wide forehead betoken . I be sow my wild oats last summer , Mary , and they bring I a crop of sorrow . but I be wise now wise and happy . 

 ' but if you be to see Lesbia again would not the old love revive ? ' 

 ' the old love be dead , Mary . there be nothing leave of it but a handful of ash , which I scatter thus to the four wind , ' with a wave of his hand towards the open casement . ' the new love absorb and master my being . if Lesbia be to re - appear at Fellside this evening , I could offer she my hand in all brotherly frankness , and ask she to accept I as a brother . here come the coach . we shall be at Fellside just in time for dinner . ' 

 CHAPTER XXII . 

 WISER than LESBIA . 


 Lady Mary and Mr. Hammond be back at Fellside at a quarter before eight , by which time the star be shin on pine wood and Fell . they manage to be in the drawing - room when dinner be announce , after the hasty of toilet ; yet her lover think Mary have never look pretty than she look that night , in her limp white cashmere gown , and with her brown hair brush into a large loose knot on the top of her head . there have be great uneasiness about they at Fellside when evening begin to draw in , and the expect hour of their return have go by . scout have be send in quest of they , but in the wrong direction . 

 ' I do not think you would be such idiot as to come down the north side of the hill in a temp , ' say Maulevrier ; ' we could see the cloud race over the crest of Seat Sandal , and know it be blow pretty hard up there , though it be calm enough down here . ' 

 ' blow pretty hard ; ' echo Hammond , ' I do not   think I be ever out in a bad gale ; and yet I have be across the Bay of Biscay when the wave strike the side of the steam like batter ram , and when the whole surface of the sea be white with seethe foam . ' 

 ' it be a most imprudent thing to go up Helvellyn in such weather , ' say Fršulein M ller , shake her head gloomily as she eat her fish . 

 Mary feel that the Fršulein 's manner bode ill . there be a storm brew . a scolding be inevitable . Mary feel quite capable of do battle with the Fršulein ; but her feeling be altogether different when she think of face that stern old lady upstairs , and of the confession she have to make . it be not that her courage falter . so far as resolution go she be as firm as a rock . but she feel that there be a terrible ordeal to be go through ; and it seem a mockery to be sit there and pretend to eat her dinner and take thing lightly , with that ordeal before she . 

 ' we do not go up the hill in bad weather , Miss M ller , ' say Mr. Hammond . ' the sun be shin and the sky be blue when we start . we could not foresee darkness and storm at the top of the hill . that be the fortune of war . ' 

 ' I be very sorry Lady Mary have not more good sense , ' reply Fršulein with unabated gloom ; but on this Maulevrier take up the cudgel . 

 ' if there be any want of sense in the business , that be my look - out , Fršulein , ' he say , glare angrily at the governess . ' it be I who advise Hammond and Lady Mary to climb the hill . and here they be , safe and sound after their journey . I see no reason why there should be any fuss about it . ' 

 ' People have different way of look at thing , reply Fršulein , plod steadily on with her dinner . Mary rise directly the dessert have be hand round , and march out of the room : like a warrior go to a battle in which the chance of defeat be strong . Fršulein M ller shuffle after she . 

 ' will you be kind enough to go to her ladyship 's room at once , Lady Mary , ' she say . ' she want to speak to you . ' 

 ' and I want to speak to she , ' say Mary . 

 she run quickly upstairs and arrive in the morning room , a little out of breath . the room be light by one low moderator lamp , under a dark red velvet shade , and there be the glow of the wood fire , which give a more cheerful light than the lamp . Lady Maulevrier be lie on her couch in a loose brocade tea - gown , with old Brussels collar and ruffle . she be as well dressed in her day of affliction and helplessness as she have be in her day of strength ; for she know the value of surrounding , and that her stateliness and power be in some manner dependent on detail of this kind . the one hand which she could use glitter with diamond , as she wave it with a little imperious gesture towards the chair on which she desire Lady Mary to seat herself ; and Mary sit down meekly , know that this chair represent the felon 's dock . 

 ' Mary , ' begin her grandmother , with freeze gravity , ' I have be surprise and shock by your conduct to - day . yes , surprise at such conduct even in you . ' 

 ' I do not think I have do anything very wrong , grandmother . ' 

 ' not wrong ! you have do nothing wrong ? you have do something absolutely outrageous . you , my granddaughter , well bear , well breed , rear under my roof , to go up Helvellyn and lose yourself in a fog alone with a young man . you could hardly have do bad if you be a Cockney tourist , ' conclude her ladyship , with ineffable disgust . 

 ' I could not help the fog , ' say Mary , quietly . the battle have to be fight , and she be not go to flinch . ' I have no intention of go up Helvellyn alone with Mr. Hammond . Maulevrier be to have go with we ; but when we get to dolly Waggon he be tired , and would not go any far . he tell I to go on with Mr. Hammond . ' 

 ' he tell you ! Maulevrier ! - a young man who have spend some of the good hour of his youth in the company of jockeys and trainer who have not   the faint idea of the fitness of thing . you allow Maulevrier to be your guide in a matter in which your own instinct should have guide you your womanly instinct ! but you have always be an unwomanly girl . you have put I to shame many a time by your hoydenish trick ; but I bear with you , believe that your madcap folly be at least harmless . to - day you have go a step too far , and have be guilty of absolute impropriety , which I shall be very slow to pardon . ' 

 ' perhaps you will be still more angry when you know all , grandmother , ' say Mary . 

 Lady Maulevrier flash her dark eye at the girl with a look which would have almost kill a nervous subject ; but Mary face she steadfastly , very pale , but as resolute as her ladyship . 

 ' when I know all ! what more be there for I to know ? ' 

 ' only that while we be on the top of Helvellyn , in the fog and the wind , Mr. Hammond ask I to be his wife . '

' 

 ' there be nothing gain by make cruel speech , grandmother , ' say Mary , firmly . ' I have promise to be John Hammond 's wife , and there be nothing you nor anyone else can say which will make I alter my mind . I wish to act dutifully to you , if I can , and I hope you will be good to I and consent to this marriage . but if you will not consent , I shall marry he all the same . I shall be full of sorrow at have to disobey you , but I have promise , and I will keep my promise . ' 

 ' you will act in open rebellion against I against the kinswoman who have rear you , and educate you , and care for you in all these year ! ' 

 ' but you have never love I , ' answer Mary , sadly . ' perhaps if you have give I some portion of that affection which you lavish on my sister I might be willing to sacrifice this new deep love for your sake to lay down my broken heart as a sacrifice on the altar of gratitude . but you never love I . you have tolerate I , endure my presence as a disagreeable necessity of your life , because I be my father 's daughter . you and Lesbia have be all the world to each other ; and I have stand aloof , outside your charmed circle , almost a stranger to you . can you wonder , grandmother , recall this , that I be unwilling to surrender the love that have be give I to - day the true heart of a brave and good man ! ' 

 Lady Maulevrier look at she for some moment in scornful wonderment ; look at she with a slow , deliberate smile . 

 ' poor child ! ' she say ; ' poor ignorant , inexperienced baby ! for what a will - o - the - wisp be you ready to sacrifice my regard , and all the privilege of your position as my granddaughter ! no doubt this Mr. Hammond have say all manner of fine thing to you ; but can you be weak enough to believe that he who half a year ago be sigh and die at the foot of your sister can have one spark of genuine regard for you ? the thing be not in nature ; it be an obvious absurdity . but it be easy enough to understand that Mr. Hammond without a penny in his pocket , and with his way to make in the world , would be very glad to secure Lady Mary Haselden and her five hundred a year , and to have Lord Maulevrier for his brother in - law ? ' 

 ' have I really five hundred a year ? shall I have five hundred a year when I marry ? ' ask Mary , suddenly radiant . 

 ' yes ; if you marry with your brother 's consent . ' 

 ' I be so glad for his sake . he could hardly starve if I have five hundred a year . he need not be oblige to emigrate . ' 

 ' has he be offer you the prospect of emigration as an additional inducement ? ' 

 ' oh , no , he do not say that he be very poor , but since you say he be penniless I think we might be oblige to emigrate . but as I have five hundred a year ' 

 ' you will stay at home , and set up a lodging - house , I suppose , ' sneered Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' I will do anything my husband please . we can live in a humble way in some quiet part of London , while Mr. Hammond work at literature or politic . I be not afraid of poverty or trouble , I be willing to endure both for his sake . ' 

 ' you be a fool ! ' say her grandmother sternly . ' and I have nothing more to say to you . go away , and send Maulevrier to I . ' 

 Mary do not obey immediately . she go over to her grandmother 's couch and knelt by her side , and kiss the poor maim hand which lie on the velvet cushion . 

 ' dear grandmother , ' she say gently . ' I be very sorry to rebel against you . but this be a question of life or death with I . I be not like Lesbia . I can not barter love and truth for worldly advantage for pride of race . do not think I so weak or so vain as to be win by a few fine speech from an adventurer . I have look up to he and reverence he ; and I must be a very foolish girl if my judgment be so poor that I can respect a worthless man . '

I never say that Mr. Hammond be worthless ; but I say that he be no fit husband for you . if you be as yield and obedient as Lesbia it would be all the well for you ; for then I should provide for your establishment in life in a become manner . but as you be wilful , and bend upon take your own way well my dear , you must take the consequence ; and when you be a struggle wife and mother , old before your time , weigh down with the weary burden of petty care , do not say , " my grandmother might have save I from this martyrdom . " '

' 

 ' my brave and good . '

 ' indeed ! '

 she have chalk her cue while she be talk , but after take her aim , she drop her arm irresolutely .

 Mary prefer the drawing - room . it be very sweet to be alone with her lover , but she be weigh down with confusion in his presence . the novelty , the wonderment of her position overpower she . she yearn for the shelter of Fršulein M ller 's wing , albeit the company of that most prosaic person be certain death to romance . 

 Miss M ller be in her accustomed seat by the fire , knit her customary muffler . she have appropriate Lady Maulevrier 's place , much to Mary 's disgust . it irk the girl to see that stout , clumsy figure in the chair which have be fill by her grandmother 's imperial form . the very room seem vulgarise by the change . 

 Fršulein look up with a surprised air when Mary and Hammond enter together , the girl smile and happy . she have expect that Mary would have leave her ladyship 's room in tear , and would have retire to her own apartment to hide her swollen eyelid and humiliate aspect . but here she be , after the fiery ordeal of an interview with her offend grandmother , not in the least crestfallen .

 ' well , your ladyship , what be the row ? ' say Maulevrier march into his grandmother 's room with a free and easy air . he be prepare for a skirmish , and he mean to take the bull by the horn . 

 ' I suppose you know what have happen to - day ? ' say her ladyship . 

 ' molly and Hammond 's expedition , yes , of course . I go part of the way with they , but I be out of training , get pump out after a couple of mile , and be not   such a fool as to go to the top . '

I tell you some time ago not to put your spoke in that wheel . there could not be a happy or a well marriage for Mary . '

 ' that be the desire of the eye , this be the love of the heart . I know that he love Mary ever so much well than he love Lesbia . I can assure your ladyship that I be not such a fool as I look . ' and I be willing to admit that the husband 's character have a great deal to do with the wife 's happiness , from a moral point of view ; but still there be material question to be consider . have your friend any mean of support a wife ? '

 ' you mean to say he would keep she in decent poverty ? can not you be explicit , Maulevrier , and say what mean the man have , whether an income or none ? if you can not tell I I must question Mr. Hammond himself . ' 

 ' pray do not do that , ' exclaim her grandson urgently . ' do not take all the flavour of romance out of Molly 's love story , by go into pound , shilling , and penny . she be very young . you would hardly wish she to marry immediately ? ' 

 ' not for the next year , at the very least . ' 

 ' then why enter upon this sordid question of way and mean . make Hammond and Mary happy by consent to their engagement , and trust the rest to Providence , and to I . take my word for it , Hammond be not a beggar , and he be a man likely to make his mark in the world . if a year hence his income be not enough to allow of his marrying , I will double Mary 's allowance out of my own purse . Hammond 's friendship have steady I , and save I a good deal more than five hundred a year . ' 

 ' I can quite believe that . I believe Mr. Hammond be a worthy man , and that his influence have be very good for you ; but that do not make he a good match for Mary . however , you seem to have settle the business among you , and I suppose I must submit . you have well all drink tea with I to morrow afternoon ; and I will receive your friend as Mary 's future husband . ' 

 ' that be the good and kind of grandmother . ' 

 ' but I should like to know more of his antecedent and his relation . ' 

 ' his antecedent be altogether creditable . he take honour at the University ; he have be like and respect everywhere . he be an orphan , and it be well not to talk to he of his family . he be sensitive on that point , like most man who stand alone in the world . '

 Maulevrier leave his grandmother soon after this , and go downstairs , whistle for very joyousness . find the billiard - room desert he repair to the drawing - room , where he find Mary play scrap of melody to her lover at the shadowy end of the room , while Fršulein sit by the fire weave her web as steadily as one of the Fatal Sisters , and with a brow prophetic of evil . 

 Maulevrier creep up to the piano , and come stealthily behind the lover . 

 ' bless you , my child , ' he say , hover over they with outspread hand . ' I be the dove come back to the ark . I be the bearer of happy tiding . Lady Maulevrier consent to your acquire the legal right to make each other miserable for the rest of your life . ' 

 ' God bless you , Maulevrier , ' say Hammond , clasp he by the hand . 

 ' only as this sister of mine be hardly out of the nursery you will have to wait for she at least a year . so say the dowager , whose word be like the law of the Medes and Persians , and altereth not . ' 

 ' I would wait for her twice seven year , as Jacob wait , and toil for she , as Jacob toil , ' answer Hammond , ' but I should like to call she my own to - morrow , if it be possible . ' 

 nothing could be happy or gayer than the tea - drinking in Lady Maulevrier 's room on the following afternoon . her ladyship have once give way upon a point know how to make her concession gracefully . she extend her hand to Mr. Hammond as frankly as if he have be her own particular choice . 

 ' I can not refuse my granddaughter to her brother 's dear friend , ' she say , ' but I think you be two most imprudent young people . ' 

 ' Providence take care of imprudent lover , just as it do of the bird in their nest , ' answer Hammond , smile . 

 ' just as much and no more , I fear . providence do not keep off the cat or the tax - gatherer . ' 

 ' bird must take care of their nest , and husband must work for their home , ' argue Hammond . ' Heaven give sweet air and sunlight , and a beautiful world to live in . ' 

 ' I think , ' say Lady Maulevrier , look at he critically , ' you be just the kind of person who ought to emigrate . you have idea that would do for the Bush or the Yosemite Valley , but which be too primitive for an over - crowded country . ' 

 ' no , Lady Maulevrier , I be not go to steal your granddaughter . when she be my wife she shall live within call . I know she love her native land , and I do not   think either of we would care to put an ocean between we and rugge old Helvellyn . ' 

 ' of course have make idiot of yourself up there in the fog and the storm you be go to worship the mountain for ever afterwards , ' say her ladyship laugh . 

 never have she seem gayer or bright . perhaps in her heart of heart she rejoice at get Mary engage , even to so humble a suitor as fortuneless John Hammond . ever since the visit of the so - call Rajah she have live as Damocles live , with the sword of destiny the avenge sword hang over she by the fine hair . every time she hear carriage wheel in the drive every time the hall - door bell ring a little loud than usual , her heart seem to stop beat and her whole be to hang suspend on a thread . if the thread be to snap , there would come darkness and death . the blow that have paralyse one side of her body must need , if repeat , bring total extinction . she who believe in no after life see in her maim and waste arm the beginning of death . she who recognise only the life of the body feel that one half of she be already dead . but month have go by , and Louis Asoph have make no sign . she begin to hope that his boasted document and witness be altogether mythical . and yet the engine of the law be slow to put in motion . he might be work up his case , line upon line , with some hard - head London lawyer ; arrange and marshal his fact ; prepare his witness ; wait for affidavit from India ; work slowly but surely , underground like the mole ; and all at once , in an hour , his case might be before the law court . his story and the story of Lord Maulevrier 's infamy might be town talk again ; as it have be forty year ago , when the true story of that crime have be happily unknown . 

 yes , with the present fear of this Louis Asoph 's revelation , of a new scandal , if not a calamity , Lady Maulevrier feel that it be a good thing to have her young granddaughter 's future in some measure secure . John Hammond have a noble outlook : bold , without impudence or self - assertion ; self - possess , without vanity . yes , assuredly a man to wrestle with difficulty , and to conquer fate .

 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 ' a young LAMB 's heart among the FULL - grown flock . '

 so there come a morning upon which Mary have to say good - bye to those two companion who have so bl and gladden her life . it be a bright sunshiny morning , and all the world look gay ; which seem very unkind of nature , Mary think . and yet , even in the sadness of this parting , she have much reason to be glad . as she stand with her lover in the library , in the three minute of tete - a - tete steal from the argus - eyed Fršulein , fold in his arm , look up at his manly face , it seem to she that the mere knowledge that she belong to he and be belove by he ought to sustain and console she even in long year of severance . yes , even if he be one of the knight of old , go to the Holy Land on a crusade full of peril and uncertainty . even then a woman ought to be brave , have such a lover .

I shall want to spend all the day and hour of my life with you ; to have not a fancy or a pursuit in which you can not share , or with which you can not sympathise .  ' tired ! '

" a tiger with his claws cut and his tooth draw an old , grey - bearded tiger , ghastly and grim , but harmless a cobra with the poison - bag pluck out of his jaw ! the venom grow again , child the snake 's venom but youth never come back . old , and helpless , and harmless !  CHAPTER XXIV . 

 ' now nothing LEFT to LOVE or HATE . ' 


 the old man sit look at Mary in silence for some moment ; not a great space of time , perhaps , as mark by the shadow on the dial behind they , but to Mary that gaze be unpleasantly prolong . he look at she as if he could read every pulsation in every fibre of her brain , and know exactly what it mean . 

 ' who be you ? ' he ask , at last . 

 ' my name be Mary Haselden . ' 

 ' Haselden , ' he repeat musingly , ' I have hear that name before . ' 

 and then he resume his former attitude , his chin rest on the handle of his crutch - stick , his eye bent upon the gravel path , their unholy brightness hide under the penthouse brow . 

 ' Haselden , ' he murmur , and repeat the name over and over again , slowly , dreamily , with a troubled tone , like some one try to work out a difficult problem . ' haselden when ? where ? ' 

 and then with a profound sigh he mutter , ' harmless , quite harmless . you may trust he anywhere . memory a blank , a blank , a blank , my lord ! ' 

 his head sink lower upon his breast , and again he sigh , the sigh of a spirit in torment , Mary think . her vivid imagination be already interested , her quick sympathy be awaken . 

 she look at he wonderingly , compassionately . so old , so infirm , and with a mind astray ; and yet there be indication in his speech and manner that tell of reason struggle against madness , like the light behind storm - cloud . he have tone that speak of a keen sensitiveness to pain , not the lunatic 's imbecile placidity . she observe he intently , try to make out what manner of man he be .

 ' who be you ? ' he ask again . 

 ' I tell you my name just now . I be Mary Haselden . ' 

 ' haselden that be a name I know once . Mary ? I think my mother 's name be Mary . yes , yes , I remember that . you have a sweet face , Mary like my mother 's . she have brown eye , like your , and auburn hair . you do not   recollect she , perhaps ? '

 he stretch out his withered old hand , small , semi - transparent , with the blue vein show darkly under the parchment - colour skin , and grasp Mary 's arm .

 she have make up her mind that this crazy old man must be a relation of Steadman 's to whom he give hospitality either with or without her ladyship 's consent . all powerful as Lady Maulevrier have ever be in her own house , it be just possible that now , when she be a prisoner in her own room , certain small liberty might be take , even by so faithful a servant as Steadman .

 ' Lady Maulevrier 's . I be Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter . ' 

 ' Lady Mau - lev - rier . ' he repeat the name in syllable . ' a good name an old title as old as the conquest . a Norman race those maulevrier . and you be Lady Maulevrier 's granddaughter ! you should be proud . the Maulevriers be always a proud race . ' 

 ' then I be no true Maulevrier , ' answer Mary gaily . 

 she be begin to feel more at her ease with the old man . he be evidently mad , as mad as a March hare ; but his madness seem only the harmless lunacy of extreme old age . he have flash of reason , too . Mary begin to feel a friendly interest in he . to youth in its flush of life and vigour there seem something so unspeakably sad and pitiable in feebleness and age the brief weak remnant of life , the wreck of body and mind , sun itself in the decline ray of a sun that be so soon to shine upon its grave . 

 ' what , be you not proud ? ' ask the old man . 

 ' not at all . I have be teach to consider myself a very insignificant person ; and I be go to marry a poor man . it would not become I to be proud . ' 

 ' but you ought not to do that , ' say the old man . ' you ought not to marry a poor man . poverty be a bad thing , my dear . you be a pretty girl , and ought to marry a man with a handsome fortune . poor man have no pleasure in this world they might just as well be dead . I be poor , as you see . you can tell by this threadbare coat ' he look down at the sleeve from which the nap be wear in place ' I be as poor as a church mouse . '

 ' yes , I be well take care of very well take care of . how long be it , I wonder how many week , or month , or year , since they have take care of I ? it seem a long , long time ; but it be all like a dream a long dream . once I use to try and wake myself . I use to try and struggle out of that weary dream . but that be age ago . I be satisfied now I be quite content now so long as the weather be warm , and I can sit out here in the sun . '

 ' do you sit here every afternoon when it be fine ? ' 

 ' yes , every day all day long sometimes when the sun be warm . ' 

 ' then I will come here to see you . ' 

 ' you must keep it a secret , then , ' say the old man , with a crafty look . ' if you do not   they will shut I up in the house , perhaps . they do not   like I to see people , for fear I should talk . I have hear Steadman say so . yet what should I talk about , heaven help I ? Steadman say my memory be quite go , and that I be childish and harmless childish and harmless . I have hear he say that . you will come again , will not   you , and you will keep it a secret ? '
