 ' a hundred year , at least . that be what it seem to I sometimes . and yet there be time when it seem only a dream . be sure you come again to - morrow . ' 

 ' yes , I promise you to come ; good - night . ' 

 ' good - night . ' 

 Mary go back to the stable . the door be still open , but how could she be sure that it would be open to - morrow ? there be no other access that she know of to the quadrangle , except through the old part of the house , and that be at time inaccessible to she . 

 she find a key a big old rusty key in the inside of the door , so she shut and lock it , and put the key in her pocket . the door she suppose have be leave open by accident ; at any rate this key make her mistress of the situation . if any question should arise as to her conduct she could have an explanation with Steadman ; but she have pledge her word to the poor mad old man , and she mean to keep her promise , if possible . 

 as she leave the stable she see Steadman rid towards the gate on his grey cob . she pass he as she go back to the house . 

 next day , and the day after that , and for many day , Mary use her key , and go into the quadrangle at sundown to sit for half an hour or so with the strange old man , who seem to take an intense pleasure in her company . the weather be grow warm as May wear on towards June , and this evening hour , between six and seven , be deliciously bright and balmy . the seat by the sundial be screen on every side by the clipped yew hedge , dense and tall , surround the circular , gravelled space , in the centre of which stand the old granite dial , with its octagonal pedestal and moss - grow step . there , as in a closely - shaded arbour , Lady Mary and her old friend be alone and unobserved . the yew - tree boundary be at least eight foot high , and Mary and her companion could hardly have be see even from the upper window of the low , old house . 

 Mary have fall into the habit of go for her walk or her ride at five o'clock every day , when she be not in attendance on Lady Maulevrier , and after her walk or ride she slip through the stable , and join her ancient friend . stable and courtyard be generally empty at this hour , the man only appear at the sound of a big bell , which summon they from their snuggery when they be want . Most of Lady Maulevrier 's servant have arrive at that respectable stage of long service in which fidelity be count as a substitute for hard work . 

 the old man be not particularly conversational , and be apt to repeat the same thing over and over again , with a sublime unconsciousness of be prosy ; but he like to hear Mary talk , and he listen with seem intelligence . he question she about the world outside his cloistered life the war and rumour of war and , although the name of the question and the man of the day seem utterly strange to he , and he have to have they repeat to he again and again , he seem to take an intelligent interest in the stir fact of the time , and listen intently when Mary give he a synopsis of her last newspaper reading . 

 when the news be exhaust , Mary hit upon a more childish form of amusement , and that be to tell the story of any novel or poem she have be lately read . this be so successful that in this manner Mary relate the story of most of Shakespeare 's play ; of Byron 's Bride of Abydos , and Corsair ; of Keats 's Lamia ; of Tennyson 's Idylls ; and of a heterogenous collection of poetry and romance , in all of which storie the old man take a vivid interest . 

 ' you be well to I than the sunshine , ' he tell Mary one day when she be leave he . ' the world grow dark when you leave I . ' 

 once at this parting moment he take both her hand , and draw she nearer to he , peer into her face in the clear evening light . 

 ' you be like my mother , ' he say . ' yes , you be very like she . and who else be it that you be like ? there be some one else , I know . yes , some one else ! I remember ! it be a face in a picture a picture at Maulevrier Castle . ' 

 ' what do you know of Maulevrier Castle ? ' ask Mary , wonderingly . 

 Maulevrier be the family seat in Herefordshire , which have not be occupy by the eld branch for the last forty year . Lady Maulevrier have let it during her son 's minority to a young branch of the family , a branch which have intermarry with the world of successful commerce , and be rich than the head of the house . this occupation of Maulevrier Castle have continue to the present time , and be likely still to continue , Maulevrier have no desire to set up housekeeping in a feudal castle in the march . 

 ' how come you to know Maulevrier Castle ? ' repeat Mary . 

 ' I be there once . there be a picture by Lely , a portrait of a Lady Maulevrier in Charles the Second 's time . the face be your , my love . I have hear of such hereditary face . my mother be proud of resemble that portrait . ' 

 ' what do your mother know of Maulevrier Castle ? ' 

 the old man do not answer . he have lapse into that dream - like condition into which he often sink , when his brain be not stimulate to attention and coherency by his interest in Mary 's narration . 

 Mary conclude that this man have once be a servant in the Maulevrier household , perhaps at the place in Herefordshire , and that all his old memory run in one groove the house of Maulevrier . 

 the freedom of her intercourse with he be undisturbed for about three week ; and at the end of that time she come face to face with James Steadman as she emerge from the circle of greenery . 

 ' you here , Lady Mary ? ' he exclaim with an angry look . 

 ' yes , I have be sit talk to that poor old man , ' Mary answer , cheerily , conclude that Steadman 's look of vexation arise from his be detect in the act of harbour a contraband relation . ' he be a very interesting character . a relation of your , I suppose ? ' 

 ' yes , he be a relation , ' reply Steadman . ' he be very old , and his mind have long be go . her ladyship be kind enough to allow I to give he a home in her house . he be quite harmless , and he be in nobody 's way . ' 

 ' of course not , poor soul . he be only a burden to himself . he talk as if his life have be very weary . have he be long in that sad state ? ' 

 ' yes , a long time . ' 

 Steadman 's manner to Lady Mary be curt at the good of time . she have always stand somewhat in awe of he , as a person delegate with authority by her grandmother , a servant who be much more than a servant . but to - day his manner be more abrupt than usual . 

 ' he speak of Maulevrier Castle just now , ' say Mary , determine not to be put down too easily . ' be he once in service there ? ' 

 ' he be . pray how do you find your way into this garden , Lady Mary ? ' 

 ' I come through the stable . as it be my grandmother 's garden I suppose I do not take an unwarrantable liberty in come , ' say Mary , draw herself up , and ready for battle . 

 ' it be Lady Maulevrier 's wish that this garden should be reserve for my use , ' answer Steadman . ' her ladyship know that my uncle walk here of an afternoon , and that , owe to his age and infirmity , he can go nowhere else ; and if only on that account , it be well that the garden should be keep private . lunatic be rather dangerous company , Lady Mary , and I advise you to give they a wide berth wherever you may meet they . ' 

 ' I be not afraid of your uncle , ' say Mary , resolutely . ' you say yourself just now that he be quite harmless : and I be really interested in he , poor old creature . he like I to sit with he a little of an afternoon and to talk to he ; and if you have no objection I should like to do so , whenever the weather be fine enough for the poor old man to be out in the garden at this hour . ' 

 ' I have a very great objection , Lady Mary , and that objection be chiefly in your interest , ' answer Steadman , firmly . ' no one who be not experience in the way of lunatic can imagine the danger of any association with they their consummate craftiness , their capacity for crime . every madman be harmless up to a certain point mild , inoffensive , perhaps , up to the very moment in which he commit some appalling crime . and then people cry out upon the want of prudence , the want of common - sense which allow such an act to be possible . no , Lady Mary , I understand the benevolence of your motive , but I can not permit you to run such a risk . ' 

 ' I be convinced that the poor old creature be perfectly harmless , ' say Mary , with suppressed indignation . ' I shall certainly ask Lady Maulevrier to speak to you on the subject . perhaps her influence may induce you to be a little more considerate to your unhappy relation . ' 

 ' Lady Mary , I beg you not to say a word to Lady Maulevrier on this subject . you will do I the great injury if you speak of that man . I entreat you ' 

 but Mary be go . she pass Steadman with her head hold high and her eye sparkle with anger . all that be generous , compassionate , womanly in her nature be up in arm against her grandmother 's steward . of all other thing , Mary Haselden most detest cruelty ; and she could see in Steadman 's opposition to her wish nothing but the most cold - hearted cruelty to a poor dependent on his charity . 

 she go in at the stable door , shut and lock it , and put the key in her pocket as usual . but she have little hope that this mode of access would be leave open to she . she know enough of James Steadman 's character , from hearsay rather than from experience , to feel sure that he would not easily give way . she be not surprised , therefore , on return from her ride on the following afternoon , to find the disuse harness - room half fill with truss of straw , and the door of communication completely block . it would be impossible for she to remove that barricade without assistance ; and then , how could she be sure that the door itself be not nail up , or secure in some way ? 

 it be a delicious sunny afternoon , and she could picture the lonely old man sit in his circle of greenery beside the dial , which for he have register so many dreary and solitary hour , wait for the little ray of social sunlight which her presence shed over his monotonous life . he have tell she that she be like the sunshine to he well than sunshine and she have promise not to forsake he . she picture he wait , with his hand clasp upon his crutch - stick , his chin rest upon his hand , his eye pore on the ground , as she have see he for the first time . and as the stable clock chime the quarter he would begin to think himself abandon , forget ; if , indeed , he take any count of the passage of time , of which she be not sure . his mind seem to have sink into a condition which be between dream and waking , a state to which the outside world seem only half real a phase of be in which there be neither past nor future , only the insufferable monotony of an everlasting now . 

 Pity be so near akin to love that Mary , in her deep compassion for this lonely , joyless , loveless existence , feel a regard which be almost affection for this strange old man , whose very name be unknown to she . true that there be much in his countenance and manner which be sinister and repellant . he be a be calculate to inspire fear rather than love ; but the fact that he have court her presence and look to she for consolation have touch Mary 's heart , and she have become reconcile to all that be forbid and disagreeable in the lunatic physiognomy . be he not the victim of a visitation which entitle he to respect as well as to pity ? 

 for some day Mary hold her peace , remember Steadman 's vehement entreaty that she should not speak of this subject to her grandmother . she be silent , but the image of the old man haunt she at all time and season . she see he even in her dream those happy dream of the girl who love and be belove , and before whom the pathway of the future smile like a vision of Paradise . she hear he call to she with a piteous cry of distress , and on wake from this troubled dream she fancy that he must be die , and that this sound in her dream be one of those ghostly warning which give notice of death . she be so unhappy about he , altogether so distressed at be compel to break her word , that she could not prevent her thought from dwell upon he , not even after she have pour out all her trouble to John Hammond in a long letter , in which her garden adventure and her little skirmish with Steadman be graphically describe . 

 to her intense discomfiture Hammond reply that he thoroughly approve of Steadman 's conduct in the matter . however agreeable Mary 's society might be to the lunatic , Mary 's life be far too precious to be put within the possibility of peril by any such tete - a - tete . if the person be the same old man whom Hammond have see on the Fell , he be a most sinister - look creature , of whom any evil act might be fairly anticipate . in a word Mr. Hammond take Steadman 's view of the matter , and entreat his dear Mary to be careful , and not to allow her warm heart to place she in circumstance of peril . 

 this be most disappointing to Mary , who expect her lover to agree with she upon every point ; and if he have be at Fellside the difference of opinion might have give rise to their first quarrel . but as she have a few hour ' leisure for reflection before the post go out , she have time to get over her anger , and to remember that promise of obedience give , half in jest , half in earnest , at the little inn beyond Dunmail Raise . so she write submissively enough , only with just a touch of reproach at Jack 's want of compassion for a poor old man who have such strong claim upon everybody 's pity . 

 the image of the poor old man be not to be banish from her thought , and on that very afternoon , when her letter be dispatch , Mary go on a visit of exploration to the stable , to see if by any chance Mr. Steadman 's plan for isolate his unhappy relative might be circumvent . 

 she go all over the stable into loose box , harness and saddle room , shed for wood , and shed for root , but she find no door open into the quadrangle , save that door by which she have enter , and which be securely defend by a barricade of straw that have be double by a fresh delivery of truss since she first see it . but while she be prowl about the sweet - scented stable , much disappointed at the result of her investigation , she stumble against a ladder which lead to an open trap - door . Mary mount the ladder , and find herself amidst the dusty atmosphere of a large hayloft , half in shadow , half in the hot bright sunlight . a large shutter be open in the sloping roof , the roof that slop towards the quadrangle , an open patch admit light and air . Mary , light and active as a squirrel , spring upon a truss of hay , and in another moment have swing herself in the opening of the shutter , and be stand with her foot on the wooden ledge at the bottom of the massive frame , and her figure support against the slope of thick thatched roof . perch , or half suspend , thus , she be just high enough to look over the top of the yew - tree hedge into the circle round the sundial . 

 yes , there be the unhappy victim of fate , and man 's inhumanity to man . there sit the shrunken figure , with droop head , and melancholy attitude the bent shoulder of feeble old age , the patriarchal lock so appeal to pity . there he sit with eye pore upon the ground just as she have see he the first time . and while she have sit with he and talk with he he have seem to awaken out of that dull despondency , gleam of pleasure have light up his wrinkle face he have grow animate , a sentient living instead of a corpse alive . it be very hard that this little interval of life , these stray gleam of gladness should be deny to the poor old creature , at the behest of James Steadman . 

 Mary would have feel less angrily upon the subject have she believe in Steadman 's supreme carefulness of her own safety ; but in this she do not believe . she look upon the house - steward 's prudence as a hypocritical pretence , an affectation of fidelity and wisdom , by which he contrive to gratify the evil tendency of his own hard and cruel nature . for some reason of his own , perhaps constrain thereto by necessity , he have give the old man an asylum for his age and infirmity : but while thus give he shelter he consider he a burden , and from mere perversity of mind refuse he all such consolation as be possible to his afflicted state , mew he up as a prisoner , cut he off from the companionship of his fellow - man . 

 two year ago , before Mary emerge from her Tomboyhood , she would have think very little of let herself out of the loft window and clamber down the side of the stable , which be well furnish with those projection in the way of gutter , drain - pipe , and century - old ivy , which make such a descent easy . two year ago Mary 's light figure would have swing itself down among the ivy leave , and she would have glory in the thought of circumvent James Steadman so easily . but now Mary be a young lady a young lady engage to be marry , and impress with the responsibility of her position , deeply sensible of a new dignity , for the preservation of which she be in a manner answerable to her lover . 

 ' what would he think of I if I go scramble down the ivy ? ' she ask herself ; ' and after he have approve of Steadman 's heartless restriction , it would be rank rebellion against he if I be to do it . poor old man , " thou art so near and yet so far , " as Lesbia 's song say . ' 

 she blow a kiss on the tip of her finger towards that sad solitary figure , and then drop back into the dusty duskiness of the loft . but although her new idea upon the subject of ' Anstand ' or good behaviour prevent she get the well of Steadman by foul mean , she be all the more intent upon have her own way by fair mean , now that the impression of the old man 's sadness and solitude have be renew by the sight of the droop figure by the sundial . 

 she go back to the house , and walk straight to her grandmother 's room . Lady Maulevrier 's couch have be place in front of the open window , from which she be watch the westward - slop sun above the long line of hill , dark Helvellyn , rugge Nabb Scarr , and verdant Fairfield , with its two giant arm stretch out to enfold and shelter the smile valley . 

 ' Heavens ! child , what an object you be ; ' exclaim her ladyship , as Mary draw near . ' why , your gown be all over dust , and your hair be why your hair be sprinkle with hay and clover . I think you have learn to be tidy , since your engagement . what have you be do with yourself ? ' 

 ' I have be up in the hayloft , ' answer Mary , frankly ; and , intent on one idea , she say impetuously , ' dear grandmother , I want you to do I a favour a very great favour . there be a poor old man , a relation of Steadman 's , who live with he , out of his mind , but quite harmless , and he be so sad and lonely , so dreadfully sad , and he like I to sit with he in the garden , and tell he story , and recite verse to he , poor soul , just as if he be a child , do not   you know , and it be such a pleasure to I to be a little comfort to he in his lonely wretched life , and James Steadman say I must not   go near he , because he may change at any moment into a dangerous lunatic , and do I some kind of harm , and I be not a bit afraid , and I be sure he will not   do anything of the kind , and , please grandmother , tell Steadman , that I be to be allow to go and sit with his poor old prisoner half an hour every afternoon . ' 

 carry along the current of her own impetuous thought , Mary have talk very fast , and have not once look at her grandmother while she be speak . but now at the end of her speech her eye seek Lady Maulevrier 's face in gentle entreaty , and she recoil involuntarily at the sight she see there . 

 the classic feature be distort almost as they have be in the bad period of the paralytic seizure . Lady Maulevrier be ghastly pale , and her eye glare with an awful fire as they gaze at Mary . her whole frame be convulse , and she , the cripple , whose right limb lie numbed and motionless upon the couch , make a struggle motion as she raise herself a little with the left arm , as if , by very force of angry will , she would have lift herself up erect before the girl who have offend she . 

 for a few moment her lip move dumbly ; and there be something unspeakably awful in those convulse feature , that livid countenance , and those voiceless syllable tremble upon the white dry lip . 

 at last speech come . 

 ' girl , you be create to torment I ; ' she exclaim . 

 ' dear grandmother , what harm have I do ? ' falter Mary . 

 ' what harm ? you be a spy . your very existence be a torment and a danger . would to God that you be marry . yes , marry to a chimney - sweep , even and out of my way . ' 

 ' if that be your only difficulty , ' say Mary , haughtily , ' I dare say Mr. Hammond would be kind enough to marry I to - morrow , and take I out of your ladyship 's way . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier 's head sink back upon her pillow , those velvet and satin pillow , rich with delicate point lace and crewel - work adornment , the labour of Mary and Fršulein , pillow which could not bring peace to the weary head , or deaden the torture of memory . the pale face recover its wonte calm , the heavy lip droop over the weary eye , and for a few moment there be silence in the room . 

 then Lady Maulevrier raise her eyelid , and look at her granddaughter imploringly , pathetically . 

 ' forgive I , Mary , ' she say . ' I do not   know what I be say just now ; but whatever it be , forgive and forget it . I be a wretched old woman , heart sick , heart sore , wear out by pain and weariness . there be time when I be beside myself ; moment when I be not much saner than Steadman 's lunatic uncle . this be one of my bad day , and you come bounce in upon I , and torture my nerve by your breathless torrent of word . pray forgive I , if I say anything rude . ' 

 ' if , ' think Mary : but she try to be charitable , and to believe that Lady Maulevrier 's attack upon she be a new phase of hysteria , so she murmur meekly , ' there be nothing for I to forgive , grandmother , and I be very sorry I disturb you . ' 

 she be go to leave the room , think that her absence would be a relief to the invalid , when Lady Maulevrier call she back . 

 ' you be ask I something something about that old man of Steadman 's , ' she say with a weary air , half indifference , half the lassitude natural to an invalid who sink under the burden of monotonous day . ' what be it all about ? I forget . ' 

 Mary repeat her request , but this time in measured tone . 

 ' my dear , I be sure that Steadman be only properly prudent . ' answer Lady Maulevrier , ' and that it would never do for I to interfere in this matter . it stand to reason that he must know his old kinsman 's temperament much well than you can , after your half - hour interview with he in the garden . pray how long have these garden scene be go on , by - the - by ? ' ask her ladyship , with a search look at Mary 's downcast face . 

 the girl have not altogether recover from the rude shock of her grandmother 's late attack . 

 ' about three week , ' falter Mary . ' but it be more than a week now since I be in the garden . it be quite by accident that I first go there . perhaps I ought to explain . ' 

 and Mary , not be gainsay , go on to describe that first afternoon when she have see the old man brood in the sun . she draw quite a pathetic picture of his joyless solitude , whilst all nature around and about he be look so glad in the spring sunshine . there be a long silence , a silence of some minute , when she have do ; and Lady Maulevrier lie with lowered eyelid , deep in thought . Mary begin to hope that she have touch her grandmother 's heart , and that her request would be grant : but she be soon undeceived . 

 ' I be sorry to be oblige to refuse you a favour , Mary , but I must stand by Steadman , ' say her ladyship . ' when I give Steadman permission to shelter his aged kinsman in my house , I make it a condition that the old man should be keep in the strict care by himself and his wife , and that nobody in this establishment should be trouble by he . this condition have be so scrupulously adhere to that the old man 's existence be know to no one in this house except you and I ; and you have discover the fact only by accident . I must beg you to keep this secret to yourself . Steadman have particular reason for wish to conceal the fact of his uncle 's residence here . the old man be not actually a lunatic . if he be we should be violate the law by keep he here . he be only imbecile from extreme old age ; the body have outlive the mind , that be all . but should any officious functionary come down upon Fellside , this imbecility might be call madness , and the poor old creature whom you regard so compassionately , and whose case you think so pitiable here , would be carry off to a pauper lunatic asylum , which I can assure you would be a much bad imprisonment than Fellside Manor . ' 

 ' yes , indeed , grandmother , ' exclaim Mary , whose vivid imagination conjure up a vision of padded cell , strait - waist - coat , murderously - incline keeper , chain , handcuff , and bread and water diet , ' now I understand why the poor old soul have be keep so close why nobody know of his existence . I beg Steadman 's pardon with all my heart . he be a much well fellow than I think he . ' 

 ' Steadman be a thoroughly good fellow , and as true as steel , ' say her ladyship . ' no one can know that so well as the mistress he have serve faithfully for nearly half a century . I hope , Mary , you have not be chatter to Fršulein or any one else about your discovery . ' 

 ' no , grandmother , I have not say a word to a mortal , but - - ' 

 ' oh , there be a " but , " be there ? I understand . you have not be so reticent in your letter to Mr. Hammond . ' 

 ' I tell he all that happen to I . there be very little to write about at Fellside ; yet I contrive to send he volume . I often wonder what poor girl do in the day of Miss Austen 's novel , when letter cost a shilling or eighteen penny for postage , and have to be pay for by the recipient . it must have be such a terrible check upon affection . ' 

 ' and upon twaddle , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' well you tell Mr. Hammond about Steadman 's old uncle . what do he say ? ' 

 ' he thoroughly approve Steadman 's conduct in forbid I to go and see he , ' answer Mary . ' I could not   help think it rather unkind of he ; but , of course , I feel that he must be right , ' conclude Mary , as much as to say that her lover be necessarily infallible . 

 ' I always think Mr. Hammond a sensible young man , and I be glad to find that his conduct do not belie my good opinion , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' and now , my dear , you have well go and make yourself decent before dinner . I be very weary this afternoon , and even our little talk have exhaust I . ' 

 ' yes , dear grandmother , I be go this instant . but let I ask one question : what be the poor old man 's name ? ' 

 ' his name ! ' say her ladyship , look at Mary with a puzzled air , like a person whose thought be far away . ' his name oh , Steadman , I suppose , like his nephew 's ; but if I ever hear the name I have forget it , and I do not   know whether the kinship be on the father 's or the mother 's side . Steadman ask my permission to give shelter to a helpless old relative , and I give it . that be really all I remember . ' 

 ' only one other question , ' plead Mary , who be brimful of curiosity upon this particular subject . ' have he be at Fellside very long ? ' 

 ' oh , I really do not   know ; a year , or two , or three , perhaps . life in this house be all of a piece . I hardly keep count of time . ' 

 ' there be one thing that puzzle I very much , ' say Mary , still linger near her grandmother 's couch , the balmy evening air caress she as she lean against the embrasure of the wide Tudor window , the sun draw near to the edge of the hill , an orb of yellow flame , soon to change to a gigantic disk of lurid fire . ' I think from the old man 's talk that he , too , must be an old servant in our family . he talk of Maulevrier Castle , and say that I remind he of a picture by Lely , a portrait of a Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' it be quite possible that he may have be in service there , though I do not remember to have hear anything about it , ' answer her ladyship , carelessly . ' the Steadmans come from that part of the country , and theirs be a hereditary service . good - night , Mary , I be utterly weary . look at that glorious light yonder , that mighty world of fire and flame , without which our little world would be dark and dreary . I often think of that speech of Macbeth 's , " I ' gin to be aweary of the sun . " there come a time , Mary , when even the sun be a burden . ' 

 ' only for such a man as Macbeth , ' say Mary , ' a man steep in crime . who can wonder that he want to hide himself from the sun ? but , dear grandmother , there ought to be plenty of happiness leave for you , even if your recovery be slow to come . you be so clever , you have such resource in your own mind and memory , and you have your grandchild , who love you dearly , ' add Mary , tenderly . 

 her nature be so full of pity that an entirely new affection have grow up in her mind for Lady Maulevrier since that terrible evening of the paralytic stroke . 

 ' yes , and whose love , as exemplify by Lesbia , be show in a hurried scrap of a letter scrawl once a week a bone throw to a hungry dog , ' say her ladyship , bitterly . 

 ' Lesbia be so lovely , and she be so surround by flatterer and admirer , ' murmur Mary , excusingly . 

 ' oh , my dear , if she have a heart she would not forget I , even in the midst of her flatterer . good - night again , Mary . do not   try to console I . for some nature consolation and soothe suggestion be like flower throw upon a granite tomb . they do just as much and just as little good to the heart that lie under the stone . good - night . ' 

 Mary stoop to kiss her grandmother 's forehead , and find it cold as marble . she murmur a love good - night , and leave the mistress of Fellside in her loneliness . 

 a footman would come in and light the lamp , and draw the velvet curtain , presently , and shut out the later glory of sunset . and then the butler himself would come and arrange the little dinner table by her ladyship 's couch , and would himself preside over the invalid 's simple dinner , which would be serve exquisitely , with all that be dainty and most costly in Salviati glass and antique silver . yet well the dinner of herb , and love and peace withal , than the choice fare or the most perfect service . 

 before the coming of the servant and the lamp there be a pause of silence and loneliness , an interval during which Lady Maulevrier lie gaze at the decline orb , the low rim of which now rest on the edge of the hill . it seem to grow large and more dazzling as she look at it . 

 suddenly she clasp her left hand across her eye , and say aloud 

 ' oh , what a hateful life ! almost half a century of lie and hypocricie and prevarication and meanness ! for what ? for the glory of an empty name ; and for a fortune that may slip from my dead hand to become the prey of rogue and adventurer . who can forecast the future ? ' 

 CHAPTER XXV . 

 CARTE BLANCHE . 


 Lady Kirkbank 's house in Arlington Street be know to half fashionable London as one of the pleasant house in town ; and it be know by repute only , to the other half of fashionable London , as a house whose threshold be not to be cross by person with any regard for their own dignity and reputation . it be not that Lady Kirkbank have ever actually forfeit her right to be consider an honest woman and a faithful wife . People who talk of the lady and she set with a contemptuous shrug of the shoulder and a dubious elevation of the eyebrow be ready , when hard push in argument , to admit that they know of no actual harm in Lady Kirkbank , no overt bad behaviour . 

 ' but well , ' say the punctilious half of society , the Pejinks and Pernickitys , the Picksomes and Unco - Goods , ' Lady Kirkbank be Lady Kirkbank ; and I would not allow my girl to visit she , do not   you know . ' ' Lady Kirkbank be receive , certainly , ' say a severe dowager . ' she go to very good house . she get ticket for the Royal enclosure . she be always at private view , and privileged show of all kind ; and she contrive to squeeze herself in at a State ball or a concert about once in two year ; but any one who can consider Lady Kirkbank good style must have a very curious idea of what a lady ought to be . ' ' Lady Kirkbank be a warm - hearted , nice creature , ' say a diplomatist of high rank , and one of her particular friend , ' but her manner be decidedly continental ! ' 

 about Sir George , society , adverse or friendly , be without strong opinion . his friend , the man who shoot over his Scotch moor , and fill the spare room in his villa at Cannes , and load his drag for Sandown or Epsom , and sponge upon he all the year round , talk of he as ' an inoffensive old party , ' ' a cheery soul , ' ' a genial old boy , ' and in like term of approval . that half of society which do not visit in Arlington Street , in whose nostril the semi - aristocratic , semi - artistic , altogether bohemian little dinner , the supper after the play , the small hour devote to Nap or Poker , have an odour as of sulphur , the reek of Tophet even this half of the great world be fain to admit that Sir George be harmless . he have never have an idea beyond the realm of sport ; he have never have a will of his own outside his stable . to shoot pigeon at Hurlington or Monaco , to keep half a dozen leather - plater , and attend every race from the Craven to the Leger , to hunt four day a week , when he be allow to spend a winter in England , and to saunter and sleep away all the hour which could not be give to sport , comprise Sir George 's idea of existence . he have never trouble himself to consider whether there might not possibly be a well way of get rid of one 's life . he be as God have make he , and be perfectly satisfied with himself and the universe ; save at such time as when a favourite horse go lame , or his banker write to tell he that his account be overdrawn . 

 Sir George have no child ; he have never have a serious care in his life . he never think , he never read . Lady Kirkbank declare that she have never see he with a book in his hand since their marriage . 

 ' I do not   believe he would know at which end to begin , ' she say . 

 what be the specific charge which the very particular people bring against Lady Kirkbank ? such charge rarely be specific . the idea that the lady belong to the fast and furious section of society , the Bohemia of the upper ten , be an idea in the air . everybody know it . no one could quite adequately explain it . 

 from thirty to fifty Lady Kirkbank have be know as a flirty matron . wherever she go , a train of man go with she ; man young and middle - aged and elderly ; handsome youth from the public office ; War , Admiralty , Foreign Office , Somerset House young man ; attractive man of mature year , with grey moustachios , military , diplomatic , horsey , what you will , but always agreeable . at home , abroad , Lady Kirkbank be never without her court ; but the court be entirely masculine . in those day the fair Georgie do not scruple to say that she hat woman , and that girl be her particular abomination . but as the year roll on Lady Kirkbank begin to find it very difficult to muster her little court , to keep she train in attendance upon she . ' the bird be wild , ' Sir George say . her young adorer find their official duty more oppressive than hitherto ; her elderly swain have threatening of gout or rheumatism which prevent their flocking round she as of old at race meeting or polo match . they be loyal enough in keep their engagement at the dinner table , for Lady Kirkbank 's cook be one of the good in London ; and the invite guest be rarely miss at the little supper after opera or play : but Georgia 's box be no long crowd with man who drop in between the act to see what she think of the singer or the piece , and her swain be no long content to sit behind her chair all the evening , see an empty corner of the stage across Georgia 's ivory shoulder , and hear the voice of invisible actor in the brief pause of Georgie 's subdued babble . 

 at fifty - five , Georgina Kirkbank tell herself sadly enough that her day , as a bright particular star , all - sufficient in her own radiance , be go . she could not live without her masculine circle , man who could bring she all the news , the gossip of the club ; where everything seem to become know as quickly as if each club have its own Asmodeus , unroofe all the housetop of the West end for inspection every night . she could not live without her courtier ; and to keep they about she she know that she must make her house pleasant . it be not enough to give good dinner , elegant little supper wash down by choicest wine ; she must also provide fair face to smile upon the feast , and bright eye to sparkle in the subdue light of low shade lamp , and many candle twinkle under coloured shade . 

 ' I be an old woman now , ' Lady Kirkbank say to herself with a sigh , ' and my own attraction will not   keep my friend about I . C'est trop connu Áa . ' 

 and now the house in Arlington Street in which feminine guest have be as one in ten , open its door to the young and the fair . pretty widow , lively girl , young wife who be not too absurdly devoted to their husband , actress of high standing and good look , these begin to be welcome effusively in Arlington Street . Lady Kirkbank begin to hunt for beauty to adorn her room , as she have hitherto hunt lion to roar at her party . she pride herself on be the first to discover this or that new beauty . that lovely girl from Scotland with the large eye that sweet young creature from Ireland with the long eyelash . she be always invent new divinity . but even this change of plan , this more feminine line of politic fail to reconcile the strict and the stern , the Queen Charlotte - ish elderly lady , and the impeccable matron , to Lady Kirkbank and her set . the girl who be launch by Lady Kirkbank never take high rank in society . when they make good marriage it be generally to be observe that they drop Lady Kirkbank soon afterwards . it be not their fault , these ingrate plead piteously ; but Edward , or Henry , or Theodore , as the case might be , have a most cruel prejudice against dear Lady Kirkbank , and the young wife be oblige to obey . 

 other there be , however , the loyal few , who have win the prize matrimonial in Lady Kirkbank 's happy hunting ground , remain true to their friend ever afterwards , and defend her character against every onslaught . 

 when Lady Maulevrier tell her grandson that she have entrust Lady Kirkbank with the duty of introduce Lesbia to society , Maulevrier shrug his shoulder and hold his peace . he know no actual harm in the matter . ' her ladyship never have any opinion of my wisdom , and this Lady Kirkbank be a friend of her own youth . she would cut up rough if I be to say a word against an old friend . besides what be the odd , if you come to think of it ? all society be fast nowadays , or at any rate all society worth live in . and then again , Lesbia be just one of those cool - head girl who would keep herself head uppermost in a maelstrom . she know on which side her bread be butter . look how easily she chuck you up because she do not think you good enough . she will make use of this Lady Kirkbank , who be a good soul , I be tell , and will make the good match of the season .  and now the season have begin , and Lady Lesbia Haselden be circulate with other aristocratic atom in the social vortex , with her head apparently uppermost .

I never discover it till I be an old woman . how I envy Cleopatra her Casar and her Antony . no mistake one of those for the other . Lady Kirkbank be a close student of all those delightful book of french memoir which bring the tittle - tattle of the Regency and the scandal of Louis the Fifteenth 's reign so vividly before we : and she have unconsciously found her manner and her way of think and talk upon that easy - go but elegant age . she do not want to seem well than woman who have be so altogether charming . she fortify the frivolity of historical parisian manner by a dash of the british sporting character . she drive , shot , jump over five - bar gate , contrive on the verge of seventy to be as active we a young woman ; and she flatter herself that the mixture of wit , audacity , sport , and good - nature be full of fascination . 

 however this might be , it be certain that a good many people like she , chiefly perhaps because she be good - natured , and a little on account of that admirable cook . 

 to Lesbia , who have be weary to loathing of her old life amidst the hill and waterfall of Westmoreland , this new life be one perpetual round of pleasure . she fling herself with all her heart and mind into the amusement of the moment ; she know neither weariness nor satiety . to ride in the park in the morning , to go to a luncheon party , a garden party , to drive in the park for half an hour after the garden party , to rush home and dress for the fourth or fifth time , and then off to a dinner , and from dinner to drum , and from drum to big ball , at which rumour say the Prince and Princess be to be present ; and so , from eleven o'clock in the morning till four or five o'clock next morning , the giddy whirl go on ; and every hour be so occupy by pleasure engagement that it be difficult to squeeze in an occasional morning for shop necessary to go to the shop sometimes , or one would not know how many thing one really want or for an indispensable interview with the dressmaker . those morning at the shop be hardly the least agreeable of Lesbia 's hour . to a girl bring up in one perpetual tete - a - tete with green hill - side and silvery watercourse , the West end shop be as garden of Eden , as Aladdin Caves , as anything , everything that be rapturous and intoxicating .  and then come the visit to the dressmaker . it happen after all that Kate Kearney be not intrust with Lady Lesbia 's frock . Miss Kearney be the fashion , and could pick and choose her customer ; and as she be a young lady of good business aptitude , she have a liking for ready money , or at least half - yearly settlement ; and , find that Lady Kirkbank be much more willing to give new order than to pay old account , she have respectfully inform her ladyship that a pressure of business would prevent she execute any further demand from Arlington Street , while the necessity of post her ledger oblige she to request the favour of an immediate cheque .

 Seraphine have make Lady Kirkbank 's clothe , off and on for the last thirty year . Seraphine and Georgia have grow old together .  Seraphine be a woman of genius and versatility and have more than one art at her finger ' end those skinny and claw - shape finger , the nail whereof be not always clean . she take charge of her customer 's figure , and make their corset , and lecture they if they allow nature to get the upper hand . 

 ' if Madame 's waist get one quarter of an inch thick it must be that I renounce to make her gown , ' she would tell a ponderous matron , with cool insolence , and the matron would stand abash before the little sallow , hooked - nose , keen eyed Jewess , like a child before a severe mother .

 ' be it that I think so ? why that jump to the eye . Madame have always that little air of Rubens , even in the flower of her youth but now it be a Rubens of the Faubourg du Temple . ' 

 and horrify at the idea of her vulgarise charm the meek matron would consent to encase herself in one of Seraphine 's severe corset , call in bitter mockery a la santť at five guineas in order that the dressmaker might measure she for a forty - guinea gown .

 Clanricarde Place be a little nook of Queen Anne house genuine Queen Anne , be it understand between Piccadilly and St. James 's Palace , and hardly five minute ' walk from Arlington Street . it be a quiet little cul de sac in the very heart of the fashionable world ; and here of an afternoon might be see the carriage of Madame Seraphine 's customer , block the whole of the carriage way , and choke up the narrow entrance to the street , which widen considerably at the inner end . 

 Madame Seraphine 's house be at the end , a narrow house , with tall old - fashioned window curtain with amber satin . it be a small , dark house , and exhale occasional odour of garlic and main sewer ; but the staircase be a gem in old oak , and the furniture in the triple telescopic drawing room , dwindle to a closet at the end , be genuine Louis Seize . 

 Seraphine herself be the only shabby thing in the house a wizene little woman , with a wicked old jewish face , and one shoulder high than the other , dress in a shiny black moire gown , year after moire have be explode , and with a rag of old lace upon her sleek black hair raven black hair , and the only good thing about her appearance .

 nothing be say about price ; but Lesbia have a vague idea that some of the thing would be rather expensive , and she venture to ask Lady Kirkbank if she be not order too many gown .

' your dear grandmother be as rich as Croesus , and she be generosity itself ; and how should I ever forgive myself if I allow you to appear in society in an inadequate style . you have to take a high place , the very high place , Lesbia ; and you must be dress in accordance with that position .  ' I be sure you would not like the effect without diamond , ' say the jeweller . ' your amethyst be very fine , but they be dark and heavy in tone , and want a good deal of lighting - up , especially for the present fashion of half - light room . if you will allow I to use my own discretion , and mix in a few brilliant , I shall be able to produce a really artistic parure ; otherwise I would not recommend you to touch they . the present setting be clumsy and inelegant ; but I really do not know that I could improve upon it , without an admixture of brilliant .  and now Lesbia 's campaign begin in real earnest a life of pleasure , a life of utter selfishness and self - indulgence , which would go far to pervert the strong mind , tarnish the pure nature . to dress and be admire that be what Lesbia 's life mean from morning till night . she have no high or nobler aim . even on Sunday morning at the fashionable church , where the woman sit on one side of the nave and the man on the other , where divinest music be as a pair of wing , on which the enraptured soul fly heavenward even here Lesbia think more of her bonnet and glove the chic or non - chic of her whole costume , than of the service . she might kneel gracefully , with her bent head , just reveal the ivory whiteness of a lovely throat , between the edge of her lace frille and the flower in her bonnet . she might look the fairest image of devotion ; but how could a woman pray whose heart be a milliner 's shop , whose high ambition be to be pretty and well dress than other woman ? 

 the season be six week old . it be Ascot week , the crown glory of the year , and Lesbia and her chaperon have secure ticket for the Royal enclosure or it may be say rather that Lesbia have secure they for the Master of the Royal Buckhounds might have omit poor old Lady Kirkbank 's familiar name from his list if it have not be for that lovely girl who go everywhere under the veteran 's wing .

 Lady Kirkbank be in no haste to marry her protťgťe nay , it be much more to her interest that Lesbia should remain single for three or four season , and that she , Lady Kirkbank , might have the advantage of close association with the young beauty , and the privilege of spend Lady Maulevrier 's money . but she would have like to be able to inform Lesbia 's grandmother of some tremendous conquest the subjugation of a worthy victim . this herd of nobody young son with courtesy title and empty pocket , ruin Guardsmen , briefless barrister what be the use of tell Lady Maulevrier about such barren victory ? Lady Kirkbank therefore content herself with expatiate upon Lesbia 's triumph in a general way : how graciously the Princess speak to she and about she ; how she have be ask to sit on the dai at the ball at Marlborough House , and have dance in the Royal quadrille . 

 ' has Lesbia happen to meet Lord Hartfield ? ' Lady Maulevrier ask , incidentally , in one of her letter . 

 no . Lord Hartfield be in London , for he have make a great speech in the Lords on a question of vital interest ; but he be not go into society , or at any rate into society of a frivolous kind . he have give himself up to politic , as so many young man do nowadays , which be altogether horrid of they . his name have appear in the list of guest at one or two cabinet dinner ; but the world of polo match and afternoon tea , dance and drum , private theatrical , and Orleans House suppers , know he not . as a competitor on the fashionable race - course , Lord Hartfield be , in common parlance , out of the running . 

 and now on this glorious June day , this Thursday of Thursdays , the Ascot Cup day , for the first time since Lesbia 's dťbut , Lady Kirkbank have occasion to smile upon an admirer whose pretension be worthy of the high consideration . 

 Mr. Smithson , of Park Lane , and Rood Hall , near Henley , and Formosa , Cowes , and Le Bouge , Deauville , and a good many other place too numerous to mention , be repute to be one of the rich commoner in England . he be a man of that uncertain period of life which enemy call middle age , and friend call youth . that he would never see a five - and - thirtieth birthday again be certain ; but whether he have pass the Rubicon of forty be open to doubt . it be possible that he be enjoy those few golden year between thirty - five and forty , which for the wealthy bachelor constitute verily the prime and summer - tide of life . Wisdom have come , experience have be buy , taste have be cultivate , the man have educate himself to the uttermost in the great school of daily life . he know his world thoroughly , whatever that world be , and he know how to enjoy every gift and every advantage which Providence have bestow upon he . 

 Mr. Smithson be a great authority on the Stock Exchange , though he have cease for the last three or four year to frequent the ' House , ' or to be see in the purlieus of Throgmorton Street . indeed he have an air of hardly know his way to the City , of be acquaint with that part of London only by hearsay . he complain that his horse shy at pass Temple Bar . and yet a few year ago Mr. Smithson 's city operation have be on a very extensive scale : it be in the rise and fall of commodity rather than of stock and share that Horace Smithson have make his money . he have exercise occult influence upon the trade of the great city , of the world itself , whereof that city be in a manner the keystone . Iron have rise or fall at his beck . at the breath of his nostril cochineal have go up in the market at an almost magical rate , as if the whole civilised world have become suddenly intent upon dye its garment red , nay , as if even the naked savage of the Gold Coast and the tribe of Central Africa be bent on stain their dusky skin with the body of the female coccus . 

 favour by a hint from Smithson , his particular friend follow his lead , and rush into the market to buy all the cochineal that could be have ; to buy at any price , since the market be rise hourly . and then , all in a moment , as the sky cloud over on a summer day , there come a dulness in the cochineal market , and the female coccus be be sell at an enormous sacrifice . and anon it leak out that Mr. Smithson have grow tired of cochineal , and have be sell for the last week or two ; and it be noise abroad that this rise and fall in cocci have bring Mr. Smithson seventy thousand pound . 

 Mr. Smithson be say to have commence life in a very humble capacity . there be some who declare he be the very youth who stoop to pick up a pin in a parisian banker 's courtyard , after his service as clerk have just be reject by the firm , and who be thereupon recognise as a youth worthy of favour and take into the banker 's office . but this touching incident of the pin be too ancient a tradition to fit Mr. Smithson , still under forty . 

 some there be who remember he eighteen year ago as an adventurer in the great wilderness of London , penniless , friendless , a Jack - of - all - trade , live as the bird of the air live , and with as little certainty of future maintenance . and then Mr. Smithson disappear for a space he go under , as his friend call it ; to re - appear fifteen year later as Smithson the millionaire . he have be in Peru , Mexico , California . he have trade in hide , in diamond , in silver , in stock and share . and now he be the great Smithson , whose voice be the voice of an oracle , who be suppose to be able to make the fortune of other man by a word , or a wink , a nod , or a little look across the crowd , and whom all the man and woman in London society short of that exclusive circle which do not open its rank to smithson be ready to cherish and admire . 

 Mr. Smithson have be in Petersburg , Paris , Vienna , all over civilised Europe during the last five week , whether on business or on pleasure bent , nobody know . he affect to be an elegant idler ; but it be say by the initiate that wherever Smithson go the market rise or fall , and hide , iron , copper , or tin , feel the influence of his presence . 

 he come back to London in time for the Cup Day , and in time to fall desperately in love with Lesbia , whom he meet for the first time in the Royal enclosure . 

 she be dress in white , purest ivory white , from top to toe radiant , dazzle , under an immense sunshade fringe with creamy marabout . her complexion untouche by Seraphine her dark and glossy hair , her large violet eye , luminous , dark almost to blackness , be all set off and accentuate by the absence of colour in her costume . even the cluster of exotic on her shoulder be of the same pure tint , gardenia and lily of the valley . 

 Mr. Smithson be formally present to the new beauty , and receive with a cool contempt which rivet his chain . he be so accustomed to be run after by woman , that it be a new sensation to meet one who be not in the least impressed by his superior merit . 

 ' I do not   suppose the girl know who I be , ' he say to himself , for although he have a very good idea of his intrinsic worth , he know that his wealth rank first among his merit . 

 but on after occasion when Lesbia have be tell all that could be tell to the advantage of Mr. Smithson , she accept his homage with the same indifference , and treat he with less favour than she accord to the ruin guardsmen and young son who be die for she . 

 CHAPTER XXVI . 

 ' proud can I never be of what I hate . ' 


 it be a Saturday afternoon , and even in that great world which have no occupation in life except to amuse itself , whose day be all holiday , there be a sort of exceptional flavour , a kind of extra excitement on Saturday afternoon , distinguish by polo match at Hurlingham , just as Saturday evening be by the production of new play at fashionable theatre . there be a great military polo match for this particular Saturday Lancers against Dragoons . it be a lovely June afternoon , and Hurlingham would be at its good . the cool greensward , the branching tree , the flow river , would afford an unspeakable relief after the block of carriage in Bond Street and the heated air of London , where even the park feel baked and arid ; and to Hurlingham Lady Kirkbank drive directly after luncheon . 

 Lesbia lean back in the barouche listen calmly , while her chaperon expatiate upon the wealth and possession of Horace Smithson . it be now ten day since the meeting at Ascot , and Mr. Smithson have contrive to see a great deal of Lesbia in that short time . he be invite almost everywhere , and he have haunt she at afternoon and evening party ; he have sup in Arlington Street after the opera ; he have play card with Lesbia , and have enjoy the felicity of win her money . his admiration be obvious , and there be a seriousness in his manner of pursue she which show that , in Lady Kirkbank 's unromantic phraseology , ' the man mean business . ' 

 ' Smithson be catch at last , and I be glad of it , ' say Georgie . 

 ' the creature be an abominable flirt , and have break more heart than any man in London . he be all but the death of one of the dear girl I know . ' 

 ' Mr. Smithson break heart ! ' exclaim Lesbia , languidly . ' I should not have think that be in his line . Mr. Smithson be not an Adonis , nor be his manner particularly fascinating . ' 

 ' my child how fresh you be ! do you suppose it be the handsome man or the fascinating man for whom woman break their heart in society ? it be the rich man they all want to marry man like Smithson , who can give they diamond , and yacht , and a hunting stud , and half a dozen fine house . those be the prize the blue ribbon of the matrimonial race - course man like Smithson , who pretend to admire all the pretty woman , who dangle , and dangle , and keep off other offer , and give ten guinea bouquet , and then at the end of the season be off to Hombourg or the Scotch moor , without a word . do you think that kind of treatment be not hard enough to break a penniless girl 's heart ? she see the golden prize within her grasp , as she believe ; she think that she and poverty have part company for ever ; she imagine herself mistress of town house and country house , yacht and stable ; and then one fine morning the gentleman be off and away ! do not you think that be enough to break a girl 's heart ? ' 

 ' I can imagine that a girl steep to the lip in poverty might be willing to marry Mr. Smithson 's house and yacht , ' answer Lesbia , in her low sweet voice , with a faint sneer even amidst the sweetness , ' but , I think it must have be a happy release for any one to be let off the sacrifice at the last moment . ' 

 ' Poor Belle Trinder do not think so . ' 

 ' who be Belle Trinder ? ' 

 ' an Essex parson 's daughter whom I take under my wing five year ago a splendid girl , large and fair , and just a trifle coarse not to be speak of in the same day with you , dearest ; but still a decidedly handsome creature . and she take remarkably well . she be a very lively girl , " never run mute , " Sir George use to say . Sir George be very fond of she . she amuse he , poor girl , with her rather brainless rattle . ' 

 ' and Mr. Smithson admire she ? ' 

 ' follow she about everywhere , send her whole flower garden in the way of bouquet and japanese basket , and floral parure for her gown , and opera box and concert ticket . their name be always couple . People use to call they Bel and the Dragon . the poor child make up her mind she be to be Mrs. Smithson . she use to talk of what she would do for her own people the poor old father , bury alive in a damp parsonage , and struggle every winter with chronic bronchitis ; the four young sister pin in dulness and penury ; the mother who hardly know what it be to rest from the continual worry of daily life . ' 

 ' poor thing ! ' sigh Lesbia , gaze admiringly at the handle of her last new sunshade . 

 ' Belle use to talk of what she would do for they all , ' pursue Lady Kirkbank . ' Father should go every year to the villa at Monte Carlo ; mother and the girl should have a month in Park Lane every season , and their autumn holiday at one of Mr. Smithson 's country house . I know the world well enough to be sure that this kind of thing would never answer with a man like Smithson . it be only one man in a thousand the modern Arthur , the modern Quixote who will marry a whole family . I tell Belle as much , but she laugh . she feel so secure of her power over the man . " he will do anything I ask he , " she say . ' 

 ' Miss Trinder must be an extraordinary young person , ' observe Lesbia , scornfully . ' the man have not propose , have he ? ' 

 ' no ; the actual proposal hung fire , but Belle think it be a settle thing all the same . everybody talk to she as if she be engage to Smithson , and those poor , ignorant vicarage girl use to write her long letter of congratulation , envy her good fortune , speculate about what she would do when she be marry . the girl be too open and candid for London society talk too much , " give the view before she be sure of her fox , " Sir George say . all this silly talk come to Smithson 's ear , and one morning we read in the post that Mr. Smithson have start the day before for Algiers , where he be to stay at the house of the English Consul , and hunt lion . we wait all day , hop for some letter of explanation , some friendly farewell which would mean a revoir . but there be nothing , and then poor Belle give way altogether . she shut herself up in her room , and go out of one hysterical fit into another . I never hear a girl sob so terribly . she be not fit to be see for a week , and then she go home to her father 's parsonage in the flat swampy country on the border of Suffolk , and eat her heart , as Byron call it . and the bad of it be that she have no actual justification for consider herself jilt . she have talk , and other people have talk , and among they they have settle the business . but Smithson have say hardly anything . he have only flirt to his heart 's content , and have spend a few hundred upon flower , glove , fan , and opera ticket , which perhaps would not have be accept by a girl with a strong sense of her own dignity . ' 

 ' I should think not , indeed , ' interject Lesbia . 

 ' but which poor Belle be only too delighted to get . ' 

 ' Miss Trinder must be very bad style , ' say Lesbia , with languid scorn , ' and Mr. Smithson be an execrable person . do she die ? ' 

 ' no , my dear , she be alive poor soul ! ' 

 ' you say she break her heart . ' 

 ' " the heart may break , yet brokenly live on , " ' quote Lady Kirkbank . ' the disappointed young woman do not   all die . they take to district visiting , or rational dressing , or china painting , or an ambulance brigade . the lucky one marry well - to - do widower with large family , and so slip into a comfortable groove by the time they be five - and - thirty . Poor Belle be still single , still bury in the damp parsonage , where she paint plate and teacup , and wear out my old gown , just as she be wear out her own life , poor creature ! ' 

 ' the idea of any one want to marry Mr. Smithson , ' say Lesbia . ' it seem too dreadful . ' 

 ' a case of real destitution , you think . wait till you have see Smithson 's house in Park Lane his team , his yacht , his orchid house in Berkshire . ' 

 Lesbia sigh . her knowledge of London society be only seven week old ; and yet already the day of disenchantment have begin ! she be have her eye open to the stern reality of life . a year ago when her appearance in the great world be still only a dream of the future , she have picture to herself the crowd of suitor who would come to woo , and she have resolve to choose the worthy . 

 what would he be like , that worthiest among the wooer , that King Arthur among her knight ? 

 first and foremost , he would be of rank high than she own a duke , a marquis , or one of the first and old among earl . title and lofty lineage be indispensable . it would be a fall , a failure , a disappointment , be she to marry a commoner , however distinguish . 

 the worthy one must be noble , therefore , and of the old nobility . he must be young , handsome , intellectual . he must stand out from among his peer by his gift of mind and person . he must have win distinction in the arena of politic or diplomacy , arm or letter . he must be ' somebody . ' 

 she have be seven week in society , and this modern Arthur have not appear . so far as she have be able to discover , there be no such person . the duke and marquis be mostly man of advanced year . the young unmarried nobility be give over to sport , play , and foolishness . she have hear of only one man who at all correspond with her ideal , and he be Lord Hartfield . but Lord Hartfield have give himself up to politic , and be no doubt a prig . Lady Kirkbank speak of he with contempt , as an intolerable person . but then Lord Hartfield be not in Lady Kirkbank 's set . he belong to that serious circle to which Lady Kirkbank 's house appear about as reputable a place of gathering as a booth on a race - course . 

 and now Lady Kirkbank tell Lesbia that this Mr. Smithson , a nobody with a great fortune , be a man whose address she , the sister of Lord Maulevrier , ought to welcome . Mr. Smithson , who claim to be a lineal descendant of that Sir Michael Carrington , standard - bearer to Coeur de Lion in the Holy Land , whose descendant change their name to Smith during the Wars of the Roses . Mr. Smithson bodily proclaim himself a scion of this good old county family , and bore on his plate and his coach panel the elephant 's head and the three demi - griffin of the Hertfordshire Smiths , who only smile and shrug their shoulder when they be compliment upon the splendid surrounding of their cousin . who could tell ? some lateral branch of the standard - bearer 's family tree might have bear this illustrious twig . 

 Lady Kirkbank and all Lady Kirkbank 's friend seem to have conspire to teach Lesbia Haselden one lesson , and that lesson mean that money be the first prize in the great game of life . money rank before everything before title , before noble lineage , genius , fame , beauty , courage , honour . money be Alpha and Omega , the beginning and the end . Mr. Smithson , whose antecedent be as cloudy as those of Aphrodite , be a great man than a peer whose broad acre only bring he two per cent . , or half of whose farm be tenantless , and his field grow cockle instead of barley . 

 yes , one by one , Lady Lesbia 's illusion be reave from she . a year ago she have fancy beauty all - powerful , a gift which must ensure to its possessor dominion over all the kingdom of the earth . rank , intellect , fame would bow down before that magical diadem . and , behold , she have be shin upon London society for seven week , and only empty head and empty pocket have bow down the frivolous , the ineligible , and Mr. Smithson . 

 another illusion which have be dispel be Lesbia 's comfortable idea of her own expectation . her grandmother have tell she that she might take rank among heiress ; and she have hold herself accordingly , deem that her place be among the wealthy . and now , since Mr. Smithson 's appearance upon the scene , Lady Kirkbank have inform her young friend with noble candour that Lady Maulevrier 's fortune , however large it might seem at Grasmere , would be a poor thing in London ; and that Lady Maulevrier 's idea about money be as old - fashioned as her notion about moral . 

 ' life be about six time as expensive as it be in your grandmother 's time . ' say Lady Kirkbank , as the carriage roll softly along the shabby road between Knightsbridge and Fulham . ' it be the pace that kill . Society , which use to jog along comfortably , like the old Brighton stage , at ten mile an hour , now go as fast as the Brighton express . in my mother 's time poor Lord Byron be hold up to the execration of respectable people as the type of cynical profligacy ; in my own time people talk about Lord Waterford ; but , my dear , the young man now be all Byrons and Waterfords , without the genius of the one or the generosity of the other . we be all go at steeplechase rate . social success without money be impossible . the rich Americans , the successful Jews , will crowd we out unless we keep pace with they . ah , Lesbia , my dear girl , there would be a great future before you if you could only make up your mind to accept Mr. Smithson . ' 

 ' how do you know that he mean to propose to I ? ' ask Lesbia , mockingly . ' perhaps he be only go to behave as he do to Miss Trinder . ' 

 ' Lady Lesbia Haselden be a very different person from a country parson 's daughter , ' answer her chaperon ; ' Smithson tell I all about it afterwards . he be really take with Belle 's fine figure and good complexion ; but one of her particular friend tell he of her foolish talk about her sister , and how well she mean to get they married when she be Mrs. Smithson . this disgust he . he go down to Essex , reconnoiter the parsonage , see one of the sister hang out cuff and collar in the orchard another feed the fowl both in shabby gown and country - make boot ; one of they with red hair and freckle . the mother be bargaining for fish with a hawker at the kitchen door . and these be the people he be expect to import into Park Lane , under ceiling paint by Leighton . these be the people he be to exhibit on board his yacht , to cart about on his drag . " I have half make up my mind to marry the girl , but I would soon have hang myself than marry her mother and sister so I take the first train for Dover , en route for Algiers , " say Smithson , and upon my word I could hardly blame the man , ' conclude Lady Kirkbank . 

 they be drive up the narrow avenue to the gate of Hurlingham by this time . Lesbia shake out her frock and look at her glove , tan - colour mousquetaire , reach up to the elbow , and embroider to match her frock . 

 to - day she be a study in brown and gold . Brown satin petticoat embroider with marsh marigold ; little bronze shoe , with marsh marigold tie on the latchet ; brown stocking with marsh marigold clock ; tunic brown foulard smother with quilling of soft brown lace ; Princess bonnet of brown straw , with a wreath of marsh marigold and a neat little buckle of brown diamond ; parasol brown satin , with an immense bunch of marsh marigold on the top ; fan to match parasol . 

 the seat in front of the field be nearly all full when Lady Kirkbank and Lesbia leave their carriage ; but their interest have be protect by a gentleman who have turn down two chair and sit between they on guard . this be Mr. Smithson . 

 ' I have be sit here for an hour keep your chair , ' he say , as he rise to greet they . ' you have no idea what work I have have , and how ferociously all the woman have look at I . ' 

 the match be go on . the Lancers be scuffle for the ball , and afford a fine display of hog - man pony and close - cropped young man in ideal boot . but Lesbia care very little about the match . she be look along the serrie rank of youth and beauty to see if anybody 's frock be smart than her own . 

 no . she could see nothing she like so well as her brown satin and buttercup . she sit down in a perfectly content frame of mind , please with herself and with Seraphine please even with Mr. Smithson , who have show himself devote by his patient attendance upon the empty chair . 

 after the match be over the two lady and their attendant stroll about the garden . other man come and flutter round Lesbia , and woman and girl exchange endear smile and pretty little word of greet with she , and envy she the brown frock and buttercup and Mr. Smithson at her chariot wheel . and then they go to the lawn in front of the club - house , which be so crowded that even Mr. Smithson find it difficult to get a tea - table , and would hardly have succeed so soon as he do if it have not be for the assistance of a couple of Lesbia 's devoted Guardsmen , who run to and fro and badger the waiter . 

 after much skirmishing they be seat at a rustic table , the blue river gleaming and glance in the distance , the good old tree spread their broad shadow over the grass , the company crowd and chatter and laugh an animate picture of pretty face , smart gown , big parasol , japanese fan . 

 Lesbia pour out the tea with the prettiest air of domesticity . 

 ' can you really pour out tea ? ' gasp a callow lieutenant , gaze upon she with goggling , enraptured eye . ' I do not think you could do anything so earthly . '

 ' I I beg your pardon how many ? ' murmur the youth , who have lose himself in gaze , and no long understand plain English . 

 Mr. Smithson frown at the intruder , and contrive to absorb Lesbia 's attention for the rest of the afternoon . he have a good deal more to say for himself than her military admirer , and be altogether more amusing . he have a little cynical air which Lesbia 's recent education have teach she to enjoy . he depreciate all her female friend abuse their gown and bonnet , and give she to understand , between the line , as it be , that she be the only woman in London worth think about . 

 she look at he curiously , wonder how Belle Trinder have be able to resign herself to the idea of marry he . 

 he be not absolutely bad look but he be in all thing unlike a girl 's ideal lover . he be short and stout , with a pale complexion , and sink faded eye , as of a man who have spend the great part of his life by candle light , and have pore much over ledger and bank book , share list and prospectus . he dress well , or allow himself to be dress by the most correct of tailor the Prince 's tailor but he never attempt to lead the fashion in his garment . he have no originality . such sublime flight as that of the man who revive corduroy , or of that daring genius who resuscitate the half - forget inverness coat , be unknown to he . he could only follow the lead of the high . he have small foot , of which he be intensely proud , podgy white hand on which he wear the most exquisite ring . he change his ring every day , like a Roman Emperor ; be report to have summer and winter ring onyx and the cool look intaglio set in filagree for warm weather fiery ruby and diamond in massive band of dull gold for winter . he be say to devote half - an - hour every morning to the treatment of his nail , which be perfect . all the inkstain of his youth have be obliterate , and those nail which have once be bite to the quick during the throe of financial study be now thing of beauty . 

 Lady Lesbia survey Mr. Smithson critically , and shudder at the thought that this person be the good substitute which the season have yet offer she for her ideal knight . she think of John Hammond , the tall , strong figure , straight and square ; the head so proudly carry on a neck which would have grace a greek arena . the straight , clearly - cut feature , the flash eye , bright with youth and hope and the promise of all good thing . yes , there be indeed a man a man in all the nobility of manhood , as God make he , an Adam before the Fall . 

 ah , if John Hammond have only possess a quarter of Mr. Smithson 's wealth how gladly would Lesbia have defy the world and marry he . but to defy the world upon nothing a year be out of the question . 

 ' why do not   he go on the Stock Exchange and make his fortune ? ' think Lesbia , pettishly , ' instead of talk vaguely about politic and literature . ' 

 she feel angry with she reject lover for have come to her empty - handed . she have see no man in London who be , or who seem to she , his equal . and yet she do not repent of have reject he . the more she know of the world and the more she know of herself the more deeply be she convince that poverty be an evil thing , and that she be not the right kind of person to endure it . 

 she be inwardly make these comparison as they stroll back to the carriage , while Mr. Smithson and Lady Kirkbank talk confidentially at her side . 

 ' do you know that Lady Kirkbank have promise and vow three thing for you ? ' say Mr. Smithson . 

 ' indeed ! I think I be past the age at which one can be compromise by other people 's promise . pray what be those three thing ? ' 

 ' first , that you will come to breakfast in Park Lane with Lady Kirkbank next Wednesday morning . I say Wednesday because that will give I time to ask some nice people to meet you ; secondly , that you will honour I by occupy my box at the Lyceum some evening next week ; and thirdly , that you will allow I to drive you down to the Orleans for supper after the play . the drive only take an hour , and the moonlight night be delicious at this time of the year . ' 

 ' I be in Lady Kirkbank 's hand , ' answer Lesbia , laugh . ' I be her good , her chattel ; she take I wherever she like . ' 

 ' but would you refuse to do I this honour if you be a free agent ? ' 

 ' I can not   tell . I hardly know what it be to be a free agent . at Grasmere I do whatever my grandmother tell I ; in London I obey Lady Kirkbank . I be transfer from one master to another . why should we breakfast in Park Lane instead of in Arlington Street ? what be the use of cross Piccadilly to eat our breakfast ? ' 

 this be a cool - head style of treatment to which Mr. Smithson be not accustom , and which charm he accordingly . young woman usually throw themselves at his head , as it be ; but here be a girl who talk to he as indifferently as if he be a tradesman offer his ware . 

 ' what a dreadfully practical person you be ! ' he exclaim . ' what be the use of cross Piccadilly ? well , in the first place , you will make I ineffably happy . but perhaps that do not   count . in the second place , I shall be able to show you some rather good picture of the french school ' 

 ' I hate the french school ! ' interject Lesbia . ' Tricky , flashy , chalky , shallow , smell of the footlight and the studio . ' 

 ' well , sink the picture . you will meet some very charming people , belong to that artist world which be not to be meet everywhere . '
