 ' my pet , if you have make it the study of your life how to treat that man you could not do it well , ' say Lady Kirkbank , when they be drive along the dusty road between dusty hedge and dusty tree , past that last remnant of country which be daily be debase into London . ' upon my word , Lesbia , I begin to think you must be a genius . ' 

 ' do you see any gown you like well than mine ? ' ask Lesbia , recline reposefully , with her little bronze shoe upon the opposite cushion . 

 ' not one Seraphine have surpass herself . ' 

 ' you be always say that . one would suppose you be a sleeping partner in the firm . but I really think this brown and buttercup be rather nice . I see that odious american girl just now Miss Miss Milwaukee , that mop - stick girl people rave about at Cannes . she be in pale blue and cream colour , a milk and water mixture , and look positively plain . ' 

 CHAPTER XXVII . 

 LESBIA CROSSES PICCADILLY . 


 Lady Kirkbank and Lady Lesbia drive across Piccadilly at eleven o'clock on Wednesday morning to breakfast with Mr. Smithson , and although Lesbia have question whether it be worth while cross Piccadilly to eat one 's breakfast , she have subsequently consider it worth while order a new gown from Seraphine for the occasion ; or , it may be , rather that the breakfast make a plausible excuse for a new gown , the pleasure of ordering which be one of those joy of a London life that have not yet lose their savour . 

 the gown , devise especially for the early morning , be simplicity itself rusticity , even . it be a Dresden shepherdess gown , make of a soft flower stuff , with rose and forget - I - not on a creamy ground . there be a great deal of creamy lace , and innumerable yard of pale azure and palest rose ribbon in the confection , and there be a coquettish little hat , the regular Dresden hat , with a wreath of rosebuds . 

 ' Dresden china incarnate ! ' exclaim Smithson , as he welcome Lady Lesbia on the threshold of his marble hall , under the glass marquise which shelter arrival at his door . ' why do you make yourself so lovely ? I shall want to keep you in one of my Louis Seize cabinet , with the rest of my Dresden ! ' 

 Lady Kirkbank have consider the occasion suitable for one of her favourite cotton frock and rustic hat a Leghorn hat , with cluster of dog - rose and honeysuckle , and a trail of the same hedge - flower to fasten her muslin fichu . 

 Mr. Smithson 's house in Park Lane be simply perfect . it be wonderful what good use a parvenu can make of his money nowadays , and how rarely he disgrace himself by any marked offence against good taste . there be so many people at hand to teach the parvenu how to furnish his house , or how to choose his stud . if he go wrong it must be by sheer perversity , an arrogant insistence upon be govern by his own ignorant inclination . 

 Mr. Smithson be too good a tactician to go wrong in this way . he have take the trouble to study the market before he go out to buy his good . he know that taste and knowledge be to be buy just as easily as chair and table , and he go to the right shop . he employ a clever Scotchman , an artist in domestic furniture , to plan his house , and make drawing for the decoration and furniture of every room and for six month he give himself up to the task of furnish . 

 money be spend like water . painter , decorator , cabinet - maker have a merry time of it . Royal Academicians be impress into the service by large offer , and the final result of Mr. MacWalter 's taste and Mr. Smithson 's bullion be a palace in the style of the italian Renaissance , frescoed ceiling , paint panel , a staircase of sculptured marble , as beautiful as a dream , a conservatory as exquisite as a jewel casket by Benvenuto Cellini , a picture gallery which be the admiration of all London , and of the enlightened foreigner , and of the inquire American . this be the house which Lesbia have be bring to see , and through which she walk with the calmly critical air of a person who have see so many palace that one more or less could make no difference . 

 in vain do Mr. Smithson peruse her countenance in the hope of see that she be impress by the splendour of his surrounding , and by the power of the man who command such splendour . Lesbia be as cold as the italian sculptor 's Reading Girl in an alcove of Mr. Smithson 's picture gallery ; and the stockbroker feel very much as Aladdin might have do if the fair Badroulbadour have show herself indifferent to the hall of the jewel window , in that magical palace which spring into be in a single night . 

 Lesbia have be impress by that story of poor Belle Trinder and by Lady Kirkbank 's broad assertion that half the young woman in London be run after Mr. Smithson ; and she have make up her mind to treat the man with supreme scorn . she do not want his house or his yacht . nothing could induce she to marry such a man , she tell herself ; but her vanity feed upon the idea of his subjugation , and her pride be gratify by the sense of her power over he . 

 the guest be few and choice . there be Mr. Meander , the poet , one of the lead light in that new sect which pride itself upon the cultivation of abstract beauty , and occasionally touch the verge of concrete ugliness . there be a newspaper man the editor of a fashionable journal and a middle - aged man of letter , playwright , critic , humourist , a man whose society be in demand everywhere , and who say sharp thing with the most supreme good - nature . the only lady whose society Mr. Smithson have deem worthy the occasion be a fashionable actress , with her young sister , the young a pretty copy of the elder , both dress picturesquely in flow cashmere gown of faint sea - green , with old lace fichu , leghorn hat , and a general limpness and simplicity of style which suit their cast of feature and delicate colouring . Lesbia wonder to see how good an effect could be produce by a costume which could have cost so little . Mr. Nightshade , the famous tragedian , have be also ask to grace the feast , but the early hour make the invitation a mockery . it be not to be suppose that a man who go to bed at daybreak would get up again before the sun be in the zenith , for the sake of Mr. Smithson 's society , or Mr. Smithson 's Strasbourg pie , for the manufacture whereof a particular breed of geese be suppose to be set apart , like sacred bird in Egypt , while a particular vineyard in the Gironde be suppose to be devote wholly and solely to the production of Mr. Smithson 's claret . it be a cabinet wine , like those rare vintage of the Rhineland which be reserve exclusively for german prince . 

 Breakfast be serve in Mr. Smithson 's small dining - room there be three apartment give up to feasting , begin with a spacious banqueting - room for great dinner , and dwindle down to this snuggery , which hold about a dozen comfortably , with ample room and verge enough for the attendant . the wall be old gold silk , the curtain a tawny velvet of deep tone , the cabinet and buffet of dark italian walnut , inlay with lapis - lazuli and amber . the fireplace be a masterpiece of cabinet work , with high narrow shelf , and curious recess hold priceless jar of oriental enamel . the deep hearth be fill with arum lily and azalia , like a font at Easter . 

 Lady Kirkbank , who pretend to adore genius , be affectionately effusive to Miss Fitzherbert , the popular actress , but she rather ignore the sister . Lesbia be less cordial , and be not enchant at find that Miss Fitzherbert shine and sparkle at the breakfast table by the gaiety of her spirit and the brightness of her conversation . there be something frank and joyous , almost to childishness , in the actress 's manner , which be full of fascination ; and Lesbia feel herself at a disadvantage almost for the first time since she have be in London . 

 the editor , the wit , the poet , the actress , have a language of their own ; and Lesbia feel herself out in the cold , unable to catch the ball as it glance past she , not quick enough to follow the wit that evoke those ripple of silvery laughter from the two fair - haired , pale - face girl in sea - green cashmere . she feel as an Englishman may feel who have make himself master of academical French , and who take up one of Zola 's novel , or go into artistic society , and find that there be another French , a complete and copious language , of which he know not a word . 

 Lesbia begin to think that she have a great deal to learn . she begin to wonder even whether , in the event of she have make rather too free use of Lady Maulevrier 's carte blanche , it might not be well to make a new departure in the art of dressing , and to wear untrimmed cashmere gown , and rag of limp lace . 

 after breakfast they all go to look at Mr. Smithson 's picture gallery . his picture be , as he have tell Lesbia , chiefly of the french school , and there may have be a remote period say , in the time of good Queen Charlotte when such picture would hardly have be exhibit to young lady . his picture be Mr. Smithson 's own unaided choice . here the individual taste of the man stand reveal . 

 there be two or three Geromes ; and in the place of honour at the end of the gallery there be a grand Delaroche , Anne Boleyn 's last letter to the king , the hapless girl - queen sit at a table in her gloomy cell in the Tower , a shaft of golden light from the narrow window streaming on the fair , disorder hair , the face bleach with unutterable woe , a sublime image of despair and self - abandonment . 

 the large picture be historical , classic , grand ; but the small picture the lively little bit of colour dot in here and there be of that new school which Mr. Smithson affect . they be of that school which be call Impressionist , in which ballet dancer and jockey , burlesque actress , mask ball , and all the humour of the side scene be represent with the sublime audacity of an art which disdain finish , and rely on chic , fougue , chien , flou , ťlan , the inspiration of the moment . Lesbia blush as she look at the ballet girl , the masker in their scanty raiment , the demi - mondaine loll out of their opera box , and half out of their gown , with false smile and frizzled hair . and then there come the work of that other school which lavish the finish of a Meissonier on the most meretricious composition . a woman in a velvet gown warm her dainty little foot on a gild fender , in a boudoir all aglow with colour and lamplight ; a cavalier in satin raiment buckle his sword - belt before a venetian mirror ; a pair of lover kiss in a sunlit corridor ; a girl in a hansom cab ; a milliner 's shop ; and so on , and so on . 

 then come the classical subject of the last new school . weak imitation of Alma Tadema . Nero admire his mother 's corpse ; Claudius interrupt Messalina 's marriage with her lover Silus ; Clodius disguise among the woman of Casar 's household ; Pyrrha 's grotto . Lady Kirkbank expatiate upon all the picture , and generally make unlucky guess at the subject of they . classical literature be not her strong point . 

 Mr. Meander , the poet , discover that all the beautiful head be like Miss Fitzherbert . ' it be the same line , ' he exclaim , ' the line of lily and flowing water the gracious ineffable upward return ripple of the true retroussť nose , the divine flou , the loveliness which have lie dormant for century nay , be at one period of debase art scorn and trample under foot by the porcine multitude , as akin to the pug and the turn - up , until discover and enshrine on the altar of the beautiful by the Boticelli Revivalists . ' 

 Miss Fitzherbert simper , and accept these remark as mere statement of obvious fact . she be accustom to hear of Boticelli and the early italian painter in connection with her own charm of face and figure . 

 Lesbia , whose faultless feature be of the aquiline type , regard the bard 's rhapsody as insufferable twaddle , and begin to think Mr. Smithson almost a wit when he make fun of the bard .

 ' here if you like , be colour and beauty , ' he murmur , caress one of the little cup with the roseate tip of his supple finger . ' these , dear Smithson , be worth all the rest of your collection ; worth vanload of your cloisonnť enamel , your dragon - jar in blood - colour and blue . this cloudy indefinable substance , not crudely transparent nor yet distinctly opaque , a something which touch the boundary line of two world the real and the ideal . and then the colour ! great heaven , can anything be lovely than this shadowy tint which be neither yellow nor green ; faint , faint as the dawn of newly - awakened day ? after the siege of blood - bedabble Delhi , Baron Rothschild send a special agent to India to buy he a little jade tea - pot which have be the joy of Eastern king . only a tea - pot . yet Rothschild deem it worth a voyage from England to India . that be what the love of the beautiful mean , in Jew or Gentile , ' conclude the bard , smile on the company , as they gather round the florentine table on which the jade specimen be set out , Lady Kirkbank look at the little cup and basin as if she think they be go to do something , after all this fuss have be make about they . it seem hardly credible that any reasonable being could have give thirty guineas for one of those bit of greenish - yellow clouded glass , unless the thing have some peculiar property of expansion or contraction . 

 after this breakfast in Park Lane Lady Lesbia and her admirer meet daily . he go to all her party ; he sit out waltz with she , in conservatory , and on staircase ; for Horace Smithson be much too shrewd a man to enter himself in the race for dance man , handicap by his forty year and his fourteen stone . he contrive to amuse Lesbia by his conversation , which be essentially mundane , depreciate people whom all the rest of the world admire , or pretend to admire , tell she of the secret spring by which the society she see around she be move . he be judicious in his revelation of hide evil , and careful to say nothing which should offend Lady Lesbia 's modesty ; yet he contrive in a very short time to teach she that the world in which she live be an utterly corrupt world , whose high priest be Satan ; that all lofty aspiration and noble sentiment be out of place in society ; and that the bad among the people she meet be the people who lay any claim to be well than their neighbour . 

 ' that be why I adore Lady Kirkbank , ' he say , confidentially . ' the dear soul never pretend to be any well than the rest of we . she gamble , and we all know she gamble ; she peg , and we all know she peg ; and she make rather a boast of be up to her eye in debt . no humbug about dear old georgie . ' 

 Lesbia have see enough of her chaperon by this time to know that Mr. Smithson 's description of the lady be correct , and , this be so , she suppose that the fact and trait of character which he tell she about in other people be also true . she thus adopt the Smithsonian , or fashionable - pessimist view of society in general , and resign herself to the idea that the world be a very wicked world , as well as a very pleasant world , that the wicked people be generally the pleasant , and that it do not much matter . 

 the fact that Mr. Smithson be at Lesbia Haselden 's foot be obvious to everybody .

 her social education have advance considerably since that summer day in the pine - wood , when John Hammond have woo she with passionate wooing . Mr. Smithson be a much less ardent suitor , and make his offer with the air of a man who expect to be accept .

 Horace Smithson turn pale as death , but if he be angry , he give no utterance to his angry feeling . he only ask if Lady Lesbia 's answer be final and on be tell that it be so , he dismiss the subject in the easy manner , and with a gentlemanlike placidity which very much astonish the lady .

I hate pianoforte recital , and I detest that starch old duchess , but I suppose I shall have to take you there or poor Smithson will be miserable , ' say Lady Kirkbank , watch Lesbia keenly over the top of the newspaper .

 CHAPTER XXVIII . 

 ' CLUBS , diamond , HEARTS , in WILD DISORDER see . ' 


 Lady Kirkbank have considerable difficulty in smooth Lesbia 's ruffled plumage . she do all in her power to undo the effect of her rash word declare that she have be carry away by temper she have speak she know not what word of no meaning . of course Lesbia 's grandfather have be a great man Governor of Madras ; altogether an important and celebrated person and Lady Kirkbank have mean nothing , could have mean nothing to his disparagement .

' but you know really now , dearest , any woman of the world would be provoke at your foolish refusal of that dear good Smithson . only think of that too lovely house in Park Lane , a palace in the style of the italian Renaissance such a house be in itself equivalent to a peerage and there be no doubt Smithson will be offer a peerage before he be much old . I have hear it confidently assert that when the present Ministry retire Smithson will be make a peer . you have no idea what a useful man he be , or what henchman 's service he have do the Ministry in financial matter . and then there be his villa at Deauville you do not   know Deauville a positively perfect place , the villa , I mean , build by the Duke de Morny in the golden day of the Empire and another at Cowes , and his palace in Berkshire , a manor , my love , with a glorious old Tudor manor - house ; and he have a pie a terre in Paris , in the Faubourg , a ground - floor furnish in the pompeian style , half - a - dozen room open one out of the other , and surround a small garden , with a fountain in the middle . some of the great people in Paris occupy the upper part of the house , and their room of course be splendid ; but Smithson 's ground - floor be the gem of the Faubourg . however , I suppose there be no use in talk any more ; for there be the gong for luncheon . '

I be so glad I happen to see it in Seraphine 's hand yesterday , just as she be go to send it to Lady Fonvielle , who give herself such intolerable air on the strength of a pretty face , and always want to get the primeure in bonnet and thing . '

 this be a vague way of speak about actual fact . Lady Lesbia might have speak with more certainty . her wardrobe and old - fashioned hang closet and chest of drawer in Arlington Street be cram to overflow with finery ; and then there be all the thing that she have grow tired of , or have think unbecoming , and have give away to Kibble , her own maid , or to Rilboche , who have in a great measure supersede Kibble on all important occasion ; for how could a Westmoreland girl know how to dress a young lady for London ball and drawing - room ?

 ' she be exquisite , ' Lesbia murmur softly , but the whisper question and the murmured answer , low as they be , provoke indignant look from a brace of damsel in venetian red , who shake their Toby frill with an outraged air .

 it be Sarah Bernhardt 's first season in London the never - to - be - forget season of the Comťdie FranÁaise . 

 ' I should love of all thing to be there , ' say Mr. Smithson , meekly . he have a couple of stall in the third row for the whole of the season . ' but how can I be sure that I shall not be turn Sir George out of door ? '

Lesbia be surprised when he present himself at the door of the box , after the fourth act . 

 Maulevrier and his sister have meet very seldom since the young lady 's dťbut . the young Earl do not go to many party , and the society he cultivate be chiefly masculine ; and as he neither play polo nor shoot pigeon his masculine pursuit do not bring he in his sister 's way .  ' how d'ye do , Lady Kirkbank ? how d'ye do , Lesbia ? just catch sight of you from below as the curtain be go down , ' say Maulevrier , shake hand with the lady and salute Mr. Smithson with a somewhat supercilious nod . ' rather surprised to see you and Lesbia here to - night , Lady Kirkbank . be not   the Demi - monde rather strong meat for babe , eh ? not exactly the play one would take a young lady to see . ' 

 ' why should a young lady be forbid to see a fine play , because there be some hard and bitter truth tell in it ? ' ask Lady Kirkbank . ' Lesbia see Madame d'Ange and all her sisterhood in the Park and about London every day of her life . why should not she see they on the stage , and hear their history , and understand how cruel their fate be , and learn to pity they , if she can ? I really think this play be a lesson in christian charity ; and I should like to see that Oliver man strangle , though Delaunay play the part divinely . what a voice ! what a manner ! how polished ! how perfect ! and they tell I he be go to leave the stage in a year or two . what will the world do without he ? ' 

 Maulevrier do not attempt to suggest a solution of this difficulty . he be watch Mr. Smithson as he leant against the back of Lesbia 's chair and talk to she . the two seem very familiar , laughingly discuss the play and the actor . Smithson know , or pretend to know , all about the latter . he tell Lesbia who make Croizette 's gown the upholsterer who furnish that lovely house of hers in the Bois the sum pay for her horse , her picture , her diamond . it seem to Lesbia , when she have hear all , that Croizette be a much - to - be - envy person . 

 Mr. Smithson have unpublishe bon - mot of Dumas at his finger end ; he know Daudet , and Sarcey , and Sardou , and seem to be thoroughly at home in parisian artistic society . Lesbia begin to think that he would hardly be so despicable a person as she have at first suppose . no wonder he and his wealth have turn poor Belle Trinder 's head . how could a rural vicar 's daughter , accustom to poverty , help be dazzle by such magnificence ? 

 Maulevrier stay in the box only a short time , and refuse Lady Kirkbank 's invitation to supper . she do not urge the point , as she have surprise one or two very unfriendly glance at Mr. Smithson in Maulevrier 's honest eye . she do not want an antagonistic brother to interfere with her plan . she have make up her mind to ' run ' Lesbia accord to her own idea , and any counter influence might be fatal . so , when Maulevrier say he be due at the Marlborough after the play she let he go . 

 ' I might as well be at Fellside and you in London , for anything I see of you , ' say Lesbia . 

 ' you be up to your eye in engagement , and I do not   suppose you want to see any more of I . ' Maulevrier answer , bluntly . ' but I will call to - morrow morning , if I be likely to find you at home . I have   some news for you . ' 

 ' then I will stay at home on purpose to see you . news be always delightful . be it good news , by - the - bye ? ' 

 ' very good ; at least , I think so . ' 

 ' what be it about ? ' 

 ' oh ! that be a long story , and the curtain be just go up . the news be about Mary . ' 

 ' about Mary ! ' exclaim Lesbia , elevate her eyebrow . ' what news can there possibly be about Mary ? ' 

 ' such news as there generally be about every nice jolly girl , at least once in her life . ' 

 ' you do not   mean that she be engaged to a curate ? ' 

 ' no , not to a curate . there go the curtain . " I will see you later , " as the Yankee President use to say when people bother he , and he do not   like to say no . ' 

 engage : Mary engage ! the idea of such an altogether unexpected event distract Lesbia 's mind all through the last act of the Demi - monde . she hardly know what the actor be talk about . Mary , her young sister ! Mary , a good look girl enough , but by no mean a beauty , and with manner utterly unformed . that Mary should be engage to be marry , while she , Lesbia , be still free , seem an obvious absurdity . 

 and yet the fact be , on reflection , easily to be account for . these unattractive girl be generally the first to bind themselves with the vow of betrothal . Lady Kirkbank have tell she of many such case . the poor creature know that their chance will be few , and therefore gratefully welcome the first wooer . 

 ' but who can the man be ? ' think Lesbia . ' Mary have be keep as seclude as a cloistered nun . there be so few family we have ever be allow to mix with . the man must be a curate , who have take advantage of grandmother 's illness to force his way into the family circle at Fellside and who have make love to Mary in some of her lonely ramble over the hill , I daresay . it be really very wrong to allow a girl to roam about in that way . ' 

 Sir George and a couple of his horsey friend be wait for supper when Lady Kirkbank and her party arrive in Arlington Street . the dining - room look a picture of comfort . the oval table , the low lamp , the cluster of candle under coloured shade , the great oriental bowl of wild flower eglantine , honeysuckle , foxglove , all the sweet hedge flower of midsummer , make a central mass of colour and brightness against the subdue and even sombre tone of wall and curtain . the room be old , the furniture old . nothing have be alter since the time of Sir George 's great grandfather ; and the whirligig of time have just now make the old thing precious . yes , those chair and table and sideboard and bookcase and wine - cooler against which Georgie 's soul have revolt in the early year of her wed life be now thing of beauty , and Georgie 's friend envy she the possession of indisputable chippendale furniture . 

 Mr. Mostyn , a distinguished owner of race - horse , with his pretty wife , make up the party . the gentleman be full of his entry for Liverpool and Chester , and discourse mysteriously with Sir George and the horsey bachelor all supper time . the lady have lately take up science as a new form of excitement , not incompatible with frock , bonnet , Hurlingham , the Ranelagh , and Sandown . she rave about Huxley and Tyndall , and be perpetually come down upon her friend with awful fact about the sun , and startling proposition about latent heat , or spontaneous generation . she know all about gas , and would hardly accept a glass of water without explain what it be make of . draw by Mr. Smithson for Lesbia 's amusement , the scientific matron be undoubtedly ' good fun . ' the race man be full of talk . Lesbia and Lady Kirkbank rave about the play they have just be see , and praise Delaunay with an enthusiasm which be calculate to make the rest of mankind burst with envy . 

 ' do you know you be make I positively wretche by your talk about that man ? ' say Colonel Delville , one of Sir George 's racing friend , and an ancient adorer of the fair Georgie 's . ' no , I tell you there be never anything offer high than five to four on the mare , ' interjectionally , to Sir George . ' there be a day when I think I be your idea of an attractive man . yes , George , a clear case of roping , ' again interjectionally . ' and to hear you rave about this play - act fellow it be too humiliating . ' 

 Lady Kirkbank simper , and then sigh . 

 ' we be get old together , ' she murmur . ' I have come to an age when one can only admire the charm of manner in the abstract the Beautiful for the sake of the Beautiful . I think if I be lie in my grave , the music of Delaunay 's voice would thrill I , under six foot of London clay . will no one take any more wine ? no . then we may as well go into the next room and begin our little Nap . ' 

 the adjoining room be Sir George 's snuggery ; and it be here that the cosy little round game after supper be always play . Sir George be not a studious person . he never read , and he never write , except an occasional cheque on account , for an importunate tradesman . his correspondence be conduct by the telegraph or telephone ; and the room , therefore , be absorb neither by book nor write desk . it be furnish solely with a view to comfort . there be a round table in the centre , under a large moderator lamp which give an exceptionally brilliant light . a divan cover with dark brown velvet occupy three side of the room . a few choice piece of old blue oriental ware in the corner enliven the dark brown wall . three or four easy chair stand about near the broad , old - fashioned fireplace , which have be improve with a modern - antique brass grate and a blue and white tile hearth . 

 ' there be not   a room in my house that look half as comfortable as this den of your , George , ' say Mr. Smithson , as he seat himself by Lesbia 's side at the card table . 

 they have agree to be partner . ' partner at card , even if we be not to be partner for life , ' Smithson have whisper , tenderly ; and Lesbia 's only reply have be a modest lowering of lovely eyelid , and a faint , faint blush . Lesbia 's blush be grow fainter every day . 

 ' that be because everything in your house be so confoundedly handsome and expensive , ' retort Sir George , who do not very much care about be call George , tout court , by a person of Mr. Smithson 's obscure antecedent , but who have to endure the familiarity for reason know only to himself and Mr. Smithson . ' no man can expect to be comfortable in a house in which every room have cost a small fortune . my wife re - arrange this den half - a - dozen year ago when we take to sittin ' here of an evenin ' . she pick up the chair and the blue pot at Bonham 's , have everythin ' cover with brown velvet nice subdue tone , suit old people hang up that yaller curtain , just for a bit of colour , and here we be . ' 

 ' it be the cosy room in town , ' say Colonel Delville , whereupon Mrs. Mostyn , while counter be be distribute , explain to the company on scientific principle why the room be comfortable , expatiate upon the effect of yellow and brown upon the retina , and some curious fact relate to the optic machinery of water - flea , as lately discover by a great naturalist . 

 unfortunately for science , the game have now begin , and the player be curiously indifferent as to the visual organ of water - flea . 

 the game go on merrily till the pearly light of dawn begin to creep through the chink of Lady Kirkbank 's yellow curtain . everybody seem gay , yet everybody could not be win . Fortune have not smile upon Lesbia 's card , or on those of her partner . the Smithson and Haselden firm have come to grief . Lesbia 's little ivory purse have be empty of its three or four half - sovereign , and Mr. Smithson have be capitalise a lose concern for the last two hour . and the play have be fast and furious , although nominally for small stake . 

 ' I be afraid to think of how much I must owe you , ' say Lesbia , when Mr. Smithson bid her good night . 

 ' oh , nothing worth speak of sixteen or seventeen pound , at most . ' 

 Lesbia feel cold and creepy , and hardly know whether it be the chill of new - bear day , or the sense of owe money to Horace Smithson . those three or four half - sovereign to - night be the end of her last remittance from Lady Maulevrier . she have have a great many remittance from that generous grandmother ; and the money have all go , somehow . it be go , and yet she have pay for hardly anything . she have account with all Lady Kirkbank 's tradesman . the money have melt away it have ooze out of her pocket at card , on the race - course , in reckless gift to servant and people , at fancy fair , for trifle buy here and there by the way - side , as it be , for the sake of buying . if she have be suddenly ask for an account of her stewardship she could not have tell what she have do with half of the money . and now she must ask for twenty pound more , and immediately , to pay Mr. Smithson . 

 she go up to her room in the clear early light , and stand like a statue , with fix thoughtful eye , while Kibble take off her finery , the pretty pale yellow gown which set off her dark brown hair , her violet eye . for the first time in her life she feel the keen pang of anxiety about money matter the necessity to think of way and mean . she have no idea how much money she have receive from her grandmother since she have begin her career in Scotland last autumn . the cheque have be send she as she ask for they ; sometimes even before she ask for they ; and she have keep no account . she think her grandmother be so rich that expenditure could not matter . she suppose that she be draw upon an inexhaustible supply . and now Lady Kirkbank have tell she that Lady Maulevrier be not rich , as the world reckon nowadays . the saving of a dowager countess even in forty year of seclusion could be but a small fund to draw upon for the expense of life at high pressure . 

 ' the sum people spend nowadays be positively appalling , ' say Lady Kirkbank . ' a man with five or six thousand a year be an absolute pauper . I be sure our existence be only genteel beggary , and yet we spend over ten thousand . ' 

 enlighten thus by the lip of the worldly - wise , Lesbia think ruefully of the bill which her grandmother would have to pay for she at the end of the season , bill of the amount whereof she could not even make an approximate guess . Seraphine 's charge have never be discuss in her hearing but Lady Kirkbank have admit that the creature be dear . 

 CHAPTER XXIX . 

 ' SWIFT SUBTLE POST , carrier of GRISLY CARE . ' 


 Maulevrier call in Arlington Street before twelve o'clock next day , and find Lesbia just return from her early ride , look as fresh and fair as if there have be no such thing as Nap or late hour in the story of her life . she be repose in a large easy chair by the open window , in habit and hat , just as she have come from the row , where she have be laugh and chat with Mr. Smithson , who jog demurely by her side on his short - legged hunter , drop out envenomed little joke about the passer by . People who see he rid by her side upon this particular morning fancy there be something more than usual in the gentleman 's manner , and make up their mind that Lady Lesbia Haselden be to be mistress of the fine house in Park Lane . Mr. Smithson have flutter and flutter for the last five season ; but this time the flutterer be catch . 

 in her newly - awaken anxiety about money matter , Lesbia have forget Mary 's engagement : but the sight of Maulevrier recall the fact . 

 ' come over here and sit down , ' she say , ' and tell I this nonsense about Mary . I be expire with curiosity . the thing be too absurd . ' 

 ' why absurd ? ' ask Maulevrier , sit where she bid he , and studiously peruse the name in his hat , as if it be a revelation . 

 ' oh , for a thousand reason , ' answer Lesbia , switch the flower in the balcony with her light little whip . ' first and foremost it be absurd to think of any one so bury alive as poor Mary be find an admirer ; and secondly well I do not   want to be rude to my own sister but Mary be not particularly attractive . ' 

 ' Mary be the dear girl in the world . ' 

 ' very likely . I only say that she be not particularly attractive . ' 

 ' and do you think there be no attraction in goodness , in freshness and innocence , candour , generosity ? ' 

 ' I do not   know . but I think that if Mary 's nose have be a thought long , and if she have keep her skin free from freckle she would have be almost pretty . ' 

 ' do you really ? luckily for Mary the man who be go to marry she think her lovely . ' 

 ' I suppose he like freckle . I once hear a man say he do . he say they be so original so much character about they . and , pray , who be the man ? ' 

 ' your old adorer , and my dear friend , John Hammond . ' 

 Lesbia turn as pale as death pale with rage and mortification . it be not jealousy , this pang which rent her shallow soul . she have cease to care for John Hammond . the whirlpool of society have spin that first fancy out of her giddy brain . but that a man who have love the high , who have worship she , the peerless , the beautiful , should calmly transfer his affection to her young sister , be to the last degree exasperating . 

 ' your friend Mr. Hammond must be a fickle fool , ' she exclaim , ' who do not know his own mind from day to day . ' 

 ' oh , but it be more than a day after you reject he that he engage himself to Molly . it be all my doing , and I be proud of my work . I take the poor fellow back to Fellside last March , bruise and break by your cruel treatment , heartsore and depressed . I give he over to Molly , and molly cure he . unconsciously , innocently , she win that noble heart . ah , Lesbia , you do not   know what a heart it be which you so nearly break . ' 

 ' girl in our rank of life can not   afford to marry noble heart , ' say Lesbia , scornfully . ' do you mean to tell I that Lady Maulevrier consent to the engagement ? ' 

 ' she cut up rather rough at first ; but molly hold her own like a young lioness and the grandmother give way . you see she have a fix idea that molly be a very second - rate sort of person compare with you , and that a husband who be not nearly good enough for you might pass muster for Molly ; and so she give way , and there be not   a happy young woman in the three kingdom than Mary Haselden . ' 

 ' what be they to live upon ? ' ask Lesbia , with an incredulous air . 

 ' Mary will have she five hundred a year . and Hammond be a very clever fellow . you may be sure he will make his mark in the world . ' 

 ' and how be they to live while he be make his mark ? five hundred a year will not   do more than pay for Mary 's frock , if she go into society . ' 

 ' perhaps they will live without society . ' 

 ' in some horrid little hovel in one of those narrow street off Ecclestone Square , ' suggest Lesbia , shudderingly . ' it be too dreadful to think of a young woman doom herself to life - long penury , just because she be so foolish as to fall in love . ' 

 ' your day for fall in love be over , I suppose , Lesbia ? ' say Maulevrier , contemplate his sister with keen scrutiny . 

 the beautiful face , so perfect in line and colour , curiously recall that other face at Fellside ; the dowager 's face , with its look of marble coldness , and the half - express pain under that outward calm . here be the face of one who have not yet know pain or passion . here be the cold perfection of beauty with unawakened heart . 

 ' I do not   know ; I be too busy to think of such thing . ' 

 ' you have do with love ; and you have begin to think of marriage , of establish yourself properly . People tell I you be go to marry Mr. Smithson . ' 

 ' People tell you more about I than I know about myself . ' 

 ' come now , Lesbia , I have a right to know the truth upon this point . your brother your only brother should be the first person to be tell . ' 

 ' when I be engage , I have no doubt you will be the first person , or the second person , ' answer Lesbia , lightly . ' Lady Kirkbank , live on the premise , be likely to be the first . ' 

 ' then you be not engage to Smithson ? ' 

 ' do not   I tell you so just now ? Mr. Smithson do I the honour to make I an offer yesterday , at about this hour ; and I do myself the honour to reject he . ' 

 ' and yet you be whisper together in the box last night , and you be rid in the row with he this morning . I just meet a fellow who see you together . do you think it be right , Lesbia , to play fast and loose with the man to encourage he , if you do not   mean to marry he ? ' 

 ' how can you accuse I of encourage a person whom I flatly refuse yesterday morning ? if Mr. Smithson like my society as a friend , must I need deny he my friendship , ask Lady Kirkbank to shut her door against he ? Mr. Smithson be very pleasant as an acquaintance ; and although I do not   want to marry he , there be no reason I should snub he . ' 

 ' Smithson be not a man to be trifle with . you will find yourself entangle in a web which you will not   easily break through . ' 

 ' I be not afraid of webs . by - the - bye , be it true that Mr. Smithson be likely to get a peerage ? ' 

 ' I have hear people say as much . Smithson have spend no end of money on electioneering , and be a power in the House , though he very rarely speak . his Berkshire estate give he a good deal of influence in that county ; at the last general election he subscribe twenty thou to the conservative cause ; for , like most man who have rise from nothing , your friend Smithson be a fine old tory . he be specially elect at the Carlton six year ago , and have make himself uncommonly useful to his party . he be suppose to be great on financial question , and come out tremendously on colonial railway or drainage scheme , about which the House in general be in profound ignorance . on those occasion Smithson score high . a man with immense wealth have always chance . no doubt , if you be to marry he , the peerage would be easily manage . Smithson 's money , back by the Maulevrier influence , would go a long way . my grandmother would move heaven and earth in a case of that kind . you have well take pity on Smithson . ' 

 Lesbia laugh . that idea of a possible peerage elevate Smithson in her eye . she know nothing of his political career , as she live in a set which ignore politic altogether . Mr. Smithson have never talk to she of his parliamentary duty ; and it be a new thing for she to hear that he have some kind of influence in public affair . 

 ' suppose I be inclined to accept he , would you like he as a brother - in - law ? ' she ask lightly . ' I think from your manner last night that you rather dislike he . ' 

 ' I do not   quite like he or any of his breed , the newly rich , who go about in society swell with the sense of their own importance , perspire gold , as it be . and one have always a faint suspicion of man who have get rich very quickly , an idea that there must be some kind of juggling . not in the case of a great contractor , perhaps , who can point to a viaduct and dock and railway , and say , " I build that , and that , and that . these be the source of my wealth . " but a man who get enormously rich by mere ciphering ! where can his money come from , except out of other people 's pocket ? I know nothing against your Mr. Smithson , but I always suspect that class of man , ' conclude Maulevrier shake his head significantly . 

 Lesbia be not much influence by her brother 's notion , she have never be teach to think he an oracle . on the contrary , she have be tell that his life hitherto have be all foolishness . 

 ' when be Mary and Mr. Hammond to be marry ? ' she ask , ' Grandmother say they must wait a year . Mary be much too young and so on , and so forth . but I see no reason for wait . ' 

 ' surely there be reason financial reason . Mr. Hammond can not be in a position to begin housekeeping . ' 

 ' oh , they will risk all that . molly be a daring girl . he propose to she on the top of Helvellyn , in a storm of wind and rain . ' 

 ' and she never write I a word about it . how very unsisterly ! ' 

 ' she be as wild as a hawk , and I daresay she be too shy to tell you anything about it . ' 

 ' pray when do it all occur ? ' 

 ' just before I come to London . ' 

 ' two month ago . how absurd for I to be in ignorance all this time ! well , I hope Mary will be sensible , and not marry till Mr. Hammond be able to give she a decent home . it would be so dreadful to have a sister muddle in poverty , and clamour for one 's cast - off gown . ' 

 Maulevrier laugh at this gloomy suggestion . 

 ' it be not easy to foretell the future , ' he say , ' but I think I may venture to promise that Molly will never wear your cast - off gown . ' 

 ' oh , you think she would be too proud . you do not   know , perhaps , how poverty genteel poverty lower one 's pride . I have hear story from Lady Kirkbank that would make your hair stand on end . I be begin to know the world . ' 

 ' I be glad of that . if you be to live in the world it be well that you should know what it be make of . but if I have a voice or a choice in the matter I have rather my sister stay at Grasmere , and remain ignorant of the world and all its way . ' 

 ' while you enjoy your life in London . that be just like the selfishness of a man . under the pretence of keep his sister or his wife secure from all possible contact with evil , he bury they alive in a country house , while he have all the wickedness for his own share in London . oh , I be begin to understand the creature . ' 

 ' I be afraid you be begin to be wise . remember that knowledge of evil be the prelude to the Fall . well , good - bye . ' 

 ' will not   you stay to lunch ? ' 

 ' no , thank , I never lunch frightful waste of time . I shall drop in at the Haute Gomme and take a cup of tea later on . ' 

 the Haute Gomme be a new club in Piccadilly , which Maulevrier and some of his friend affect . 

 Lesbia go towards the drawing - room door with her brother , and just as he reach the door she lay her hand caressingly upon his shoulder . he turn and star at she , somewhat surprised , for he and she have never be give to demonstration of affection . 

 ' Maulevrier , I want you to do I a favour , ' she say , in a low voice , blush a little , for the thing she be go to ask be a new thing for she to ask , and she have a deep sense of shame in make her demand . ' I I lose money at Nap last night . only seventeen pound . Mr. Smithson and I be partner , and he pay my loss . I want to pay he immediately , and - - ' 

 ' and you be too hard up to do it . I will write you a cheque this instant , ' say Maulevrier goodnaturedly ; but while he be write the cheque he take occasion to remonstrate with Lesbia on the foolishness of card playing . 

 ' I be oblige to do as Lady Kirkbank do , ' she answer feebly . ' if I be to refuse to play it would be a kind of reproach to she . ' 

 ' I do not   think that would kill Lady Kirkbank , ' reply Maulevrier , with a touch of scorn . ' she have have to endure a good many imply reproach in her day , and they do not   seem to have hurt she very much . I wish to heaven my grandmother have choose any one else in London for your chaperon . ' 

 ' I be afraid Lady Kirkbank 's be rather a rowdy set , ' answer Lesbia , coolly ; ' and I sometimes feel as if I have throw myself away . we go almost everywhere at least , there be only just a few house to which we be not ask . but those few make all the difference . it be so humiliating to feel that one be not in quite the good society . however , Lady Kirkbank be a dear , good old thing , and I be not go to grumble about she . ' 

 ' I have   make the cheque for five - and - twenty . you can cash it at your milliner 's , ' say Maulevrier . ' I should not like Smithson to know that you have be oblige to ask I for the money . ' 

 ' apropos to Mr. Smithson , do you know if he be in quite the good society ? ' ask Lesbia . 

 ' I do not   know what you mean by quite the good . a man of Smithson 's wealth can generally poke his nose in anywhere , if he know how to behave himself . but of course there be people with whom money and fine house have no weight . the Conservatives be all civil to Smithson because he come down handsomely at General Elections , and be useful to they in other way . I believe that Smithson 's wife , if she be a thorough - breed one , could go into any society she like , and make her house one of the most popular in London . perhaps that be what you really want to ask . 

 ' no , it be not , ' answer Lesbia , carelessly ; ' I be only talk for the sake of talk . a thousand thank for the cheque , you good of brother . ' 

 ' it be not worth talk about ; but , Lesbia , do not   play card any more . believe I , it be not good form . ' 

 ' well , I will try to keep out of it in future . it be horrid to see one 's sovereign melt away ; but there be a delightful excitement in win . ' 

 ' no doubt , ' answer Maulevrier , with a remorseful sigh . 

 he speak as a reform plunger , and with many a bitter experience of the race - course and the card - room . even now , though he have steady himself wonderfully , he could not get on without a little mild gambling half - crown pool , whist with half - guinea point but when he condescend to such small stake he feel that he have settle down into a respectable middle - aged player , and have a right to rebuke the folly of youth . 

 Lesbia fly to the piano and sing one of her little german ballad directly Maulevrier be go . she feel as if a burden have be lift from her soul , now that she be able to pay Mr. Smithson without wait to ask Lady Maulevrier for the money . and as she sing she meditate upon Maulevrier 's remark about Smithson . he know nothing to the man 's discredit , except that he have grow rich in a short space of time . surely no man ought to be blame for that . and he think that Mr. Smithson 's wife might make her house the most popular in London . Lesbia , in her mind 's eye , beheld an imaginary Lady Lesbia Smithson give dance in that magnificent mansion , entertain royal personage . and the doorway would be festoon with rose , as she have see they the other night at a ball in Grosvenor Square ; but the house in Grosvenor Square be a hovel compare with the Smithsonian Palace . 

 Lesbia be begin to be a little tired of Lady Kirkbank and her surrounding . life take prestissimo be apt to pall . Lesbia sigh as she finish her little song . she be begin to look upon her existence as a problem which have be give to she to solve , and the solution just at present be all dark . 

 as she rise from the piano a footman come in with two letter on a salver bulky letter , such package as Lesbia have never see before . she wonder what they could be . she open the thick envelope first . it be Seraphine 's bill such a bill , page after page on creamy Bath post , write in an elegant italian hand by one of Seraphine 's young woman . 

 Lesbia look at it aghast with horror . the total at the foot of the first page be appalling , ever so much more than she could have suppose the whole amount of her indebtedness ; but the total go on increase at the foot of every page , until at sight of the final figure Lesbia give a wild shriek , like a wretched creature who have receive a telegram announce bitterest loss . 

 the final total be twelve hundred and ninety - three pound seventeen and sixpence ! 

 Thirteen hundred pound for clothe in eight week ! 

 no , the thing be a cheat , a mistake . they have send she somebody else 's bill . she have not have half these thing . 

 she read the first page , her heart beat violently as she pore over the figure , her eye dim and cloud with the trouble of her brain . 

 yes , there be her court dress . the description be too minute to be mistake ; and the court dress , with feather , and shoe , and glove , and fan , come to a hundred and thirty pound . then follow innumerable item . the very simple of her gown cost five - and - twenty pound frock about which Seraphine have talk so carelessly , as if two or three more or less could make no difference . bonnet and hat , at five or seven guineas apiece , swell the account . parasol and fan be of fabulous price , as it seem to Lesbia ; and the shoe and stocking to match her various gown occur again and again between the more important item , like the refrain of an old ballad . all the useless and unnessary thing which she have order , because she think they pretty or because she be tell they be fashionable , rise up against she in the figure of the bill , like the record of forget sin at the Day of Judgment . 

 she sink into a chair , pallid with consternation , and sit with the bill in her lap , turn the page listlessly , and star at the figure . 

 ' it can not be so much , ' she cry to herself . ' it must be add up wrong ; ' and then she feebly try to cast up a column ; but arithmetic not be one of those accomplishment which Lady Maulevrier deem necessary to a patrician beauty 's success in life , Lesbia 's education have be somewhat neglect upon this point , and she fling the bill from she in a rage , unable to hold the figure in her brain . 

 she open the second envelope , her jeweller 's account . at the very first item she give another scream , fainter than the first , for her mind be get harden against such shock . 

 ' to re - set a suite of amethyst , with forty - four fine brazilian brilliant , three hundred and fifteen pound . ' 

 then follow the trifle she have buy at different visit to the shop casual purchase , buy on the impulse of the moment . these swell the account to a little over eight hundred pound . Lesbia sit like a statue , numb by despair , appal at the idea of owe over two thousand pound . 

 CHAPTER XXX . 

 ' ROSES choked among THORNS and THISTLES . ' 


 Lady Lesbia eat no luncheon that day . she go to her own room and have a cup of tea to steady her nerve , and send to ask Lady Kirkbank to go to she as soon as she have finish luncheon . Lady Kirkbank 's luncheon be a serious business , a substantial leisurely meal with which she fortify herself for the day 's work . it enable she to endure all the fatigue of visit and park , and to be airily indifferent to the charm of dinner ; for Lady Kirkbank be not one of those matron who with advanced year take to gourmandise as a kind of fine art . she give good dinner , because she know people would not come to Arlington Street to eat bad one ; but she be not a person who live only to dine . at luncheon she give her healthy appetite full scope , and eat like a ploughman . 

 she find Lesbia in her white muslin dressing - gown , with cheek as pale as the gown she wear . she be sit in an easy chair , with a low tea - table at her side , and the two bill be in the tray among the tea - thing . 

 ' have you any idea how much I owe Seraphine and Cabochon ? ' she ask , look up despairingly at Lady Kirkbank . 

 ' what , have they send in their bill already ? ' 

 ' already ! I wish they have send they before . I should have know how deeply I be get into debt . ' 

 ' be they very heavy ? ' 

 ' they be dreadful ! I owe over two thousand pound . how can I tell Lady Maulevrier that ? two thousand one hundred pound ! it be awful . ' 

 ' there be woman in London who would think very little of owe twice as much , ' say Lady Kirkbank , in a comfort tone , though the fact , seriously consider , could hardly afford comfort . ' your grandmother say you be to have carte blanche . she may think that you have be just a little extravagant ; but she can hardly be angry with you for have take she at her word . two thousand pound ! yes , it certainly be rather stiff . ' 

 ' Seraphine be a cheat ! ' exclaim Lesbia , angrily . ' her price be positively exorbitant ! ' 

 ' my dear child , you must not say that . Seraphine be positively moderate in comparison with the new people . ' 

 ' and Mr. Cabochon , too . the idea of his charge I three hundred guineas for re - set those stupid old amethyst . ' 

 ' my dear , you would have diamond mix with they , ' say Lady Kirkbank , reproachfully . 

 Lesbia turn away her head with an impatient sigh . she remember perfectly that it be Lady Kirkbank who have persuade she to order the diamond setting ; but there be no use in talk about it now . the thing be do . she be two thousand pound in debt two thousand pound to these two people only and there be ever so many shop at which she have account glover , bootmaker , habit - maker , the tailor who make her Newmarket coat and cloth gown , the stationer who supply she with note - paper of every variety , monogramme , floral ; sporting , illuminate with this or that device , the folly of the pass hour , hatch by penniless Invention in a garret , pander to the vanity of the idle . 

 ' I must write to my grandmother by this afternoon 's post , ' say Lesbia , with a heavy sigh . 

 ' impossible . we have to be at the Ranelagh by four o'clock . Smithson and some other man be to meet we there . I have promise to drive Mrs. Mostyn down . you have well begin to dress . ' 

 ' but I ought to write to - day . I have well ask for this money at once , and have do with it . two thousand pound ! I feel as if I be a thief . you say my grandmother be not a rich woman ? ' 

 ' not rich as the world go nowadays . nobody be rich now , except your commercial magnate , like Smithson . great peer , unless their money be in London ground - rent , be great pauper . to own land be to be destitute . I do not   suppose two thousand pound will break your grandmother 's bank ; but of course it be a large sum to ask for at the end of two month ; especially as she send you a good deal of money while we be at Cannes . if you be engage about to make a really good match you could ask for the money as a matter of course ; but as it be , although you have be tremendously admire , from a practical point of view you be a failure . ' 

 a failure . it be a hard word , but Lesbia feel it be true . she , the reign beauty , the cynosure of every eye , have make no conquest worth talk about , except Mr. Smithson . 

 ' do not   tell your grandmother anything about the bill for a week or two , ' say Lady Kirkbank , soothingly . ' the creature can wait for their money . give yourself time to think . ' 

 ' I will , ' answer Lesbia , dolefully . 

 ' and now make haste , and get ready for the Ranelagh . my love , your eye be dreadfully heavy . you must use a little belladonna . I will send Rilboche to you . ' 

 and for the first time in her life , Lesbia , too depressed to argue the point , consent to have her eye doctor by Rilboche . 

 she be gay enough at the Ranelagh , and look she lovely at a dinner party that evening , and go to three party after the dinner , and go home in the faint light of early morning , after sit out a late waltz in a balcony with Mr. Smithson , a balcony bank round with hot - house flower which be begin to droop a little in the chilly morning air , just as beauty droop under the search eye of day . 

 Lesbia put the bill in her desk , and give herself time to think , as Lady Kirkbank advise she . but the thinking progress result in very little good . all the thought of which she be capable would not reduce the total of those two dreadful account . and every day bring some fresh bill . the stationer , the bootmaker , the glover , the perfumer , people who have court Lady Lesbia 's custom with an air which imply that the honour of serve fashionable beauty be the first consideration , and the question of payment quite a minor point these now begin to ask for their money in the most prosaic way . every straw add to Lesbia 's burden ; and her heart grow heavy with every post . 

 ' one can see the season be wan when these people begin to pester with their account , ' say Lady Kirkbank , who always talk of tradesman as if they be her natural enemy . 

 Lesbia accept this explanation of the avalanche of bill , and never suspect Lady Kirkbank 's influence in the matter . it happen , however , that the chaperon , have her own reason for wish to bring Mr. Smithson 's suit to a successful issue , have tell Seraphine and the other people to send in their bill immediately . Lady Lesbia would be leave London in a week or so , she inform these purveyor , and would like to settle everything before she go away . 

 Mr. Smithson appear in Arlington Street almost every day , and be full of scheme for new pleasure or pleasure as nearly new as the world of fashion can afford . he be particularly desirous that Sir George and Lady Kirkbank , with Lady Lesbia , should stay at his Berkshire place during the Henley week . he have a large steam launch , and the regatta be a kind of carnival for his intimate friend , who be not too proud to riot and batten upon the parvenu 's luxurious hospitality , albeit they be apt to talk somewhat slightingly of his antecedent . 

 Lady Kirkbank feel that this invitation be a turning point , and that if Lesbia go to stay at Rood Hall , her acceptance of Mr. Smithson be a certainty . she would see he at his place in Berkshire in the most flattering aspect ; his surrounding as lord of the manor , and owner of one of the fine old place in the county , would lend dignity to his insignificance .  ' my dear , Mr. Smithson have forgive you , ' answer her chaperon . ' he be the soul of good nature . '

' I do not   want to go to Rood Hall , but I do not   want to spoil your Henley week . could not I run down to Grasmere for a week , with Kibble to take care of I , and see dear grandmother ? I could tell she about those dreadful bill . ' 

 ' bury yourself at Grasmere in the height of the season ! not to be think of ! besides , Lady Maulevrier object before to the idea of your travel alone with Kibble . no ! if you can not   make up your mind to go to Rood Hall , George and I must make up our mind to stay away . but it will be rather hard line ; for that Henley week be quite the jolly thing in the summer . ' 

 ' then I will go , ' say Lesbia , with a resign air . ' not for world would I deprive you and Sir George of a pleasure . ' 

 in her heart of heart she rather wish to see Rood Hall . she be curious to behold the extent and magnitude of Mr. Smithson 's possession . she have see his italian villa in Park Lane , the perfection of modern art , modern skill , modern taste , revive the old eternally beautiful form , recreate the Pitti Palace the home of the Medici the hall of dead and go Doges and now she be tell that Rood Hall a genuine old English manor - house , in perfect preservation be even more interesting than the villa in Park Lane . at Rood Hall there be ideal stable and farm , hot - house without number , rise garden , lawn , the river , and a deer park . 

 so the invitation be accept , and Mr. Smithson immediately lay himself at Lesbia 's foot , as it be , with regard to all other invitation for the Henley festival . whom should he ask to meet she ? whom would she have ? 

 ' you be very good , ' she say , ' but I have really no wish to be consult . I be not a royal personage , remember . I could not presume to dictate . ' 

 ' but I wish you to dictate . I wish you to be imperious in the expression of your wish . ' 

 ' Lady Kirkbank have a well right than I , if anybody be to be consult , ' say Lesbia , modestly . 

 ' Lady Kirkbank be an old dear , who get on delightfully with everybody . but you be more sensitive . your comfort might be mar by an obnoxious presence . I will ask nobody whom you do not like who be not thoroughly simpatico . have you no particular friend of your own choosing whom you would like I to ask ? ' 

 Lesbia confess that she have no such friend . she like everybody tolerably ; but she have not a talent for friendship . perhaps it be because in the London season one be too busy to make friend . 

 ' I can fancy two girl get quite attach to each other , out of the season , ' she say , ' but in May and June life be all a rush and a scramble - - ' 

 ' and one have no time to gather wayside flower of friendship , ' interject Mr. Smithson . ' still , if there be no people for whom you have an especial liking , there must be people whom you detest . ' 

 Lesbia own that it be so . detestation come of itself , naturally . 

 ' then let I be sure I do not ask any of your pet aversion , ' say Mr. Smithson . ' you meet Mr. Plantagenet Parsons , the theatrical critic , at my house . shall we have he ? ' 

 ' I like all amusing people . ' 

 ' and Horace Meander , the poet . shall we have he ? he be brimful of conceit and affectation , but he be a tremendous joke . ' 

 ' Mr. Meander be charming . ' 

 ' suppose we ask Mostyn and his wife ? her scrap of science be rather good fun . ' 

 ' I have not   the faint objection to the Mostyns , ' reply Lesbia . ' but who be " we " ? ' 

 ' we be you and I , for the nonce . the invitation will be issue ostensibly by I , but they will really emanate from you . ' 

 ' I be to be the shadow behind the throne , ' say Lesbia . ' how delightful ! ' 

 ' I would rather you be the sovereign ruler , on the throne , ' answer Smithson , tenderly . ' that throne shall be empty till you fill it . ' 

 ' please go on with your list of people , ' say Lesbia , check this gush of sentiment . 

 she begin to feel somehow that she be drift from all her mooring , that in accept this invitation to Rood Hall she be allow herself to be ensnare into an alliance about which she be still doubtful . if anything well have appear in the prospect of her life if any worthy suitor have come forward , she would have whistle Mr. Smithson down the wind ; but no worthy suitor have offer himself . it be Smithson or nothing . if she do not accept Smithson , she would go back to Fellside heavily burden with debt , and an obvious failure . she would have run the gauntlet of a London season without definite result ; and this , to a young woman so impressed with her own transcendent merit , be a most humiliating state of thing . 

 other people 's name be suggest by Mr. Smithson and approve by Lesbia , and a house party of about fourteen in all be make up . Mr. Smithson 's steam launch would comfortably accommodate that number . he have a couple of barge for chance visitor , and keep an open table on board they during the regatta . 

 the visit arrange , the next question be gown . Lesbia have gown enough to have stock a draper 's shop ; but then , as she and Lady Kirkbank deplore , the difficulty be that she have wear they all , some as many as three or four time . they be doubtless all marked and know . some of they have be describe in the society paper . at Henley she would be expect to wear something distinctly new , to introduce some new fashion of gown or hat or parasol . no matter how ugly the new thing might be , so long as it be startling ; no matter how eccentric , provide it be original . 

 ' what be I to do ? ' ask Lesbia , despairingly . 

 ' there be only one thing that can be do . we must go instantly to Seraphine and insist upon she invent something . if she have no idea ready she must telegraph Worth and get he to send something over . your old thing will do very well for Rood Hall . you have no end of pretty gown for morning and evening ; but you must be original on the race day . your gown will be in all the paper . ' 

 ' but I shall be only get deep into debt , ' say Lesbia , with a sigh . 

 ' that can not   be help . if you go into society you must be properly dress . we will go to Clanricarde Place directly after luncheon , and see what that old harpy have to show we . ' 

 Lesbia have a rather uncomfortable feeling about face the fair Seraphine , without be able to give she a cheque upon account of that dreadful bill . she have quite accept Lady Kirkbank 's idea that bill never need be discharge in full , and that the true system of finance be to give an occasional cheque on account , as a sop to Cerberus . true , that while Cerberus fatten on the sop the bill seem always grow ; and the final crash , when Cerberus grow savage and sop could be no more accept , be too awful to be think about . 

 Lesbia enter Seraphine 's Louis - seize drawing - room with a faint expectation of unpleasantness ; but after a little whispering between Lady Kirkbank and the dressmaker , the latter come to Lesbia smile graciously , and seemingly full of eagerness for new order . 

 ' miladi say you want something of the most original tant soit peu risquť for ' Enley , ' she say . ' let we see now , ' and she tap her forehead with a gold thimble which nobody have ever see her use , but which look respectable . ' there be ze dress that Chaumont wear in zis new play , Une Faute dan le Passť . yes , zere be the watare dress a boat party at Bougival , a toilet of the most new , striking , ťcrasant , what you English call a ' screamer . " ' 

 ' what a genius you be , Fifine , ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , rapturously . ' the Faute dan le Passť be only produce last week . no one will have think of copy Chaumont 's gown yet awhile . the idea be an inspiration . ' 

 ' what be the boat costume like ? ' ask Lady Lesbia , faintly . 

 ' an exquisite combination of simplicity with ťlan , ' answer the dressmaker . ' a skin - tight indigo silk Jersey bodice , closely stud with dark blue bead , a flounced petticoat of indigo and amber foulard , an amber scarf draw tightly round the hip , and a dark blue toque with a large bunch of amber poppy . Tan - colour mousquetaire glove , and hessian boot of tan - coloured kid . ' 

 ' hessian boot ! ' ejaculate Lesbia . 

 ' but , yes , Miladi . the petticoat be somewhat short , you comprehend , to escape the damp of the deck , and , after all , Hessians be much less indelicate than silk stocking , leg a cru , as one may say . ' 

 ' Lesbia , you will look enchant in yellow Hessians , ' say Lady Kirkbank , ' let the dress be put in hand instantly , Seraphine . ' 

 Lesbia be incline to remonstrate . she do not admire the description of the costume , she would rather have something less outrageous . 

 ' outrageous ! it be only original , ' exclaim her chaperon . ' if Chaumont wear it you may be sure it be perfect . ' 

 ' but on the stage , by gaslight , in the midst of unreality , ' argue Lesbia . ' that make such a difference . ' 

 ' my dear , there be no difference nowadays between the stage and the drawing - room . whatever Chaumont wear you may wear . and now let we think of the second day . I think as your first costume be to be nautical , and rather masculine , your second should be somewhat languishing and vaporeux . Creamy indian muslin , wild flower , a large leghorn hat . ' 

 ' and what will Miladi herself wear ? ' ask the french woman of Lady Kirkbank . ' she must have something of new . ' 

 ' no , at my age , it do not   matter . I shall wear one of my cotton frock , and my Dunstable hat . ' 

 Lesbia shudder , for Lady Kirkbank in her cotton frock be a spectacle at which youth laugh and age blush . but after all it do not matter to Lesbia . she would have like a less rowdy chaperon ; but as a foil to her own fresh young beauty Lady Kirkbank be admirable . 

 they drive down to Rood Hall early next week , Sir George convey they in his drag , with a change of horse at Maidenhead . the weather be peerless ; the country exquisite , approach from London . how different that river landscape look to the eye of the traveller return from the wild West of England , the woode gorge of Cornwall and Devon , the Tamar and the Dart . then how small and poor and mean seem silvery Thames , glide peacefully between his willowy bank , sing his lullaby to the whispering sedge ; a poor little river , a flat commonplace landscape , say the traveller , fresh from moorland and tor , from the rocky shore of the Atlantic , the deep cleft of the great , red hill . 

 to Lesbia 's eye the placid stream and the green pasture , breathing odour of meadow - sweet and clover , seem pass lovely . she be pleased with her own hat and parasol too , which make she graciously dispose towards the landscape ; and the last packet of glove from North Audley Street fit without a wrinkle . the glovemaker be begin to understand her hand , which be a study for a sculptor , but which have its little peculiarity . 

 nor be she ill - disposed to Mr. Smithson , who have come up to town by an early train , in order to lunch in Arlington Street and go back by coach , seat just behind Lady Lesbia , who have the box seat beside Sir George . 

 the drive be delightful . it be a few minute after five when the coach drive past the picturesque old gate - house into Mr. Smithson 's Park , and Rood Hall lie on the low ground in front of they , with its back to the river . it be an old red brick house in the Tudor style , with an advanced porch , and four project wing , three story high , with picturesque spire roof overtop the main building . around the house run a boldly - carve stone parapet , bear the heron and bulrush which be the cognisance of the noble race for which the mansion be build . numerous projecting mullione window break up the line of the park front . Lesbia be fain to own that Rood Hall be even well than Park Lane . in London Mr. Smithson have create a palace ; but it be a new palace , which still have a faint flavour of brick and mortar , and which be apt to remind the spectator of that wonderful erection of Aladdin , the famous Parvenu of Eastern story . here , in Berkshire , Mr. Smithson have drop into a nest which have be keep warm for he for three century , air and beautify by generation of a noble race which have obligingly decay and dwindle in order to make room for Mr. Smithson . here the Parvenu have buy a home mellow by the slow growth of year , touch into poetic beauty by the chasten finger of time . his artist friend tell he that every brick in the red wall be ' precious , ' a mystery of colour which only a painter could fitly understand and value . here he have buy association , he have buy history . he have buy the dust of Elizabeth 's senator , the bone of her court beauty . the coffin in the Mausoleum yonder in the ferny depth of the Park , the village church just outside the gate these have all go with the property . 

 Lesbia go up the grand staircase , through the long corridor , in a dream of wonder . bring up at Fellside , in that new part of the Westmoreland house which have be build by her grandmother and have no history , she feel thrill by the sober splendour of this fine old manorial mansion . all be sound and substantial , as if create yesterday , so well preserve have be the good and chattel of the noble race ; and yet all wear such unmistakeable mark of age . the deep rich colouring of the wainscot , the fade hue of the tapestry , the drapery of costly velvet and brocade , be all sober by the passing of year . 

 Mr. Smithson have show his good taste in have keep all thing as Sir Hubert Heronville , the last of his race , have leave they ; and the Heronvilles have be one of those grand old Tory race which change nothing of the past . 

 Lady Lesbia 's bedroom be the State chamber , which have be occupy by king and queen in day of yore . that grandiose four - poster , with the carve ebony column , cut velvet curtain , and plume of ostrich feather , have be build for Elizabeth , when she deign to include Rood Hall in one of her royal progress . Charles the First have rest his weary head upon those very pillow , before he go on to the Inn at Uxbridge , where he be to be lodge less luxuriously . James the Second have stay there when Duke of York , with Mistress Anne Hyde , before he acknowledge his marriage to the multitude ; and Anne 's daughter have occupy the same room as Queen of England forty year later ; and now the Royal Chamber , with adjacent dressing - room , and oratory , and spacious boudoir all in the same suite , be reserve for Lady Lesbia Haselden . 

 ' I be afraid you be spoil I , ' she tell Mr. Smithson , when he ask if she approve of the room that have be allot to she . ' I feel quite ashamed of myself among the ghost of dead and go queen . ' 

 ' why so ? surely the Royalty of beauty have as divine a right as that of an anointed sovereign . ' 

 ' I hope the Royal personage do not   walk , ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , in her girlish tone ; ' this be just the house in which one would expect ghost . ' 

 Whereupon Mrs. Mostyn hasten to enlighten the company upon the real cause of ghost - seeing , which she have lately study in Carpenter 's ' Mental Physiology , ' and favour they with a diluted version of the view of that authority . 

 this be at afternoon tea in the library , where the brass - wire bookcase , fill with mighty folio and handsome octavos in old binding , look as if they have not be open for a century . the literature of past age furnish the room , and make a delightful background . the literature of the present lie about on the table , and testify that the high intellectual flight of the inhabitant of Rood Hall be a dip into the Contemporary or the Nineteenth Century , or the perusal of the last new scandal in the shape of Reminiscences or Autobiography . one large round table be consecrate to Mudie , another to Rolandi . on the one side you have Mrs. Oliphant , on the other Zola , exemplify the genius of the two nation . 

 after tea Mr. Smithson 's visitor , most of whom have arrive in Sir George 's drag , explore the ground . these be lovely beyond expression in the low afternoon light . cedar of Lebanon spread their broad shadow on the velvet lawn , yews and Wellingtonias of mighty growth make an atmosphere of gloom in some part of the ground . one great feature be the lady ' Garden , a spot apart , a great square garden surround with a laurel wall , eight foot high , contain a rose garden , where the choice specimen grow and flourish , while in the centre there be a circular fish - pond with a fountain . there be a Lavender Walk too , another feature of the ground at Rood Hall , an avenue of tall lavender bush , much affect by the stately dame of old . 

 modern manner prefer the river terrace , as a pleasant place on which to loiter after dinner , to watch the boat flash by in the evening light , or the sun go down behind a fringe of willow on the opposite bank . this italian terrace , with its statue , and carve vas fill with rose , fuchsia , and geranium , be the great point of rendezvous at Rood Hall an ideal spot whereon to linger in the deepening twilight , from which to gaze upon the moonlit river later on in the night . 

 the window of the drawing - room , and music - room , and ballroom open on to this terrace , and the royal wing the tower - shape wing now devote to Lady Lesbia , look upon the terrace and the river . 

 ' lovely as your house be altogether , I think this river view be the good part of it , ' say Lady Lesbia , as she stroll with Mr. Smithson on the terrace after dinner , dress in indian muslin which be almost as poetical as a vapour , and with a cloud of delicate lace wrap round her head . ' I think I shall spend half of my life at my boudoir window , gloat over that delicious landscape . ' 

 Horace Meander , the poet , be discourse to a select group upon that peculiar quality of willow which cause they to shiver , and quiver , and throw little light and shadow on the river , and on the subtle , ineffable beauty of twilight , which perhaps , however utterly beautiful in the abstract , would have be more agreeable to he personally if he have not be surround by a cloud of gnat , which refuse to be buffet off his laurel - crown head . 

 while Mr. Meander poetise in his usual eloquent style , Mrs. Mostyn , as a still new light , discourse as eloquently to little a knot of woman , impart valuable information upon the anatomical structure and individual peculiarity of those various insect which be the pest of a summer evening . 

 ' you do not   like gnat ! ' exclaim the lady ; ' how very extraordinary . do you know I have spend day and week upon the study of their habit and dear little way . they be the most interesting creature far superior to we in intellect . do you know that they fight , and that they have tribe which be life - long enemy like those dreadful Corsicans and that they make little sepulchre in the bark of tree , and bury each other alive , if they can ; and they hold vestry , and have burial board . they be most absorbing creature , if you only give yourself up to the study of they ; but it be no use to be half - hearted in a study of that kind . I go without so much as a cup of tea for twenty - four hour , watch my gnat , for fear the opening of the door should startle they . another time I shall make the nursery governess watch for I . '
