 ' how interesting , how noble of you , ' exclaim the other lady ; and then they begin to talk about bonnet , and about Mr. Smithson , to speculate how much money this house and all his other house have cost he , and to wonder if he be really rich , or if he be only one of those great financial windbag which so often explode and leave the world aghast , marvel at the ease with which it have be delude . 

 they wonder , too , whether Lady Lesbia Haselden mean to marry he . 

 ' of course she do , my dear , ' answer Mrs. Mostyn , decisively . 

 ' you do not   suppose that after have study the habit of gnat I can not read such a poor shallow creature as a silly vain girl . of course Lady Lesbia mean to marry Mr. Smithson 's fine house ; and she be only amuse herself and swell her own importance by let he dangle in a kind of suspense which be not suspense ; for he know as well as she do that she mean to have he . ' 

 the next day be give up , first to see the house , an amusement which last very well for an hour or so after breakfast , and then to wander in a desultory manner , to row and canoe , and a little sailing , and a good deal of scream and pretty timidity upon the blue bright river ; to gather wild flower and fern in rustic lane , and to an al fresco luncheon in the wood at Medmenham , and then dinner , and then music , an evening spend half within and half without the music - room , cigarette sparkle , like glowworm on the terrace , tall talk from Mr. Meander , long quotation from his own muse and that of Rossetti , a little Shelley , a little Keats , a good deal of Swinburne . the festivity be late on this second evening , as Mr. Smithson have invite a good many people from the neighbourhood , but the house party be not the less early on the following morning , which be the first Henley day . 

 it be a peerless morning , and all the brasswork of Mr. Smithson 's launch sparkle and shine in the sun , as she lie in front of the terrace . a wooden pier , a portable construction , be throw out from the terrace step , to enable the company to go on board the launch without the possibility of wet foot or damage raiment . 

 Lesbia 's Chaumount costume be a success . the woman praise it , the man star and admire . the dark - blue silken jersey , sparkle with closely studded indigo bead , fit the slim graceful figure as a serpent 's scale fit the serpent . the coquettish little blue silk toque , the careless cluster of gold - colour poppy , against the glossy brown hair , the large sunshade of old gold satin line with indigo , the flounced petticoat of soft indian silk , the dainty little tan - colour boot with high heel and point toe , be all perfect after their fashion ; and Mr. Smithson feel that the liege lady of his life , the woman he mean to marry willy nilly , would be the belle of the race - course . nor be he disappointed . everybody in London have hear of Lady Lesbia Haselden . her photograph be in all the west - end window , be enshrine in the album of South Kensington and Clapham , Maida Vale and Haverstock Hill . People whose circle be far remote from Lady Lesbia 's circle , be as familiar with her beauty as if they have know she from her cradle . and all these outsider want to see she in the flesh , just as they always thirst to behold royal personage . so when it become know that the beautiful Lady Lesbia Haselden be on board Mr. Smithson 's launch , all the people in the small boat , or on neighbouring barge , make it their business to have a good look at she . the launch be almost mob by those inquisitive little boat in the interval between the race . 

 ' what be the people all star and hustle one another for ? ' ask Lesbia , innocently . she have see the same hustling and whispering and star in the hall at the opera , when she be wait for her carriage ; but she choose to affect unconsciousness . ' what do they all want ? ' 

 ' I think they want to see you , ' say Mr. Smithson , who be sit by her side . ' a very natural desire . ' 

 Lesbia laugh , and lower the big yellow sunshade , so as to hide herself altogether from the starer . 

 ' how silly ! ' she exclaim . ' it be all the fault of those horrid photographer : they vulgarise everything and everybody . I will never be photograph again . ' 

 ' oh yes , you will , and in that frock . it be the prettiest thing I have   see for a long time . why do you hide yourself from those poor wretch , who keep row backwards and forwards in an obviously aimless way , just to get a peep at you en passant ? what happiness for we who live near you , and can gaze when we will , without all those absurd manoeuvre . there go the signal and now for a hard - fought race . ' 

 Lesbia pretend to be interested in the racing she pretend to be gay , but her heart be as heavy as lead . the burden of debt , which have be grow ever since Seraphine send in her bill , be weigh she down to the dust . 

 she owe three thousand pound . it seem incredible that she should owe so much , that a girl 's frivolous fancy and extravagance could amount to such a sum within so short a span . but thoughtless purchase , ignorant order , have run on from week to week , and the main result be an indebtedness of close upon three thousand pound . 

 three thousand pound ! the sum be continually sound in her ear like the cry of a screech owl . the very ripple of the river flow so peacefully under the blue summer sky seem to repeat the word . three thousand pound ! ' be it much ? ' she wonder , have no standard of comparison . ' be it very much more than my grandmother will expect I to have spend in the time ? will it trouble she to have to pay those bill ? will she be very angry ? ' 

 these be question which Lesbia keep ask herself , in every pause of her frivolous existence ; in such a pause as this , for instance , while the people round she be stand breathless , open - mouthed , gaze after the boat . she do not care a straw for the boat , who win , or who lose the race . it be all a hollow mockery . indeed it seem just now that the only real thing in life be those accursed bill , which would have to be pay somehow . 

 she have tell Lady Maulevrier nothing about they as yet . she have allow herself to be advise by Lady Kirkbank , and she have take time to think . but thought have give she no help . the day be glide onward , and Lady Maulevrier would have to be tell . 

 she meditate perplexedly about her grandmother 's income . she have never hear the extent of it , but have take for grant that Lady Maulevrier be rich . would three thousand pound make a great inroad on that income ? would it be a year 's income ? half a year 's ? Lesbia have no idea . life at Fellside be carry on in an elegant manner with considerable luxury in house and garden a luxury of flower , a lavish expenditure of labour . yet the expenditure of Lady Maulevrier 's existence , spend always on the same spot , must be as nothing to the money spend in such a life as Lady Kirkbank 's , which involve the keeping up of three or four house , and costly journey to and fro , and incessant change of attire . 

 no doubt Lady Maulevrier have save money ; yes , she must have save thousand during her long seclusion , Lesbia argue . her grandmother have tell she that she be to look upon herself as an heiress . this could only mean that Lady Maulevrier have a fortune to leave she ; and this be so , what could it matter if she have anticipate some of her portion ? and yet there be in her heart of heart a terrible fear of that stern dowager , of the cold scorn in those splendid eye when she should stand reveal in all her foolishness , her selfish , mindless , vain extravagance . she , who have never be reprove , shrink with a sickly dread from the idea of reproof . and to be tell that her career as a fashionable beauty have be a failure ! that would be the bitter pang of all . 

 soon come luncheon , and Heidseck , and then an afternoon which be gayer than the morning have be , inasmuch as every one babble and laugh more after luncheon . and then there be five o'clock tea on deck , under the striped japanese awning , to the jingle of banjo , enliven by the wit of black - face minstrel , amidst wherry and canoe and gondola , and ponderous houseboat , and snort launch , crowd the side of the sunlit river , in full view of the crowd yonder in front of the Red Lion , and here on this nearer bank , and all along either shore , fringe the green meadow with a gaudy border of smartly - dress humanity . 

 it be a gay scene , and Lesbia give herself up to the amusement of the hour , and talk and chaff as she have learn to talk and chaff in one brief season , hold she own against all comer . 

 Rood Hall look lovely when they go back to it in the gloaming , an Elizabethan pile crown with tower . the four wing with their conical roof , the massive project window , grey stone , ruddy brickwork , lattice reflect the sunlight , italian terrace and blue river in the foreground , cedar and yew at the back , all make a splendid picture of an english ancestral home . 

 ' nice old place , be not   it ? ' ask Mr. Smithson , see Lesbia 's admire gaze as the launch near the terrace . they two be stand in the bow , apart from all the rest . 

 ' nice ! it be simply perfect . ' 

 ' oh no , it be not . there be one thing want yet . ' 

 ' what be that ? ' 

 ' a wife . you be the only person who can make any house of mine perfect . will you ? ' he take her hand , which she do not withdraw from his grasp . he bend his head and kiss the little hand in its soft swedish glove . 

 ' will you , Lesbia ? ' he repeat earnestly ; and she answer softly , ' yes . ' 

 that one brief syllable be more like a sigh than a speak word , and it seem to she as if in the utterance of that syllable the three thousand pound have be pay . 

 CHAPTER XXXI . 

 ' kind be MY love to - DAY , TO - morrow KIND . ' 


 while Lady Lesbia be drain the cup of London folly and London care to the dreg , Lady Mary be lead her usual quiet life beside the glassy lake , where the green hill - side and sheep walk be reflect in all their summer verdure under the cloudless azure of a summer sky . a monotonous life pass dull as see from the outside and yet Mary be very happy , happy even in her solitude , with the grave deep joy of a satisfied heart , a mind at rest . all life have take a new colour since her engagement to John Hammond . a sense of new duty , an awakening earnestness have give a graver tone to her character . her spirit be less wild , yet not less joyous than of old . the joy be holy , deep . 

 her lover 's letter be the chief delight of her lonely day . to read they again and again , and ponder upon they , and then to pour out all her heart and mind in answer they . these be pleasure enough for her young life . Hammond 's letter be such as any woman might be proud to receive . they be not love - letter only . he write as friend to friend ; not descend from the proud pinnacle of masculine intelligence to the low level of feminine silliness ; not write down to a simple country girl 's capacity ; but write fully and fervently , as if there be no subject too lofty or too grave for the understanding of his betrothed . he write as one sure of be sympathise with , write as to his second self : and Mary show herself not unworthy of the honour thus render to her intellect . 

 there be one world which have newly open to Mary since her engagement , and that be the world of politic . Hammond have tell she that his ambition be to succeed as a politician to do some good in his day as one of the govern body ; and of late she have make it her business to learn how England and the world outside England be govern . 

 she have no natural lean to the study of political economy . instead , she have always imagine any question relate to the government of her country to be inherently dry - as - dust and uninviting . but have John Hammond devote his day to the study of coptic manuscript , or the arrow - head inscription upon assyrian tablet , she would have toil she hard in the endeavour to make herself a coptic scholar , or an adept in the cuneiform character . if he have be a student of Chinese , she would not have be discomfit by such a trifle as the fifty thousand character in the chinese alphabet . 

 and so , as he be to make his name in the arena of public life , she set herself to acquire a proper understanding of the science of politic ; and to this end she gorge herself with english history , Hume , Hallam , Green , Justin McCarthy , Palgrave , Lecky , from the day of Witenagemote to the Reform Bill ; the Repeal of the Corn Laws , the Disestablishment of the Irish Church , Ballot , Trade Unionism , and unreciprocate Free Trade . no question be deep enough to repel she ; for be not her lover interested in the dry thereof ; and what concern he and his welfare must need be full of interest for she . 

 to this end she read the debate religiously day by day ; and she one day venture shyly to suggest that she should read they aloud to Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' would it not be a little rest for you if I be to read your Times aloud to you every afternoon , grandmother ? ' she ask . ' you read so many book , French , English , and german , and I think your eye must get a little tired sometimes . ' 

 Mary venture the remark with some timidity , for those falcon eye be fix upon she all the time , bright and clear and steady as the eye of youth . it seem almost an impertinence to suggest that such eye could know weariness . 

 ' no , Mary , my sight hold out wonderfully for an old woman , ' reply her ladyship , gently . ' the new theory of the last oculist whose book I dip into a very amusing and interesting book , by - the - bye be that the sight improve and strengthen by constant use , and that an agricultural labourer , who hardly use his eye at all , have rarely in the decline of life so good a sight as the watchmaker or the student . I have read immensely all my life , and find myself no bad for that indulgence . but you may read the debate to I if you like , my dear , for if my eye be strong , I myself be very tired . sick to death , Mary , sick to death . ' 

 the splendid eye turn from Mary , and look away to the blue sky , to the hill in their ineffable beauty of colour and light shifting , change with every moment of the summer day . intense weariness , a settle despair , be express in that look tearless , yet sad than all tear . 

 ' it must be very monotonous , very sad for you , ' murmur Mary , her own eye brim over with tear . ' but it will not be always so , dear grandmother . I hope a time will come when you will be able to go about again , to resume your old life . ' 

 ' I do not hope , Mary . no , child , I feel and know that time will never come . my strength be ebb slowly day by day . if I live for another year , live to see Lesbia marry , and you , too , perhaps well , I shall die at peace . at peace , no ; not - - ' she falter , and the thin , semi - transparent hand be press upon her brow . ' what will be say of I when I be dead ? ' 

 Mary fear that her grandmother 's mind be wander . she come and kneel beside the couch , lay and her head against the satin pillow , tenderly , caressingly . 

 ' dear grandmother , pray be calm , ' she murmur . 

 ' Mary , do not look at I like that , as if you would read my heart . there be heart that must not be look into . mine be like a charnel - house . monotonous , yes ; my life have be monotonous . no conventual gloom be ever deep than the gloom of Fellside . my boy do nothing to lighten it for I , and his son follow in his father 's footstep . you and Lesbia have be my only consolation . Lesbia ! I be so proud of her beauty , so proud and fond of she , because she be like I , and recall my own youth . and see how easily she forget I . she have go into a new world , in which my age and my infirmity have no part ; and I be as nothing to she . ' 

 Mary change from red to pale , so painful be her embarrassment . what could she say in defence of her sister ? how could she deny that Lesbia be an ingrate , when those rare and hurried letter , so careless in their tone , express the selfishness of the writer in every syllable , tell but too plainly of forgetfulness and ingratitude ? 

 ' dear grandmother , Lesbia have so much to do her life be so full of engagement , ' she falter feebly . 

 ' yes , she go from party to party she give herself up heart and mind and soul to pleasure which she ought to consider only as the trivial mean to great end ; and she forget the woman who rear she , and care for she , and watch over she from her infancy , and who try to inspire she with a noble ambition . yes , read to I , child , read . give I new thought , if you can , for my brain be weary with grind the old one . there be a grand debate in the Lords last night , and Lord Hartfield speak . let I hear his speech . you can read what be say by the man before he ; never mind the rest . ' 

 Mary read Lord Somebody 's speech , which be pass dull , but which prepare the ground for a magnificent and exhaustive reply from Lord Hartfield . the question be an important one , affect the well - being of the mass , and Lord Hartfield speak with an eloquence which rise in force and fire as he wind himself like a serpent into the heart of his subject beginning quietly , soberly , with no opening flash of rhetoric , but rise gradually to the topmost height of oratory . 

 ' what a speech ! ' cry Lady Maulevrier , delight , her cheek glow , her eye kindling ; ' what a noble fellow the speaker must be ! oh , Mary , I must tell you a secret . I love that man 's father . yes , my dear , I love he fondly , dearly , truly , as you love that young man of your ; and he be the only man I ever really love . Fate part we . but I have never forget he never , Mary , never . at this moment I have but to close my eye and I can see his face see he look at I as he look the last time we meet . he be a young son , poor , his future quite hopeless in those day ; but it be not my fault we be part . I would have marry he yes , wed poverty , just as you be go to marry this Mr. Hammond ; but my people would not let I ; and I be too young , too helpless , to make a good fight . oh , Mary , if I have only fight hard enough , what a happy woman I might have be , and how good a wife . ' 

 ' you be a good wife to my grandfather , I be sure , ' falter Mary , by way of say something consolatory . 

 a dark frown come over Lady Maulevrier 's face , which have soften to deepest tenderness just before . 

 ' a good wife to Maulevrier , ' she say , in a mocking tone . well , yes , as good a wife as such a husband deserve . ' I be well than Casar 's wife , Mary , for no breath of suspicion ever rest upon my name . but if I have marry Ronald Hollister , I should have be a happy woman ; and that I have never be since I part from he . ' 

 ' you have never see the present Lord Hartfield , I think ? ' 

 ' never ; but I have watch his career , I have think of he . his father die while he be an infant , and he be bring up in seclusion by a widow mother , who keep he tie to her apron - string till he go to Oxford . she idolise he , and I be tell she teach herself latin and Greek , mathematic even , in order to help he in his boyish study , and , later on , read greek play and latin poetry with he , till she become an exceptional classic for a woman . she be her son 's companion and friend , sympathise with his taste , his pleasure , his friendship ; devote every hour of her life , every thought of her mind to his welfare , his interest , walk with he , ride with he , travel half over Europe , yacht with he . her friend all declare that the lad would grow up an odious milksop ; but I be tell that there never be a manly man than Lord Hartfield . from his boyhood he be his mother 's protector , help to administer her affair , acquire a premature sense of responsibility , and escape almost all those vice which make young man detestable . his mother die within a few month of his majority . he be break - hearted at lose she , and leave Europe immediately after her death . from that time he have be a great traveller . but I suppose now that he have take his seat in the House of Lords , and have speak a good many time , he mean to settle down and take his place among the foremost man of his day . I be tell that he be worthy to take such a place . ' 

 ' you must feel warmly interested in watch his career , ' say Mary , sympathetically . 

 ' I be interested in everything that concern he . I will tell you another secret , Mary . I think I be get into my dotage , my dear , or I should hardly talk to you like this , ' say Lady Maulevrier , with a touch of bitterness . 

 Mary be sit on a stool by the sofa , close to the invalid 's pillow . she clasp her grandmother 's hand and kiss it fondly . 

 ' dear grandmother , I think you be talk to I like this to - day because you be begin to care for I a little , ' she say , tenderly . 

 ' oh , my dear , you be very good , very sweet and forgiving to care for I at all , after my neglect of you , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , with a sigh . ' I have keep you out in the cold so long , Mary . Lesbia well , Lesbia have be a kind of infatuation for I , and like all infatuation mine have end in disappointment and bitterness . ambition have be the bane of my life , Mary ; and when I could no long be ambitious for myself when my own existence have become a mere death in life , I begin to dream and to scheme for the aggrandisement of my granddaughter . Lesbia 's beauty , Lesbia 's elegance seem to make success certain and so I dream my dream which may never be fulfil . ' 

 ' what be your dream , grandmother ? may I know all about it ? ' 

 ' that be the secret I speak of just now . yes , Mary , you may know , for I fear the dream will never be realise . I want my Lesbia to become Lord Hartfield 's wife . I would have bring they together myself , could I have but go to London ; but , fail that , I fancy Lady Kirkbank would have divine my wish without be tell they , and would have introduce Hartfield to Lesbia ; and now the London season be draw to a close , and Hartfield and Lesbia have never meet . he hardly go anywhere , I be tell . he devote himself exclusively to politic ; and he be not in Lady Kirkbank 's set . a terrible disappointment to I , Mary ! ' 

 ' it be a pity , ' say Mary . ' Lesbia be so lovely . if Lord Hartfield be fancy - free he ought to fall in love with she , could they but meet . I think that in London all fashionable people know each other , and be continually meet . ' 

 ' it use to be so in my day , Mary . Almack 's be a common ground , even if there have be no other . but now there be circle and circle , I believe , ring that touch occasionally , but never break and mingle . I be afraid poor Georgie 's set be not quite so nice as I could have wish . yet Lesbia write as if she be in rapture with her chaperon , and with all the people she meet . and then Georgie tell I that this Mr. Smithson whom Lesbia have refuse be a very important personage , a millionaire , and very likely to be make a peer . ' 

 ' a new peer , ' say Mary , make a wry face . ' one would rather have an old commoner . I always fancy a newly - make peer must be like a newly - build house , glaring , and star , and arid and uncongenial . ' 

 ' C'est selon , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' one would not despise a Chatham or a Wellington because of the newness of his title ; but a man who have only money to recommend he - - ' 

 Lady Maulevrier leave her sentence unfinishe , save by a shrug ; while Mary make another wry face . she have that grand contempt for sordid wealth which be common to young people who have never know the want of money . 

 ' I hope Lesbia will marry some one well than Mr. Smithson , ' she say . 

 ' I hope so too , dear ; and yet do you know I have an idea that Lesbia mean to accept Mr. Smithson , or she would hardly have consent to go to his house for the Henley week . here be a letter from Georgie Kirkbank which you will have to answer for I to - morrow a letter full of rapture about Mr. Smithson 's place in Berkshire , Rood Hall . I remember the house well . I be there nearly fifty year ago , when the Heronvilles own it ; and now the Heronvilles be all dead or ruin , and this city person be master of the fine old mansion . it be a strange world , Mary . ' 

 from that time forward Mary and her grandmother be on more confidential term , and when , two day later , Fellside be startle into life by the unexpected arrival of Lord Maulevrier and Mr. Hammond , the dowager seem almost as pleased as her granddaughter at the arrival of the young man . 

 as for Mary , she be almost beside herself with joy when she hear their voice from the lawn , and , rush to the shrubbery , see they walk up the hill , as she have see they on that first evening nearly a year ago , when John Hammond come as a stranger to Fellside . 

 she try to take her joy soberly , though her eye be dance with delight , as she go to the porch to meet they . 

 ' what extraordinary young man you be , ' she say , as she emerge breathless from her lover 's embrace . ' the idea of your descending upon we without a moment 's notice . why do you not write or telegraph , that your room might be ready ? ' 

 ' be I to understand that all the spare room at Fellside be keep as damp as at the bottom of the lake ? ' ask Maulevrier . ' I do not think any preparation be necessary ; but we can go back if we be not want , can not   we , Jack ? ' 

 ' you darle , ' cry Mary , hang affectionately upon her brother 's arm . ' you know I be only joke , you know how enraptured I be to have you . ' 

 ' to have I , only I , ' say Maulevrier . ' Jack do not   count , I suppose ? ' 

 ' you know how glad I be , and that I want to hide my gladness , ' answer Mary , radiant and blushing like the rich red rose in the porch . ' you man be so vain . and now come and see grandmother , she will be cheer by your arrival . she have be so good to I just lately , so sweet . ' 

 ' she might have be good and sweet to you all your life , ' say Hammond . ' I be not prepared to be grateful to she at a moment 's notice for any crumb of affection she may throw you . ' 

 ' oh but you must be grateful , sir ; and you must love she and pity she , ' retort Mary . ' think how sadly she have suffer . we can not be too kind to she , or too fond of she , poor dear . ' 

 ' Mary be right , ' say Hammond , half in jest and half in earnest . ' what wonderful instinct these young woman have . ' 

 ' come and see her ladyship ; and then you must have dinner , just as you have that first evening , ' say Mary . ' we will act that first evening over again , Jack ; only you can not   fall in love with Lesbia , as she be not   here . ' 

 ' I do not   think I surrender that first evening , Mary . though I think your sister the lovely girl I have ever see . ' 

 ' and what do you think of I , sir ? tell I that , ' say Mary . 

 ' shall I tell you the truth , the whole truth , and nothing but the truth ? ' 

 ' of course . ' 

 ' then I freely confess that I do not think about you at all . you be there a pretty , innocent , bright young maiden , with big brown eye and auburn hair ; but I think no more about you than I do about the Gainsborough on the wall , which you very much resemble . ' 

 ' that be most humiliating , ' say Mary , pout a little in the midst of her bliss . 

 ' no , dearest , it be only natural , ' answer Hammond . ' I believe if all the happy lover in this world could be question , at least half of they would confess to have think very little about each other at first meeting . they meet , and touch hand , and part again , and never guess the mystery of the future , which wrap they round like a cloud , never say of each other . there be my fate ; and then they meet again , and again , as hazard will , and never know that they be drift to their doom . ' 

 Mary rang bell and give order , just as she have do in that summer gloame a year ago . the young man have arrive just at the same hour , on the stroke of nine , when the eight o'clock dinner be over and do with ; for a tete - a - tete meal with Fršulein M ller be not a feast to be prolong on account of its felicity . perhaps they have so contrive as to arrive exactly at this hour . 

 Lady Maulevrier receive they both with extreme cordiality . but the young man see a change for the bad in the invalid since the spring . the face be thin , the eye too bright , the flush upon the hollow cheek have a hectic tinge , the voice be feebler . Hammond be remind of a falcon or an eagle pining and waste in a cage . 

 ' I be very glad to see you , Mr. Hammond , ' say Lady Maulevrier , give he her hand , and address he with unwonted cordiality . ' it be a happy thought that bring you and Maulevrier here . when an old woman be as near the grave as I be her relative ought to look after she . I shall be glad to have a little private conversation with you to - morrow , Mr. Hammond , if you can spare I a few minute . ' 

 ' as many hour , if your ladyship please , ' say Hammond . ' my time be entirely at your service . ' 

 ' oh , no , you will want to be roam about the hill with Mary , discuss your plan for the future . I shall not encroach too much on your time . but I be very glad you be here . ' 

 ' we shall only trespass on you for a few day , ' say Maulevrier , ' just a flying visit . ' 

 ' how be it that you be not both at Henley ? ' ask Mary . ' I think all the world be at Henley . ' 

 ' who be Henley ? what be Henley ? ' demand Maulevrier , pretend ignorance . 

 ' I believe Maulevrier have lose so much money back his college boat on previous occasion that he be glad to run away from the regatta this year , ' say Hammond . 

 ' I have a sister there , ' reply his friend . ' that be an all - sufficient explanation . when a fellow 's woman - kind take to go to race and regatta it be high time for he to stop away . ' 

 ' have you see Lesbia lately ? ' ask his grandmother . 

 ' about ten day ago . ' 

 ' and do she seem happy ? ' 

 Maulevrier shrug his shoulder . 

 ' she be vacillate between the refusal or the acceptance of a million of money and four or five fine house . I do not   know whether that condition of mind mean happiness . I should call it an intermediate state . ' 

 ' why do you make silly joke about serious question ? do you think Lesbia mean to accept this Mr. Smithson ? ' 

 ' all London think so . ' 

 ' and be he a good man ? ' 

 ' good for a hundred thousand pound at half an hour 's notice . ' 

 ' be he worthy of your sister ? ' 

 Maulevrier pause , look at his grandmother with a curious expression , and then reply 

 ' I think he be quite . ' 

 ' then I be content that she should marry he , ' say Lady Maulevrier , ' although he be a nobody . ' 

 ' oh , but he be a very important nobody , a nobody who can get a peerage next year , back by the Maulevrier influence , which I suppose would count for something . ' 

 ' Most of my friend be dead , ' say Lady Maulevrier , ' but there be a few survivor of the past who might help I . ' 

 ' I do not   think there will be any difficulty or doubt about the peerage . Smithson stump up very handsomely at the last General Election , and the Conservatives be not strong enough to be ungrateful . " these have no master . " ' 

 CHAPTER XXXII . 

 WAYS and MEANS . 


 the three day that follow be among the happy day of Mary Haselden 's young life . Lady Maulevrier have become strangely indulgent . a soften influence of some kind have work upon that haughty spirit , and it seem as if her whole nature be change or it might be , Mary think , that this soft side of her character have always be turn to Lesbia , while to Mary herself it be altogether new . Lesbia have be the peach on the sunny southern wall , ripen and redden in a flood of sunshine ; Mary have be the stunt fruit grow in a north - east corner , hide among leaf , blow upon by cold wind green and hard and sour for lack of the warm bright light . and now Mary feel the sunshine , and grow glad and gay in those glowing beam . 

 ' dear grandmother , I believe you be begin to love I , ' she say , bend over to arrange the invalid 's pillow in the July morning , the fresh mountain air blow in upon old and young from the great open window , like a caress . 

 ' I be begin to know you , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , gently . 

 ' I think it be the magic of love , Mary , that have sweeten and soften your nature , and endear you to I . I think you have grow ever so much sweet a girl since your engagement . or it may be that you be the same always , and it be I who be blind . Lesbia be all in all to I . all in all and now I be nothing to she , ' she murmur , to herself rather than to Mary . 

 ' I be so proud to think that you see an improvement in I since my engagement , ' say Mary , modestly . ' I have try very hard to improve myself , so that I might be more worthy of he . ' 

 ' you be worthy , Mary , worthy of the good and the high : and I believe that , although you be make what the world call a very bad match , you be marry wisely . you be wed yourself to a life of obscurity ; but what do that matter , if it be a happy life ? I have know what it be to pursue the phantom fortune , and to find youth and hope and happiness vanish from the pathway which I follow . ' 

 ' dear grandmother , I wish you have be able to marry the man of your choice , ' answer Mary , tenderly . 

 she be ready to weep over that waste life of her grandmother 's ; to weep for that forced parting of true lover , albeit the tragedy be half a century old . 

 ' I should have be a happy woman and a well woman if fate have be kind to I , Mary , ' answer Lady Maulevrier , gravely ; ' and now that I be daily draw nearer the land of shadow , I will not stand in the way of faithful lover . I have a fancy , Mary , that I have not many month to live . ' 

 ' only an invalid 's fancy , ' say Mary , stoop down to kiss the pale forehead , so full of thought and care ; ' only a morbid fancy , nurse in the monotony of this quiet room . Maulevrier and Jack and I must find some way of amuse you . ' 

 ' you will never amuse I out of that conviction , my dear . I can see the shadow lengthen and the sand run out . there be but a few grain leave in the glass , Mary ; and while those last I should like to see you and Mr. Hammond marry . I should like to feel that your fate be settle before I go . God know what confusion and trouble may follow my death . ' 

 this be say with a sharp ring of despair . 

 ' I be not go to leave you , grandmother , ' say Mary . 

 ' not even for the man you love ? you be a good girl , Mary . Lesbia have forsake I for a less temptation . ' 

 ' Grandmother , that be hardly fair . it be your own wish to have Lesbia present this season , ' remonstrate Mary , loyal to the absent . 

 ' true , my dear . I see she be very tired of her life here , and I think it be well . but I be sorely afraid London have spoil she . no , Mary , you can stay with I to the end , if you like . there be room enough for you and your husband under this roof . I like this Mr. Hammond . his be the only face that ever recall the face of the dead . yes , I like he ; and although I know nothing about he except what Maulevrier tell I and that be of the scanty still I feel , somehow , that I can trust he . send your lover to I , Mary . I want to have a serious talk with he . ' 

 Mary run off to obey , flutter , blushing , and tremble . this idea of marriage in the immediate future be to be the last degree startling . a year have seem a very long time ; and she have be tell that she and her lover must wait a year at the very least ; so that vision of marriage have seem afar off in the dim shadowland of the future . she have be tell nothing by her lover of where she be to live , or what her life be to be like when she be his wife . and now she be tell that they be to be marry almost immediately , that they be to live in the house where she have be rear , in that familiar land of hill and water , that they be to roam about the dale and mountain together , they two , as man and wife . the whole thing be wonderful , bewildering , impossible almost . 

 this be on the first morning after Mr. Hammond 's arrival . Maulevrier have go off to hunt the Rotha for otter , and be up to his waist in the water , no doubt , by this time . Hammond be stroll up and down the terrace in front of the house , look at the green expanse of Fairfield , the dark bulk of Seat Sandal , the near crest of Helm Crag and Silver Howe . 

 ' you be to come to her ladyship directly , please , ' say Mary , go up to he . 

 he take both her hand , draw she nearer to he , smile down at she . they have be sit side by side at the breakfast table half - an - hour ago , he wait upon she as she pour out the tea ; yet by his tender greeting and the delight in his face it might have be suppose they have not meet for week . such be the sweet inanity of love . 

 ' what do her ladyship want with I , darling ? and why be you blush ? ' he ask . 

 ' I I think she be go to talk about our marriage , ' falter Mary . 

 ' " why , I will talk to she upon this theme until mine eyelid can no long wag , " ' quote Hammond . ' take I to she , Mary . I hope her ladyship be grow sensible . ' 

 ' she be very kind , very sweet . she have change so much of late . ' 

 Mary go with he to the door of her ladyship 's sitting - room , and there leave he to go in alone . she go to the library that room over which a gloomy shadow seem to have hang ever since that awful winter afternoon when Mary find Lady Maulevrier lie on the floor in the twilight . but it be a noble room , and in her studious hour Mary love to sit here , wall round with book , and able to consult or dip into as many volume as she like . to - day , however , her mind be not attuned to study . she sit with a volume of Macaulay open before she : but her thought be not with the author . she be wonder what those two be say in the room overhead , and find all attempt at read futile , she let her head sink back upon the cushion of her deep luxurious chair , and sit with her dreamy eye fix on the summer landscape and her thought with her lover . 

 Lady Maulevrier look very wan and tired in the bright morning light , when Mr. Hammond seat himself beside her sofa . the change in her appearance since the spring be more marked to - day than it have seem to he last night in the dim lamplight . yes , there be need here for a speedy settlement of all earthly matter . the traveller be near the mysterious end of the journey . the summon might come at any hour . 

 ' Mr. Hammond , I feel a confidence in your integrity , your goodness of heart , and high principle which I never think I could feel for a man of whom I know so little , ' begin Lady Maulevrier , gravely . ' all I know of you or your antecedent be what my grandson have tell I and I must say that the information so give have be very meagre . and yet I believe in you and yet I be go to trust you , wholly , blindly , implicitly and I be go to give you my granddaughter , ever so much soon than I intend to give she to you . soon , very soon , if you will have she ! ' 

 ' I will have she to - morrow , if there be time to get a special licence , ' exclaim Hammond , bend down to kiss the dowager 's hand , radiant with delight . 

 ' you shall marry she very soon , if you like , marry she by special licence , in this room . I should like to see your wedding . I have a strange impatience to behold one of my granddaughter happily marry , to know that her future be secure , that come weal , come woe , she be safe in the protection of a brave true man . I be not scared by the idea of a little poverty . that be often the good education for youth . but while you and I be alone we may as well talk about way and mean . perhaps you may hardly feel prepared to take upon yourself the burden of a wife this year . ' 

 ' as well this year as next . I be not afraid . ' 

 ' young man be so rash . however , as long as I live your responsibility will be only nominal . this house will be Mary 's home , and yours whenever you be able to occupy it . of course I should not like to interfere with your professional effort but if you be cultivate literature , why book can be write at Fellside well than in London . this lakeland of our have be the nursery of deathless writer . but I feel that my day be number and when I be dead well death be always a cause of change and trouble of some kind , and Mary will profit very little by my death . the bulk of my fortune be leave to Lesbia . I have teach she to consider herself my heiress ; and it would be unjust to alter my will . ' 

 ' pray do not dream of such a thing there be no need Mary will be rich enough , ' exclaim Hammond , hastily . 

 ' with five hundred a year and the fruit of your industry , ' say Lady Maulevrier . ' yes , yes , with modest aspiration and simple habit , people can live happily , honourably , on a few hundred a year . and if you really mean to devote yourself to literature , and do not mind bury yourself alive in this lake district until you have make your name as a writer , why the problem of way and mean will be easily solve . ' 

 ' Dear Lady Maulevrier , I be not afraid of way and mean . that be the last question which need trouble you . I tell Lesbia when I offer myself to she nearly a year ago , that if she would trust I , if she would cleave to I , poverty should never touch she , sordid care should never come near her dwelling . but she could not believe I . she be like Thomas the twin . I could show she no palpable security for my promise and she would not believe for the promise ' sake . Mary trust I ; and Mary shall not regret her confidence . ' 

 ' ah ! it be different with Lesbia , ' sigh Lady Maulevrier . ' I teach she to be ambitious . she have be school to set a high price upon herself . I know she care for you very much , even . but she could not face poverty ; or , if you like , I will say that she could not face an obscure existence sacrifice her ambition , a justifiable ambition in one so lovely , at the bidding of her first wooer . and then , again , she be tell that if she marry you , she would for ever forfeit my regard . you must not blame she for obey I . ' 

 ' I do not blame she ; for I have win the peerless pearl the jewel above all price a perfect woman . and now , dear Lady Maulevrier , give I but your consent , and I be off to York this afternoon , to interview the archbishop , and get the special licence , which will allow I to we d my darling here by your couch to - morrow afternoon . ' 

 ' I have no objection to your get the licence immediately ; but you must let I write a cheque before you go . a special licence be expensive I believe it cost fifty pound . ' 

 ' if it cost a thousand I should not think it dear . but I have a notion that I shall be able to get the licence cheap . you have make I wild with happiness . ' 

 ' but you must not refuse my cheque . ' 

 ' indeed I must , Lady Maulevrier . I be not quite such a pauper as you think I . ' 

 ' but fifty pound and the expense of the journey ; an outlay altogether unexpected on your part . I begin to fear that you be very reckless . a spendthrift shall never marry my granddaughter , with my consent . ' 

 ' I have never yet spend above half my income . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier look at he in wonderment and perplexity . have the young man go suddenly out of his mind , overwhelm by the greatness of his bliss ? 

 ' but I think you be poor , ' she falter . 

 ' it have please you to think so , dear Lady Maulevrier ; but I have more than enough for all my want , and I shall be able to provide a fitting home for my Mary , when you can spare she to preside over her own establishment . ' 

 ' Establishment ' seem rather a big word , but Lady Maulevrier suppose that in this case it mean a cook and housemaid , with perhaps later on a boy in button , to break window and block the pantry sink with missing teaspoon . 

 ' well , Mr. Hammond , this be quite an agreeable surprise , ' she say , after a brief silence . ' I really think you be poor as poor as a young man of gentlemanlike habit could be , and yet exist . perhaps you will wonder why , think this , I bring myself to consent to your marriage with my granddaughter . ' 

 ' it be a great proof of your confidence in I , or in Providence , ' reply Hammond , smile . 

 ' it be no such thing . I be govern by a sentiment a memory . it be my love for the dead which soften my heart towards you , John Hammond . ' 

 ' indeed ! ' he murmur , softly . 

 ' there be but one man in this world I ever fondly love the love of my youth my dearest and well , in the day when my heart be fresh and innocent and unambitious . that man be Ronald Hollister , afterwards Lord Hartfield . and yours be the only face that ever recall his to my mind . it be but a vague likeness a look now and then ; but slight as that likeness be it have be enough to make my heart yearn towards you , as the heart of a mother to her son . ' 

 John Hammond kneel beside the sofa , and bent his handsome face over the pale face on the pillow , imprint such a kiss as a son might have give . his eye be full of tear . 

 ' Dear Lady Maulevrier , think that it be the spirit of the dead which bless you for your fidelity to old memory , ' he say , tenderly . 

 CHAPTER XXXIII . 

 BY SPECIAL LICENCE . 


 after that interview with John Hammond all the arrangement for the marriage be plan by Lady Maulevrier with a calm and business - like capacity which seem extraordinary in one so frail and helpless . for a little while after Hammond leave she she remain lose in a reverie , deeply affect by the speech and manner of her granddaughter 's lover , as he give she that first kiss of duty and affection , the affection of one who in that act declare the allegiance of a close and holy bond . 

 yes , she tell herself , this marriage , humble as it might be , be altogether satisfactory . her own feeling towards the man of her granddaughter 's choice be one of instinctive affection . her heart have yearn to he from the beginning of their acquaintance ; but she have school herself to hide all indication of she like for he , she have make every effort to keep he at a distance , deem his very merit a source of danger in a household where there be two fresh impressionable girl . 

 and despite all her caution and care he have succeed in win one of those girl : and she be glad , very glad , that he have so succeed in baffle her prudence . and now it be agreeable to discover that he be not quite such a pauper as she have suppose he to be . 

 her heart feel light than it have be for some time when she set about plan the wedding . 

 the first step in the business be to send for James Steadman . he come immediately , grave and quiet as of old , and stand with his serious eye bent upon the face of his mistress , await her instruction . 

 ' Lady Mary be go to be married to Mr. Hammond , by special licence , in this room , to - morrow afternoon , if it can be manage so soon , ' say Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' I be very glad to hear it , my lady , ' answer Steadman , without the faint indication of surprise . 

 ' why be you so particularly glad ? ' ask his mistress , look at he sharply . 

 ' because Lady Mary 's presence in this house be a source of danger to your arrangement . she be very energetic and enterprise very shrewd and well , she be a woman so I suppose there can be no harm in say she be somewhat inquisitive . thing will be much safe here when Lady Mary be go ! ' 

 ' but she will not be go she be not go away except for a very brief honeymoon . I can not possibly do without she . she have become necessary to my life , Steadman ; and there be so little left of that life now , that there be no need for I to sacrifice the last gleam of sunshine . the girl be very sweet , and loving , and true . I be not half fond enough of she in the past ; but she have make herself very dear to I of late . there be many thing in this life , Steadman , which we only find out too late . ' 

 ' but , surely , my lady , Lady Mary will leave Fellside to go to a home of her own after her marriage . ' 

 ' no , I tell you , Steadman , ' his mistress answer , with a touch of impatience ; ' Lady Mary and her husband will make this house their home so long as I be here . it will not be long . ' 

 ' God grant it may be very long before you cease to be mistress here , ' answer Steadman , with real feeling ; and then in a low tone he go on : ' pardon I , my lady , for the suggestion , but do you think it wise to have Mr. Hammond here as a resident ? ' 

 ' why should it not be wise ? Mr. Hammond be a gentleman . ' 

 ' true , my lady ; but any accident , such as that which bring Lady Mary into the old garden - - ' 

 ' no such accident need occur it must not occur , Steadman , ' exclaim Lady Maulevrier , with kindle eye . she who have so long rule supreme be not inclined to have any desire of hers question . ' there must have be gross carelessness that day carelessness on your part , or that stable door would never have be leave open . the key ought to have be in your possession . it ought not to have be in the power of the stableman to open that door . as to Mr. Hammond 's presence at Fellside , I can not see any danger any reason why harm should come of it , more than of Lord Maulevrier 's presence here in the past . ' 

 ' the two gentleman be so different , my lady , ' say Steadman , with a gloomy brow . ' his lordship be so light - hearted and careless , his mind take up with his horse , gun , dog , fishing , shooting , and all kind of sport . he be not a gentleman to take much notice of anything out of his own line . but this Mr. Hammond be different a very thoughtful gentleman , an inquire mind , as one would say . ' 

 ' Steadman , you be get cowardly in your old age . the danger such a risk as you hint at , must be grow less and less every day . after forty year of security - - ' 

 ' Security ' echoed Steadman , with a monosyllabic laugh which express intense bitterness . ' say forty year during which I have feel myself upon the edge of a precipice every day and every hour . security ! but perhaps you be right , my lady , I be grow old and nervous , a feebler man than I be a few year ago , feebler in body and mind . let Mr. Hammond make his home here , if it please your ladyship to have he . so long as I be well and able to get about there can be no danger of anything awkward happen . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier look alarmed . 

 ' but you have no expectation of fall ill , I hope , Steadman ; you have no premonition of any malady ? ' 

 ' no , my lady , none except the malady of old age . I feel that I be not the man I once be , that be all . my brain be get woolly , and my sight be cloud now and then . and if I be to fall ill suddenly - - ' 

 ' oh , it would be terrible , it would be a dire calamity ! there be your wife , certainly , to look after thing , but - - ' 

 ' my wife would do she good , my lady . she be a faithful creature , but she be not yes , without any unkindness I must say that Mrs. Steadman be not a genius ! ' 

 ' oh , Steadman , you must not fail I ! I be horror - stricken at the mere idea , ' exclaim Lady Maulevrier . ' after forty year great God ! it would be terrible . Lesbia , Mary , Maulevrier ! the great , malignant , babble world outside these door . I be hem round with peril . for God 's sake preserve your strength . take care of your health . you be my strong rock . if you feel that there be anything amiss with you , or that your strength be fail , consult Mr. Horton neglect no precaution . the safety of this house , of the family honour , hang upon you . ' 

 ' pray do not agitate yourself , my lady , ' entreat Steadman . ' I be wrong to trouble you with my fear . I shall not fail you , be sure . although I be get old , I shall hold out to the end . ' 

 ' the end can not be very far off , ' say Lady Maulevrier , gloomily . 

 ' I think that forty year ago , my lady . but you be right the end must be near now . yes , it must be near . and now , my lady , your order about the wedding . ' 

 ' it will take place to - morrow , as I tell you , in this room . you will go to the Vicar and ask he to officiate . his two daughter will no doubt consent to be Lady Mary 's bridesmaid . you will make the request in my name . perhaps the Vicar will call this afternoon and talk matter over with I . Lady Mary and her husband will go to Cumberland for a brief honeymoon a week at most and then they will come back to Fellside . tell Mrs. Power to prepare the east wing for they . she will make one of the room into a boudoir for Lady Mary ; and let everything be as bright and pretty as good taste can make it . she can telegraph to London for any new furniture that may be want to complete her arrangement . and now send Lady Mary to I . ' 

 Mary come , fresh from the pine - wood , where she have be walk with her lover ; her lover of to - day , her husband to - morrow . he have tell she how he be to start for York directly after luncheon , and to come back by the early train next day , and how they two be to be married to - morrow afternoon . 

 ' it be more wonderful than any dream that I ever dream . ' exclaim Mary . ' but how can it be ? I have not even a wedding gown . ' 

 ' a fig for wedding gown ! it be Mary I be to we d , not her gown . be you clothe like patient Grisel I should be content . besides you have no end of pretty gown . and you be to be dress for travel , remember ; for I be go to carry you off to Lodore directly we be married , and you will have to clamber up the rocky bed of the waterfall to see the sun set behind the Borrowdale hill in your wedding gown . it have well be one of those neat little tailor gown which become you so well . ' 

 ' I will wear whatever you tell I , ' answer Mary . ' I shall always dress to please you , and not the outside world . ' 

 ' will you , my Griselda . some day you shall be dress as Grisel be 

 " in a cloth of gold that brighte shone , 
 with a coroune of many a riche stone . " 
 ' yes , you darling , when you be Lord Chancellor ; and till that day come I will wear tailor gown , linsey - wolsey , anything you like , ' cry Mary , laugh . 

 she run to her grandmother 's room , ineffably content , without a thought of trousseau or finery ; but then Mary Haselden be one of those few young woman for whom life be not a question of fashionable raiment . 

 ' Mary , I be go to send you off upon your honeymoon to - morrow afternoon , ' say Lady Maulevrier , smile at the bright , happy face which be bent over she . ' will you come back and nurse a fretful old woman when the honeymoon be over ? ' 

 ' the honeymoon will never be over , ' answer Mary , joyously . ' our wed life be to be one long honeymoon . but I will come back in a very few day , and take care of you . I be not go to let you do without I , now that you have learn to love I . ' 

 ' and will you be content to stay with I when your husband have go to London ? ' 

 ' yes , but I shall try to prevent his go very often , or stay very long . I shall try to wind myself into his heart , so that there will be an ache void there when we be part . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier proceed to tell Mary all her arrangement . three handsome room in the east wing , a bedroom , dressing - room , and boudoir , be to be make ready for the newly - married couple . Fršulein M ller be to be dismiss with a retire pension , in order that Lady Mary and her husband might feel themselves master and mistress in the low part of the house . 

 ' and if your husband really mean to devote himself to literature , he can have no well workshop than the library I have put together , ' say Lady Maulevrier . 

 ' and no well adviser and guide than you , dear grandmother , you who have read everything that have be write worth read during the last half century . ' 

 ' I have read a great deal , Mary , but I hardly know if I be any wiser on that account , ' answer Lady Maulevrier . ' after all , however much of other people 's wisdom we may devour , it be in ourselves that we be thus , or thus . our past folly rise up against we at the end of life ; and we see how little our book - learning have help we to stand against foolish impulse , against evil passion . " be good , " Mary , " and let who will be wise , " as the poet say . a faithful heart be your only anchor in the stormy sea of life . my dear , I be so glad you be go to be marry . ' 

 ' it be very sudden , ' say Mary . 

 ' very sudden ; yet in your case that do not much matter . you have quite make up your mind about Mr. Hammond , I believe . ' 

 ' make up my mind ! I begin to worship he the first night he come here . ' 

 ' foolish child . well , there be no need to wait for settlement . you have only your allowance as Lord Maulevrier 's daughter a first charge on the estate , which can not be make away with or anticipate , and of which no husband can deprive you . ' 

 ' he shall have every sixpence of it , ' murmur Mary . 

 ' and Mr. Hammond , though he tell I he be well off than I suppose , can have nothing to settle . so there will be nothing forfeit by a marriage without settlement . ' 

 Mary could not enter upon the question . it be even of less importance than the wedding gown . 

 the gong sound for luncheon . 

 ' Steadman 's dogcart be to take Mr. Hammond to the station at half - past two , ' say Lady Maulevrier , ' so you have well go and give he his luncheon . ' 

 Mary need no second bidding . she fly downstairs , and meet her lover in the hall . 

 what a happy luncheon it be ! Fršulein ' mounched , and mounche , and mounche , ' like the sailor 's wife eat chestnut : but those two lover lunch upon moonshine , upon each other 's little word and little look , upon their own ineffable bliss . they sit side by side , and help each other to the nice thing on the table , but neither could eat , and they get considerably mixed in their way of eat , take chutnee with strawberry cream , and currant jelly with asparagus . what do it matter ? everything taste of bliss . 

 ' you have have absolutely nothing to eat , ' say Mary , piteously , as the dogcart come grind round upon the dry gravel . 

 ' oh , I have do splendidly thank . I have just have a macaroon and some of that capital gorgonzola . God bless you , dearest , and a revoir , a revoir to - morrow . ' 

 ' and to - morrow I shall be Mary Hammond , ' cry Mary , clasp her hand . ' be not   it capital fun ? ' 

 they be in the porch alone . the servant be all at dinner , save the groom with the cart . Miss M ller be still munch at the well - spread table in the dining - room . 

 John Hammond fold his sweetheart in his arm for one brief embrace ; there be no time for loitering . in another moment he be spring into the cart . a shake of the rein , and he be drive slowly down the steep avenue . 

 ' life be full of parting , ' Mary say to herself , as she watch the last glimpse of the dogcart between the tree down in the road below , ' but this one be to be very short , thank God . ' 

 she wonder what she should do with herself for the rest of the afternoon , and finally , find that she be not want by her grandmother until afternoon tea , she set out upon a round of visit to her favourite cottager , to bid they a long farewell as a spinster . 

 ' you will be away a long time , I suppose , Lady Mary ? ' say one of her humble friend ; ' you will be go to Switzerland or Italy , or some of those foreign part where great lady and gentleman travel for their honeymoon ? ' 

 but Mary declare that she would be absent a week at long . she be come back to take care of her invalid grandmother ; and she be not go to marry a great gentleman , but a man who would have to work for his living . 

 she go back to Fellside , and read the Times , and pour out Lady Maulevrier 's tea , and sit on her low stool by the sofa , and the old and the young woman be as happy and confidential together as if they have be always the near and dearest to each other . her ladyship have see Miss M ller , and have inform that excellent person that her service at Fellside would no long be require after Lady Mary 's marriage ; but that her devotion to her duty during the last fourteen year should be reward by a pension which , together with her saving , would enable she to spend the rest of her day in repose . Miss M ller be duly grateful , and own to a tender longing for the Heimath , and declare herself ready to retire from her post whenever her ladyship please . 

 ' I shall go back to Germany directly I leave you , and I shall live and die there , unless I be want by one of my old pupil . but should Lady Lesbia or Lady Mary need my service for their daughter , in day to come , they can command I . for no one else will I abandon the Fatherland . ' 

 the Fršulein thus easily dispose of , Lady Mary feel that matrimony would verily mean independence . and yet she be prepared to regard her husband as her master . she mean to obey he in all meekness and reverence of spirit . 

 she spend the rest of the afternoon and the whole of the evening in her grandmother 's sitting - room , din tete - a - tete with the invalid for the first time since her illness . Lady Maulevrier talk much of Mary 's future , and of Lesbia 's ; but it be evident that she be full of uneasiness upon the latter subject . 

 ' I do not   know what Lesbia be go to do with her life , ' she say , with a sigh . ' her letter tell I of nothing but gown and party ; and Georgina Kirkbank can only expatiate upon Mr. Smithson 's wealth , and the grand position he be go to occupy by - and - by . I should like to see both my granddaughter marry before I die yes , I should like to see Lesbia 's fate secure , if she be to be only Lady Lesbia Smithson . ' 

 ' she can not fail to make a good match , grandmother , ' say Mary . 

 ' I be begin to lose faith in her future , ' answer Lady Maulevrier . ' there seem to be a fatality about the career of particularly attractive girl . they be too confident of their power to succeed in life . they trifle with fortune , fascinate the wrong people , and keep the right people at arm 's length . I think if I have be Lesbia 's guide in society her first season would have count for more than it be likely to count for under Lady Kirkbank 's management . I should have awaken Lesbia from the dream of dress and dance the mere butterfly life of a girl who never look beyond the present moment . but now go and give order about your packing , Mary . it be past ten , and Clara have well pack your trunk early to - morrow morning . ' 

 Clara be a modest easedale damsel , who have be promote to be Lady Mary 's personal attendant , when the more mature Kibble have go away with Lady Lesbia . Mary require very little wait upon , but she be not the less glad to have a neat little smile maiden devoted to her service , ready to keep her room neat and trim , to go on errand to the cottager , to arrange the flower in the old china bowl , and to make herself generally useful . 

 it seem a strange thing to have to furnish a trousseau from the wardrobe of everyday life a trousseau in which nothing , except half - a - dozen pair of glove , a pair of boot , and a few odd and end of lace and ribbon would be actually new . Mary think very little of the matter , but the position of thing strike she maid as altogether extraordinary and unnatural . 

 ' you should have see the thing Miss Freeman have , Lady Mary , ' exclaim the damsel , ' the daughter of that cotton - spin gentleman from Manchester , who live at the Gables you should have see her new gown and thing when she be marry . Mrs. Freeman 's maid keep company with my brother James he be in the stable at Freeman 's , you know , Lady Mary and she ask I in to look at the trousseau two day before the wedding . I never see such beautiful dress such hat such bonnet such jacket and mantle . it be like go into one of those grand shop at York , and have all the thing in the shop pull out for one to look at such silk and satin and trim ah ! how those dress be trim . the mystery be how the young lady could ever get herself into they , or sit down when she would get one of they on . ' 

 ' instrument of torture , Clara . I should hate such gown , even if I be go to marry a rich man , as I suppose Miss Freeman be . ' 

 ' not a bit of it , Lady Mary . she be only go to marry a Bolton doctor with a small practice ; but her maid tell I she be determined she would get all she could out of her pa , in case he should lose all his money and go bankrupt . they say that trousseau cost two thousand pound . ' 

 ' well , Clara , I would rather have my tailor gown , in which I can scramble about the ghyll and crag just as I like . ' there be a pale yellow indian silk , smother with soft yellow lace , which would serve for a wedding gown ; for indifferent as Mary be to the great clothe question , she want to look in some wise as a bride . a neat chocolate - colour cloth , almost new from the tailor 's hand , with a little cloth toque to match , would do for the wedding journey . all the detail of Mary 's wardrobe be the perfection of neatness . she have grow very neat and careful in her habit since her engagement , anxious to be industrious and frugal in all thing a really handy housewife for a hard - work bread - winner . and now she be tell that Mr. Hammond be not so poor as she have think . she would not be oblige to stint herself , and manage , as she have suppose when she go about among the cottager , take lesson in household economy . it be almost a disappointment . 

 she and Clara finish the packing that night , Mary be much too excited for the possibility of sleep . there be not much to pack , only one roomy american trunk a trunk which hold everything a Gladstone bag for thing that might possibly be want in a hurry , and a handsome dressing - bag , Maulevrier 's last birthday gift to his sister . 

 Mary have receive no gift from her lover , save the plain gold engagement ring , and a few new book send straight from the publisher . Clara take care to inform her young mistress that Miss Freeman 's sweetheart have send she all manner of splendid present , scent bottle , photograph album , glove box , and other thing of beauty , albeit his mean be suppose to be nil . it be evident that Clara disapprove of Mr. Hammond 's conduct in this matter , and even suspect he of meanness . 

 ' he do ought to have send you his photograph , Lady Mary , ' say Clara , with a reproachful air . 

 ' I daresay he would have do so , Clara , but he have be photograph only once in his life . ' 

 ' lawk a mercy , Lady Mary ! why most young gentleman have themselves photograph in every new place they go to ; and as Mr. Hammond have be a traveller , like his lordship , I make sure he would have be photograph in knickerbocker and every other kind of attitude . ' 

 Mary have not refrain from ask for her lover 's portrait ; and he have tell she that he have carefully abstain from have his countenance reproduce in any manner since his fifteenth year , when he have be photograph at his mother 's desire . 

 ' the present fashion of photograph star out of every stationer 's window make a man 's face public property , ' he tell Mary . ' I do not   want every street arab in London to recognise I . ' 

 ' but you be not a public man , ' say Mary . ' your photograph would not be in all the window ; although , in my humble opinion , you be a very handsome man . ' 

 Hammond blush , laugh , and turn the conversation , and Mary have to exist without any picture of her lover . 

 ' Millais shall paint I in his grand Reynolds manner by - and - by , ' he tell Mary . 

 ' Millais ! oh , Jack ! when will you and I be able to give a thousand or so for a portrait ? ' 

 ' ah , when , indeed ? but we may as well enjoy our day - dream , like Alnaschar , without smash our basket of crockery . ' 

 and now Mary , who have manage to exist without the picture , be to have the original . he be to be all her own her master , her lord , her love , after to - morrow unto eternity , in life , and in the grave , and in the dim hereafter beyond the grave , they two be to be one . in heaven there be to be no marrying or give in marriage , Mary be tell ; but her own heart cry aloud to she that the happily wed must remain link in heaven . God would not part the bless soul of true lover . 

 a short sleep , break by happy dream , and it be morning , Mary 's wedding morning , fairest of summer day , July in all her beauty . Mary go to her grandmother 's room , and wait upon she at breakfast . 

 Lady Maulevrier be in excellent spirit . 

 ' everything be arrange , Mary . I have have a telegram from Hammond , who have get the licence , and will come at half - past one . at three the Vicar will come to marry you , his daughter , Katie and Laura , act as your bridesmaid . ' 

 ' Bridesmaids ! ' exclaim Mary . ' I forget all about bridesmaid . be I really to have any ? ' 

 ' you will have two girl of your own age to bear you company , at any rate . I have ask dear old Horton to be present ; and he , Fršulein , and Maulevrier will complete the party . it will not be a brilliant wedding , Mary , or a costly ceremonial , except for the licence . ' 

 ' and poor Jack will have to pay for that , ' say Mary , with a long face . 

 ' Poor Jack refuse to let I pay for it , ' answer Lady Maulevrier . ' he be vastly independent , and I fear somewhat reckless . ' 

 ' I like he for his independence ; but he must not   be reckless , ' say Mary , severely . 

 he be to be the master in all thing ! and yet she be to exercise a restrain influence , she be to guard he against his own weakness , his too generous impulse . her voice be to be the voice of prudence . this be how Mary understand the marriage tie . 

 under ordinary condition Mary would have be in the avenue , lie in wait for her lover , eager to get the very first glimpse of he when he arrive , to see he before he have brush the dust of the journey from his raiment . but to - day she hang back . she stay in her grandmother 's room and sit beside the sofa , shy , and even a little downcast . this lover who be so soon to be transform into a husband be a formidable personage . she dare not rush forth to greet he . perhaps he have change his mind by this time , and be sorry he have ever ask she to marry he . perhaps he think he be be hustle into a marriage . he have be tell that he be to wait at least a year . and now , all in a moment , he be send off to get a special licence . how could she be quite sure that he like this kind of treatment ? 

 if there be any faith to be place in the human countenance , Mr. Hammond be in no wise an unwilling bridegroom ; for his face beam with happy light as he come into the room presently , follow by an elderly man with grey hair and whisker , and in a strictly professional frock coat , whom the butler announce as Mr. Dorncliffe . Lady Maulevrier look startled , somewhat offend even at this intrusion , and she give Mr. Dorncliffe a very haughty salutation , which be almost more crush than no salutation at all . 

 Mary stand up by her grandmother 's sofa , and look rather frightened . 

 ' Dear Lady Maulevrier , ' say Hammond , ' I venture to telegraph to my lawyer to meet I at York last night , and come on here with I this morning . he have prepare a settlement , which I should like you to hear he read , and which he will explain to you , if necessary , while molly and I go for a stroll in the ground . ' 

 he have never call she molly before . he put his arm round she with a proud air of possession , even under her grandmother 's eye . and she nestle close up to his side , forget everything but the delight of belong to he . 

 they go downstairs , and through the billiard room to the terrace , and from the terrace to the tennis lawn , where John Hammond sit read Heine nearly a year ago , just before he propose to Lesbia . 

 ' do you remember that day ? ' ask Mary , look at he , solemnly . 

 ' I remember every day and every hour we have spend together since I begin to love you , ' answer Hammond . 

 ' ah , but this be before you begin to love I , ' say Mary , with a piteous little grimace . ' this be while you be love Lesbia as hard as ever you could . do not   you remember the day you propose to she a lovely summer day like this , the lake just as blue , the sun shin upon Fairfield just as it be shin now , and you sit there read Heine those sweet , sweet verse , that seem make of sigh and tear ; and every now and then you pause and look up at Lesbia , and there be more love in your eye than in all Heine 's poetry , though that brim over with love . ' 

 ' but how do you know all this , molly ? you be not here . ' 

 ' I be not very far off . I be behind those bush , watch and listen . I know you be in love with Lesbia , and I think you despise I , and I be very , very wretched ; and I listen afterwards when you propose to she there behind the pine tree and I hat she for refuse you , and I be afraid I hat you for propose to she . ' 

 ' when I ought to have be propose to my Molly , blind fool that I be , ' say Hammond , smile tenderly at she , smile , though his eye be dim with tear . ' my own sweet love , it be a terrible mistake , a mistake that might have cost I the happiness of a lifetime . but Fate be very good to I , and let I have my Mary after all . and now let we sit down under the old red beech and talk till it be time to go and get ready for our wedding . I suppose one ought to brush one 's hair and wash one 's hand for that kind of thing , even when the function be not on a ceremonious scale . ' 

 Mary laugh . 

 ' I have a pretty gown than this to be marry in , although it be not   a wedding gown , ' she say . 

 ' oh , by - the - by , I have something for you , ' say her lover , ' something in the way of ornament , but I do not   suppose you would care to wear they to - day . I will run and get they . ' 

 he go back to the house , leave Mary sit on the rustic bench under the fine old copper beech , a tree that have be stand long before Lady Maulevrier enlarge the old stone house into a stately villa . he return in a few minute , bring a morocco bag about the size of those usually carry by lawyer or lawyer ' clerk . 

 ' I do not   think I have give you anything since we be engage , Mary , ' he say , as he seat himself by her side . 

 Mary blush , remember how Clara , the maid , have remark upon this fact . 

 ' you give I my ring , ' she say , look down at the massive band of gold , ' and you have give I ever so many delightful book . ' 

 ' those be very humble gift , molly : but to - day I have bring you a wedding present . ' 

 he open the bag and take out a red morocco case , and then half - a - dozen more red morocco case of various shape and size . the first look new , but the other be old - fashioned and pass shabby , as if they have be knock about broker ' shop for the last quarter of a century . 

 ' there be my wedding gift , Mary , ' he say , hand she the new case . 

 it contain an exquisitely paint miniature of a very beautiful woman , in a large oval locket set with sapphire . 

 ' you have ask I for my portrait , dear , ' he say . ' I give you my mother 's rather than my own , because I love she as I never think to love again , till I know you . I should like you to wear that locket sometimes , Mary , as a kind of link between the love of the past and the love of the present . be my mother live , she would welcome and cherish my bride and my wife . she be dead , and you and she can never meet on earth : but I should like you to be familiar with the face which be once the light of my life . ' 

 Mary 's eye fill with tear as she gaze at the face in the miniature . it be the portrait of a woman of about thirty a face of exquisite refinement , of calm and pensive beauty . 

 ' I shall treasure this picture always , above all thing , ' she say : but ' why do you have it set so splendidly , Jack ? no gem be need to give your mother 's portrait value in my eye . ' 

 ' I know that , dear , but I want to make the locket worth wear . and now for the other case . the locket be your lover 's free gift , and be yours to keep and to bequeath to your child . these be heirloom , and yours only during your husband 's lifetime . ' 

 he open one of the large case , and on a bed of black velvet Mary beheld a magnificent diamond necklace , with a large pendant . he open another and display a set of spray for the hair . another contained earring , another bracelet , the last a tiara . 

 ' what be they for ? ' gasp Mary . 

 ' for my wife to wear . ' 

 ' oh , but I could never wear such thing , ' she exclaim , with an idea that these must be stage jewellery . ' they be paste , of course very beautiful for people who like that kind of thing but I do not . ' 

 she feel deeply shocked at this evidence of bad taste on the part of her lover . how the thing flash in the sunshine but so do the crystal drop in the old venetian girandole . 

 ' no , Molly , they be not paste ; they be brazilian diamond , and , as Maulevrier would say , they be as good as they make they . they be heirloom , molly . my dear mother wear they in her summer - tide of wed happiness . my grandmother wear they for thirty year before she ; my great grandmother wear they at the Court of Queen Charlotte , and they be wear at the Court of Queen Anne . they be nearly two hundred year old ; and those central stone in the tiara come out of a cap wear by the Great Mogul , and be the large table diamond know . they be historic , Mary . ' 

 ' why , they must be worth a fortune . ' 

 ' they be value at something over seventy thousand pound . ' 

 ' but why do not   you sell they ? ' exclaim Mary , open her eye wide with surprise , ' they would give you a handsome income . ' 

 ' they be not mine to sell , molly . do not I tell you that they be heirloom ? they be the family jewel of the Countesses of Hartfield . ' 

 ' then what be you ? ' 

 ' Ronald Hollister , Earl of Hartfield , and your adore lover ! ' 

 Mary give a cry of surprise , a cry of distress even . 

 ' oh , that be too dreadful ! ' she exclaim ; ' grandmother will be so unhappy . she have set her heart upon Lesbia marry Lord Hartfield , the son of the man she love . ' 

 ' I get wind of her wish more than a year ago , ' say Hartfield , ' from your brother ; and he and I hatch a little plot between we . he tell I Lesbia be not worthy of his friend 's devotion tell I that she be vain and ambitious that she have be educate to be so . I determine to come and try my fate . I would try to win she as plain John Hammond . if she be a true woman , I tell myself , vanity and ambition would be blow to the four wind , provide I could win her love . I come , I see she ; and to see be to love she . God know I try honestly to win she ; but I have swear to myself that I would woo she as John Hammond , and I do not waver in my resolution no , not when a word would have turn the scale . she like I , I think , a little ; but she do not like the notion of an obscure life as the wife of a hardworke professional man . the pomp and vanity of this world have it against love or liking , and she give I up .
