I thank God that the pomp and vanity prevail ; for this happy chance give I Mary , my sweet wordsworthian damsel , find , like the violet or the celandine , by the wayside , in Wordsworth 's own country . ' 

 ' and you be Lord Hartfield ! ' exclaim Mary , still lose in wonder , and with no elation at this change in the aspect of her life . ' I always know you be a great man . but poor grandmother ! it will be a dreadful disappointment to she . ' 

 ' I think not . I think she have learn my Molly 's value ; rather late , as I learn it ; and I believe she will be glad that one of her granddaughter should marry the son of her first lover . let we go to she , love , and see if she be reconcile to the idea , and whether the settlement be ready for execution . Dorncliffe and his clerk be work at it half through the night . ' 

 ' what be the good of a settlement ? ' ask Mary . ' I be sure I do not   want one . ' 

 ' Lady Hartfield must not be dependent upon her husband 's whim or pleasure for her milliner 's bill or her private charity , ' answer her lover , smile at her eagerness to repudiate anything business - like . 

 ' but I would rather be dependent on your pleasure . I shall never have any milliner 's bill ; and I be sure you would never deny I money for charity . ' 

 ' you shall not have to ask I for it , except when you have exceed your pin - money . I hope you will do that now and then , just to afford I the pleasure of do you a favour . ' 

 ' Hartfield , ' repeat Mary , to herself , as they go towards the house ; ' shall I have to call you Hartfield ? I do not   like the name nearly so well as Jack . ' 

 ' you shall call I Jack for old sake 's sake , ' say Hartfield , tenderly . 

 ' how do you think of such a name as Jack ? ' 

 ' rather an effort of genius , be not   it . well , first and foremost I be christen Ronald John all the Hollisters be christen John name of the founder of the race ; and , secondly , Maulevrier and I be always plain Mr. Morland and Mr. Hammond in our travel , and always call each other Jack and Jim . ' 

 ' how nice ! ' say Mary ; ' would you very much mind our be plain Mr. and Mrs. Hammond , while we be on our honeymoon trip ? ' 

 ' I should like it of all thing . ' 

 ' so should I. People will not take so much notice of we , and we can do what we like , and go where we like . ' 

 ' delightful ! we will even disguise ourselves as Cook 's tourist , if you like . I would not mind . ' 

 they be at the door of Lady Maulevrier 's sitting room by this time . they go in , and be greet with smile . 

 ' let I look at the Countess of Hartfield that be to be in half an hour , ' say her ladyship . ' oh , Mary , Mary , what a blind idiot I have be , and what a lucky girl you be ! I tell you once that you be wise than Lesbia , but I little think how much wise you have be . ' 

 CHAPTER XXXIV . 

 ' our love be new , and then but in the spring . ' 


 Henley Regatta be over . it have pass like a tale that be tell ; like Epsom and Ascot , and all the other glory of the London season . happy those for whom the glory of Henley , the grace of Ascot , the fever of Epsom , be not as weary as a twice - tell tale , bring with they only bitter memory of youth that have flee , of hope that have wither , of day - dream that have never be realise . there be some to whom that mad hastening from pleasure to pleasure , that rush from scene to scene of excitement , that eager crowd into one day and night of gaiety which might fairly relieve the placid monotony of a month 's domesticity , a month 's professional work some there be to whom this Vanity Fair be as a treadmill or the turning of a crank , the felon 's deep humiliation , purposeless , unprofitable , labour . 

 the regatta be over , and Lady Kirkbank and her charge hasten back to Arlington Street . Theirs be the very first departure ; albeit Mr. Smithson plead hard for a prolongation of their visit . the weather be exceptionally lovely , he urge . water picnic be delightful just now the bank be alive with the colour of innumerable wild flower , as beautiful and more poetical than the gorgeous flora of the Amazon or the Paraguay river . and Lady Lesbia have develop a genius for punting ; and lean against her pole , with her hair fly loose and sleeve roll up above the elbow , she be a subject for canvas or marble , Millais or Adams Acton . 

 ' when we be in Italy I will have she model , just in that attitude , and that dress , ' say Mr. Smithson . ' she will make a lovely companion for my Reading girl : one all repose and reverie , the other all life and action . Dear Lady Kirkbank , you really must stay for another week at least . why go back to the smoke and sultriness of town ? here we can almost live on the water ; and I will send to London for some people to make music for we in the evening , or if you miss your little game at " Nap , " we will play for an hour or so every night . it shall not be my fault if my house be not pleasant for you . ' 

 ' your house be charming , and I shall be here only too often in the day to come ; you will have more than enough of I then , I promise you , ' reply Georgie , with her girlish laugh , ' but we must not stop a day long now . People would begin to talk . besides , we have engagement for every hour of the week that be come , and for a fortnight after ; and then I suppose I ought to take Lesbia to the North to see her grandmother , and to discuss all the preparation and arrangement for this very serious event in which you and Lesbia be to be the chief performer . ' 

 ' I shall be very glad to go to Grasmere myself , and to make the acquaintance of my future grandmother - in - law , ' say Mr. Smithson . 

 ' you will be charm with she . she belong to the old school something of a fossil , perhaps , but a very dignified fossil . she have grow old in a rustic seclusion , and know less of our world than a mother abbess ; but she have read immensely , and be wonderfully clever . I be bind to tell you that she have very lofty idea about her granddaughter ; and I believe she will only be reconcile to Lesbia 's marriage with a commoner by the notion that you be sure of a peerage . I venture to hint as much in my letter to Lady Maulevrier yesterday . ' 

 a shade of sullenness creep over Horace Smithson 's visage . 

 ' I should hope that such settlement as I be in a position to make will convince Lady Maulevrier that I be a respectable suitor for her granddaughter , ex peerage , ' he say , somewhat haughtily . 

 ' my dear Smithson , do I not tell you that poor Lady Maulevrier be a century behind the time , ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , with an aggrieved look . ' if she be one of we , of course she would know that wealth be the paramount consideration , and that you be quite the good match of the season . but she be dreadfully arriťrťe , poor dear thing ; and she must have amuse herself with the day - dream of see Lesbia a duchess , or something of that kind . I shall tell she that Lesbia can be one of the queen of society without have strawberry leaf on her coach panel , and that my dear friend Horace Smithson be a much well match than a seedy duke . so do not   look cross , my dear fellow ; in I you have a friend who will never desert you . ' 

 ' thank , ' say Smithson , inwardly resolve that , so soon as this little transaction of his marriage be over , he would see as little of Georgie Kirkbank and her cotton frock and schoolgirl hat as bare civility would allow . 

 he have promise she that she should be the rich by a neat little bundle of fat and flourish railway stock when his happiness be secure , and he be not go to break his promise . but he do not mean to give George and Georgie free quarter at Rood Hall , or at Cowes , or Deauville ; and he mean to withdraw his wife altogether from Lady Kirkbank 's pinchbeck set . 

 what be Lesbia 's feeling in the early morning after the last day of the regatta , as she slowly pace the lavender walk in the Ladies ' Garden , alone ? for happily Mr. Smithson be not so early a riser as the grasmere - breed damsel , and she have this fresh morning hour to herself . of what be she think as she pace slowly up and down the broad gravel walk , between two row of tall old bush , on which mass of purple blossom stand up from the pale grey foliage , silvery where the summer breeze touch it ? 

 well , she be think first what a grand old place Rood Hall be , and that it be in a manner hers henceforward . she be to be mistress of this house , and of other house , each after its fashion as perfect as Rood Hall . she be to have illimitable money at her command , to spend and give away as she like . she , who yesterday have be torture by the idea of owe a paltry three thousand pound , be henceforward to count she thousand by the hundred . her sens reel before that dazzle vision of figure with row of cipher after they , one cipher more or less meaning the difference between thousand and million . everybody have agree in assure she that Mr. Smithson be inordinately rich . everybody have consider it his or her business to give her information about the gentleman 's income ; clearly imply thereby that in the opinion of society Mr. Smithson 's merit as a suitor be a question of so much bullion . 

 could she doubt she who have learn in one short season to know what the world be make of and what it most value could she , steep to the lip in the wisdom of Lady Kirkbank 's set , doubt for an instant that she be make a well match in the eye of society , than if she have marry a man of the high lineage in all England , a peer of the high rank , without large mean ? she know that money be power , that a man might begin life as a pot - boy or a greengrocer , a knacker or a dust contractor , and climb to the topmost pinnacle , be he only rich enough . she know that society would eat such a man 's dinner and dance at his wife 's ball , and pretend to think he an altogether exceptional man , make believe to admire he for his own sake , to think his wife most brilliant among woman , if he be only rich enough . and could she doubt that society would bow down to she as Lady Lesbia Smithson ? she have learn a great deal in her single season , and she know how society be influence and govern , almost as well as Sir Robert Walpole know how human nature could be mould and direct at the will of a shrewd diplomatist . she know that in the fashionable world every man and every woman , every child even , have his or her price , and may be buy and sell at pleasure . she have her price , she , Lesbia , the pearl of Grasmere ; and the price have be fairly bid she have surrender to the bidder . 

 ' I suppose I always mean to marry he , ' she think , pause in her promenade to gaze across the verdant landscape , a fertile vale , against a background of low hill . all the landscape , to the edge of those hill , belong to Mr. Smithson . ' yes , I must have mean to give way at last , or I should hardly have tolerate his attention . it would have be a pity to refuse such a place as this ; and , he be quite gentlemanlike ; and as I have do with all romantic idea , I do not see why I should not learn to like he very much . ' 

 she dismiss the idea of Smithson lightly , with this conclusion , which she believe very virtuous ; and then as she resume she walk her thought revert to the Park Lane Palace . 

 ' I hardly know whether I like it , ' she muse languidly ; ' beautiful as it be , it be only a reproduction of bygone splendour , and it be painfully excruciating now . for my own part I would much rather have the shabby old house which have belong to one 's ancestor , which have come to one as a heritage , by divine right as it be , instead of be buy with newly make money . to my mind it would rank higher . yet I doubt if anybody nowadays set a pin 's value upon ancestor . People ask , who be he ? but they only mean , how much have he ? and provide a person be not absolutely in trade , not actually engage in sell soap , or match , or mustard , society do not   care a straw how his money have be make . the only secondary question be , how long will it last ? and that be of course important . ' 

 Musing thus , wordly wisdom personify , the maiden look up and see her lover enter at the light little iron gate which give entrance to this feminine Eden . she go to meet he , look all simplicity and freshness in her white morning gown and neat little dunstable hat . it seem to he as he gaze at she almost as if this delicate , sylph - like beauty be some wild white flower of the wood personify . 

 she give he her hand graciously , but he draw she to his breast and kiss she , with the air of a man who be exercise an indisputable right . she suppose that it be his right , and she submit , but release herself as quickly as possible . 

 ' my dearest , how lovely you look in this morning light , ' he exclaim , ' while all the other woman be upstairs make up their face to meet the sun , and we shall see every shade of bismuth by - and - by , from pale mauve to purple . ' 

 ' it be very uncivil of you to say such a thing of your guest , ' exclaim Lesbia . 

 ' but they all indulge in bismuth you must be quite aware of that . they call the stuff by different name Blanc Rosati , Creme de l'Imperatrice , milk of Beauty , Perline , Opaline , Ivorine but it mean bismuth all the same . expose your fashionable beauty to the fume of sewer - gas , and that dazzle whiteness would turn to a dull brown hue , or even black . thank heaven , my Lesbia wear real lily and rose . have you be here long ? ' 

 ' about half an hour ' 

 ' I only wish I have know . I should not have dawdle so long over my dressing . ' 

 ' I be very glad you do not know , ' Lesbia answer coolly . ' do you suppose I never want to be alone ? life in London be perpetual turmoil ; one 's eye grow weary with ever - move crowd , one 's ear ache with try to distinguish one voice among the buzz of voice . ' 

 ' then why go back to town ? why go back to the turmoil and the treadmill ? it be only a kind of treadmill , after all , though we choose to call it pleasure . stay here , Lesbia , and let we live upon the river , and among the flower , ' urge Smithson , with as romantic an air as if he have never hear of contango , or bulling and bear ; and yet only half an hour ago , while his valet be shave he , he be debate within himself whether he should be bear or bull in his influence upon certain stock . 

 it be suppose that he never go near the city , that he have shake the dust of Lombard Street and the House off his shoe , that his fortune be make , and he have no further need of speculation . yet the proverb hold good with the stock - jobber . ' he who have once drunk will drink again . ' of that fountain there be no satiety . 

 ' stay and hear the last of the nightingale , ' he murmur ; ' we be famous for our nightingale . ' 

 ' I wonder you do not   order a fricassťe of their tongue , like that loathsome person in roman history . ' 

 ' I hope I shall never resemble any loathsome person . why can you not stay ? ' 

 ' why , because it be not etiquette , Lady Kirkbank say . ' 

 ' Lady Kirkbank , eh ? la belle farce , Lady Kirkbank stand out for etiquette . ' 

 ' do not   laugh at my chaperon , sir . upon what rock can a poor girl lean if you undermine her faith in her chaperon , sir . ' 

 ' I hope you will have a well guardian before you be a month old . I mean to be a very strong rock , Lesbia . you do not know how firmly I shall stand between you and all the peril of society . you have be but poorly guard hitherto . ' 

 ' you talk as if you mean to be an abominable tyrant , ' say Lesbia . ' if you do not   take care I shall change my mind , and recall my promise . ' 

 ' not on that account , Lesbia : every woman like a man who stand up for his own . it be only your invertebrate husband whose wife drift into the divorce court . I mean to keep and hold the prize I have win . when be it to be , dearest our wedding day ? ' 

 ' not for age , I hope some time next summer , at the early . ' 

 ' you would not be so cruel as to keep I wait a year ? ' 

 ' why not ? ' 

 ' you would not ask that if you love I . ' 

 ' you be ask too much , ' say Lesbia , with a flash of defiance . ' there have be nothing say about love yet . you ask I to be your wife , and I say yes mean that at some remote period such a thing might be . ' 

 she know that the man be her slave slave to her beauty , slave to her superior rank and she be determine not to lessen the weight of his chain by so much as a feather . 

 ' do not that promise imply something like love ? ' he ask , earnestly . 

 ' perhaps it imply a little gratitude for your devotion , which I have neither court nor encourage ; a little respect for your talent , your perseverance a little admiration for your wonderful success in life . perhaps love may follow these sentiment , naturally , easily , if you be very patient ; but if you talk about our be marry before next year , you will simply make I hate you . ' 

 ' then I will say very little , except to remind you that there be no earthly reason why we should not be marry next month . October and November be the good month for Rome , and I hear you say last night you be pin to see Rome . ' 

 ' what then can not Lady Kirkbank take I to Rome ? ' 

 ' and introduce you to the rowdy people in the city , ' cry Mr. Smithson . ' Lesbia , I adore you . it be the dream of my life to be your husband ; but if you be go to spend a winter in Italy with Lady Kirkbank , I renounce my right , I surrender my hope . you will not be the wife of my dream after that . ' 

 ' do you assert a right to control my life during our engagement ? ' 

 ' some little right ; above all , the privilege of choose your friend . and that be one reason why I most fervently desire our marriage should not be delay . you would find it difficult , impossible perhaps , to get out of Lady Kirkbank 's claw while you be single ; but once my wife , that amiable old person can be make to keep she distance . ' 

 ' Lady Kirkbank 's claw ! what a horrible way in which to speak of a friend . I think you adore Lady Kirkbank . ' 

 ' so I do . we all adore she , but not as a guide for youth . as a speciman of the elderly female of the latter half of the nineteenth century , she be perfect . such gush , such juvenility , such broad view , such an utter absence of starch ; but as a lamp for the footstep of girlhood no , there we must pause . ' 

 ' you be very ungrateful . do you know that poor Lady Kirkbank have be most strenuous in your behalf ? ' 

 ' oh , yes , I know that . ' 

 ' and you be not grateful ? ' 

 ' I intend to be very grateful , so grateful as to entirely satisfy Lady Kirkbank . '

Miss Trinder be shock bad style , and her design be transparent from the very beginning ! I let she flirt as much as she like ; and when she begin to be seriously sentimental I take wing for the East . ' 

 ' be she pretty ? ' ask Lesbia , not displeased at this contemptuous summing up of poor Belle Trinder 's story . 

 ' if you admire the Flemish type , as illustrate by Rubens , she be lovely . a complexion of lily and rose cabbage rose , bien entendu , which be apt to deepen into peony after champagne and mayonaise at Ascot or Sandown a figure oh well a tremendous figure hair of an auburn that touch perilously on the confine of red large , serviceable foot , and an appetite the appetite of a ploughman 's daughter rear upon short common . ' 

 ' you be very cruel to a girl who evidently admire you . ' 

 ' a fig for her admiration ! she want to live in my house and spend my money . ' 

 ' there go the gong , ' exclaim Lesbia ; ' pray let we go to breakfast . you be hideously cynical , and I be wofully tired of you . ' 

 and as they stroll back to the house , by lavender walk and rise garden , and across the dewy lawn , Lesbia question herself as to whether she be one whit well or more dignified than Isabella Trinder . she wear her rue with a difference , that be all . 

 CHAPTER XXXV . 

 ' all FANCY , PRIDE , and FICKLE maidenhood . ' 


 the return to Arlington Street mean a return to the ceaseless whirl of gaiety . even at Rood Hall life have be as near an approach to perpetual motion as one could hope for in this world ; but the excitement and the hurrying and scamper in Berkshire have a rustic flavour ; there be moment that be almost repose , a breathing space between the blue river and the blue sky , in a world that seem make of green field and hang wood , the plashing of water , and the song of the lark . but in London the very atmosphere be charge with hurry and agitation ; the freshness be go from the verdure of the park ; the glory of the rhododendron have fade ; the Green Park below Lady Kirkbank 's mansion be baked and rusty ; the tower of the Houses of Parliament yonder be dimly see in a mist of heat . London air taste of smoke and dust , vibrate with the incessant roll of carriage , and the trampling of multitudinous foot . 

 there be woman of rank who can take the London season quietly , and live their own life in the midst of the whirl and the riot woman for whom that squirrel - like circulation round and round the fashionable wheel have no charm woman who only receive people they like , only go into society that be congenial . but Lady Kirkbank be not one of these . the advance of age make she only more keen in the pursuit of pleasure . she would have abandon herself to despair have the glass over the mantelpiece in her boudoir cease to be choke and litter with card have her book of engagement show a blank page . happily there be plenty of people if not all of they the good people who want Sir George and Lady Kirkbank at their party . the gentleman be sport and harmless , the lady be good - natured , and just sufficiently eccentric to be amusing without degenerate into a bore . and this year she be ask almost everywhere , for the sake of the beauty who go under her wing . Lesbia have be as a pearl of price to her chaperon , from a social point of view ; and now that she be engage to Horace Smithson she be likely to be even more valuable . 

 Mr. Smithson have promise Lady Kirkbank , sportively as it be , and upon the impulse of the moment , as he would have offer to wager a dozen of glove , that be he so happy as to win her protťgťe 's hand he would find she an investment for , say , a thousand , which would bring she in twenty per cent . ; nay , more , he would also find the thousand , which would have be the initial difficulty on poor Georgie 's part . but this little matter be in Georgie 's mind a detail , compare with the advantage to accrue to she indirectly from Lesbia 's union with one of the rich man in London . 

 Lady Kirkbank have bring about many good match , and have be too often reward with base ingratitude upon the part of her protťgťes , after marriage ; but there be a touch of Arcady in the good soul 's nature , and she be always trustful . she tell herself that Lesbia would not be ungrateful , would not basely kick down the ladder by which she have mount to height empyrean , would not cruelly shelve the friend who have pioneer she to high fortune . she count upon make the house in Park Lane as her own house , upon be the prime mover of all Lesbia 's hospitality , the supervisor of her visit list , the shadow behind the throne . 

 there be ball and party nightly , dinner , luncheon , garden - party ; and yet there be a sense of wan in the glory of the world everybody feel that the fag - end of the season be approach . all the really great entertainment be over the Cabinet dinner , the Reception at the Foreign Office , the last of the State ball and concert . some of the good people have already leave town ; and senator be begin to complain that they see no prospect of early deliverance . there be Goodwood still to look forward to ; and after Goodwood the Deluge or rather Cowes Regatta , about which Lady Kirkbank 's set be already talk . 

 Lady Lesbia be to be at Cowes for the Regatta week . that be a settled thing . Mr. Smithson 's schooner - yacht , the Cayman , be to be her hotel . it be to be Lady Kirkbank and Lady Lesbia 's yacht for the nonce ; and Mr. Smithson be to live on shore at his villa , and at that aristocratic club to which , by Maulevrier 's influence , and on the score of his approach marriage with an earl 's daughter , he have be just select . he would be only Lady Kirkbank 's visitor on board the Cayman . the severe etiquette of the situation would therefore not be infringe ; and yet Mr. Smithson would have the happiness of see his betroth sole and sovereign mistress of his yacht , and of spend the long summer day at her foot . even to Lady Lesbia this idea of the yacht be not without its charm . she have never be on board such a yacht as the Cayman ; she be a good sailor , as testify by many an excursion , in hire sailing boat , at Tynemouth , and St. Bees ; and she know that she would be the queen of the hour . she accept Mr. Smithson 's invitation for the Cowes week more graciously than she be will not to receive his attention , and be pleased to say that the whole thing would be rather enjoyable . 

 ' it will be simple enchantment , ' exclaim the more enthusiastic Georgie Kirkbank . ' there be nothing so rapturous as life on board a yacht ; there be a flavour of adventure , a sansgene , a in short everything in the world that I like . I shall wear my cotton frock , and give myself up to enjoyment , lie on the deck and look up at the blue sky , too deliciously idle even to read the last horrid thing of Zola 's . ' 

 but the Cowes Regatta wag nearly three week ahead ; and in the meantime there be Goodwood , and the ravel thread of the London season have to be wind up . and by this time it be know everywhere that the affair between Mr. Smithson and Maulevrier 's sister be really on . ' it be as settle a business as the entry and bet for next year 's Derby , ' say one lounger to another in the smoking - room of the Haute Gomme . ' play or pay , do not   you know . '

' the least error of taste on your dear girl 's part would have froissťd he . man with that immense wealth be always suspicious , ready to imagine mercenary motif , on their guard against be trap . but Lesbia have I at she back , and she manage he perfectly . he be positively her slave ; and you will be able to twist he round your little finger in the matter of settlement . you may do what you like with he , for the ground have be thoroughly prepare by I .  Lady Maulevrier 's reply be not enthusiastic . she have no doubt Mr. Smithson be a very good match , accord to the modern estimate of matrimonial alliance , in which money seem to be the Alpha and Omega . but she have cherish view of another kind . she have hop to see her dear granddaughter wear one of those noble and historic name which be a badge of distinction for all time . she have hop to see she enter one of those grand old family which be a kind of royalty . and that Lesbia should marry a man whose sole distinction consist of an immense fortune amass heaven know how , be a terrible blow to her pride . 

 ' but it be not the first , ' write Lady Maulevrier . ' my pride have receive crush blow in day past , and I ought to be humble to the dust . but there be a stubborn resistance in some nature which stand firm against every shock . you and Lesbia will both be surprised to hear that Mary , from whom I expect so little , have make a really great match . she be marry yesterday afternoon in my morning room , by special licence , to the Earl of Hartfield , the lover of her choice , whom we at Fellside have all know as plain John Hammond . he be an admirable young man , and sure to make a great figure in the world , as no doubt you know well than I do , for you be in the way of hear all about he . his courtship of Mary be quite an idyll ; and the happy issue of this romantic love - affair have cheer and comfort I more than anything that have happen since Lesbia leave I . ' 

 this letter , write in Fršulein 's niggle little hand , Lady Kirkbank hand to Lesbia , who read it through in silence ; but when she come to that part of the letter which tell of her sister 's marriage , her cheek grow ashy pale , her brow contract , and she start to her foot and star at Lady Kirkbank with wild , dilate eye , as if she have be sting by an adder .

' what right have he to come to we under false colour , to pretend to be poor , a nobody with only the vague hope of make a decent position in the future ? and to offer himself under such impossible condition to a girl bring up as I have be a girl educate by one of the proud and most ambitious of woman to force I to renounce everything except he ? how could he suppose that any girl , so place , could decide in his favour ? if he have love I he would have tell I the truth he would not have make it impossible for I to accept he . ' 

 Lesbia ignore these consolatory remark . she be pace the room like a tigress , her delicate cambric handkerchief grasp between her two hand , and tear and rent by the convulsive action of her finger . she could have throw herself from the balcony on to the spike of the area railing , she could have dash herself against yonder big plate - glass window look towards the Green Park , like a bird which shatter his little life against the glass barrier which he mistake for the open sky . she could have fling herself down on the floor and grovel , and tear her hair she could have do anything mad , wicked , desperate , in the wild rage of this moment .

' she exclaim ; ' he never love I . if he have he would have tell I the truth . what , when I be in his arm , my head upon his breast , my whole be surrender to he , adore he , what more could he want ? he must have know that this mean real love . and why should he put it upon I to fight so hard a fight to brave my grandmother 's anger to be curse by she to face poverty for his sake ? I never profess to be a heroine . he know that I be a woman , with all a woman 's weakness , a woman 's fear of trial and difficulty in the future . it be a cowardly thing to use I so . ' 

 ' it be , ' say Lady Kirkbank , in the same soothe tone ; ' but if you like this Hammond - Hartfield creature a little in those old day , I know you have outlive that like long ago . '

 she be tame down a little by this time , ashamed of that outbreak of violent passion , feel that she have reveal too much to Lady Kirkbank .

' 

 ' Rich , yes ; and nothing but rich ; while Lord Hartfield be a man of the very high standing , belong to the flower of english nobility . Rich , yes ; Mr. Smithson be rich ; but , as Lady Maulevrier say , he have make his money heaven know how . ' 

 ' Mr. Smithson have not make his money heaven know how , ' answer Lady Kirkbank , indignantly . ' he have make it in cochineal , in iron , in gunpowder , in coal , in all kind of commodity . everybody in the City know how he have make his money , and that he have a genius for turn everything into gold . if the gold change back into one of the base metal , it be only when Mr. Smithson have make all he want to make . and now he have quite do with the City . the House be the only business of his life ; and he be become a power in the House . you have every reason to be proud of your choice , Lesbia .  ' she seem a harmless kind of girl , ' say Lady Kirkbank , as if she have be talk of a housemaid .

 ' but she could not have know that he be Lord Hartfield . ' 

 ' no ; but he be a man ; and that be enough for she . ' 

 from this time forward there be a change in Lady Lesbia 's style and manner a change very much for the bad , as old - fashioned people think ; but to the taste of some among Lady Kirkbank 's set , the change be an improvement . she be gayer than of old , gay with a reckless vivacity , intensely eager for action and excitement , for card and racing , and all the strong stimulant of fashionable life . Most people ascribe this increase vivacity , this electric manner , to the fact of her engagement to Horace Smithson . she be giddy with her triumph , dazzle by a vision of the gold which be soon to be her .

 ' who tell you that ? ' ask his friend .

' Seraphine , the dressmaker , be complain want to see the colour of Lady Lesbia Haselden 's money vulgar curiosity ask my old mother if she think the account be safe , and so on . that be how I come to know all about it . ' 

 ' well , she will be able to pay Seraphine next season . ' 

 Lord Maulevrier come back to London directly after his sister 's wedding . the event , which come off so quietly , so happily , fill he with unqualified joy . he have hop from the very first that his Molly would win the cup , even while Lesbia be make all the running , as he say afterwards . and Molly have win , and be the wife of one of the good young man in England . Maulevrier , albeit unused to the melting - mood , shed a tear or two for very joy as the sister he love and the friend of his boyhood and youth stand side by side in the quiet room at Grasmere , and speak the solemn word that make they one for ever .

 they all talk patronisingly of Smithson , and seem to think it rather a wonderful fact that he do not drop his aspirate or eat pea with a knife . 

 ' a man of stirling metal , ' say the gossip , ' who can hold his own with many a fellow bear in the purple . ' 

 Maulevrier call in Arlington Street , but Lady Kirkbank and her protťgťe be out ; and it be at a cricket match at the Orleans Club that the brother and sister meet for the first time after Lord Hartfield 's wedding , which by this time have be in all the paper ; a very simple announcement : 

 ' on the 29th inst . , at Grasmere , by the Reverend Douglass Brooke , the Earl of Hartfield to Mary , young daughter of the ninth Earl of Maulevrier . ' 

 Lesbia be the centre of a rather noisy little court , in which Mr. Smithson be conspicuous by his superior reserve .

 Maulevrier receive the cool possible greeting . Lesbia turn pale with anger at sight of he , for his presence remind she of the most humiliating passage in her life ; but the big red satin sunshade conceal that pale angry look , and nothing in Lesbia 's manner betray emotion .

 ' I have be at Grasmere . '

 ' Solemn ! what , with my Tomboy sister as bride ! impossible ! ' 

 ' your sister cease to be a Tomboy when she fall in love . she be a sweet and womanly woman , and will make an adorable wife to the fine fellow I know . I hear I be to congratulate you , Lesbia , upon your engagement with Mr. Smithson . ' 

 ' if you think I be the person to be congratulate , you be at liberty to do so . my engagement be a fact . ' 

 ' oh , of course , Mr. Smithson be the winner . but as I hope you intend to be happy , I wish you joy . I be tell Smithson be a really excellent fellow when one get to know he ; and I shall make it my business to be well acquaint with he . ' 

 Smithson be stand just out of hearing , watch the bowling . Maulevrier go over to he and shake hand , their acquaintance hitherto have be of the slight , and very shy upon his lordship 's part ; but now Smithson could see that Maulevrier mean to be cordial . 

 CHAPTER XXXVI . 

 a RASTAQUOUERE . 


 there be a dinner party in one of the new house in Grosvenor Place that evening , to which Lady Kirkbank and Lesbia have be bid . the new house belong to a new man , who be suppose to have make million out of railway , and other gigantic achievement in the engineering line ; and the new man and his wife be friend of Mr. Smithson , and have make the simple Georgie 's acquaintance only within the last three week . 

 ' of course they be stupid , my dear , ' she remark , in response to some slighting remark of Lesbia 's , ' but I be always willing to know rich people . one drop in for so many good thing ; and they never want any return in kind . it be quite enough for they to be allow to spend their money upon we . ' 

 the house be gorgeous in all the glory of the very late fashion in upholstery ; hall Algerian ; dining - room Pompeian ; drawing - room early italian ; music - room Louis Quatorze ; billiard - room mediaval English . the dinner be as magnificent as dinner can be make . three - fourth of the guest be the haute gomme of the financial world , and perspire gold . the other third belong to a class which Mr. Smithson describe somewhat contemptuously as the shake - back nobility . an irish peer , a young son of a ducal house that have run to seed , a political agitator , a grass widow whose title husband be governor of an obscure colony , an ancient dowager with hair which be too luxuriant to be anything but a wig , and diamond which be so large as to suggest paste . 

 Lesbia sit by she affiance at the glitter table , light with cluster of wax candle , which shine upon a level parterre of tea rose , gardenia , and gloire de Malmaison carnation ; from which rise at interval group of silver - gilt dolphin , support shallow golden dish pile with peach , grape , and all the costly produce of Covent Garden . 

 Conversation be not particularly brilliant , nor have the guest an elated air . the thermometer be near eighty , and at this period of the season everybody be tired of this kind of dinner , and would gladly have forego the great achievement of culinary art , in favour of a chicken and a salad , eat under green leaf , in a garden at Wargrave or Henley , within sound of the ripple river . 

 on Lesbia 's right hand there be a portly personage of jewish type , dark to swarthiness , and somewhat oily , whose every word suggest bullion . he and Mr. Smithson be evidently acquaintance of long standing , and Mr. Smithson present he to Lesbia , whereupon he join in their conversation now and then . 

 his talk be of the usual standard . he have see everything worth see in London and in Paris , between which city he seem to oscillate with such frequency that he might be say to live in both place at once . he have his stall at Covent Garden , and his stall at the Grand Opera . he be a subscriber at the Theatre FranÁais . he have see all the race at Longchamps and Chantilly , as well as at Sandown and Ascot . but every now and then he and Mr. Smithson drift from the customary talk about opera and race , picture and french novel , to the wide world of commerce and speculation , mine , waterwork , and foreign loan and Lesbia leant back in her chair , and fan herself languidly , with half - close eyelid , while two or three course go round , she give the little supercilious look at each entrťe offer to she , to be observe on such occasion , as if the thing offer be particularly nasty . 

 she wonder how long the two man be go to prose about mine and share , in those subdue half - mysterious voice , tell each other occult fact in half - express phrase , utterly dark to the outside world ; but , while she be languidly wonder , a change in her lover 's manner startle she into keenest curiosity . 

 ' Montesma be in Paris , ' say Mr. Sampayo , the dark gentleman ; ' I din last week with he at the Continental . ' 

 Mr. Smithson 's complexion fade curiously , and a leaden darkness come over his countenance , as of a man whose heart and lung suddenly refuse their office . but in a few moment he be smile feebly . 

 ' indeed ! I think he be play out year ago . ' 

 ' a man of that kind be never play out . Don Gomez de Montesma be as clever as Satan , as handsome as Apollo , and he bear one of the old name in Castile . such a man will always come to the front . C'est un rastaquouere mais rastaquouere de bon genre . you know he intimately la bas , I believe ? ' 

 ' in Cuba ; yes , we be pretty good friend once . ' 

 ' and be useful to each other , no doubt , ' say Mr. Sampayo , pleasantly . ' be that argentiferous copper Company in sixty - four your or his ? ' 

 ' there be a good many people concern in it . ' 

 ' no doubt ; it take a good many people to work that kind of thing , but I fancy you and Montesma be about the only two who come out of it pleasantly . and he and you do a little in the shipping line , do not   you african produce ? however , that be an old song . you have have so many good thing since then . ' 

 ' do Montesma talk of come to London ? ' 

 ' he do not talk about it ; but he would hardly go back to the tropic without have a look round on both side of the Channel . he be always fond of society , pretty woman , dancing , and amusement of all kind . I have no doubt we shall see he here before the end of the season . ' 

 Mr. Smithson pursue the subject no far . he turn to Lesbia , who have be curiously interested in this little bit of conversation interested first because Smithson have seem agitated by the mention of the Spaniard 's name ; secondly , because of the description of the man , which have a romantic sound . the very word tropic suggest a romance . and Lesbia , whose mind be jade by the monotony of a London season , the threadbare fabric of society conversation , kindle at any image which appeal to her fancy . 

 Clever as Satan , handsome as Apollo , scion of an old castilian family , fresh from the tropic . her imagination dwelt upon the idea which these word have conjure up . 

 three day after this she be at the opera with her chaperon , her lover in attendance as usual . the opera be " Faust , " with Nillson as Marguerite . after the performance they be to drive down to Twickenham on Mr. Smithson 's drag , and to dance and sup at the Orleans . the last ball of the season be on this evening ; and Lesbia have be persuade that it be to be a particular recherchť ball , and that only the very nice people be to be present . at any rate , the drive under the light of a July moon would be delicious ; and if they do not like the people they find there they could eat their supper and come away immediately after , as Lady Kirkbank remark philosophically . 

 the opera be nearly over that grand scene of Valentine 's death be on and Lesbia be listen breathlessly to every note , watch every look of the actor , when there come a modest little knock at the door of her box . she dart an angry glance round , and shrug her shoulder vexatiously . what Goth have dare to knock during that thrilling scene ? 

 Mr. Smithson rise and creep to the door and quietly open it . 

 a dark , handsome man , who be a total stranger to Lesbia , glide in , shake hand with Smithson as he enter . 

 till this moment Lesbia 's whole being have be absorb in the scene that bitter anathema of the brother , the sister 's cry of anguish and shame . where else be there tragedy so human , so enthral grief that so wring the spectator 's heart ? it need a Goethe and a Gounod to produce this masterpiece . 

 in an instant , in a flash , Lesbia 's interest in the stage be go . her first glance at the stranger tell she who he be . the olive tint , the eye of deep black , the grand form of the head and perfect chiselling of the feature could belong only to that scion of an old castilian race whom she have hear describe the other evening ' clever as Satan , handsome as Apollo . ' 

 yes , this must be the man , Don Gomez de Montesma . there be nothing in Mr. Smithson 's manner to indicate that the Spaniard be an unwelcome guest . on the contrary , Smithson receive he with a cordiality which in a man of naturally reserve manner seem almost rapture . the curtain fall , and he present Don Gomez to Lady Kirkbank and Lady Lesbia ; whereupon dear Georgie begin to gush , after she will not , and to ask a good many question in a manner that be too girlish to seem impertinent . 

 ' how perfectly you speak English ! ' she exclaim . ' you must have live in England a good deal . ' 

 ' on the contrary , it be my misfortune to have live here very little , but I have know a good many English and Americans in Cuba and in Paris . ' 

 ' in Cuba ! do you really come from Cuba ? I have always fancy that Cuba must be an altogether charming place to live in like Biarritz or Pau , do not   you know , only far away . do please tell I where it be , and what kind of a place . ' 

 Geographically , Lady Kirkbank 's mind be a blank . it be quite a revelation to she to find that Cuba be an island . 

 ' it must be a lovely spot ! ' exclaim the fervid creature . ' let I see , now , what do we get from Cuba ? cigar and and tobacco . I suppose in Cuba everybody smoke ? ' 

 ' man , woman , and child . '

 ' there be no aborigine . the Indians whom Columbus find soon perish off the face of the island . european civilisation generally have that effect . but one of our most benevolent captain - general provide we with an import population of nigger . '

I have always long to live among a slave population , dear submissive black thing dress in coral necklace and feather , instead of the horrid over - feed wretch we have to wait upon we . and if the aborigine be not want it be just as well for they to die out , do not   you know , ' prattle Lady Kirkbank . 

 ' it be very accommodating of they , no doubt . yet we could employ half a million of they , if we have they , in drain our swamp . agriculture suffer by the loss of indian labour . ' 

 ' I suppose they be like the creature in Pizarro , poor dear yellow thing with brass bracelet , ' say Lady Kirkbank . ' I remember see Macready as Rolla when I be quite a little thing . ' 

 and now the curtain rise for the last act . 

 ' do you care about stay for the end ? ' ask Mr. Smithson of Lesbia . ' it will make we rather late at the Orleans . '

 Lady Lesbia have a pretty little way of always oppose any suggestion of her sweetheart . she be resolve to treat he as badly as a future husband could be treat . in consent to marry he she have do he a favour which be a great deal more than such a person have any right to expect . 

 she leant forward to watch and listen , with her elbow rest on the velvet cushion her head upon her hand , and she seem absorb in the scene . but this be mere outward seem . all the enchantment of music and acting be over . she only hear and see vaguely , as if it be a shadowy scene enact ever so far away . every now and then her eye glance involuntarily toward Don Gomez , who stand lean against the back of the box , pale , languid , graceful , poetic , an altogether different type of manhood from that with which she have of late be satiate . 

 those deep dark eye of his have a dreamy look . they gaze across the dazzle house , into space , above Lady Lesbia 's head . they seem to see nothing ; and they certainly be not look at she . 

 Don Gomez be the first man she ever remember to have be present to she who do not favour she with a good deal of hard staring , more or less discreetly manage , during the first ten minute of their acquaintance . on he her beauty fall flat . he evidently fail to recognise her supreme loveliness . it might be that she be the wrong type for Cuba . every nation have its own Venus ; and that far away spot beyond the torrid zone might have a somewhat barbarous idea of beauty . at any rate , Don Gomez be apparently unimpressed . and yet Lesbia flatter herself that she be look she good to - night , and that her costume be a success . she wear a white satin gown , short in the skirt , for the luxury of freedom in waltzing , and make with Quaker - like simplicity , the bodice high to the throat , fit she like a sheath . 

 her only ornament be a garland of scarlet poppy wreathe from throat to shoulder , and a large diamond heart which Mr. Smithson have lately give she ; ' a bullock 's heart , ' as Lady Kirkbank call it .

 ' why should not you go with we , Don Gomez ? ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , in a gush of hospitality . ' the drive will be charming not equal to your tropical Cuba but intensely nice . and the garden will be something too sweet on such a night as this . I know they when the dear Duc d'Aumale be there . ay de mi , such a man ! ' 

 Lady Kirkbank sigh , with the air of have know his Altťsse Royale intimately . 

 ' I should be charm , ' say Don Gomez , ' if I think my friend Smithson want I . would you really like to have I , Smithson ? ' 

 ' I should be enchant . ' 

 ' and there be room on the drag ? ' 

 ' room enough for half - a - dozen . I be only take Sir George Kirkbank and Colonel Delville whom we be to pick up at the Haute Gomme and Mr. and Mrs. Mostyn , who be in the stall . ' 

 ' a nice snug little party , ' exclaim that charming optimist , Lady Kirkbank . ' I hate a crowd on a drag . the way some of the member of the four - in - hand Club load their coach on parade remind I of a beanfeast ! ' 

 they find Lady Kirkbank 's footman and one of Mr. Smithson 's groom wait in the hall of the opera house . the groom to conduct they to the spot where the drag be wait ; the footman to carry wrap and take his mistress 's final order . there be a bohemian flavour in the little walk to the great fruit garden , which be odorous of bruise peach and stale salad as they pass it . Waggon - load of cabbage and other garden stuff be stand about by the old church ; the roadway be litter with the refuse of the market ; and the air be faint and heavy with the scent of herb and flower .

Mr. Smithson seat himself a minute afterwards , and drive off by King Street and Leicester Square and on to Piccadilly , steer cleverly through the traffic of cab and carriage , which be at its apogee just now , when all the theatre be disgorge their crowd . Piccadilly be quieter , yet there be plenty of carriage , late people go to party and early people go home , horse slip and slide on stone or wood , half the roadway up , and luminous with lantern . they stop in front of the Haute Gomme , where they pick up Sir George Kirkbank and Colonel Delville , a big man with a patriarchal head , suppose to be one of the fine whist player in London , and to make a handsome income by his play . he have ride in the Balaclava charge , be a favourite everywhere , and , albeit no genius , be much cleverer than his friend and school - fellow , George Kirkbank . they have be at Eton together , have both make love to the lively Georgie , and have be inseparable for the last thirty year . 

 ' could not   get on without Delville , ' say Sir George ; ' dooced smart fellow , sir . know the rope ; and do all the thinking for both of we . ' 

 and now they be fairly start , and the team fall into a rattle pace , with the road pretty clear before they . Hyde Park be one umbrageous darkness , edge by long line of golden light . coolness and silence enfold all thing in the summer midnight , and Lesbia , not prone to romance , sink into a dreamy state of mind , as she lean back in her seat and watch the shadowy tree glide by , the long vista of lamp and verdure in front of she . she be glad that no one talk to she , for talk of any kind must have break the spell . Don Gomez sit like a statue in his place behind she . from Lady Kirkbank , the loquacious , come a gentle sound of snoring , a subdue , ladylike snore , breathe softly at interval , like a sigh . Mr. Smithson have his team , and his own thought , too , for occupation , thought which to - night be not altogether pleasant . 

 at the back of the coach Mrs. Mostyn be descant on the evolution of the nautilus , and the relationship of protoplasm and humanity , to Colonel Delville , who sit smile placidly behind an immense cigar , and accept the most stupendous fact and the most appalling theory with a friendly little nod of his handsome head . 

 Mr. Mostyn frankly sleep , as it be his custom to do upon all convenient occasion . he call it recuperate . 

 ' Frank ought to be delightfully fresh , for he recuperate all the way down , ' say his wife , when they alight in the dewy garden at Twickenham , in front of the lamp - light portico . 

 ' I would not   have mind his recuperating if he have not   snore so abominably , ' remark Colonel Delville . 

 it be nearly one o'clock , and the ball have thin a little , which make it all the well for those who remain . Mr. Smithson 's order have be give two day ago , and the very good of the waiter have be tell off for his especial service . the lady go upstairs to take off their wrapping and muffling , and Lesbia emerge dazzle from her brown velvet Newmarket , while Lady Kirkbank , bend closely over the look - glass , like a witch over a caldron , repair her complexion with cotton wool . 

 they go through the conservatory to the octagon dining - room , where the supper be ready , a special supper , on a table by a window , a table laden with exotic and brilliant with glass and silver . the supper be , of course , perfect in its way . Mr. Smithson 's chef have be down to see about it , and Mr. Smithson 's own particular champagne and the claret grow in his own particular clo in the Gironde , have be send down for the feast . no common cuisine , no common wine could be good enough ; and yet there be a day when the cheap gargote in Belleville or Montmartre be good enough for Mr. Smithson . there have be day on which he do not dine at all , and when the fume of a gibelotte steam from a workman 's restaurant make his mouth water . 

 the supper be all life and gaiety . everyone be hungry and thirsty , and freshen by the drive , except Lesbia . she be singularly silent , eat hardly anything , but drink three or four glass of champagne . 

 Don Gomez be not a great talker . he have the air of a prince of the blood royal , who expect other people to talk and to keep he amuse . but the little he say be to the point . he have a fine baritone , very low and subdue , and have a languor which be almost insolent , but not without its charm . there be an air of originality about the manner and the man . 

 he be the typical rastaquouere , a man of finished manner , and unknown antecedent , a foreigner , apparently rich , obviously accomplish , but with that indefinable air which bespeak the adventurer ; and which give society as fair a warning as if the man wear a placard on his shoulder with the word cave . 

 but to Lesbia this Spaniard be the first really interesting man she have meet since she see John Hammond ; and her interest in he be much more vivid than her interest in Hammond have be at the beginning of their acquaintance . that pale face , with its tint of old ivory , those thin , finely - cut lip , indicative of diabolical craft , could she but read aright , those unfathomable eye , touch her fancy as it have never yet be touch , awake within she that latent vein of romance , self - abnegation , supreme foolishness , which lurk in the nature of every woman , be she chaste as ice and pure as snow . 

 the supper be long . it be past two o'clock , and the ballroom be thinly occupy , when Mr. Smithson 's party go there . 

 ' you will not   dance to - night , I suppose ? ' say Smithson , as Lesbia and he go slowly down the room arm in arm . it be in a pause between two waltz . the wide window at the end be open to the summer night , and the room be delightfully cool . ' you must be horribly tired ? ' 

 ' I be not in the least tired , and I mean to waltz , if anyone will ask I , ' reply Lesbia , decisively . 

 ' I ought to have ask you to dance , and then it would have be the other way , ' say Smithson , with a touch of acrimony . ' surely you have dance enough in town , and you might be oblige for once in a way , and come and sit with I in the garden , and listen to the nightingale . ' 

 ' there be no nightingale after June . there be the Manola , ' as the band strike up , ' my very favourite waltz . ' 

 Don Gomez be at her elbow at this moment 

 ' may I have the honour of this waltz with you , Lady Lesbia ? ' he ask ; and then with a serio - comic glance at his stoutish friend , ' I do not   think Smithson waltz ? ' 

 ' I have be tell that nobody can waltz who have be bear on this side of the Pyrenees , ' answer Lesbia , withdraw her arm from her lover 's , and slip , it through the Spaniard 's , with the air of a slave who obey a master . 

 Smithson look dagger , and retire to a corner of the room glowering . be a man twenty - two time millionaire , like the parisian Rothschild , he could not find armour against the poison arrow of jealousy . Don Gomez possess many of those accomplishment which make man dangerous , but as a dancer he be hor ligne ; and Horace Smithson know that there be no surer road to a girl 's fancy than the magic circle of a waltz . 

 those two be float round the room in the old slow legato step , which recall to Smithson the picture of a still more spacious room in an island under the Southern Cross the blue water of the bay shin yonder under the starlight of the tropic , fire - fly gleam and flash in the foliage beyond the open window , fire - fly flash amidst the gauzy drapery of the dancer , and this same Gomez revolve with the same slow languid grace , his arm encircle the svelte figure of a woman whose southern beauty outshone Lesbia 's blonde english loveliness as the tropical star outshine the lamp that light our colder sky . yes , every detail of the scene flash back into his mind , as if a curtain have be suddenly pluck back from a long - hide picture . the Cuban 's tall slim figure , the head gently bend towards his partner 's head , as at this moment , and those dark eye look up at he , intoxicate with that nameless , indefinable fascination which it be the lot of some man to exercise . 

 ' he rob I of she ! ' think Smithson , gloomily . ' will he rob I of this one too ? surely not ! Havana be Havana and this one be not a Creole . if I can not trust that lovely piece of marble , there be no woman on earth to be trust . ' 

 he turn his back upon the dancer , and go out into the garden . his soul be wring with jealousy , yet he could watch no long . there be too much pain there be too many bitter memory of shame , and loss , and ignominy evoke by that infernal picture . if he have be free he would have assert his authority as Lesbia 's future husband ; he would have take she away from the Orleans ; he would have tell she plainly and frankly that Don Gomez be no fit person for she to know ; and he would have so plan that they two should never meet again . but Horace Smithson be not free . he be bind hand and foot by those fetter which the chain of past event have forge stern fact which the man himself may forget , or try to forget , but which other people never forget . there be generally some dark spot in the history of such man as Smithson man who climb the giddiest height of this world with that desperate rapidity which imply many a perilous leap from crag to crag , many a moraine skim over , and many an awful gulf span by a hair - breadth bridge . Mr. Smithson 's history be not without such spot ; and the dark of all have relation to his career in Cuba . the story have be know by very few perhaps completely know only by one man ; and that man be Gomez de Montesma . 

 for the last fifteen year the most fervent desire of Horace Smithson 's heart have be the hope that tropical nature , in any one of her various disagreeable form , would be oblige enough to make an end of Gomez . but the force of nature have not work on Mr. Smithson 's side . no loathsome leprosy have eat his enemy 's flesh ; neither cayman nor crocodile , neither Juba snake nor poisonous spider have mark he for its prey . the tropical sun have leave he unsmitten . he have live and he have prosper ; and he be here , like a guilty conscience incarnate , to spoil Horace Smithson 's peace . 

 ' I must be diplomatic , ' Smithson say to himself , as he walk up and down an avenue of irish yew , in a solitary part of the ground , smoke his cigarette , and hear the music swell and sink in the distance . ' I will give she a hint as to that man 's character , and I will keep they apart as much as I can . but if he force himself upon I there be no help for it . I can not afford to be uncivil to he . ' 

 ' can not afford ' in this instance mean ' dare not , ' and Horace Smithson 's thought as he pace the yew - tree walk be full of gloom . 

 during that long meditation he make up his mind on one point , namely , that , let he suffer what pang he might , he must not betray his jealousy . to do that would be to lower himself in Lesbia 's eye , and to play into his rival 's hand ; for a jealous man be almost always contemptible in the sight of his mistress . he would carry himself as if he be sure of her fidelity ; and this very confidence , with a woman of honour , a girl rear as Lesbia have be rear , would render it impossible for she to betray he . he would show himself high - minded , confident , generous , chivalrous , even ; and he would trust to chance for the issue . Chance be Mr. Smithson 's only idea of divinity ; and Chance have hitherto be kind to he . there have be dark hour in his life , but the darkness have not last long ; and the lucky accident of his career have be of a nature to beguile he into the belief that among the favourite of Destiny he stand first and foremost . 

 while Mr. Smithson muse thus , alone and in the darkness , Montesma and Lady Lesbia be wander arm in arm in another and lovely part of the ground , where golden light be scatter like cuban fire - fly among the foliage of seringa and magnolia , arbutus and rhododendron , while at interval a sudden flush of rosy light be shed over garden and river , as if by enchantment , surprise a couple here and there in the midst of a flirtation which have begin in darkness . 

 the ground be lovely in the balmy atmosphere of a July night , the river glide with mysterious motion under the star , great mass of gloom darken the stream with an almost awful look where the wood of Petersham and Ham House cast their dense shadow on the water . Don Gomez and his companion wander by the river side to a spot where a group of magnolia shelter they from the open lawn , and where there be some rustic chair close to the balustrade which protect the parapet . in this spot , which be a kind of island , divide from the rest of the ground by the intervene road , they find themselves quite alone , and in the midst of a summer stillness which be break only by the low , lazy ripple of the tide run seaward . the light of Richmond look far away , and the little town with its variety of level have an italian air in the distance . 

 from the ballroom , faint and fitful , come the music of a waltz . 

 ' I be afraid I have   bring you too far , ' say Don Gomez . 

 ' on the contrary , it be a relief to get away from the light and the people . how delicious this river be ! I be bring up on the shore of a lake ; but after all a lake be horribly tame . its limit be always star one in the face . there be no room for one 's imagination to wander . now a river like this suggest an infinity of possibility , drift on and on and on into undiscovered region , by ever - vary shore . I feel to - night as if I should like to step into that little boat yonder , ' point to a light skiff bob gently up and down with the tide , at the bottom of a flight of step , ' and let the stream take I wherever it choose . ' 

 ' if I could but go with you , ' say Gomez , in that deep and musical tone which make the commonest word seem melody , ' I would ask for neither compass nor rudder . what could it matter whither the boat take I ? there be no place under the star which would not be a paradise with you . ' 

 ' please do not   make a dreamy aspiration the occasion for a compliment , ' exclaim Lesbia , lightly . ' what I say be so silly that I do not   wonder you think it right to say something just a little sillier . but moonlight and running water have a curious effect upon I ; and I , who be the most prosaic among woman , become ridiculously sentimental . ' 

 ' I can not believe that you be prosaic . ' 

 ' I assure you it be perfectly true . I be of the earth , earthy ; a woman of the world , in my first season , ambitious , fond of pleasure , vain , proud , exact , all those thing which I be tell a woman ought not to be . ' 

 ' you pain I when you so slander yourself ; and I shall make it the business of my life to find out how much truth there be in that self - slander . for my own part I do not believe a word of it ; but as it be rude to contradict a lady I will only say that I reserve my opinion . ' 

 ' be you to stay long in England ? ' ask Lesbia . 

 she be lean against the stone balustrade in a careless attitude , as of one who be weary , her elbow on the stone slab , and her head throw back against her arm . the white satin gown , mould to her figure , have a statuesque air , and she look like a marble statue in the dim light , every line of the graceful form expressive of repose . 

 ' that will depend . I be not particularly fond of London . a very little of your English Babylon satisfy I , in a general way ; but there be condition which might make England enchanting . where do you go at the end of the season ? ' 

 ' first to Goodwood , and then to Cowes . Mr. Smithson be so kind as to place his yacht at Lady Kirkbank 's disposal , and I be to be her guest on board the Cayman , just as I be in Arlington Street . ' 

 ' the Cayman ! that name be a reminiscence of Mr. Smithson 's south american travel . ' 

 ' no doubt ! be he long in South America ? ' 

 ' three or four year . ' 

 ' but not in Cuba all that time , I suppose ? ' 

 ' he have business relation with Cuba all that time , and oscillate between our island and the main . he be rather fortunate in his little adventure with we make almost as much money as General Tacon , of blessed memory . but I dare say Smithson have tell you all his adventure in that part of the world . ' 

 ' no , he very rarely talk about his travel : and I be not particularly interested in commercial speculation . there be always so much to think of and talk about in the business of the moment . be you fond of Cuba ? ' 

 ' not passionately . I always feel as if I be an exile there , and yet one of my ancestor be with Columbus when he discover the island , and my race be among the early settler . my family have give three Captain - general to Cuba : but I can not forget that I belong to an old world , and have forfeit that which ought to have be a brilliant place in Europe for the luxurious obscurity of a colony . ' 

 ' but you must be attach to a place in which your family have live for so many generation ? ' 

 ' I like the star and the sea , the mountain and savanna , the tropical vegetation , and the dreamy , half - oriental life ; but at good it be a kind of stagnation , and after a residence of a few month in the island of my birth I generally spread my wing for the wide world of the old continent or the new . ' 

 ' you must have travel so much , ' say Lesbia , with a sigh . ' I have be nowhere and see nothing . I feel like a child who have be shut up in a nursery all its life , and know of no world beyond four wall . ' 

 ' not to travel be not to live , ' say Don Gomez . 

 ' I be to be in Italy next November , I believe , ' say Lesbia , not care to own that this italian trip be to be her honeymoon . 

 ' Italy ! ' exclaim the Spaniard , contemptuously . ' once the finish school of the english nobility ; now the happy hunting - ground of the Cockney tourist and the prosperous Yankee . all the poetry of Italy have be dry up , and the whole country vulgarise . if you want romance in the old world go to Spain ; in the new , try Peru or Brazil , Mexico or California . ' 

 ' I be afraid I be not adventurous enough to go so far . ' 

 ' no : woman cling to beat track . ' 

 ' we obey our master , ' answer Lesbia , meekly . 

 ' ah , I forget . you be to have a master and soon . I hear as much before I see you to - night . ' 

 Lesbia half rise , as if to leave this cool retreat above the ripple tide . 

 ' yes , it be all settle , ' she say ; ' and now I think I must go back . Lady Kirkbank will be wonder what have become of I . ' 

 ' let she wonder a little long , ' say Don Gomez . ' why should we hurry away from this delightful spot ? why break the spell of the river ? life have so few moment of perfect contentment . if this be one with you as it be with I let we make the most of it . Lady Lesbia , do you see those weed yonder , drift with the tide , drift side by side , touch as they drift ? they have meet heaven know how , and will part heaven know where , on their way to the sea ; but they let themselves go with the tide . we have meet like those poor weed . do not   let we part till the tide part we . ' 

 Lesbia give a little sigh , and submit . she have talk of woman obey their master ; and the implication be that she mean to obey Mr. Smithson . but there be a fate in these thing ; and the man who be to be her master , whose light breath be to sway she , whose light look be to rule she , be here at her side in the silence of the summer night . 

 they talk long , but of indifferent subject ; and their talk might have be hear by every member of the Orleans Club , and no harm do . yet word and phrase count for very little in such a case . it be the tone , it be the melody of a voice , it be the magic of the hour that tell . 

 the tide come , in the person of Mr. Smithson , and part these two weed that be drift towards the great mysterious ocean of fate . 

 ' I have be hunt for you everywhere , ' he say , cheerfully . ' if you want another waltz , Lady Lesbia , you have well take the next . I believe it be to be the last . at any rate our party be clamour to be drive home . I find poor Lady Kirkbank fast asleep in a corner of the drawing - room . ' 

 ' will you give I that last waltz ? ' ask Don Gomez . 

 Lady Lesbia feel that the long - suffer Smithson have endure enough . womanly instinct constrain she to refuse that final waltz : but it seem to she as if she be make a tremendous sacrifice in so do . and yet she have waltz to her heart 's content during the season that be wan , and know all the waltz play by all the fashionable band . she give a little sigh , as she say 

 ' no , I must not indulge myself . I must go and take care of Lady Kirkbank . ' 

 Mr. Smithson offer his arm , and she take it and go away with he , leave Don Gomez to follow at his leisure . there would be some delay no doubt before the drag start . the lamp have go out among the foliage , and the star be wan a little , and there be a faint cold light creep over the garden which mean the advent of morning . Don Gomez stroll towards the lighted house , smoke a cigarette . 

 ' she be very lovely , and she be well not quite spoil by her entourage , and they tell I she be an heiress sure to inherit a fine fortune from some ancient grandmother , bury alive in Westmoreland , ' he muse . ' what a splendid opportunity it would be if if the business could be arrange on the square . but as it be well as it be there be the chance of an adventure ; and when do a Montesma ever avoid an adventure , although there be dagger or poison lurk in the background ? and here there be neither poison nor steel , only a lovely woman , and an infatuated stockbroker , about whom I know enough to disgrace and ruin an archbishop . poor Smithson ! how very unlucky that I should happen to come across your pathway in the heyday of your late love affair . we have have our little adventure in that line already , and we have measure sword together , metaphorically , before to - night . when it come to a question of actual sword my Smithson decline . pas si bete . ' 

 CHAPTER XXXVII . 

 LORD HARTFIELD REFUSES a fortune . 


 a honeymoon among lake and mountain , amidst the gorgeous confusion of Borrowdale , in a little world of wild , strange loveliness , shut in and isolate from the prosaic outer world by the vast and tower mass of Skiddaw and Blencathara a world of one 's own , as it be , a world steep in romance and poetry , dear to the soul of poet . there be many such honeymoon every summer ; indeed , the mountain path , the waterfall and lake swarm with happy lover ; and this land of hill and water seem to have be make expressly for honeymoon traveller ; yet never go truer lover wander by lake and torrent , by hill and valley , than those two whose brief honeymoon be now draw to a close . 

 it be altogether a magical time for Mary , this dawn of a new life . the immensity of her happiness almost frighten she . she could hardly believe in it , or trust in its continuance . 

 ' be I really , really , really your wife ? ' she ask on their last day , bend down to speak to her husband , as he lead her pony up the rough way of Skiddaw . ' it be all so dreadfully like a dream . ' 

 ' thank God , it be the very truth , ' answer Lord Hartfield , look fondly at the fresh young face , brighten by the summer wind , which faintly stir the auburn hair under the neat little hat . 

 ' and be I actually a Countess ? I do not   care about it one little bit , you know , except as a stupendous joke . if you be to tell I that you have be only make fun of poor grandmother and I , and that those diamond be glass , and you only plain John Hammond , it would not   make the faint difference . indeed , it would be a weight off my mind . it be an awfully oppressive thing to be a Countess . ' 

 ' I be sorry I can not relieve you of the burden . the law of the land have make you Lady Hartfield ; and I hope you be prepare your mind for the duty of your position . ' 

 ' it be very dreadful , ' sigh Mary . ' if her ladyship be as well and as active as she be when first you come to Fellside , she could have help I ; but now there will be no one , except you . and you will help I , will not   you Jack ? ' 

 ' with all my heart . ' 

 ' my own true Jack , ' with a little fervent squeeze of his sunburnt hand . ' in society I suppose I shall have to call you Hartfield . " Hartfield , please ring the bell . " " give I a footstool , Hartfield . " how odd it sound . I shall be blurt out the old dear name . ' 

 ' I do not   think it will much matter . it will pass for one of Lady Hartfield 's little way . every woman be suppose to have little way , do not   you know . one have a little way of drop her friend ; another have a little way of not pay her dressmaker ; another 's little way be to take too much champagne . I hope Lady Hartfield 's little way will be her devotion to her husband . ' 

 ' I be afraid I shall end by be a nuisance to you , for I shall love you ridiculously , ' answer Mary , gaily ; ' and from what you have tell I about society , it seem to I that there can be nothing so unfashionable as an affectionate wife . will you mind my be quite out of fashion , Jack ? ' 

 ' I should very much object to your be in the fashion . ' 

 ' then I be happy . I do not   think it be in my nature to become a woman of fashion ; although I have cure myself , for your sake , of be a hoyden . I have so school myself for what I think our new life be to be ; so train myself to be a manage economical wife , that I feel quite at sea now that I be to be mistress of a house in Grosvenor Square and a place in Kent . still , I will bear with it all ; yes , even endure the weight of those diamond for your sake . ' 

 she laugh , and he laugh . they be quite alone among the hill hardy mountaineer both and they could be as foolish as they like . she rest her head upon his shoulder , and he and she and the pony make one as they climb the hill , close together . 

 ' our last day , ' sigh Mary , as they go down again , after a couple of blissful hour in that wild world between earth and sky . ' I shall be glad to go back to poor grandmother , who must be sadly lonely ; but it be so sweet to be quite alone with you . ' 

 they leave the Lodore Hotel in an open carriage , after luncheon next day , and post to Fellside , where they arrive just in time to assist at Lady Maulevrier 's afternoon tea . she receive they both with warm affection , and make Hartfield sit close beside her sofa ; and every now and then , in the pause of their talk , she lay she waste and too delicate finger upon the young man 's strong brown hand , with a caressing gesture . 

 ' you can never know how sweet it be to I to be able to love you , ' she say tenderly . ' you can never know how my heart yearn to you from the very first , and how hard it be to keep myself in check and not be too kind to you . oh , Hartfield , you should have tell I the truth . you should not have come here under false colour . ' 

 ' should I not , Lady Maulevrier ? it be my only chance of be love for my own sake ; or , at least of know that I be so loved . if I have come with my rank and my fortune in my hand , as it be one of the good match of the year what security could I ever have feel in the disinterested love of the girl who choose I ? as plain John Hammond I woo and be reject ; as plain John Hammond I woo and win ; and the prize which I so win be a pearl above price . not for world , be the last year to be live over again , would I have one day of my life alter . ' 

 ' well , I suppose I ought to be satisfied . I want you for Lesbia , and I have get you for Mary . good of all , I have get you for myself . Ronald Hollister 's son be mine ; he be of my kin ; he belong to I ; he will not forsake I in life ; he will be near I , God grant , when I die . ' 

 ' Dear Lady Maulevrier , as far as in I lie , I will be to you as a son , ' say Lord Hartfield , very solemnly , stoop to kiss her hand . 

 Mary come away from her tea - table to embrace her grandmother . 

 ' it make I so happy to have win a little of your regard , ' she murmur , ' and to know that I have marry a man whom you can love . ' 

 ' of course you have hear of Lesbia 's engagement ? ' Lady Maulevrier say presently , when they be take their tea . 

 ' Maulevrier write to we about it . ' 

 ' to we . ' how nice it sound , think Mary , as if they be a firm , and a letter write to one be write to both . 

 ' and do you know this Mr. Smithson ? ' 

 ' not intimately . I have meet he at the Carlton . ' 

 ' I be tell that he be very much esteemed by your party , and that he be very likely to get a peerage when this Ministry go out of office . ' 

 ' that be not improbable . peerage be to be have if a man be rich enough ; and Smithson be suppose to be inordinately rich . ' 

 ' I hope he have character as well as money , ' say Lady Maulevrier , gravely . ' but do you think a man can become inordinately rich in a short time , with unblemished honour ? ' 

 ' we be tell that nothing be impossible , ' answer Hartfield . ' faith can remove a mountain ; only one do not often see it do . however , I believe Mr. Smithson 's character be fairly good as millionaire go . we do not inquire too closely into these thing nowadays . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier sigh and hold her peace . she remember the day when she have protest vehemently , passionately , against Lesbia 's marriage with a poor man . and now she have an unhappy feeling about Mr. Smithson 's wealth , a doubt , a dread that all might not be well with those million , that some portion of that golden tide might flow from impure source . she have live remote from the world , but she have read the paper diligently , and she know how often the splendour of commercial wealth have be suddenly obscure behind a black cloud of obloquy . she could not rejoice heartily at the idea of Lesbia 's engagement . 

 ' I be to see the man early in August , ' she say , as if she be talk of a butler . ' I hope I may like he . Lady Kirkbank tell I it be a brilliant marriage , and I must take her word . what can I do for my granddaughter a useless log a prisoner in two room ? '
