 ' it be very hard , ' murmur Mary , tenderly , ' but I do not see any reason why Lesbia should not be happy . she like a brilliant life ; and Mr. Smithson can give she as much gaiety and variety as she can possibly desire . and , after all , yacht , and horse , and villa , and diamond be nice thing . ' 

 ' they be the thing for which half the world be ready to cheat or murder the other half , ' say Lady Maulevrier , bitterly . she have tell herself long ago that wealth be power , and she have sacrifice many thing , her own peace , her own conscience among they , in order that her child and grandchild should be rich ; and , know this , she feel it ill become she to be scrupulous , and to inquire too closely as to the source of Mr. Smithson 's wealth . he be rich , and the world have no fault to find with he . he have attend the last levťe . he go into reputable society . and he could give Lesbia all those thing which the world call good . 

 Fršulein M ller have pack her heavy old german trunk , and have go back to the Heimath , laden with present of all kind from Lady Maulevrier ; so Mary and her husband feel as if Fellside be really their own . they din with her ladyship , and leave she for the night an hour after dinner ; and then they go down to the garden , and roam about in the twilight , and talk , and talk , and talk , as only true lover can talk , be they Strephon and Daphne in life 's glad morning , or grey - haired Darby and Joan ; and lastly they go down to the lake , and row about in the moonlight , and talk of King Arthur 's death , and of that mystic sword , Excalibur , ' work by the lonely maiden of the lake . ' 

 they spend three happy day in wander about the neighbourhood , revisit in the delicious freedom of their wed life those spot which they have see together , when Mary be still in bondage , and the eye of propriety , as represent by Miss M ller , be always upon she . now they be free to go where they please to linger where they like they belong to each other , and be under no other dominion . 

 the dogcart , James Steadman 's dogcart , which he have rarely use during the last six month , be put in requisition and Lord Hartfield drive his wife about the country . they go to the Langdale Pikes , and to Dungeon Ghyll ; and , stand beside the waterfall , Mary tell her husband how miserable she have feel on that very spot a little less than a year ago , when she believe that he think she plain and altogether horrid . whereupon he have to console she with many kiss and sweet word , for the bygone pain on her part , the neglect on his . 

 ' I be a wretch , ' he say , ' blind , besotte , imbecile . ' 

 ' no , no , no . Lesbia be very lovely and I could not expect you would care for I till she be go away . how glad I be that she go , ' add Mary , naively . 

 the sky , which have be cloudless all day , begin to darken as Lord Hartfield drive back to Fellside , and Mary draw a little close to the driver 's elbow , as if for shelter from an impending tempest . 

 ' you have your waterproof , of course , ' he say , look down at she , as the first big drop of a thunder shower dash upon the splash - board . ' no young woman in the Lake country would think of be without a waterproof . ' 

 Mary be duly provide , and with the help of the groom put herself into a snug little tartan Inverness , while Hartfield send the cart spinning along twelve mile an hour . 

 they be at Fellside before the storm develop its full power , but the sky be leaden , the landscape dull and blot , the atmosphere heavy and stifle . the thunder grumble hoarsely , far away yonder in the wild gorge of Borrowdale ; and Mary and her husband make up their mind that the temp would come before midnight . 

 Lady Maulevrier be suffer from the condition of the atmosphere . she have go to bed , prostrate with a neuralgic headache , and have give order that no one but she maid should go near she . so Lord Hartfield and his wife din by themselves , in the room where Mary have eat so many uninteresting dinner tete - a - tete with Fršulein ; and in spite of the storm which howl , pelt , and lighten every now and then , Mary feel as if she be in Paradise . 

 there be no chance of go out after dinner . the lake look like a pool of ink , the mountain be monster of dark and threatening aspect , the rain rattle against the window , and run from the verandah in miniature water - spout . there be nothing to do but stay in door , in the sultry , dusky house . 

 ' let we go to my boudoir , ' say Mary . ' let I enjoy the full privilege of have a boudoir my very own room . be not   it too good of grandmother to have it make so smart for I ? ' 

 ' nothing can be too good for my Mary , ' answer her husband , still in the doting stage , ' but it be very nice of her ladyship and the room be charming . ' 

 delightful as the new boudoir might be , they dawdle in the picture gallery , that long corridor on which all the upper room open , and at one end of which be the door of Lady Maulevrier 's bedroom , at right angle with that red - cloth door , which be never open , except to give egress or ingress to James Steadman , who keep the key of it , as if the old part of Fellside House have be an enchanted castle . Lord Hartfield have not forget that summer midnight last year , when his meditation be disturb by a woman 's pierce cry . he think of it this evening , as Mary and he lower their voice on draw near Lady Maulevrier 's door . she be asleep within there now , perhaps , that strange old woman ; and at any moment an awful shriek , as of a soul in mortal agony , might startle they in the midst of their bliss . 

 the lamp be light below ; but this upper part of the house be wrap in the dull grey twilight of a stormy evening . a single lamp burn dimly at the further end of the corridor , and all the rest be shadow . 

 Mary and her husband walk up and down , talk in subdue tone . he be explain the necessity of his be in London next week , and promise to come back to Fellside directly his business at the House be over . 

 ' it will be delightful to read your speech , ' say Mary ; ' but I be silly and selfish enough to wish you be a country squire , with no business in London . and yet I do not   wish that either , for I be intensely proud of you . ' 

 ' and some day , before we be much old , you will sit in your robe in the peeress 's gallery . ' 

 ' oh , I could not , ' cry Mary . ' I should make a fool of myself , somehow . I should look like a housemaid in borrow plume . remember , I have no Anstand I have be tell so all my life . ' 

 ' you will be one of the prettiest peeress who ever sit in that gallery , and the pure , and true , and dear , ' protest her lover - husband . 

 ' oh , if I be good enough for you , I be satisfied . I marry you , and not the House of Lords . but I be afraid your friend will all say , " Hartfield , why in heaven 's name do you marry that uncultivated person ? " look ! ' 

 she stop suddenly , with her hand on her husband 's arm . it be grow momentarily dark in the corridor . they be at the end near the lamp , and that other end by Lady Maulevrier 's door be in deep darkness , yet not too dark for Lord Hartfield to see what it be to which Mary point . 

 the red - cloth door be open , and a faint glimmer of light show within . a man be stand in the corridor , a small , shrunken figure , bent and old . 

 ' it be Steadman 's uncle , ' say Mary . ' do let I go and speak to he , poor , poor old man . ' 

 ' the madman ! ' exclaim Hartfield . ' no , Mary ; go to your room at once . I will get he back to his own den . ' 

 ' but he be not mad at any rate , he be quite harmless . let I just say a few word to he . surely I be safe with you . ' 

 Lord Hartfield be not inclined to dispute that argument ; indeed , he feel himself strong enough to protect his wife from all the lunatic in Bedlam . he go towards the end of the corridor , keep Mary well behind he ; but Mary do not mean to lose the opportunity of renew her acquaintance with Steadman 's uncle . 

 ' I hope you be well , poor old soul , ' she murmur , gently , lovingly almost , nestle at her husband 's side . 

 ' what , be it you ? ' cry the old man , tremulous with joy . ' oh , I have be look for you look look wait , wait for you . I have be hop for you every hour and every minute . why do not   you come to I , cruel girl ? ' 

 ' I try with all my might , ' say Mary , ' but people block up the door in the stable , and they would not   let I go to you ; and I have be rather busy for the last fortnight , ' add Mary , blush in the darkness , ' I I be married to this gentleman . ' 

 ' married ! ah , that be a good thing . he will take care of you , if he be an honest man . ' 

 ' I think he be an honest man , but he have turn out to be an earl , ' answer Mary , proudly . ' my husband be Lord Hartfield . ' ' Hartfield Hartfield , ' the old man repeat , feebly . ' surely I have hear that name before . ' 

 there be no violence in his manner , nothing but imbecility ; so Lord Hartfield make up his mind that Mary be right , and that the old man be quite harmless , worthy of all compassion and kindly treatment . 

 this be the same old man whom he have meet on the Fell in the bleak March morning . there be no doubt in his mind about that , although he could hardly see the man 's face in the shadowy corridor . 

 ' come , ' say the man , ' come with I , my dear . you forget I , but I have not forget you . I mean to leave you my fortune . come with I , and I will show you your legacy . it be all for you every rupee every jewel . ' 

 this word rupee startle Lord Hartfield . it have a strange sound from the lip of a Westmoreland peasant . 

 ' come , child , come ! ' say the man impatiently . ' come and see what I have leave you in my will . I make a new will every day , but I leave everything to you every will be in your favour . but if you be married you have well have your legacy at once . your husband be strong enough to take care of you and your fortune . ' 

 ' poor old man , ' whisper Mary ; ' pray let we humour he . ' 

 it be the usual madman 's fancy , no doubt . boundless wealth , exalted rank , sanctity , power these thing all belong to the lunatic . he be the lord of creation , and , feed by such fancy , he enjoy flash of wild happiness in the midst of his woe . 

 ' come , come , both of you , ' say the old man , eagerly , breathless with impatience . 

 he lead the way across the sacred threshold , look back , beckon to they with his waste old hand , and Mary for the first time in her life enter that house which have seem to she from her very childhood as a temple of silence and mystery . the passage be dimly light by a little lamp on a bracket . the old man creep along stealthily , look back , with a face full of cunning , till he come to a broad landing , from which an old staircase , with massive oak banister , lead down to the square hall below . the ceiling be low , the passage be narrow . all thing in the house be curiously different from that spacious mansion which Lady Maulevrier have build for herself . 

 a door on the landing stand ajar . the old man push it open and go in , follow by Mary and her husband . 

 they both expect to see a room humble almost to poverty an iron bedstead , perhaps , and such furniture as the under servant in a nobleman 's household be privileged to enjoy . both be alike surprised at the luxury of the apartment they enter , and which be evidently reserve exclusively for Steadman 's uncle . 

 it be a sitting - room . the furniture be old - fashioned , but almost as handsome as any in Lady Maulevrier 's apartment . there be a large sofa of most comfortable shape , cover with dark red velvet , and furnish with pillow and foot rug , which would have satisfy a Sybarite of the first water . beside the sofa stand a hookah , with all appliance in the oriental fashion ; and half a dozen long cherry - wood pipe neatly arrange above the mantelpiece show that Mr. Steadman 's uncle be a smoker of a luxurious type . 

 in the centre of the room stand a large writing table , with a case of pigeon - hole at the back , a table which would not have disgrace a Prime Minister 's study . a pair of wax candle , in tall silver candlestick , light this table , which be litter with paper , in a wild confusion that too plainly indicate the condition of the owner 's mind . the oak floor be cover with persian prayer rug , old and fade , but of the rich quality . the window curtain be dark red velvet ; and through an open doorway Mary and her husband see a corresponding luxury in the arrangement of the adjoining bedroom . 

 the whole thing seem wild and strange as a fairy tale . the weird and wizene old man , grin and nod his head at they . the handsome room , rich with dark , subdue colour , in the dim light of four wax candle , two on the table , two on the mantelpiece . the perfume of stephanotis and tea - rose , blend faintly with the all - pervade odour of latakia and turkish attar . all be alike strange , bear in mind that this old man be a recipient of Lady Maulevrier 's charity , a hanger - on upon a confidential servant , who might be suppose to be generously treat if he have the run of his tooth and the shelter of a decent garret . verily , there be something regal in such hospitality as this , accord to a pauper lunatic . 

 where be Steadman , the alert , the watchful , all this time ? Mary wonder . they have meet no one . the house be as mute as if it be under the spell of a magician . it be like that awful chamber in the arabian story , where the young man find the magic horse , and start on his fatal journey . Mary feel as if here , too , there must be peril ; here , too , fate be work . 

 the old man go to the writing table , push aside the paper , and then stoop down and turn a mysterious handle or winch under the knee - hole , and the writing - desk move slowly on one side , while the pigeon - hole sank , and a deep well full of secret drawer be lay open . 

 from one of these secret drawer the old man take a bunch of key , nodding , chuckle , mutter to himself as he grope for they with tremulous hand . 

 ' Steadman be uncommonly clever think he know everything but he do not   know the trick of this table . I could hide a regiment of Sepoys in this table , my dear . well , well , perhaps not sepoy too big , too big but I could hide all the State paper of the Presidency . there be drawer enough for that . ' 

 Hartfield watch he intently , with thoughtful brow . there be a mystery here , a mystery of the deep dye ; and it be for he it must need be his task , welcome or unwelcome , to unravel it . 

 this be the Maulevrier skeleton . 

 ' now , come with I , ' say the old man , clutch Mary 's wrist , and draw she towards the half - open door lead into the bedroom . 

 she have a feeling of shrinking , for there be something uncanny about the old man , something that might be life or death , might belong to this world or the next ; but she have no fear . in the first place , she be courageous by nature , and in the second her husband be with she , a tower of strength , and she could know no fear while he be at her side . 

 the strange old man lead the way across his bedroom to an inner chamber , oak pannelle , with very little furniture , but hold much treasure in the shape of trunk , portmanteaux all very old and dusty and two large wooden case , band with iron . 

 before one of these case the man kneel down , and apply a key to the padlock which fasten it . he give the candle to Lord Hartfield to hold , and then open the box . it seem to be full of book , which he begin to remove , heap they on the floor beside he ; and it be not till he have clear away a layer of dingy volume that he come to a large metal strong box , so heavy that he could not lift it out of the chest . 

 slowly , tremulously , and with quickened breathing , he unlock the box where it be , and raise the lid . 

 ' look , ' he say eagerly , ' this be her legacy this be my little girl 's legacy . ' 

 Lord Hartfield bend down and look at the old man 's treasure , by the waver light of the candle ; Mary look over his shoulder , breathless with wonder . 

 the strong box be divide into compartment . one , and the large , be fill with rouleaux of coin , pack as closely as possible . the other contain jewel , set and unset diamond , emerald , ruby , sapphire which flash back the flicker flame of the candle with glinting of rainbow light . 

 ' these be all for she all all , ' exclaim the old man . ' they be worth a prince 's ransom . those rouleaux be all gold ; those gem be priceless . they be the dowry of a princess . but they be hers now yes , my dear , they be yours because you speak sweetly , and smile prettily , and be very good to a lonely old man and because you have my mother 's face , dear , a smile that recall the day of my youth . lift out the box and take it away with you , if you be strong enough , you , you , ' he say , touch Lord Hartfield . ' hide it somewhere keep it from she . let no one know no one except your wife and you must be in the secret . ' 

 ' my dear sir , it be out of the question impossible that my wife or I should accept one of those coin or the small of those jewel . ' 

 ' why not , in the devil 's name ? ' 

 ' first and foremost , we do not know how you come in possession of they ; secondly , we do not know who you be . ' 

 ' they come to I fairly enough bequeath to I by one who have the right to leave they . would you have have all that gold leave for an adventurer to wallow in ? ' 

 ' you must keep your treasure , sir , however it may have come to you , ' answer Lord Hartfield firmly . ' my wife can not take upon herself the burden of a single gold coin least of all from a stranger . remember , sir , to we your possession of this wealth nay , your whole existence be a mystery . ' 

 ' you want to know who I be ? ' say the old man draw himself up , with a sudden hauteur which be not without dignity , despite his shrunken form and grotesque appearence . ' well , sir . I be - - ' 

 he check himself abruptly , and look round the room with a scared expression . 

 ' no , no , no , ' he mutter ; ' caution , caution ! they have not do with I yet ; she warn I they be lie in wait ; I must not   walk into their trap . ' and then turn to Lord Hartfield , he say , haughtily , ' I shall not condescend to tell you who I be , sir . you must know that I be a gentleman , and that be enough for you . there be my gift to your wife ' pointing to the chest ' take it or leave it . ' 

 ' I shall leave it , sir , with all due respect . ' 

 a frightful change come over the old man 's face at this determined refusal . his eye glower at Lord Hartfield under the heavy scowling brow ; his bloodless lip work convulsively . 

 ' do you take I for a thief ? ' he exclaim . ' be you afraid to touch my gold that gold for which man and woman sell their soul , blast their life with shame , and pain , and dishonour , all the world over ? do you stand aloof from it refuse to touch it , as if it be infect ? and you , too , girl ! have you no sense ? be you an idiot ? ' 

 ' I can do nothing against my husband 's wish , ' Mary answer , quietly ; ' and , indeed , there be no need for we to take your money . we be rich without it . please leave that chest to a hospital . it will be ever so much well than give it to we . ' 

 ' you tell I you be go to marry a poor man ? ' 

 ' I know . but he cheat I , and turn out to be a rich man . he be a horrid impostor , ' say Mary , draw close to her husband , and smile up at he . 

 the old man fling down the lid of his strong box , which shut with a sonorous clang . he lock it , and put the key in his pocket . 

 ' I have do with you . ' he say . ' you can go your way , both of you . Fools , fool , fool ! the world be people with rogue and fool ; and , by heaven , I would rather have to do with the rogue ! ' 

 he fling himself into an arm - chair , one of the few object of furniture in the room , and leave they to find their way back alone . 

 ' good - night , sir , ' say Lord Hartfield ; but the old man make no reply . he sit frown sullenly . 

 ' good - night , sir , ' say Mary , in her gentle voice , breathe infinite pity . 

 ' good - night , child , ' he growl . ' I be sorry you have marry an ass . ' 

 this be more than Mary could stand , and she be about to reply with some acrimony , when her husband put his hand upon her lip and hurry she away . 

 on the landing they meet Mrs. Steadman , a stout , commonplace person , who always have the same half - frightened look , as of one who live in the shadow of an abide terror , obviously cow and brow - beat by her husband , accord to the Fellside household . 

 at sight of Lord Hartfield and his wife she look a little more frightened than usual . 

 ' goodness gracious , Lady Mary ! how ever do you come here ? ' she gasp , not yet have quite realise the fact that Mary have be promote . 

 ' we come to please Steadman 's uncle he bring we in here , ' Mary answer , quietly . 

 ' but where do you find he ? ' 

 ' in the corridor just by her ladyship 's room . ' 

 ' then he must have take the key out of Steadman 's pocket , or Steadman must have leave it about somewhere , ' mutter Mrs. Steadman , as if explain the matter to herself , rather than to Mary . ' my poor husband be not the man he be . and so you meet he in the corridor , and he bring you in here . poor old gentleman ! he get madder and madder every day . ' 

 ' there be method in his madness , ' say Lord Hartfield . ' he talk very much like sanity just now . have your husband have the charge of he long ? ' 

 Mrs. Steadman answer somewhat confusedly . 

 ' a goodish time , sir . I can not   quite exactly say time pass so quiet in a place like this . one hardly keep count of the year . ' 

 ' forty year , perhaps ? ' 

 Mrs. Steadman blench under Lord Hartfield 's steadfast look a look which question more searchingly than his word . 

 ' forty year , ' she repeat , with a faint laugh . ' oh , dear no , sir , not a quarter as long . it be not   so many year , after all , since Steadman 's poor old uncle go a little queer in his head ; and Steadman , have such a quiet home here , and plenty of spare room , make bold to ask her ladyship if he might give the poor old man a home , where he would be in nobody 's way . ' 

 ' and the poor old man seem to have a very luxurious home , ' answer Lord Hartfield . ' pray when and where do Mr. Steadman 's uncle learn to smoke a hookah ? ' 

 Simple as the question be , it prove too much for Mrs. Steadman . she only shake her head , and falter some unintelligible reply . 

 ' where be your husband ? ' ask Lord Hartfield ; ' I should like to have a little talk with he , if he be disengage . ' 

 ' he be not very well , my lord , ' answer Mrs. Steadman . ' he have be ail off and on for the last six month , but I could not   get he to see the doctor , or to tell her ladyship that he be in bad health . and about a week ago he break down altogether , and fall into a kind of drowsy state . he keep about , and he do his work pretty much the same as usual , but I can see that it be too much for he . if you like to come downstairs I can let you through the low door into the hall ; and if he should have wake up since I have leave he he will be at your lordship 's service . but I would rather not wake he out of his sleep . ' 

 ' there be no occasion . what I have to say will keep till to - morrow . ' 

 Lord Hartfield and his wife follow Mrs. Steadman downstairs to the low dark hall , where an old eight - day clock tick with hoarse and solemn beat , and a fine stag 's head over each doorway give evidence of some former Haselden 's sporting taste . the door of a small panel parlour stand half - way open ; and within the room Lord Hartfield see James Steadman asleep in an arm - chair by the fire , which burn as brightly as if it have be Christmas time . 

 ' he be so chilly and shivery this afternoon that I be oblige to light a fire , ' say Mrs. Steadman . 

 ' he seem to be sleep heavily , ' say Hartfield . ' do not   awaken he . I will see he to - morrow morning before I go to London . ' 

 ' he sleep half the day just as heavy as that , my lord , ' say the wife , with a troubled air . ' I do not   think it can be right . ' 

 ' I do not   think so either , ' answer Lord Hartfield . ' you have well call in the doctor . ' 

 ' I will , my lord , to - morrow morning . James will be angry with I , I daresay ; but I must take upon myself to do it without his leave . ' 

 she lead the way along a passage correspond with the one above , and unlock a door open into a lobby near the billiard - room . 

 ' come , molly , see if you can beat I at a fifty game , ' say Lord Hartfield , with the air of a man who want to shake off the impression of some dominant idea . 

 ' of course you will annihilate I , but it will be a relief to play , ' answer Mary . ' that strange old man have give I a shock . everything about his surrounding be so different from what I expect . and how could an uncle of Steadman 's come by all that money and those jewel if they be jewel , and not bit of glass which the poor old thing have chop up , in order to delude himself with an imaginary treasure ? ' 

 ' I do not think they be bit of glass , Molly . ' 

 ' they sparkle tremendously almost as much as my our the family diamond , ' say Mary , puzzle how to describe that property which she hold in right of her position as countess regnant ; ' but if they be real jewel , and all those rouleaux real money , how could Steadman 's uncle become possessed of such wealth ? ' 

 ' how , indeed ? ' say Lord Hartfield , choose his cue 

 CHAPTER XXXVIII . 

 ON BOARD the ' CAYMAN . ' 


 Goodwood have come and go , a brief bright season of loss and gain , fine gown , flirtation , lobster en mayonaise , champagne , sunshine , dust , glare , babble of many voice , success , failure , triumph , humiliation . a very pretty picture to contemplate from the outside , this little world in holiday clothe , frame in greenery ! but just on the Brocken , where the nice girl among the dancer have the unpleasant peculiarity of drop a little red mouse out of her mouth so too here under different form there be red mouse drop about among the company . here a hint of come insolvency ; there a whisper of a threatened divorce suit , stave off for a while , compromise , family secret , little difficulty everywhere ; betroth couple smilingly accept congratulation , who should never have be affiance be truth and honour the rule of life ; forsake wife pretend to think their husband model of fidelity ; jovial creature with ruin star in their face ; household divide and sham union ; almost everybody live above his or her mean ; and the knowledge that nobody be any well or any happy than his neighbour society 's only fountain of consolation . 

 Lady Lesbia 's gown and parasol have be admire , her engagement have furnish an infinity of gossip , and the fact of Montesma 's constant attendance upon she have give zest to the situation , just that flavour of peril and fatality which the soul of society loveth . 

 ' be she go to marry they both ? ' ask an ancient dowager of the ever - young type . 

 ' no , dear Lady Sevenoaks , she can only marry one , do not   you know ; but the other be nice to go about with ; and I believe it be the other she really like . ' 

 ' it be always the other that a woman like , ' answer the dowager ; ' I be madly in love with this peruvian no , I think you say Cuban myself . I wish some good - natured creature would present he to I . if you know anybody who know he , tell they to bring he to my next afternoon Saturday . but why do choose machin Smithson allow such a handsome hanger - on ? after marriage I could understand that he might not be able to help himself ; but before marriage a man generally have some kind of authority . ' 

 the world wonder a little , just as Lady Sevenoaks wonder , at Smithson 's complacency in allow a man so attractive as Montesma to be so much in the society of his future wife , yet even the censorious could but admit that the Cuban 's manner offer no ground for offence . he come to Goodwood ' on his own hook , ' as society put it : and every man who wear a decent coat and be not a welsher have a right to enjoy the prettiest race - course in England . he spend a considerable part of the day in Lesbia 's company ; but since she be the centre of a little crowd all the time , there could be no offence in this . he be a stranger , know very few people , and have nothing to do but to amuse himself . Smithson be an old and familiar friend , and be in a measure bind to give he hospitality . 

 Mr. Smithson have recognise that obligation , but in a somewhat spar manner . there be a dozen unoccupied bedchamber in the Park Lane Renaissance villa ; but Smithson do not invite his cuban acquaintance to shift his quarter from the Bristol to Park Lane . he be civil to Don Gomez : but anyone who have take the trouble to watch and study the conduct and social relation of these two man would have see that his civility be a forced civility , and that he endure the Spaniard 's society under constraint of some kind . 

 and now all the world be flock to Cowes for the regatta , and Lesbia and her chaperon be establish on board Mr. Smithson 's yacht , the Cayman ; and the captain of the Cayman and all her crew be deliver over to Lesbia to be her slave and to obey her light breath . the Cayman be to lie at anchor off Cowes for the regatta week ; and then she be to sail for Hyde , and lie at anchor there for another regatta week ; and she be to be a float hotel for Lady Lesbia so long as the young lady would condescend to occupy she . 

 the captain be an altogether exceptional captain , and the crew be a pick crew , ruddy face , sandy whisker for the most part , Englishmen all , honest , hardy fellow from between the Nore and the Wash , talk in an honest provincial patois , dash with sea slang . they be the very pink and pattern of cleanliness , and the Cayman herself from stem to stern be dazzle and spotless to an almost painful degree . 

 not content with the exist arrangement of the yacht , which be at once elegant and luxurious , Mr. Smithson have send down a Bond Street upholsterer to refit the saloon and Lady Lesbia 's cabin . the dark velvet and morocco which suit a masculine occupant would not have harmonise with girlhood and beauty ; and Mr. Smithson 's saloon , as originally design , have something of the air of a tabagie . the Bond Street man strip away all the velvet and morocco , pluck up the Turkey carpet , drape the scuttle - port with pale yellow cretonne garnish with orange pompon , subdue the glare of the skylight by a blind of oriental silk , cover the divan with persian saddlebag , the floor with a delicate indian matting , and furnish the saloon with all that be most feminine in the way of bamboo chair and tea - table , japanese screen and fan of gorgeous colouring . here and there against the fluted yellow drapery he fasten a large Rhodes plate ; and the thing be do . Lady Lesbia 's cabin be all bamboo and embroider India muslin . an oval glass , frame in Dresden biscuit , adorn the side , a large white bearskin cover the floor . the berth be pretty enough for the cradle of a duchess 's first baby . even Lesbia , spoil by much indulgence and unlimited credit , give a little cry of pleasure at sight of the nest that have be make ready for she . 

 ' really , Mr. Smithson be immensely kind ! ' she exclaim . 

 ' Smithson be always kind , ' answer Lady Kirkbank , ' and you do not   half enough appreciate he . he have give I his very own cabin such a dear little den ! there be his cigar box and everything lovely on the shelf , and his own particular dressing - case put open for I to use all the back of all the brush repoussť silver , and all the scent - bottle fill expressly for I . if the yacht would only stand quite still , I should think it more delicious than the good house I ever stay in : only I do not   altogether enjoy that little way it have of gurgle up and down perpetually . ' 

 Mr. Smithson 's chief butler , a german Swiss , and a treasure of intelligence , have come down to take the domestic arrangement of the yacht into his control . the Park Lane chef be also on board , Mr. Smithson 's steward act as his subordinate . this great man grumble sorely at the smallness of his surrounding ; for the most luxurious yacht be a poor substitute for the spacious kitchen and storeroom and stillroom of the London mansion . there be a cabin for Lady Kirkbank 's Rilboche and Lady Lesbia 's Kibble , where the two might squabble at their leisure ; in a word , everything have be do that forethought could do to make the yacht as perfect a place of sojourn as any float habitation , from Noah 's Ark to the Orient steamer , have ever be make . 

 it be between four and five upon a delicious July afternoon that Lady Kirkbank and her charge come on board . the maid and the luggage have be send a day in advance , so that everything might be in its place , and the empty box all stow away , before the lady arrive . they have nothing to do but walk on board and fle themselves into the low luxurious chair ready for they on the deck , a little weary by the heat and dust of a railway journey , and with that delicious sense of languid indifference to all the care of life which seem to be in the very atmosphere of a perfect summer afternoon . 

 a striped awning cover the deck , and great basket of rose pink , and red , and yellow be place about here and there . Tea be ready on a low table , a swing brass kettle hiss merrily , with an air of supreme homeliness . 

 Mr. Smithson have accompany his fiancťe from town , and now sit read the Globe , and meekly wait for his tea , while Lesbia take a languid survey of the shore and the flotilla of boat , little and big , and while Lady Kirkbank rhapsodise about the yacht , praise everything , and call everything by a wrong name . he be to be their guest all day , and every day . they be to have enough of he , as Lesbia have observe to her chaperon , with a spice of discontent , not quite so delighted with the arrangement as her faithful swain . to he the idea be rapture . 

 ' you have contrive somehow to keep I very much at a distance hitherto , ' he tell Lesbia , ' and I feel sometimes as if we be almost stranger ; but a yacht be the good place in the world to bring two people together , and a week at Cowes will make we nearer to each other and more to each other than three month in London ; ' and Lesbia have say nothing , inwardly revolt at the idea of become any nearer and dearer to this man whom she have pledge herself to marry . she be to be his wife yes , some day and it be his desire the some day should be soon : but in the interval her dear privilege be the power to keep he at a distance . 

 and yet she could not make up her mind to break with he , to say honestly , ' I never like you much , and now we be engaged I find myself like you less and less every day . save I from the irrevocable wickedness of a loveless marriage . forgive I , and let I go . ' no , this she could not bring herself to say . she do not like Mr. Smithson , but she value the position he be able to give she . she want to be mistress of that infinite wealth she could not renounce that right to which she fancy she have be bear , her right to be one of the Queens of Society : and the only man who have offer to crown she as queen , to find she a palace and a court , be Horace Smithson . without Mr. Smithson her first season would have result in dire failure . she might perhaps have endure that failure , and be content to abide the chance of a second season , have it not be for Mary 's triumph . but for Mary to be a Countess , and for Lesbia to remain Lesbia Haselden , a nobody , dependent upon the caprice of a grandmother whose mean might after all be but limit no , such a concatenation as that be not to be endure . Lesbia tell herself that she could not go back to Fellside to remain there indefinitely , a spinster and a dependent . she have learn the true value of money ; she have find out what the world be like ; and it seem to she that some such person as Mr. Smithson be essential to her existence , just as a butler be a necessity in a house . one may not like the man , but the post must be fill . 

 again , if she be to throw over Mr. Smithson , and speculate upon her chance of next year , what hope have she of do well in her second season than in her first ? the horizon be blank . there be no great parti likely to offer himself for competition . she have see all that the market could produce . wealthy bachelor , high - bear lover , could not drop from the moon . Lesbia , school by Lady Kirkbank , know her peerage by heart ; and she know that , have miss Lord Hartfield , there be really no one in the Blue Book worth wait for . thus , care only for those thing which wealth can buy , she have make up her mind that she could not do without Horace Smithson 's money ; and she must therefore need resign herself to the disagreeable necessity of take Smithson and his money together . the great auctioneer fate would not divide the lot . 

 she tell herself that for she a loveless marriage be , after all , no prodigious sacrifice . she have find out that heart make but a small figure in the sum of her life . she could do without love . a year ago she have fancy herself in love with John Hammond . in her seclusion at St. Bees , in the long , dull August day , saunter up and down by the edge of the sea , in the melancholy sunset hour , she think that her heart be break , that life be worthless without the man she love . she have think and feel all this , but not strongly enough to urge she to any great effort , not keenly enough to make she burst her chain . she have prefer to suffer this loss than to sacrifice her chance of future aggrandisement . and now she look back and remember those sunset walk by the sea , and all her thought and feeling in those silent summer hour ; and she smile at herself , half in scorn , half in pity , for her own weakness . how easily she have learn to do without he who at that hour seem the well part of her existence . a good deal of gaiety and praise , a little mild flirtation at Kirkbank Castle , and lo ! the image of her first lover begin to grow dim and blur , like a faded photograph . a season at Cannes , and she be cure . a week in London , and that first love be a thing of the past , a dream from which the dreamer awaketh , forget the thing that he have dream . 

 remember all this she tell herself that she have no heart , that love or no love be a question of very little moment , and that the personal quality of the man whom she choose for a husband matter nothing to she , provide that his land and house and social status come up to her standard of merit . she have see Mr. Smithson 's house and land ; and she be distinctly assure that he would in due course be raise to the peerage . she have , therefore , every reason to be satisfied . 

 have thus reason out the circumstance of her new life , she accept her fate with a languid grace , which harmonise with her delicate and patrician beauty . nobody could have for a moment suppose from her manner that she love Horace Smithson ; but nobody have the right to think that she detest he . she accept all his attention as a thing of course . the flower which he strew beneath her footstep , the pearl which he melt in her wine metaphorically speak be just ' good enough ' and no more . this afternoon , when Mr. Smithson ask she how she like the arrangement of the saloon and cabin , she say she think they would do very nicely . ' they would do . ' nothing more . 

 ' it be dreadfully small , of course , ' she say , ' when one be accustom to room : but it be rather amusing to be in a sort of doll 's house , and on deck it be really very nice . ' 

 this be the most Mr. Smithson have for his pain , and he seem to be content therewith . if a man will marry the prettiest girl of the year he must be satisfied with such scant civility as conscious perfection may give he . we know that Aphrodite be not altogether the most comfortable wife , and that Helen be a cause of trouble . 

 Mr. Smithson sit in a bamboo chair beside his mistress , and look ineffably happy when she hand he a cup of tea . Sky and sea be one exquisite azure the colour of the boat glance in the sunshine as if they have be jewel ; here an emerald rudder , there a gunwale paint with liquid ruby . white sail , white frock , white duck make vivid patch of light against the blue . the landscape yonder shone and sparkle as if it have be incandescent . all the world of land and sky and sea be steep in sunshine . a day on which to do nothing , read nothing , think nothing , only to exist . 

 while they sit bask in the balmy atmosphere , look lazily at that bright , almost insupportable picture of blue sea under blue sky , there come the dip of oar , make music , and a sound of coolness with every plash of water . 

 ' how good it be of somebody to row about , just to give we that nice soothe sound , ' murmur Lesbia . 

 Lady Kirkbank , with her dear old head throw back upon the cushion of her luxurious chair , and her dear little cornflower hat just a thought on one side , be sleep the sleep of the just , and unconsciously reveal the little golden arrangement which give variety to her front tooth . 

 the soothe sound come near and nearer , close under the Cayman 's quarter , and then a brown hand clasp the man - rope , and a light slim figure swing itself upon deck , while the boat bob and splash below . 

 it be Montesma , who have not be expect till the racing , which be not to begin for two day . a faint , faint rose bloom flush Lady Lesbia 's cheek at sight of he ; and Mr. Smithson give a little look of vexation , just one rapid contraction of the eyebrow , which resume their conventional placidity the next instant . 

 ' so good of you , ' he murmur . ' I really do not expect you till the beginning of the week . ' 

 ' London be simply insupportable in this weather most of all for a man bear in the Havanas . my soul thirst for blue water . so I say to myself , this good Smithson be at Cowes ; he will give I the run of his yacht and a room at his villa . why not go to Cowes at once ? ' 

 ' the room be at your service . I have only two or three of my people at Formosa , but just enough to look after a bachelor friend . ' 

 ' I want very little service , my dear fellow , ' answer Montesma , pleasantly . ' a man who have cross the Cordilleras and camp in the primeval forest on the shore of the Amazon , learn to help himself . so this be the Cayman ? Muy deleitoso , mi amigo . a float Paradise in little . if the ark have be like this , I do not   think any of the passenger would have want the flood to dry up . ' 

 he shake hand with Lady Lesbia as he speak , and with Lady Kirkbank , who look at he as if he be part of her dream , and then he sink into the chair on Lesbia 's left hand , with the air of be establish for the rest of the day . 

 ' I have leave my portmanteaux at the end of the pier , ' he say lazily . ' I dare say one of your fellow will be good enough to take they to Formosa for I ? ' 

 Mr. Smithson give the necessary order . all the beauty have go out of the sea and the sky for he , all the contentment from his mind ; and yet he be in no position to rebel against Fate in no position to say directly or indirectly , ' Don Gomez de Montesma , I do not   want you here , and I must request you to transfer yourself elsewhither . ' 

 Lesbia 's feeling be curiously different . the very sight of that nervous brown hand upon the rope just now have send a strange thrill through her vein . she who believe herself heartless could scarce trust herself to speak for the vehement throbbing of her heart . a sense of joy too deep for word possess she as she recline in her low chair , with droop eyelid , yet feel the fire of those dark southern eye upon her face , scorch she like an actual flame . 

 ' Lady Lesbia , may I have a cup of tea ? ' he ask ; not because he want the tea , but only for the cruel delight of see if she be able to give it to he calmly . 

 her hand shake , flutter , wander helplessly , as she pour out that cup of tea and hand it to Montesma , a feminine office which she have perform placidly enough for Mr. Smithson . the Spaniard take the cup from she with a quiet smile , a subtle look which seem to explore the inmost depth of her consciousness . 

 yes , this man be verily her master . she know it , and he know it , as that look of his tell she . vain to play her part of languid indifference vain to struggle against her bondage . in heart and spirit she be at his foot , an odalisque , recognise and bow down to her sultan . 

 happily for the general peace , Mr. Smithson have be look away seaward , with a somewhat troubled brow , while that little cup and saucer episode be be enact . and in the next minute Lesbia have recover her self - command , and resume that graceful languor which be one of her charm . she be weak , but she be not altogether foolish ; and she have no idea of succumb to this new influence of yield herself up to this conqueror , who seem to take her life into his hand as if it be a bit of thistledown . her agitation of those first few minute be due to the suddenness of his appearance the reaction from dulness to delight . she have be tell that he be not to be at Cowes till Monday , and lo ! he be here at her side , just as she be think how empty and dreary life be without he . 

 he drop into his place so naturally and easily , make himself so thoroughly at home and so agreeable to every one , that it be almost impossible for Horace Smithson to resent his audacity ! Mr. Smithson 's vital might be devour by the gnawing of the green - eyed monster , but however fierce that gnawing be , he do not want to seem jealous . Montesma be there as the very incarnation of some experience in Mr. Smithson 's past career , and he dare not object to the man 's presence . 

 and so the summer day wear on . they have the yacht all to themselves that evening , for the racing yacht be fulfil engagement in other water , and the gay company of pleasure - seeker have not yet fully assemble . they be drop in one by one , all the evening , and Cowes road grow full of life with every hour of the summer night . 

 Mr. Smithson and his guest din in the saloon , a snug little party of four , and sit long over dessert , deep into the dusk ; and they talk of all thing under heaven , thing frivolous , thing grave , but most of all about that fair , strange world in far - off southern water , the sunny island of the Caribbean Sea , and the dreamy , luxurious life of that tropical clime , half spanish , half oriental , wholly independent of european conventionality . Lesbia listen , enchant by the picture . what be Park Lane palace , and Berkshire manor , the petty splendour of the architect and the upholsterer , weigh against a world in which all nature be on a grander scale ? Mr. Smithson might give her fine house and costly upholstery ; but only the Tropic of Cancer could give she large and bright star , a world of rich colouring , a land of perpetual summer , night luminous with fire - fly , garden in which the fern and the cactus be as forest tree , and where humming - bird flash among the foliage like live flower ; nay , where the flower themselves take the form of the animal world and seem instinct with life and motion . 

 ' yes , ' say Mr. Smithson , with his gentlemanlike drawl , ' Spanish America and the West Indies be delightful place to talk about . there be so many thing one leave out of the picture thief , nigger , jigger , snake , mosquito , yellow Jack , creep , crawl creature of all kind . I always feel very glad I have be to South America . ' 

 ' why ? ' 

 ' in order that I may never go there again , ' reply Mr. Smithson . 

 ' I be begin to hope you would take I there some day , ' say Lesbia . 

 ' never again , no , not even for your sake . no man should ever leave Europe after he be five - and - thirty ; indeed , I doubt if after that age he should venture beyond the Mediterranean . that be the sea of civilisation . anything outside it mean barbarism . ' 

 ' I hope we be go to travel by - and - by , ' say Lesbia ; ' I have be mew up in Grasmere half my life , and if you be go to confine I to the shore of the Mediterranean , which be , after all , only a large lake , for the other half of my life , my existence will be a dull piece of work after all . I agree with what Don Gomez say the other night : " not to travel be not to live . " ' 

 they go on deck presently and sit in the summer darkness , light only by the star , and by the light of the yacht , and the faintly gleam window of the lighted town , sit long and late , in a state of ineffable repose . Lady Kirkbank , fortify by the produce of Mr. Smithson 's particular clo , and by a couple of glass of green Chartreuse , sleep profoundly . she have not enjoy herself so much for the last three month . she have be stretch on Society 's rack , and she have be grind in Society 's mill ; and neither mind nor body have be her own to do what she like withal . she have toil early and late , and have spar herself in no wise . and now the trouble be over for a space . here be rest and respite . she have do her duty as a chaperon , have provide her charge with the very good thing the matrimonial market offer . she have pay her creditor something on account all round , and have leave they appeased and trustful , if not content . Sir George have go oft alone to drink the water at Spa , and to fortify himself for Scotland and the grouse season . she be her own mistress , and she could fold her hand and take her rest , eat and drink and sleep and be merry , all at Mr. Smithson 's expense . 

 the yacht come flock in next day , like a flight of white - wing sea bird , and Mr. Smithson have enough to do receive visitor upon the Cayman . he be fully occupy ; but Montesma have nothing to do , except to amuse Lady Lesbia and her chaperon , and in this onerous task he succeed admirably . Lesbia find that it be too warm to be on the deck when there be perspire people , whose breath must be ninety by the thermometer , perpetually come on board ; so she and Lady Kirkbank sit in the saloon , and have the more distinguished guest bring down to they as to a Court ; and the shrewd of the guest be quick to divine that no company beyond that of Don Gomez de Montesma be really want in that rose - scented saloon . 

 the Spaniard teach Lady Kirkbank monte , which delight she , and which she vow she would introduce at her supper party in the half season of November , when she should be in London for a week or two , as a bird of passage , flit southward . he begin to teach Lesbia Spanish , a language for which she have take a sudden fancy ; and it be curious what tender accent , what hide meaning even a grammar can take from such a teacher . Spanish come easily enough to a learner who have be thoroughly drill in French and italian , and who have be teach the rudiment of Latin ; so by the end of a lesson , which go on at interval all day , the pupil be able to lisp a passage of Don Quixote in the sweetest Castilian , very sweet to the ear of Don Gomez a kind of baby language , precious as the first half - form syllable of infancy to mother . 

 Montesma have nothing to do but to amuse himself and his companion all day in the saloon , amidst odour of rose and peach , in a shadowy coolness make by strip silken blind ; but Mr. Smithson be not so much his own master . that innumerable company of friend which be the portion of the rich man give to hospitality would not let the owner of the Cayman go scot - free . 

 at a place like Cowes , on the eve of the regatta week , the freelance of society expect to find entertainment ; and Mr. Smithson have to maintain his character for princely hospitality at the sacrifice of his feeling as a lover . every ripple of Lesbia 's silvery laughter , every deep tone of Montesma 's voice , from the cabin below , send a pang to his jealous soul ; and yet he have to smile , and to order more champagne cup , and to be lavish of his good cigar , albeit insist that his friend should smoke their cigar in the bow well to leeward , so that no foul breathing of tobacco should pollute his Cleopatra galley . 

 Cleopatra be very happy meanwhile , sublimely indifferent even to the odour of tobacco . she have she Antony at her foot , look up at she , as she recite her lesson , with darkly luminous eye , obviously worship she , obviously intent on win she without count the cost . when have a Montesma ever count the cost to himself or other the cost in gold , in honour , in human life ? the record of Cuba in the palmy day of the slave trade would tell how lightly they hold the last ; and for honour , well , the private hell of island and main could tell their tale of specially print playing card , in which the sword or star on the back of each card have a secret language of their own , and be as finger - post for the initiate player . 

 Mr. Smithson have business on shore , and be fain to leave the yacht for an hour or two before dinner . he invite Don Gomez to go with he , but the offer be graciously decline . 

 ' Amigo , I do not   care even to look at land in such weather . it be so detestably dry , ' he plead . ' it be only the sound of the sea gurgle against the hull that reconcile one to existence . go , and be happy at your club , and send off those occult telegram of your , dearest . I shall not leave the Cayman till bed - time . ' 

 he look as fresh and cool as if utterly unaffected by the heat , which to a Cuban must have be a merely lukewarm condition of the atmosphere . but he affect to be prostrate , and Smithson could not insist . he have his card to play in a game which require extreme caution , and there be no friendly indicator on the back of his king and ace . he be feel his way in the dark , and do not know how much mischief Montesma be prepared to do . 

 when the owner of the yacht be go Don Gomez propose an adjournment to the deck for afternoon tea , and the trio sit under the awning , tea - drinking and gossip for the next hour . Lady Kirkbank tell the steward to say not at home to everybody , just as if she have a street door . 

 ' there be a good deal of the dolce far niente about this , ' say Montesma , presently ; ' but do not   you think we have be anchor in sight of that shabby little town quite long enough , and that it would be rather nice to spread our wing and sail round the island before the racing begin ? ' 

 ' it would be exquisite , ' say Lesbia . ' I be very tired of inaction , though I dearly love learn Spanish , ' she add , with a lovely smile , and a look that be half submissive , half mutinous . ' but I have really be begin to wonder whether this boat can move . ' 

 ' you will see that she can , and at a smart pace , too , if I sail she . shall we circumnavigate the island ? we can set sail after dinner . ' 

 ' will Mr. Smithson consent , do you think ? ' 

 ' why do Smithson exist , except to obey you ? ' 

 ' I do not   know if Lady Kirkbank would quite like it , ' say Lesbia , look at her chaperon , who be wave a big japanese fan , slowly , unsteadily , and with a somewhat drunken air , the while she slide into dreamland . 

 ' quite like what ? ' she murmur , drowsily . 

 ' a little sail . ' 

 ' I should dearly love it , if it do not   make I sea - sick . ' 

 ' sea - sick on a glassy lake like this ! impossible , ' say Montesma . ' I consider the thing settle . we set sail after dinner . ' 

 Mr. Smithson come back to the yacht just in time to dress for dinner . Don Gomez excuse himself from put on his dress suit . he be go to sail the yacht himself , and he be dress for his work , picturesquely , in white duck trouser , white silk shirt , and black velvet shooting jacket . he din with the permission of the lady , in this costume , in which he look so much handsomer than in the livery of polite life . he have a red scarf tie round his waist , and when at his work by - and - by , he wear a little red silk cap , just stick lightly on his dark hair . the dinner to - day be all animation and even excitement , very different from the languorous calm of yesterday . Lesbia seem a new creature . she talk and laugh and flash and sparkle as she have never yet do within Mr. Smithson 's experience . he contemplate the transformation with wonder not unmixe with suspicion . never for he have she be so brilliant never in response to his glance have her violet eye thus kindle , have her smile be so entrancingly sweet . he watch Montesma , but in he he could find no fault . even jealousy could hardly take objection to the Spaniard 's manner to Lady Lesbia . there be not a look , not a word that hint at a private understanding between they , or which seem to convey deep meaning than the common language of society . no , there be no ground for fault - finding ; and yet Smithson be miserable . he know this man of old , and know his influence over woman . 

 Mr. Smithson hand over the management of the yacht without a murmer , albeit he pretend to be able to sail she himself , and be in the habit of take the command for a couple of hour on a sunny afternoon , much to the amusement of skipper and crew . but Montesma be a sailor bear and breed the salt keen breath of the sea have be the first breath in his nostril he have manage his light felucca before he be twelve year old , have sail every inch of the Caribbean Sea , and northward to the furthermost of the Bahamas before he be fifteen . he have live more on the water than on the land in that wild boyhood of his ; a boyhood in which book and professor have play but small part . Montesma 's school have be the world , and beautiful woman his only professor . he have learn arithmetic from the transaction of bubble company ; modern language from the lip of the woman who love he . he be a crack shot , a perfect swordsman , a reckless horseman , and a dancer in whom dance almost rise to genius . beyond these limit he be as ignorant as dirt ; but he have a cleverness which serve as a substitute for book learning , and he seldom fail in impress the people he meet with the idea that he , Gomez de Montesma , be no ordinary man . 

 directly after dinner the preparation for an immediate start begin ; very much to the disgust of skipper and crew , who be not in the habit of work after dinner ; but Montesma care nothing for the short answer of the captain , or the black look of the man . 

 Lesbia want to learn all about everything the name of every sail , of every rope . she stand near the helmsman , a slim graceful figure in a white gown of some soft material , with never a jewel or a flower to relieve that statuesque simplicity . she wear no hat , and the rich chesnut hair be roll in a loose knot at the back of the small Greek - look head . Montesma come to she every now and then to explain what be be do ; and by - and - by , when the canvas be all up , and the yacht be skim over the water , like a giant swan bear by the current of some vast strong river , he come and stay by her side , and they two sit make little baby sentence in Spanish , he as teacher and she as pupil , with no one near they but the sailor . 

 the owner of the Cayman have disappear mysteriously a quarter of an hour after the sail be unfurl , and Lady Kirkbank have totter down to the saloon . 

 ' I be not go cabin , ' she falter , when Lesbia remonstrate with she , ' only go saloon sofa lie down little Smithson take care you , ' not perceive that Smithson have vanish , ' shall be quite close . ' 

 so Lesbia and Don Gomez be alone under the summer star , murmur little bit of Spanish . 

 ' it be the only true way of learn a language , ' he say ; ' grammar be a delusion . ' 

 it be a very delightful and easy way of learn , at any rate . Lesbia recline in her bamboo chair , and fan herself indolently , and watch the shadowy shore of the island , cliff and hill , down and woode crest , flit past she like dream - picture , and her lip slowly shape the word of that soft lisping language so simple , so musical a language make for lover and for song , one would think . it be wonderful what rapid progress Lesbia make . 

 she hear a church clock on the island striking , and ask Don Gomez the hour . 

 ' ten , ' he say . 

 ' Ten ! surely it must be later . it be past eight before we begin dinner , and we have be sail for ever so long . captain , kindly tell I the time , ' she call to the skipper , who be loll over the gunwale near the foremast smoking a meditative pipe . 

 ' twelve o'clock , my lady . ' 

 ' Heavens , can I possibly have be sit here so long . I should like to stay on deck all night and watch the sailing ; but I must really go and take care of poor Lady Kirkbank . I be afraid she be not very well . ' 

 ' she have a somewhat distracted air when she go below , but I daresay she will sleep off her trouble . if I be you I should leave she to herself . ' 

 ' impossible ! what can have become of Mr. Smithson ? ' 

 ' I have a shrewd suspicion that it be with Smithson as with poor Lady Kirkbank . ' 

 ' do you mean that he be ill ? ' 

 ' precisely . ' 

 ' what , on a calm summer night , sail over a sea of glass . the owner of a yacht ! ' 

 ' rather ignominious for poor Smithson , be not   it ? but man who own yacht be only mortal , and be sometimes wretched sailor . Smithson be feeble on that point , as I know of old . ' 

 ' then be not   it rather cruel of we to sail his yacht ? ' 

 ' yacht be mean for sailing , and again , sea - sickness be suppose to be a wholesome exercise . ' 

 ' good - night . ' 

 ' good - night , ' both good night in Spanish , and with a touch of tenderness which the word could hardly have express in English . 

 ' must you really go ? ' plead Montesma , hold her hand just a thought long than he have ever hold it before . 

 ' ah , the little more , and how much it be , ' say the poet . 

 ' really and truly . ' 

 ' I be so sorry . I wish you could have stay on deck all night . ' 

 ' so do I , with all my heart . this calm sea under the starlit sky be like a dream of heaven . ' 

 ' it be very nice , but if you stay I think I could promise you considerable variety . we shall have a temp before morning . ' 

 ' of all thing in the world I should love to see a thunderstorm at sea . ' 

 ' be on the alert then , and Captain Parkes and I will try to oblige you . ' 

 ' at any rate you have make it impossible for I to sleep . I shall stay with Lady Kirkbank in the saloon . good - night , again . ' 

 ' good - night . ' 

 CHAPTER XXXIX . 

 in storm and DARKNESS . 


 Lesbia find Lady Kirkbank prostrate on a low divan in the saloon , sleepless , and very cross . the atmosphere reek with red lavender , sal - volatile , eau de Cologne , and brandy , which latter remedy poor Georgie have take freely in her agony . Kibble , the faithful Grasmere girl , sit by the divan , fan the sufferer with a large japanese fan . Rilboche have naturally , as a Frenchwoman , succumb utterly to her own feeling , and be moan in her berth , wail out every now and then that she would never have take service with Miladi have she suspect she to be capable of such cruelty as to take she to live for week upon the sea . 

 if this be the state of affair now while the ocean be only gently stir , what would it be by - and - by if the temp should really come ? 

 ' what can you be think of , stay on deck all night with those man ? ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , peevishly . ' it be hardly respectable . ' 

 she would have be still more inclined to object have she know that Lesbia 's companion have be ' that man ' rather than ' those man . ' 

 ' what do you mean by all night ? ' Lesbia retort , contemptuously ; ' it be only just twelve . ' 

 ' only twelve . I think we be close upon daylight . I have suffer an eternity of agony . ' 

 ' I be very sorry you should be ill ; but really the sea have be so deliciously calm . ' 

 ' I believe I should have suffer less if it have be diabolically rough . oh , that monotonous flip - flap of the water , that slow heaving of the boat ! nothing could be bad . ' 

 ' I be glad to hear you say that , for Don Gomez say we be likely to have a temp . ' 

 ' a tempest ! ' shriek Georgie . ' then let he stop the boat this instant and put I on shore . tell he to land I anywhere on the Needles even . I could stop at the lighthouse till morning . a storm at sea will be simply my death . ' 

 ' Dear Lady Kirkbank , I be only joke , ' say Lesbia , who do not want to be worry by her chaperon 's nervous apprehension : ' so far the night be lovely . ' 

 ' give I a spoonful more brandy , my good creature , ' to Kibble . ' Lesbia , you ought never to have bring I into this miserable state . I consent to stay on board the yacht ; but I never consent to sail on she . ' 

 ' you will soon be well , dear Lady Kirkbank ; and you will have such an appetite for breakfast to - morrow morning . ' 

 ' where shall we be at breakfast time ? ' 

 ' Off St. Catherine 's point , I believe just half way round the island . ' 

 ' if we be not at the bottom of the sea , ' groan Georgie . 

 they be now in the open Channel , and the boat dip and rise to large billow than have encounter her course before . Lady Kirkbank lie in a state of collapse , in which life seem only sustainable by occasional teaspoonful of cognac gently tilt down her throat by the patient Kibble . 

 Lesbia go to her cabin , but with no intention of remain there . she be firmly convinced that the storm would come , and she mean to be on deck while it be rag . what harm could thunder or lightning , hail or rain , do to she while he be by to protect she ? he would be busy sail the boat , perhaps , but still he would have a moment now and then in which to think of she and care for she . 

 yes , the storm be come . there be a livid look upon the water , and the atmosphere be heavy with heat ; the sky to windward black as a funeral pall . Lesbia be almost fearless , yet she feel a thrill of awe as she look into that dense blackness . to leeward the star be still visible ; but that gigantic mass of cloud come creep slowly , solemnly over the sky , while the shadow flit fast across the water , swallow up that ghastly electric glare . 

 Lesbia wrap herself in a white cashmere sortie de bal and steal up the companion . Montesma be work at the rope with his own hand , call direction to the sailor to shorten and take in the canvas , urge they to increase effort by work at the rope with his own hand , spring up the rigging and on deck , flash backwards and forwards amidst the rigging like a being of supernatural power . he have take off his jacket , and be clothe from top to toe in white , save for that streak of scarlet which tightly girdle his waist . his tall flexible form , perfect in line as a greek statue of Hermes , stand out against the background of black night . his voice , with its tone of brief imperious command , the proud carriage of his head , the easy grace of his rapid movement , all proclaim the man bear to rule over his fellow - man . and it be these master spirit , these bear ruler , whom woman instinctively recognise as their sovereign lord , and for whom woman count no sacrifice too costly . 

 in the midst of his activity Montesma suddenly see that white - rob figure stand at the top of the companion , and fly to her side . the boat be pitch heavily , dip into the trough of the sea at an angle of forty - five degree , as it seem to Lesbia . 

 ' you ought not to be here , ' say Montesma ; ' it be much rough than I expect . ' 

 ' I be not afraid , ' she answer ; ' but I will go back to my cabin if I be in your way . ' 

 ' in my way '   with deep tenderness : ' yes , you be in my way , for I shall think of nothing else now you be here . but I believe we have do all that need be do to the yacht , and I can take care of you till the storm be over . ' 

 he put his arm round she as the stem dip , and lead she towards the stern , guide her footstep , support she as her light figure sway against he with the motion of the boat . a vivid flash of lightning show he her face as they stand for an instant lean against each other , his arm encircle she . ah , what deep feeling in that countenance , once so passionless ; what a new light in those eye . it be like the awakening of a long dormant soul . 

 he take the helm from the captain and stand steer the vessel , and call out his order , with Lesbia close beside he , hold she with his disengage arm , draw she near he as the vessel pitch violently , draw her nearer still when they ship a sea , and a great fountain of spray enfold they both in a dense cloud of salt water . 

 the thunder roar and rattle , as if it begin and end close beside they . fork lightning zigzag amidst the rigging . Sheet lightning enwrap those two in a luminous atmosphere , reveal face that be pale with passion , lip that tremble with emotion . there be but scant opportunity for speech , and neither of these two feel the need of word . to be together , bind near to each other than they have ever be yet , than they might ever be again , in the midst of thunder and lightning and dense cloud of spray . this be enough . once when the Cayman pitch with exceptional fury , when the thunder crash and roar loudest , Lesbia find her head lie on Montesma 's breast and his arm round she , his lip upon her face . she do not wrench herself from that forbid embrace . she let those lip kiss hers as never mortal man have kiss she before . but an instant later , when Montesma 's attention be distract by his duty as steersman , and he let she go , she slip away in the darkness , and melt from his sight and touch like a modern Undine . he dare not leave the helm and follow she then . he send one of the sailor below a little later , to make sure that she be safe in her cabin ; but he see she no more that night . 

 the storm abate soon after daybreak , and the morning be lovely ; but Don Gomez and Lady Lesbia do not meet again till the church bell on the island be ring for morning service , and then the lady be safe under the wing of her chaperon , with her affiance husband in attendance upon she at the breakfast table in the saloon . 

 she receive Montesma with the faint inclination of the head , and she carefully avoid all occasion of speech with he during the leisurely , long spin - out meal . she be as white as her muslin gown , and her eye tell of a sleepless night . she talk a little , very little to Lady Kirkbank and Mr. Smithson ; to the Spaniard not at all . and yet Montesma be in no manner dash by this appearance of deep offence . so might Francesca have look the morning after that little scene over the book ; yet she sacrifice her salvation for her lover all the same . it be a familiar stage upon the journey which Montesma know by heart . here the inclination of the road be so many degree more or less ; for this hill you be command to put on an extra horse ; at this stage it be forbid to go more than eight mile an hour , and so on , and so on . Montesma know every inch of the ground . he put on a melancholy look , and talk very little . he have be on deck all night , and so there be an excuse for his be quiet . 

 Lady Kirkbank relate her impression of the storm , and talk enough for four . she have suffer the pang of purgatory , but her natural cheeriness assert itself , and she make no moaning about past agony which have exercise a really delightful influence on her appetite . Mr. Smithson also be cheerful . he have pay his annual tribute to Neptune , and might hope to go scot - free for the rest of the season . 

 ' if I have stay on deck I must have have my finger in the pie ; so I think it well to go below and get a good night 's rest in the steward 's cabin , ' he say , not care to confess his suffering as frankly as Lady Kirkbank admit her . 

 after breakfast , which be prolong till noon , Montesma ask Smithson to smoke a cigarette on deck with he . 

 ' I want to talk to you on a rather serious matter , ' he say . 

 Lesbia hear the word , and look up with a frightened glance . could he mean to attempt anything desperate ? be he go to confess the fatal truth to Horace Smithson , to tell she affiance lover that she be untrue to her bond , that she love he , Montesma , as fondly as he love she , that their two soul have mingle like two flame fan by the same current , and thence have rise to a conflagration which must end in ruin , if she be not set free to follow where her heart have go , free to belong to that man whom her spirit choose for lord and master . her heart leap at the hope that Montesma be go to do this , that he be strong enough to break her bond for she , powerful and rich enough to secure she a brilliant future . yet this last consideration , which hitherto have be paramount , seem now of but little moment . to be with he , to belong to he , would be enough for bliss . albeit that in such a choice she forfeit all that she have ever possess or hop for of earthly prosperity . adventurer , beggar , whatever he might be , she choose he , and love he with all the strength of a weak soul newly awaken to passionate feeling . 

 unhappily for Lesbia Haselden , Montesma be not at all the kind of man to take so direct and open a course as that which she imagine possible . 

 his business with Mr. Smithson be of quite a different kind . 

 ' Smithson , do you know that you have an utterly incompetent crew ? ' he say , gravely , when they two be stand aft , light their cigarette . 

 ' indeed I do not . the man be all experienced sailor , and the captain rank high among yachtsman . ' 

 ' english yachtsman be not particularly good judge of sailor . I tell you your skipper be no sailor , and his man be fool . if it have not be for I the Cayman would have go to piece on the rock last night , and if you be to cross to St. Malo , as you talk of do , for the regatta there , you have well sack these man and let I get you a south american crew . I know of a fellow who be in London just now the captain of a Rio steamer , who will send you a crew of pick man , if you give I authority to telegraph to he . ' 

 ' I do not   like foreign sailor , ' say Smithson , look perplexed and worried ; ' and I have perfect confidence in Wilkinson . ' 

 ' which be as much as to say that you consider I a liar ! go to the bottom your own way , mon ami : ce n'est pas mon affaire , ' say Montesma , turn on his heel , and leave his friend to his own device . 

 have he press the point , Smithson would have suspect he of some evil motive , and would have be resolute in his resistance ; but as he say no more about it , Smithson begin to feel uncomfortable . 

 he be no sailor himself , know absolutely nothing about the navigation of his yacht , though he sometimes pretend to sail she ; and he have no power to judge of his skipper 's capacity or his man 's seamanship . he have engage the captain wholly on the strength of the man 's reputation , guarantee by certain certificate which seem to mean a great deal . but after all such certificate might mean very little such a reputation might be no real guarantee . the sailor have be engage by the captain , and their ruddy face and thoroughly british appearence , the exquisite cleanliness which they maintain in every detail of the yacht , have seem to Mr. Smithson the perfection of seamanship . 

 but it be not the less true that the clean of yacht , with deck of spotless whiteness , sail of unsullied purity , brass shin and sparkling like gold fresh from the goldsmith 's , might be spike upon a rock , or might founder on a sand - bank , or heel over under too much canvas . Mr. Smithson be inclined to suspect any proposition of Montesma 's ; yet he be not the less disturbed in mind by the assertion . 

 the day wear on , and the yacht sail merrily over a summer sea . Mr. Smithson fidget about the deck uneasily , watch every movement of the sailor . no boat could be sail well , as it seem to he ; but in such weather and over such water any boat must need go easily . it be in the blackness of night , amidst the fury of the storm , that Montesma 's opinion have be form . Smithson begin to think that his friend be right . the sailor have honest countenance , but they look horribly stupid . could man with such vacuous grin , such an air of imbecile good - nature , be capable of act wisely in any terrible crisis ? could they have nerve and readiness , quickness , decision , all those grand qualite which be need by the seaman who have to contend with the fury of the element ? 

 Mr. Smithson and his guest have breakfast too late for the possibility of luncheon . they be in Cowes Roads by one o'clock . a fleet of yacht have arrive during their absence , and the scene be full of life and gaiety . Lady Lesbia hold a levťe at the afternoon tea , and have a crowd of her old admirer around her adorer whose presence in no wise disturb Horace Smithson 's peace . he would have be content that his wife should go through life with a herd of such worshipper follow in her footstep . he know the aimless innocence , the almost infantine simplicity of the typical Johnnie , Chappie , Muscadin , Petit Creve , Gommeux call he by what name you will . from these he fear no evil . but in that one follower who give no outward token of his worship he dread peril . it be Montesma he watch , while dragoon with close - cropped hair , and imbecile youth with head rigid in four - inch collar , be hang about Lady Lesbia 's low bamboo chair , and administer obsequiously to the small necessity of the tea - table . 

 it be while this tea - table business be go on that Mr. Smithson take the opportunity of set his mind at rest , be it possible , as to the merit of Captain Wilkinson . among his visitor this afternoon there be the owner of three or four racing yacht a man renowned for his victory , at home and abroad . 

 ' I think you know something of my captain , Wilkinson , before I engage he , ' say Smithson , with assumed carelessness . 

 ' I know every skipper on board every boat in the squadron , ' answer his friend . ' a good fellow , Wilkinson thoroughly honest fellow . ' 

 ' honest ; oh yes , I know all about that . but how about his seamanship ? his certificate be wonderfully good , but they be not everything . 

 ' everything , my dear fellow , ' cry the other ; ' they be next to nothing . but I believe Wilkinson be a tolerable sailor . ' 

 this be not encouraging . 

 ' he have never be unlucky , I believe . ' 

 ' my dear Smithson , you be a great authority in the City , but you be not very well up in the record of the yacht world , or you would know that your Captain Wilkinson be skipper on the Orinoco when she run aground on the Chesil Bank , come home from Cherbourg Regatta , fifteen life lose , and the yacht , in less than half an hour , ground to powder . that be rather a bad case , I remember ; for though it be a tempestuous night , the accident would never have happen if Wilkinson have not mistake the light . so you see his Trinity House paper do not   prevent his go wrong . '
