 good heaven ! this be the strong confirmation of Montesma 's charge . the man be a stupid man , an incapable man , a man to whose intelligence and care human life should never be trust . a fig for his honesty ! what would honesty be worth in a hurricane off the Chesil Beach ? what would honesty serve a ship spit on the Jailors off Jersey ? Montesma be right . if the Cayman be to make a trip to St. Malo she must be navigate by competent man . Horace Smithson hat foreign sailor , copper - face ruffian , with flash black eye which seem to threaten murder , do you but say a rough word to they ; sleek , raven - haired scoundrel , with bowie - knife in their girdle , ready for mutiny . but , after all , life be worth too much to be risk for a prejudice , a sentiment . 

 perhaps that St. Malo business might be avoid ; and then there need be no change in captain or crew . the yacht must be safe enough lie at anchor in the roadstead . by - and - by , when the visitor have depart , and Mr. Smithson be reposefully enjoy his tea by Lady Lesbia 's side , he approach the subject . 

 ' do you really care about cross to St. Malo after this really prefer the idea to Ryde ? ' 

 ' Infinitely , ' exclaim Lesbia , quickly . ' Ryde would only be Cowes ever again a less Cowes ; and I think when you first propose it that the plan be rather stupid , though I do not want to be uncivil and say so . but I be delighted with Don Gomez de Montesma 's amendment , substitute St. Malo for Ryde . in the first place the trip across will be delicious ' Lady Kirkbank give a faint groan ' and in the second place I be die to see Brittany . ' 

 ' I doubt if you will highly appreciate St. Malo . it be a town of many and various smell . ' 

 ' but I want to smell those foreign smell of which one hear much . at least it be an experience . we need not be on shore any long than we like . and I want to see that fine rocky coast , and Chateaubriand 's tomb on the what's - its - name . so nice to be bury in that way . ' 

 ' then you have set your heart on go to St. Malo , and would not like any change in our plan ? ' 

 ' any change will be simply detestable , ' answer Lesbia , all the more decidedly since she suspect a desire for change on the part of Mr. Smithson . 

 she be in no amiable humour this afternoon . all her nerve seem strained to their utmost tension . she be irritated , tremulous with nervous excitement , incline to hate everybody , Horace Smithson most of all . in her cabin a little later on , when she be change her gown for dinner , and Kibble be somewhat slow and clumsy in the lacing of the bodice , she wrench herself from the girl 's hand , fling herself into a chair , and burst into a flood of passionate tear . 

 ' o God ! that I be on one of those island in the Caribbean Sea an island where Europeans never come where I might lie down among the poisonous tropical flower , and sleep the rest of my day away . I be sick to death of my life here ; of the yacht , the people everything . ' 

 ' this air be too relaxing , Lady Lesbia , ' the girl murmur , soothingly ; ' and you do not   have your natural rest last night . shall I get you a nice strong cup of tea ? ' 

 ' Tea ! no . I have be live upon tea for the last twenty - four hour . I have eat nothing . my mouth be parch and burn . oh , Kibble ! ' fling her head upon the girl 's buxom arm , and let it rest there , ' what a happy creature you be not a care not a care . ' 

 ' I be sure you can not   have any care , Lady Lesbia , ' say Kibble , with an incredulous smile , try to smooth the disorder hair , anxious to make haste with the unfinished toilet , for it be within a few minute of eight . 

 ' I be full of care . I be in debt horribly in debt get deep and deep every day and I be go to sell myself to the only man who can pay my debt and give I fine house , and finery like this , ' pluck at the crepe de chine gown , with its flossy fringe , its delicate lace , a marvel of artistic expenditure ; a garment which look simplicity itself , and yet be so cleverly contrive as to cost five - and - thirty guineas . the great effect in it require to be study with a microscope . 

 ' but surely , dear Lady Lesbia , you will not   marry Mr. Smithson , if you do not   love he ? ' 

 ' do you suppose love have anything to do with marriage in society ? ' 

 ' oh , Lady Lesbia , it would be so unkind to he , so cruel to yourself . ' 

 ' cruel to myself . yes , I be cruel to myself . I have the chance of happiness a year ago , and I lose it . I have the chance of happiness now yes , of consummate bliss and have not   the courage to snatch at it . take off this horrid gown , Kibble ; my head be split : I shall not   go to dinner . ' 

 ' oh , Lady Lesbia , you be treading on the pearl embroidery , ' remonstrate poor Kibble , as Lesbia kick the new gown from under her foot . 

 ' what do it matter ! ' she exclaim with a bitter little laugh . ' it have not be pay for perhaps it never will be . ' 

 the dinner be silent and gloomy . it be as if a star have be suddenly blot out of the sky . Smithson , ordinarily so hospitable , have be too much disturb in mind to ask any of his friend to stay to dinner ; so there be only Lady Kirkbank , who be too tired to be lively , and Montesma , who be inclined to be thoughtful . Lesbia 's absence , and the idea that she be ill , give the feast almost a funereal air . 

 after dinner Smithson and Montesma sit on deck , smoke their cigar , and lazily watch the light on sea , and the light on shore ; these brilliant in the foreground , those dim in the distance . 

 ' you can telegraph to your Rio Janeiro friend to - morrow morning , if you like , ' say Smithson , presently , ' and tell he to send a first - rate skipper and crew . Lady Lesbia have make up her mind to see St. Malo Regatta , and with such a sacred charge I can not   be too careful . ' 

 ' I will wire before eight o'clock to - morrow , ' answer Montesma , ' you have decide wisely . your respectable English Wilkinson be an excellent man but nothing would surprise I less than his reduce your Cayman to matchwood in the next gale . ' 

 CHAPTER XL . 

 a note of ALARM . 


 that strange scene in the old house at Fellside make a profound impression upon Lord Hartfield . he try to disguise his trouble , and do all in his power to seem gay and at perfect ease in his wife 's company ; but his mind be full of anxiety , and Mary love he too well to be for a moment in doubt as to his feeling . 

 ' there be something wrong , Jack , ' she say , while they be breakfast at a table in the verandah , with the lake and the bill in front of they and the sweet morning air around they . ' you try to talk and to be lively , but there be a little perpendicular wrinkle in your forehead which I know as well as the letter of the alphabet , and that little line mean worry . I use to see it in the old day , when you be break your heart for Lesbia . why can not you be frank and confide in I . it be your duty , sir , as my husband . ' 

 ' be it my duty to halve my burden as well as my joy ? how do I know if those girlish shoulder be strong enough to bear the weight of they ? ' 

 ' I can bear anything you can bear , and I will not   be cheat out of my share in your worry . if you be oblige to have a tooth out , I would have one out too , for company . ' 

 ' I hope the dentist would be too conscientious to allow that . ' 

 ' tell I your trouble , Hartfield , ' she say , earnestly , lean across the table , bring her grave intelligent face near to he . 

 they be quite alone , he and she . the servant have do their ministering . behind they there be the empty dining - room , in front of they the sunlit panorama of lake and hill . there could not be a safe place for tell secret . 

 ' tell I what it be that worry you , ' Mary plead again . 

 ' I will , dear . after all perfect trust be the good ; nay , it be your due , for you be brave enough and true enough to be trust with secret that mean life and death . in a word , then , Mary , the cause of my trouble be that old man we see the other night . ' 

 ' Steadman 's uncle ? ' 

 ' do you really believe that he be Steadman 's uncle ? ' 

 ' my grandmother tell I so , ' answer Mary , redden to the root of her hair . 

 to this girl , who be the soul of truth , there be deep shame in the idea that her kinswoman , the woman whom of all the world she most owe reverence and honour , could be deem capable of falsehood . 

 ' do you think my grandmother would tell I an untruth ? ' 

 ' I do not believe that man be a poor dependent , an old servant 's kinsman , shelter and care for in this house for charity 's sake . forgive I , Mary , if I doubt the word of one you love ; but there be position in life in which a man must judge for himself . would Mr. Steadman 's kinsman be lodge as that old man be lodge ; would he talk as that old man talk ; and last and great perplexity of all , would he possess a treasure of gold and jewel which must be worth many thousand ? ' 

 ' but you can not know for certain that those thing be valuable ; they may be rubbish that this poor old man have scrap together and hoard for year , glass jewel buy at country fair . those rouleaux may contain lead or copper . ' 

 ' I do not think so , Mary . the stone have all the brilliancy of valuable gem , and then there be other in the fine filagree setting goldsmith 's work which bear the stamp of an eastern world . take my word for it , that treasure come from India ; and it must have be bring to England by Lord Maulevrier . it may have exist all these year without your grandmother 's knowledge . that be quite possible ; but it seem to I impossible that such wealth should be within the knowledge and the power of a pauper lunatic . ' 

 ' but if that unhappy old man be not a relation of Steadman 's support here by my grandmother 's benevolence , who can he be , and why be he here ? ' ask Mary . 

 ' oh , Molly dear , these be two question which I can not answer , and which yet ought to be answer somehow . since that night I have feel as if there be a dark cloud lower over this house a cloud almost as terrible in its menace of danger as the forshadowing of fate in a greek legend . for your sake , for the honour of your race , for my own self - respect as your husband , I feel that this mystery ought to be solve , and all dark thing make light before your grandmother 's death . when she be go the master - key to the past will be lose . ' 

 ' but she will be spar for many year , I hope , spar to sympathise with my happiness , and with Lesbia 's . ' 

 my dear girl , we can not hope that . the thread of her life be wear very thin . it may snap at any moment . you can not look seriously in your grandmother 's face , and yet delude yourself with the hope that she have year of life before she . ' 

 ' it will be very hard to part , just as she have begin to care for I , ' say Mary , with her eye full of tear . 

 ' all such parting be hard , and your grandmother 's life have be so lonely and joyless that the memory of it must always have a touch of pain . one can not say of she as we can of the happy ; she have live her life all thing have be give to she , and she fall asleep at the close of a long and glorious day . for some reason which I can not understand , Lady Maulevrier 's life have be a prolonged sacrifice . ' 

 ' she have always give we to understand that she be fond of Fellside , and that this seclude life suit she , ' say Mary , meditatively . 

 ' I can not help doubt her sincerity on that point . Lady Maulevrier be too clever a woman , and forgive I , dear , if I add too worldly a woman , to be content to live out of the world . the bird must have chafe its breast against the bar of the cage many and many a time when you think that all be peace . be sure , Mary , that your grandmother have a powerful motive for spend all her day in this place , and I can but think that the old man we see the other night have some part in that motive . do you remember tell I of her ladyship 's vehement anger when she hear you have make the acquaintance of her pensioner ? ' 

 ' yes , she be very angry , ' Mary answer , with a troubled look . ' I never see she so angry she be almost beside herself say the harsh thing to I talk as if I have do some dreadful mischief . ' 

 ' would she have be so moved , do you think , unless there be some fatal secret involve in that man 's presence here ? ' 

 ' I hardly know what to think . tell I everything . what be it that you fear ? what be it that you suspect ? ' 

 ' to tell you my fear and suspicion be to tell you a family secret that have be keep from you out of kindness all the year of your life and I hardly think I could bring myself to that if I do not know what the world be , and how many good - natured friend Lady Hartfield will meet in society , by - and - by , ready to tell she , by hint and innuendo , that her grandfather , the Governor of Madras , come back to England under a cloud of disgrace . ' 

 ' my poor grandfather ! how dreadful ! ' exclaim Mary , pale with pity and shame . ' do he deserve his disgrace , poor unhappy creature or be he the victim of false accusation ? ' 

 ' I can hardly tell you that , Mary , any more than I can tell whether Warren Hastings deserve the abuse that be wreak upon he at one time , or the acquittal that give the lie to his slanderer in after year . the event occur forty year ago the story be only half know then , and like all such story form the basis for every kind of exaggeration and perversion . ' 

 ' do Maulevrier know ? ' falter Mary . 

 ' Maulevrier know all that be know by the general public , and no more . ' 

 ' and you have marry the granddaughter of a disgrace man , ' say Mary , with a piteous look . ' do you know when you marry I ? ' 

 ' as much as I know now , dear love . if you have be Jonathan Wild 's granddaughter you would have be just as dear to I . I marry you , dear ; I love you ; I believe in you . all the grandfather in Christendom would not shake my faith by one tittle . ' 

 she throw herself into his arm , and sob upon his breast . but sweet as this assurance of his love be to she , she be not the less stricken by shame at the thought of possible infamy in the past , a shameful memory for ever brood over her name in the present . 

 ' society never forget a scandal , ' she say ; ' I have hear Maulevrier say that . ' 

 ' Society have a long memory for other people 's sin , but it only avenge its own wrong . give the wicked fairy Society a bad dinner , or leave she out of your invitation list for a ball , and she will twit you with the crime or the misfortune of a remote ancestor she will go about talk of your grandfather the leper , or your great aunt who run away with her footman . but so long as the wicked fairy get all she want out of you , she care not a straw for the misdeed of past generation . ' 

 he speak lightly , laughingly almost , and then he order the dogcart to be bring round immediately , and he drive Mary across the hill towards Langdale , to bring the colour back to her blanched cheek . he bring she home in time to give her grandmother an hour for letter - writing before luncheon , while he walk up and down the terrace below Lady Maulevrier 's window , meditate the course he be to take . 

 he be to leave Westmoreland next day to take his place in the House of Lords during the last important debate of the session . he make up his mind that before he leave he would seek an interview with Lady Maulevrier , and boldly ask she to explain the mystery of that old man 's presence at Fellside . he be her kinsman by marriage , and he have swear to honour she and to care for she as a son ; and as a son he would urge she to confide in he , to unburden her conscience of any dark secret , and to make the crooked thing straight , before she be call away . 

 while he be forecast this interview , meet imaginary objection , argue point which might have to be argue , a servant come out to he with an ochre envelope on a little silver tray that unpleasant - look envelope which seem always a presage of trouble , great or small . 

 ' Lord Maulevrier , Albany , to Lord Hartfield , Fellside , Grasmere . 

 ' for God 's sake come to I at once . I be in great trouble ; not on my own account , but about a relation . ' 

 a relation except his grandmother and his two sister Maulevrier have no relation for whom he care a straw . this message must have relation to Lesbia . be she ill dying , the victim of some fatal accident , runaway horse , boat upset , train smash ? there be something ; and Maulevrier appeal to his near and good friend . there be no withstanding such an appeal . it must be answer , and immediately . 

 Lord Hartfield go into the library and write his reply message , which consist of six word . 

 ' go to you by first train . ' 

 the next train leave windermere at three . there be just time to get a fresh horse put in the dogcart , and a Gladstone bag pack . 

 CHAPTER XLI . 

 PRIVILEGED information . 


 Lord Hartfield do not arrive at Euston Square until near eleven o'clock at night . a hansom deposit he at the entrance to the Albany just as the clock of St. James 's Church chime the hour . he find only Maulevrier 's valet . his lordship have wait indoor all the evening , and have only go out a quarter of an hour ago . he have go to the Cerberus , and beg that Lord Hartfield would be kind enough to follow he there . 

 Lord Hartfield be not fond of the Cerberus , and indeed deem that lively place of rendezvous a very dangerous sphere for his friend Maulevrier ; but in the face of Maulevrier 's telegram there be no time to be lose , so he walk across Piccadilly and down St. James 's Street to the fashionable little club , where the man be drop in after the theatre and dinner , and where sheafs of bank note be be exchange for those various coloured counter which represent diver value , from the respectable ' pony ' to the modest ' chip . ' 

 Maulevrier be in the first room Hartfield look into , stand behind some man who be play . 

 ' that be something like friendship , ' he exclaim , when he see Lord Hartfield , and then he hook his arm through his friend 's , and lead he off to the dining room . 

 ' come and have some supper , old fellow , ' he say , ' and I can tell you my trouble while you be eat it . James , bring we a grill , and a lobster , and a bottle of Mumms , number 27 , you know . ' 

 ' yes , my lord . ' 

 ' sorry to find you in this den , Maulevrier , ' say Lord Hartfield . 

 ' have not   touch a card . have not   do half an hour 's punt this season . but it be a kind of habit with I to wander in here now and then . I know so many of the member . one poor devil lose nine thousand one night last week . rather rough upon he , be not   it ? all ready money at this shop , do not   you know . ' 

 ' thank God , I know nothing about it . and now , Maulevrier , what be wrong , and with whom ? ' 

 ' everything be wrong , and with my sister Lesbia . ' 

 ' good heaven ! what do you mean ? ' 

 ' only this , that there be a fellow after she whose very name mean ruin to woman a spanish - american adventurer reckless , handsome , a gambler , seducer , duellist , dare - devil . the man she be to marry seem to have neither nous nor spunk to defend she . everybody at Goodwood see the game that be be play , everybody at Cowes be watch the card , bet on the result . yes , great God , the man at the Squadron Club be stake their money upon my sister 's character even monkey that she bolt with Montesma five to three against the marriage with Smithson ever come off . ' 

 ' be this true ? ' 

 ' it be as true as your marriage with Molly , as true as your loyalty to I . I be tell of it all this morning at the Haute Gomme by a man I can rely upon , a really good fellow , who would not leave I in the dark about my sister 's danger when all the smoking - room in Pall Mall be snigger about it . my first impulse be to take the train for Cowes ; but then I know if I go alone I should let my temper get the well of I . I should knock somebody down throw somebody out of the window make a devil of a scene . and this would be fatal for Lesbia . I want your counsel , your cool head , your steady common - sense . " not a step forward without Jack , " I say to myself , so I bolt off and send that telegram . it relieve my feeling a little , but I have   have a wretched day . ' 

 ' waiter , bring I a Bradshaw , or an A b c , ' say Lord Hartfield . 

 he have eat nothing but a biscuit since breakfast , but he be ready to go off at once , supperless , if there be a train to carry he . unluckily there be no train . the mail have start . nothing till seven o'clock next morning . 

 ' eat your supper , old fellow , ' say Maulevrier . ' after all , the danger may not be so desperate as I fancy this morning . Slander be the favourite amusement of the age we live in . we must allow a margin for exaggeration . ' 

 ' a very liberal margin , ' answer Hartfield . ' no doubt the man who warn you mean honestly , but this scandal may have grow out of the mere trifle . the feebleness of the Masher 's brain be only exceed by the foulness of the Masher 's tongue . I daresay this rumour about Lady Lesbia have its beginning and end among the Masher specie . ' 

 ' I hope so , but I have see those two together I meet they at Victoria one evening after Goodwood . Old Kirkbank be shuffle on ahead , carry Smithson with she , absorb his attention by fussification about her carriage . Lesbia and that cuban devil be in the rear . they look as if they have all the world to themselves . Faust and Marguerite in the garden be not in it for the expression of intense absorbing feeling compare with those two . I be not an intellectual party , but I know something of human nature , and I know when a man and woman be in love with each other . it be one of the thing that never have be , that never can be hide . ' 

 ' and you say this Montesma be a dangerous man ? ' 

 ' deadly . ' 

 ' well , we must lose no time . when we be on the spot it will be easy to find out the truth ; and it will be your duty , if there be danger , to warn Lesbia and her future husband . 

 ' I would much rather shoot the Cuban , ' say Maulevrier . ' I never know much good come of a warning in such a case : it generally precipitate matter . if I could play ťcartť with he at the club , find he sport an extra king , throw my card in his face , and accept his challenge for an exchange of shot on the sand beyond Cherbourg there would be something like satisfaction . ' 

 ' you say the man be a gambler ? ' 

 ' report say something bad of he . report say he be a cheat . ' 

 ' we must not be dependent upon society gossip , ' reply Lord Hartfield . ' I have an idea , Maulevrier . the more we know about this man Montesma , I think you call he - - ' 

 ' Gomez de Montesma . ' 

 ' the more fully we be acquaint with Don Gomez de Montesma 's antecedent the well we shall be able to cope with he , if we come to handy - grip . it be too late to start for Cowes , but it be not too late to do something . Fitzpatrick , the political - economist , spend a quarter of a century in South America . he be a very old friend know my father and I can venture to knock at his door after midnight all the more as I know he be a night - worker . he be very likely to enlighten we about your cuban hidalgo . ' 

 ' you shall finish your supper before I let you stir . after that you may do what you like . I be always a child in your hand , Jack , whether it be climb a mountain or cross the horse - shoe Fall . I consider the business in your hand now . I will go with you wherever you like , and do what you tell I . when you want I to kick anybody , or fight anybody , you can give I the office and I will do it . I know that Lesbia 's interest be safe in your hand . you once care very much for she . you be her brother - in - law now , and , next to I , you be her natural protector , take into account that her future husband be a cad and do not   score . ' 

 ' meet I at Waterloo at ten minute to seven to - morrow morning , and we will go down to Cowes together . I be off to find Fitzpatrick . good night . ' 

 so they part . Lord Hartfield walk across the Park to Great George Street , where Mr. Fitzpatrick have chamber of a semi - official character , on the first floor of a solemn - looking old house , spacious , gloomy without and within , wall sombre with the subdue colouring of decoration half a century old . 

 the lighted window of those first - floor room tell Lord Hartfield that he be not too late . he ring the bell , which be answer with the brief delay by a sleepy - look clerk , who have be take shorthand note for Mr. Fitzpatrick 's great book upon ' Protection versus Free Trade . ' the clerk look sleepy , but his employer have as brisk an air as if he be just begin the day ; although he have be work without intermission since nine o'clock that evening , and have do a long day 's work before dinner . he be walk up and down the spacious unluxurious room , half office , half library , smoke a cigar . upon a large table in the centre of the room stand two powerful reading lamp with green shade , illuminate a chaotic mass of book and pamphlet , heap and scatter all over the table , save just on that spot between the two lamp , which accommodate Mr. Fitzpatrick 's blot pad and inkpot , a pewter inkpot which hold about a pint . 

 ' how d'ye do , Hartfield ? glad you have   look I up at last , ' say the Irishman , as if a midnight call be the most natural thing in the world . ' just come from the House ? ' 

 ' no ; I have   just come from Westmoreland . I think I should find you among those everlasting book of your , late as it be . can I have a few word alone with you ? ' 

 ' certainly . Morgan , you can go away for a bit . ' 

 ' home , sir ? ' 

 ' home well yes , I suppose it be late . you look sleepy . I should have be glad to finish the chapter on Beetroot Sugar to - night but it may stand over for the morning . be sure you be early . ' 

 ' yes , sir , ' the clerk respond with a faint sigh . 

 he be pay handsomely for late hour , liberally reward for his shorthand service ; and yet he wish the great Fitzpatrick have not be quite so industrious . 

 ' now , my dear Hartfield , what can I do for you ? ' ask Fitzpatrick , when the clerk have go . ' I can see by your face that you have   something serious in hand . can I help you ? ' 

 ' you can , I believe , in a very material way . you be five - and - twenty year in Spanish America ? ' 

 ' rather more than less . ' 

 ' here , there , and everywhere ? ' 

 ' yes ; there be not a city in South America that I have not live in for something between a day and a year . ' 

 ' you know something about most man of any mark in that part of the world , I conclude ? ' 

 ' it be my business to know man of all kind . I have my mission from the Spanish Government . I be engage to examine the condition of commerce throughout the colony , the working of protection as against free trade , and so on . strange , by - the - bye , that Cuba , the last place to foster the slave trade , be of all spot of the earth the first to carry free - trade principle into practical effect , long before they be recognise in any european country . ' 

 ' strange to I that you should speak of Cuba so soon after my come in , ' answer Lord Hartfield . ' I be here to ask you to help I to find out the antecedent of a man who hail from that island . ' 

 ' I ought to know something about he , whoever he be , ' reply Mr. Fitzpatrick , briskly . ' I spend six month in Cuba not very long before my return to England . Cuba be one of my fresh memory ; and I have a pretty tight memory for fact , name and figure . never could remember two line of poetry in my life . ' 

 ' do you ever hear of , or meet with , a man call Montesma Gomez de Montesma ? ' 

 ' could not   have stop a month in Havana without hear something about that gentleman , ' answer Fitzpatrick , ' I hope he be not   a friend of your , and that you have not lend he money ? ' 

 ' Neither ; but I want to know all you can tell I about he . ' 

 ' you shall have it in black and white , out of my cuban note - book , ' reply the other , unlock a drawer in the official table ; ' I always take note of anything worth recording , on the spot . a man be a fool who trust to memory , where personal character be at stake . Montesma be as well know at Havana as the Morro Fort or the Tacon Theatre . I have hear story enough about he to fill a big volume ; but all the fact record there ' strike the morocco cover of the note - book ' have be thoroughly sift ; I can vouch for they . ' 

 he look at the index , find the page , and hand the book to Lord Hartfield .

 Lord Hartfield read three or four page of plain statement as to various adventure by sea and land in which Gomez de Montesma have figure , and the reputation which he bear in Cuba and on the Main . 

 ' you can vouch for this ? ' he say at last , after a long silence . 

 ' for every syllable . ' 

 ' the story of his marriage ? ' 

 ' Gospel truth : I know the lady . ' 

 ' and the rest ? ' 

 ' all true . ' 

 ' a thousand thank . I know now upon what ground I stand . I have to save an innocent , high - breed girl from the clutch of a consummate scoundrel . ' 

 ' shoot he , and shoot she , too , if there be no well way of save she . it will be an act of mercy , ' say Mr. Fitzpatrick , without hesitation . 

 CHAPTER XLII . 

 ' shall it be ? ' 


 while Lord Hartfield sit in his friend 's office in Great George Street read the life story of Gomez de Montesma , tell with the cruel precision and the unvarnished language of a criminal indictment , the hero of that history be glide round the spacious ballroom of the Cowes Club , with Lady Lesbia Haselden 's dark - brown head almost recline on his shoulder , her violet eye look up at his every now and then , shyly , entrancingly , as he bend his head to talk to she . 

 the Squadron Ball be in full swing between midnight and the first hour of morning . the flower have not lose their freshness , the odour of dust and feverish human breath have not yet pollute the atmosphere . the window be open to the purple night , the purple sea . the star seem to be close outside the verandah , shin on purpose for the dancer ; and these two the man tall , pale , dark , with flash eye and short , sleek raven hair , small head , noble bearing ; the girl divinely lovely in her marble purity of complexion , her classical grace of form these two be , as every one avow and acknowledge , the handsome couple in the room . 

 ' we be none of we in it compare with they , ' say a young naval commander to his partner , whereupon the young lady look somewhat sourly , and reply that Lady Lesbia 's feature be undeniably regular and her complexion good , but that she be want in soul . 

 ' be she ? ' ask the sailor , incredulously , ' look at she now . what do you call that , if it be not   soul ? ' 

 ' I call it simply disgraceful , ' answer his partner , sharply turn away her head . 

 Lesbia be look up at the Spaniard , her lip faintly part , all her face listen eagerly as she catch some whisper word , breathe among the soft ripple of her hair , from lip that almost touch her brow . People can not go on waltzing for ten minute in a dead silence , like automatic dancer . there must be conversation . only it be well that the lip should do most of the talking . when the eye have so much to say society be apt to be censorious . 

 Mr. Smithson be smoke a cigarette on the lawn with a sporting peer . a man to whom tobacco be a necessity can not be always on guard ; but it be quite possible that in the present state of Lady Lesbia 's feeling Smithson would have have no restraining influence have he be ever so watchful . to what act in the passion drama have her love come to - night as she float round the room , with her head incline towards her lover 's breast , the strong pulsation of his heart sound in her ear , like the rhythmical beat of the bass yonder in Waldteufel 's last waltz ? be there still the uncertainty as to the dťnouement which mark the third act of a good play ? or be there the dread foreboding , the sense of impending doom which should stir the spectator with pity and terror as the fourth act hurry to its passionate close ? who could tell ? she have be full of life and energy to - day on board the yacht during the racing , in which she seem to take an ardent interest . the Cayman have follow the racer for three hour through a freshen sea , much to Lady Kirkbank 's disgust , and Lesbia have be the soul of the party . the same yesterday . the yacht have only get back to Cowes in time for the ball , and all have be hurry and excitement while the lady dress and cross to the club , the spray dash over their opera mantle , poor Lady Kirkbank 's complexion yellow with mal de mer , in spite of a double coating of Blanc de Fedora , the last fashionable cosmetic . 

 to - night Lesbia be curiously silent , depress even , as it seem to those who be interested in observe she ; and all the world be interested in a famous beauty . she be very pale , even her lip be colourless , and the large violet eye and firmly pencil brow alone give colour to her face . she look like a marble statue , the eye and eyebrow accentuate with touch of colour . those lovely eye have a heavy look , as of trouble , weariness ; nay , absolute distress . 

 never have she look less brilliant than to - night ; never have she look more beautiful . it be the loveliness of a newly - awaken soul . the wonderful Pandora - casket of life , with its infinite evil , its little good , have give up its secret . she know what passionate love really mean . she know what such love mostly mean self - sacrifice , surrender of the world 's wealth , severance from friend , the breaking of all old tie . to love as she love mean the crossing of a river more fatal than the Rubicon , the casting of a die more desperate than that which Casar fling upon the board when he take up arm against the Republic . 

 the river be not yet cross , but her foot be on the margin , wet with the ripple of the stream . the fatal die be not yet cast , but the dice - box be in her hand ready for the throw . Lesbia and Montesma dance together not too often , three waltz out of sixteen but when they be so waltz they be the cynosure of the room . that bet of which Maulevrier have hear be rife to - night , and the odd upon the Cuban have go up . it be nine to four now that those two would be over the border before the week be out . 

 Mr. Smithson be not neglectful of his affianced . he take she into the supper - room , where she drink some Moselle cup , but eat nothing . he sit out three or four waltz with she on the lawn , listen to the murmer of the sea , and talk very little . 

 ' you be look wretchedly ill to - night , Lesbia , ' he say , after a dismal silence . 

 ' I be sorry that I should put you to shame by my bad look , ' she answer , with that keen acidity of tone which indicate irritated nerve . 

 ' you know that I do not   mean anything of the kind ; you be always lovely , always the lovely everywhere ; but I do not   like to see you so ghastly pale . ' 

 ' I suppose I be over - fatigued : that I have do too much in London and here . life in Westmoreland be very different , ' she add , with a sigh , and a touch of wonder that the Lesbia Haselden , whose methodical life have never be stir by a ruffle of passion , could have be the same flesh and blood yes , verily , the same woman , whose heart throb so vehemently to - night , whose brain seem on fire . 

 ' be you sure there be nothing the matter ? ' he ask , with a faint quiver in his voice . 

 ' what should there be the matter ? ' 

 ' who can say ? God know that I know no cause for evil . I be honest enough , and faithful enough , Lesbia . but your face to - night be like a presage of calamity , like the dull , livid sky that go before a thunderstorm . '

 ' I be sorry for that . ' 

 ' so be I. I would ever so much rather have stay out here . ' 

 two hour later , in the steely morning light , when sea and land and sky have a metallic look as if light by electricity , Lady Lesbia stand with her chaperon and her affiance husband on the landing stage belong to the club , ready to step into the boat in which six swarthy seaman in red shirt and cap be to row they back to the yacht . Mr. Smithson draw the warm sortie de bal , with its gold - colour satin lining and white fox border , close round Lesbia 's slender form . 

 ' you be shiver , ' he say ; ' you ought to have warm wrap . 

 ' this be warm enough for St. Petersburg . I be only tired very tired . ' 

 ' the Cayman will rock you to sleep . ' 

 Don Gomez be stand close by , wait for his host . the two man be to walk up the hill to Formosa , a village with a classic portico , delightfully situated above the town . 

 ' what time be we to come to breakfast ? ask Mr. Smithson . 

 ' not too early , in mercy 's name . two o'clock in the afternoon , three , four ; why not make it five combine breakfast with afternoon tea , ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank , with a tremendous yawn . ' I never be so thoroughly fag ; I feel as if I have be beat with stick , basti what be its name . ' 

 she be lean all her weight upon Mr. Smithson , as he hand she down the step and into the boat . her normal weight be not a trifle , and this morning she be heavy with champagne and sleep . carefully as Smithson support she she give a lurch at the bottom of the step , and plunge ponderously into the boat , which dip and careen under she , whereat she shriek , and implore Mr. Smithson to save she . 

 all this occupy some minute , and give Lesbia and the Cuban just time for a few word that have to be say somehow . 

 ' good - night , ' say Montesma , as they clasp hand ; ' good - night ; ' and then in a low voice he say , ' well , have you decide at last ? shall it be ? ' 

 she look at he for a moment or so , pale in the starlight , and then murmur an almost inaudible syllable . 

 ' yes . ' 

 he bent quickly and press his lip upon her gloved hand , and when Mr. Smithson look round they two be stand apart , Montesma in a listless attitude , as if tired of wait for his host .

 ' now really , ' he ask , just before the boat put off , ' when be we to be with you to - morrow ? '

 the boat be glide off , the oar dipping , as he speak . how swiftly it shoot from his ken , flash in and out among the yacht , where the lamp be burn dimly in that clear radiance of new - bear day .
