 CHAPTER XLIII . 

 ' alas , for SORROW be all the end of this ' 


 Lady Kirkbank retire to her cabin directly she get on board the Cayman . 

 ' good - night , child ! I be more than half asleep , ' she say ; ' and I think if there be to be an earthquake an hour hence I should hardly hear it . go to your berth directly , Lesbia ; you look positively awful . I have see girl look bad after ball before now , but I never see such a spectre as you look this morning . ' 

 Poor Georgie 's own complexion leave something to be desire . the Blanc de Fedora have be a brilliant success for the first two hour : after that the warm room begin to tell upon it , and there come a greasiness , then a streakiness , and now all that be leave of an alabaster skin be a livid patch of purplish paint here and there , upon a crow's - foot ground . the eyebrow , too , have give in , and narrow line of Vandyke brown meander down Lady Kirkbank 's cheek . the frizzy hair have go altogether wrong , and have a wild look , suggestive of the witch in Macbeth , and the scraggy neck and poor old shoulder show every year of their age in the ghastly morning light . 

 Lesbia wait in the saloon till Lady Kirkbank have bolt herself into her cabin , and then she go up to the deck wrap in her satin - line , fur - border cloak , and coil herself in a bamboo arm - chair , and nestle her bare head into a turkish pillow , and try to sleep , there with the cool morning breeze blow upon her burn forehead , and the plish - plash of seawater soothe her ear . 

 there be only three or four sailor on deck , weird , almost diabolical - look creature , Lesbia think , in striped shirt , with bare arm , of a shin bronze complexion , flash black eye , sleek raven hair , a sinister look . what specie of man they be Mestizoes , Coolies , Yucatekes she know not , but she feel that they be something wild and strange , and their presence fill she with a vague fear . he , whose influence now rule her life , have tell she that these man be bear mariner , and that she be twenty time safe with they than when the yacht have be under the control of those honest , grin red - whisker English Jack Tars . but she like the english sailor well , all the same ; and she shrink from the faint contact with these tawny - visage stranger , pluck away the train of her gown as they pass her chair , lest they should brush against her drapery . 

 on deck this morning , with only those dark face near , she have a sense of loneliness , of helplessness , of abandonment even . unbidden the image of her home at Grasmere flash into her mind all thing so calm , so perfectly order , such a sense of safety , of home no peril , no temptation , no fever only peace : and she have grow sick to death of peace . she have pray for temp : and the temp have come . 

 there be a heavenly quiet in the air in the early summer morning , only the creaking of a spar , the scream of a seagull now and then . how pale the lamp be grow on board the yacht . Paler still , yellow , and dim , and blur yonder in the town . the eastward face window be golden with the rise sun . yes , this be morning . the yacht be move away yonder , majestical , swan - like , white sail shin against the blue . 

 she close her eye , and try to sleep ; but sleep would not come . she be always listen listen for the dip of oar , listen for a snatch of melody from a mellow baritone whose every accent she know so well . 

 it come at last , the sound her soul long for . she lie among her cushion with closed eye , listen , drink in those rich ripe note as they come near and nearer , to the measure of dip oar , ' La donna e mobile ' 

 Nearer and nearer , until the little boat grind against the hull . she lift her heavy eyelid as Montesma leapt over the gunwale , almost into her arm . he be at her side , kneel by her low chair , kiss the little hand , chill with the freshness of morning . 

 ' my own , my very own , ' he murmur , passionately . 

 he care no more for those copper - face Helots yonder than if they have be make of wood . he have fate in his own hand now , as it seem to he . he go to the skipper and give he some order in Spanish , and then the sail be unfurl , the Cayman spread her broad white wing , and move off among those other yacht which be glide , gliding , glide out to sea , melt from Cowes road like a vision that fadeth with the broad light of morning . 

 when the sail be up and the yacht be run merrily through the water , Montesma go back to Lady Lesbia , and they two sit side by side , gild and glorify in the vivid light of sunrise , talk as they have never talk before , her head upon his shoulder , a smile of ineffable peace upon her lip , as of a weary child that have find rest . 

 they be sail for Havre , and at Havre they be to be marry by the english chaplain , and from Havre they be to sail for the Havana , and to live there ever afterwards in a fairy - tale dream of bliss , break only by an annual visit to Paris , just to buy gown and bonnet . Surrendered be all Lesbia 's ambitious hope forget go ; her desire to reign princess paramount in the kingdom of fashion her thirst to be wealthy among the wealthy go forget . her dream now be of the dolce far niente of a tropical climate , a boudoir giving on the caribbean sea , cigarette , coffee , night spend in a foreign opera house , the languid , reposeful existence of a spanish dama with he , with he . it be for his sake that she have modify all her idea of life . to be with he she would have be content to dwell in the tent of the Patagonians , on the wild and snow - clothe Pampas . a love which be strong enough to make her sacrifice duty , the world , her fair fame as a well - breed woman , be a love that recke but little of the path along which her lover 's hand be to lead . for he , to be with he , she renounce the world . the rest do not count . 

 the summer hour glide past they . the Cayman be far out at sea ; all the other yacht have vanish , and they be alone amidst the blue , with only a solitary three - master yonder , on the edge of the horizon . More than once Lesbia have talk of go below to change her ball gown for the attire of everyday life ; but each time her lover have detain she a little long , have plead for a few more word . Lady Kirkbank would be astir presently , and there would be no more solitude for they till they be marry , and could shake she off altogether . so Lesbia stay , and those two drink the cup of bliss , hush by the monotonous sing - song of the sea , the rhythm of the swing sail . but now it be broad morning . the hour when society , however late it may keep its revel overnight , be apt to awaken , be it only to call for a cup of strong tea and to turn again on the pillow of lassitude , after that refreshment , like the sluggard of Holy Writ . at ten o'clock the sun send his golden arrow across the silken coverlet of her berth and awaken Lady Kirkbank , who open her eye and look about languidly . the little cabin be heave itself up and down in a curious way ; Mr. Smithson 's cigar - case be slop as if they be go to fall upon Lady Kirkbank 's couch , and the look - glass , with all its dainty appliance , be make an angle of forty - five degree . there be more swirling and washing of water against the hull than ever Georgie Kirkbank have hear in Cowes Roads . 

 ' mercy on I ! this horrid thing must be move , ' she exclaim to the empty air . ' it must have break loose in the night . ' 

 she have no confidence in those savage - look sailor , and she have a vision of the yacht drift at the mercy of wind and wave , drift for day , week , and month ; drift to the German Ocean , drift to the North Pole . Mr. Smithson and Montesma on shore no one on board to exercise authority over those fearful man . 

 perhaps they have mutiny , and be carry off the yacht as their booty , with Lesbia and her chaperon , and all their gown . 

 ' I be almost glad that harpy Seraphine have my diamond , ' think poor georgie , ' or I should have have they with I on board this hateful boat . ' 

 and then she rap vehemently against the panel of the cabin , and scream for Rilboche , whose den be adjacent . 

 Rilboche , who detest the sea , make her appearance after some delay , look even green than her mistress , who , in rise from her berth , already begin to suffer the agony of sea - sickness . 

 ' what do this mean ? ' exclaim Lady Kirkbank ; ' and where be we go ? ' 

 ' that be what I should like to know , my lady . but I daresay Lady Lesbia and Mr. Montesma can tell you . they be both on deck . ' 

 ' Montesma ! why , we leave he on shore ! ' 

 ' yes , my lady , but he come on board at five o'clock this morning . I look at my watch when I hear he land , and he and Lady Lesbia have be sit on deck ever since . ' 

 ' and now it be ten . five hour on deck impossible ! ' 

 ' time do not   seem long when one be happy , my lady , ' murmur Rilboche , in her own language . 

 ' help I to dress this instant , ' scream her mistress : ' that dreadful Spaniard be elope with we . ' 

 despite the hideous depression of that malady which strike down Kaiser and beggar with the same rough hand , Lady Kirkbank contrive to get herself dress decently , and to stagger up the companion to that part of the deck where the persian carpet be spread , and the bamboo chair and table be set out under the striped awning . Lesbia and her lover be sit together , he give she a first lesson in the art of smoke a cigarette . he have tell she playfully that every man , woman , and child in Cuba be a smoker , and she have besought he to let she begin , and now , with infinite coquetry , be take her first lesson . 

 ' you shameless minx ! ' exclaim Georgie , pale with anger . 

 ' where be Smithson my poor , good Smithson ? ' 

 ' fast asleep in his bed at Formosa , I hope , dear Lady Kirkbank , ' the Cuban answer , with perfect sang froid . ' Smithson be out of it , as you idiomatic English say . I hope , Lady Kirkbank , you will be as kind to I as you have be to Smithson ; and depend upon it I shall make Lady Lesbia as good a husband as ever Smithson could have do . ' 

 ' you ! ' exclaim the matron , contemptuously . ' you ! - a foreigner , an adventurer , who may be as poor as Job , for anything I know about you . ' 

 ' Job be once rather comfortably off , Lady Kirkbank ; and I can answer for it that Montesma 's wife will know none of the pang of poverty . ' 

 ' if you be a beggar I would not care , ' say Lesbia , draw nearer to he . 

 they have both rise at Lady Kirkbank 's approach , and be stand side by side , confront she . Lesbia have shrink from the idea of poverty with John Hammond ; yet , for this man 's sake , she be ready to face penury , ruin , disgrace , anything . 

 ' do you mean to tell I that Lord Maulevrier 's sister , a young lady under my charge , answerable to I for her conduct , be capable of jilt the man to whom she have solemnly bind herself , in order to marry you ? ' demand Lady Kirkbank , turn to Montesma . 

 ' yes ; that be what I be go to do , ' answer Lesbia , boldly . ' it would be a great sin to keep my promise than to break it . I never like that man , and you know it . you badger I into accept he , against my own well judgment . you drift I so deeply into debt that I be willing to marry a man I loathe in order to get my debt pay . this be what you do for the girl place under your charge . but , thank God , I have release myself from your clutch . I be go far away to a new world , where the memory of my old life can not follow I . People may be angry or pleased ! I do not care . I shall be the wife of the man I have choose out of all the world for my husband the man God make to be my master . ' 

 ' you be - - ' gasp Lady Kirkbank . ' I can not   say what you be . I never in my life feel so tempt to use improper language . ' 

 ' Dear Lady Kirkbank , be reasonable , ' plead Montesma ; ' you can have no interest in see Lesbia marry to a man she dislike . ' 

 Georgia redden a little , remember that she be interested to the amount of some thousand in the Smithson and Haselden alliance ; but she take a high ground than mercenary consideration . 

 ' I be interested in do the very good for a young lady who have be entrust to my care , the granddaughter of an old friend , ' she answer , with dignity . ' I have no objection to you in the abstract , Don Gomez . you have always be vastly civil , I be sure - - ' 

 ' stand by we in our day of need , Lady Kirkbank , and you will find I the staunch friend you ever have . ' 

 ' I be bind in honour to consider Mr. Smithson , Lesbia , ' say Lady Kirkbank . ' I wonder that a decently - bring up girl can behave so abominably . ' 

 ' it would be more abominable to marry a man I detest . I have make up my mind , Lady Kirkbank . we shall be at Havre to - morrow morning , and we shall be married to - morrow shall we not , Gomez ? ' 

 she let her head sink upon his breast , and his arm enfold she . thus shelter , she feel safe , thus and thus only . she have throw her cap over the mill ; snap her finger at society ; care not a jot what the world might think or say of she . this man would she marry and no other ; this man 's fortune would she follow for good or evil . he have that kind of influence with woman which be almost ' possession . ' it smell of brimstone . 

 ' come , my dear good soul , ' say Montesma , smile at the angry matron , ' why not take thing quietly ? you have have a good many girl under your wing ; and you must know that youth and maturity see life from a different standpoint . in your eye my old friend Smithson be an admirable match . you measure he by his house , his stable , his banker 's book ; but Lesbia would rather marry the man she love , and take the risk of his fate . I be not a pauper , Lady Kirkbank , and the home to which I shall take my love be pretty enough for a princess of the blood royal , and for her sake I shall grow rich yet , ' he add , with his eye kindling ; ' and if you care to pay we a visit next February in our parisian apartment I will promise you as pleasant a nest as you can wish to occupy . ' 

 ' how do I know that you will ever bring she back to Europe ? ' say Lady Kirkbank , piteously . ' how do I know that you will not bury she alive in your savage country , among blackamoor , like those horrid sailor , over there kill she , perhaps , when you be tired of she ? ' 

 at these word of Lady Kirkbank 's , fling out at random , Montesma blanch , and his deep black eye meet her with a strangely sinister look . 

 ' yes , ' she cry , hysterically ' kill she , kill she ! you look as if you could do it . ' 

 Lesbia nestle close to her lover 's heart . 

 ' how dare you say such thing to he , ' she cry , angrily . ' I trust he , do not   you see ; trust he with my whole heart , with all my soul . I shall be his wife to - morrow , for good or evil . ' 

 ' very much for evil , I be afraid , ' say Lady Kirkbank . ' perhaps you will be kind enough to come to your cabin and take off that ball gown , and make yourself just a little less disreputable in outward appearance , while I get a cup of tea . ' 

 Lesbia obey , and go down to her cabin , where Kibble be wait with a fresh white muslin frock and all its belonging , lay out ready for her mistress , sorely perplex at the turn which affair be take . she have never like Horace Smithson , although he have give she tip which be almost a provision for her old age ; but she have think it a good thing that her mistress , who be frightfully extravagant , should marry a millionaire ; and now they be sail over the sea with a lot of coloured sailor , and the millionaire be leave on shore . 

 Lady Kirkbank go into the saloon , where breakfast be lay ready , and where the steward be in attendance with that air of be absolutely unconscious of any domestic disturbance , which be the mark of a well - train servant . 

 Lesbia appear in something less than an hour , newly dressed and fresh looking , in her pure white gown , her brown hair bind in a coronet round her small greek head . she sit down by Lady Kirkbank 's side , and try to coax she into good humour . 

 ' why can not   you take thing pleasantly , dear ? ' she plead . ' do now , like a good soul . you hear he say he be well off , and that he will take I to Paris next winter , and you can come to we there on your way from Cannes , and stay with we till Easter . it will be so nice when the Prince and all the good people be in Paris . we shall only stay in Cuba till the fuss about my run away be all over , and people have forget , do not   you know . as for Mr. Smithson , why should I have any more compunction about jilt he than he have about that poor Miss Trinder ? by - the - bye , I want you to send he back all his present for I . they be almost all in Arlington Street . I bring nothing with I except my engagement ring , ' look down at the half - hoop of diamond , and pull it off her finger as she talk . ' I have a kind of presentiment - - ' 

 ' you mean that you have make up your mind to throw he over . ' 

 ' no . but I feel there be breaker ahead . it might have come to throw myself into the sea . perhaps you would have like that well than what have happen . ' 

 ' I do not   know , I be sure . the whole thing be disgraceful . London will ring with the scandal . what be I to say to Lady Maulevrier , to your brother ? and pray how do you propose to get marry at Havre ? you can not be marry in a french town by merely hold up your finger . there be no registry office . I be sure I have no idea how the thing be do . ' 

 ' Don Gomez have arrange all that everything have be think of everything have be plan . a steamer will take we to St. Thomas , and another steamer will take we on to Cuba . ' 

 ' but the marriage the licence ? ' 

 ' I tell you everything have be provide for . please take this ring and send it to Mr. Smithson when you go back to England . ' 

 ' send it to he yourself . I will have nothing to do with it . ' 

 ' how dreadfully disagreeable you be , ' say Lesbia , pouting , ' just because I be marry to please myself , instead of to please you . it be frightfully selfish of you . ' 

 Montesma come in at this moment . he , too , have dress himself freshly , and be look his handsome , in that buccaneer style of costume which he wear when he sail the yacht . he and Lesbia breakfast at their ease , while Lady Kirkbank recline in her bamboo arm - chair , feel very unhappy in her mind and far from well . Neptune and she could not accommodate themselves . 

 after a leisurely breakfast , enliven by talk and laughter , the cabin window open , the sun shin , the freshen breeze blow in , Lesbia and Don Gomez go on deck , and he recline at her foot while she read to he from the page of her favourite Keats , read languidly , lazily , yet exquisitely , for she have be teach to read as well as to sing . the poetry seem to have be write on purpose for they ; and the sky and the atmosphere around they seem to have be make for the poetry . and so , with interval of stroll on the deck , and an hour or so dawdle away at luncheon , and a leisurely afternoon tea , the day wear on to sunset , and they go back to Keats , while Lady Kirkbank sulk and sleep in a corner of the saloon . 

 ' this be the happy day of my life , ' Lesbia murmur , in a pause of their reading , when they have drop endymion 's love to talk of their own . 

 ' but not of mine , my angel . I shall be happy still when we be far away on broad water , beyond the reach of all who can part we . ' 

 ' can any one part we , Gomez , now that we have pledge ourselves to each other ? ' she ask , incredulously . 

 ' ah , love , such pledge be sometimes break . all woman be not lion - hearte . while the sea be smooth and the ship run fair , all be easy enough ; but when tempest and peril come that be the test , Lesbia . will you stand by I in the temp , love ? ' 

 ' you know that I will , ' she answer , with her hand lock in his two hand , clasp as with a life - long clasp . 

 she could not imagine any severe ordeal to be go through . if Maulevrier hear of her elopement in time for pursuit , there would be a fuss , perhaps an angry bother rag and fuming . but what of that ? she be her own mistress . Maulevrier could not prevent her marrying whomsoever she please . 

 ' swear that you will hold to I against all the world , ' he say , passionately , turn his head to look across the stern of the vessel . 

 ' against all the world , ' she answer , softly . 

 ' I believe your courage will be test before long , ' he say ; and then he cry to the skipper , ' crowd on all sail , Tomaso . that boat be chase we . ' 

 Lesbia spring to her foot , look as he look to a spot of vivid white on the horizon . Montesma have snatch up a glass and be watch that distant spot . 

 ' it be a steam - yacht , ' he say . ' they will catch we . ' 

 he be right . although the Cayman strain every timber so that her keel cut through the water like a boomerang , wind and steam beat wind without steam . in less than an hour the steam - yacht be beside the Cayman , and Lord Maulevrier and Lord Hartfield have board Mr. Smithson 's deck . 

 ' I have come to take you and Lady Kirkbank back to Cowes , Lesbia , ' say Maulevrier . ' I be not go to make any undue fuss about this little escapade of your , provide you go back with Hartfield and I at once , and pledge yourself never to hold any further communication with Don Gomez de Montesma . ' 

 the Spaniard be stand close by , silent , white as death , but ready to make a good fight . that pallor of the clear olive skin be not from want of pluck ; but there be the deadly knowledge of the ground he stand upon , the doubt that any woman , least of all such a woman as Lady Lesbia Haselden , could be true to he if his character and antecedent be reveal to she . and how much or how little these two man could tell she about himself or his past life be the question which the next few minute would solve . 

 ' I be not go back with you , ' answer Lesbia . ' I be go to Havre with Don Gomez de Montesma . we be to be marry there as soon as we arrive . ' 

 ' to be marry at Havre , ' cry Maulevrier . ' an appropriate place . a sailor have a wife in every port , do not   you know . ' 

 ' we have well go down to the cabin , ' say Hartfield , lay his hand upon his friend 's shoulder . ' if Lady Lesbia will be good enough to come with we we can tell she all that we have to tell quietly there . ' 

 Lord Hartfield 's tone be unmistakeable . everything be know . 

 ' you can talk at your ease here , ' say Montesma , face the two man with a diabolical recklessness and insolence of manner . ' not one of these fellow on board know a dozen sentence of English . ' 

 ' I would rather talk below , if it be all the same to you , Senor ; and I should be glad to speak to Lady Lesbia alone . ' 

 ' that you shall not do unless she desire it , ' answer Montesma . 

 ' no , he shall hear all that you have to say . he shall hear how I answer you , ' say Lesbia . 

 Lord Hartfield shrug his shoulder . 

 ' as you please , ' he say . ' it will make the disclosure a little more painful than it need have be ; but that can not be help . ' 

 CHAPTER XLIV . 

 ' OH , SAD KISSED MOUTH , how SORROWFUL it be ! ' 


 they all go down to the saloon , where Lady Kirkbank sit , look the image of despair , which change to delighted surprise at sight of Lord Hartfield and his friend . 

 ' do you give your consent to my sister 's elopement with this man , Lady Kirkbank ? ' Maulevrier ask , brusquely . 

 ' I give my consent ! good gracious ! no . he have elope with I ever so much more than with your sister . she know all about it , I have   no doubt : but the wretch run away with I in my sleep . ' 

 ' I be glad , for your own self - respect , that you have no hand in this disgraceful business , ' reply Maulevrier ; and then turn to Lord Hartfield , he say , ' Hartfield , will you tell my sister who and what this man be ? will you make she understand what kind of pitfall she have escape ? upon my soul , I can not speak of it . ' 

 ' I recognise no right of Lord Hartfield 's to interfere with my action , and I will hear nothing that he may have to say , ' say Lesbia , stand by her lover 's side , with head erect and eye dark with anger . 

 ' your sister 's husband have the strong right to control your action , Lady Lesbia , when the family honour be at stake , ' answer Hartfield , with grave authority . ' accept I at least as a member of your family , if you will not accept I as your disinterested and devoted friend . ' 

 ' friend ! ' echo Lesbia , scornfully . ' you might have be my friend once . your friendship then would have be of some value to I , if you have tell I the truth , instead of approach I with a lie upon your lip . you talk of honour , Lord Hartfield ; you , who come to my grandmother 's house as an impostor , under a false name ! ' 

 ' I go there as a man stand on his own merit , assume no rank save that which God give he among his fellow - man , claim to be possess of no fortune except intellect and industry . if I could not win a wife with such credential , it be well for I never to marry at all , Lady Lesbia . but we have no time to speak of the past . I be here as your brother 's friend , here to save you . ' 

 ' to part I from the man to whom I have give my heart . that you can not do . Gomez , why do you not speak ? tell he , tell he ! ' cry Lesbia , with a voice strangle by sob ; ' tell he that I be to be your wife to - morrow , at Havre . your wife ! ' 

 ' Dear Lady Lesbia , that can not be , ' say Lord Hartfield , sorrowfully , pity she in her helplessness , as he might have pity a young bird in the fowler 's net . ' I be assure upon undeniable authority that Senor Montesma have a wife live at Cuba ; and even be this not so be he free to marry you his character and antecedent would for ever forbid such a marriage . ' 

 ' a wife ! no , no , no ! ' shriek Lesbia , look wildly from one to the other . ' it be a lie a lie , invent by my brother , who always hat I by you , who fool and deceive I ! it be a lie , an infamous invention ! Don Gomez , speak to they : for pity 's sake answer they ! do not   you see that they be drive I mad ? ' 

 she fling herself into his arm , she bury she dishevel head upon his breast ; she cling to he with hand that writhe convulsively in her agony . 

 Maulevrier spring across the cabin and wrench she from her lover 's grasp . 

 ' you shall not pollute she with your touch , ' he cry ; ' you have poison her mind already . Scoundrel , seducer , slave - dealer ! do you hear , Lesbia ? shall I tell you what this man be what trade he follow yonder , on his native island this spanish hidalgo this all - accomplish gentleman lineal descendant of the Cid fine flower of andalusian chivalry ? it be not enough for he to cheat at card , to float bubble company , bogus lottery . his profligate extravagance , his love of sybarite luxury , require a large resource than the petty scheme which enrich small man . a slave ship , which could earn nearly twenty thousand pound on every voyage , and which could make two run in a year that be the trade for Don Gomez de Montesma , and he carry it on merrily for six or seven year , till the british cruiser get too keen for he , and the good old game be play out . you see that scar upon the hidalgo 's forehead , Lesbia a token of knightly prowess , you think , perhaps . no , my girl , that be the mark of an english cutlass in a scuffle on board a slaver . a merry trade , Lesbia the live cargo stow close under hatch have rather a bad time of it now and then short ration of food and water , yellow Jack . they die like rotten sheep sometimes bad then for the dealer . but if he can land the bulk of his human ware safe and sound the profit be enormous . the Captain - General take his capitation fee , the blackie be draft off to the sugar plantation , and everybody be satisfied ; but I think , Lesbia , that your british prejudice would go against marriage with a slave - trader , be he ever so free to make you his wife , which this particular dealer in blackamoor be not . ' 

 ' be this true , this part of their vile story ? ' demand Lesbia , look at her lover , who stand apart from they all now , his arm fold , his face deadly pale , the low lip quiver under the grinding of his strong white tooth . 

 ' there be some truth in it , ' he answer , hoarsely . ' everybody in Cuba have a finger in the african trade , before your british philanthropy spoil it . Mr. Smithson make sixty thousand pound in that line . it be the foundation of his fortune . and yet he have his misfortune in run his cargo a ship burn , a freight roast alive . there be some very black story in Cuba against poor Smithson . he will never go there again . ' 

 ' Mr. Smithson may be a scoundrel ; indeed , I believe he be a pretty bad speciman in that line , ' say Lord Hartfield . ' but I doubt if there be any story that can be tell of he quite so bad as the history of your marriage , and the event that go before it . I have be tell the story of the beautiful Octoroon , who love and trust you , who share your good and evil fortune for the most desperate year of your life , be almost accept as your wife , and whose strangle corpse be find in the harbour while the bell be ring for your marriage with a rich planter 's heiress the lady who , no doubt , now patiently await your return to her native island . ' 

 ' she will wait a long time , ' say Montesma , ' or fare ill if I go back to she . Lesbia , his lordship 's story of the Octoroon be a fable an invention of my cuban enemy , who hate we old Spaniards with a poisonous hatred . but this much be true . I be a married man bind , fetter by a tie which I abhor . our Havre marriage would have be bigamy on my part , a delusion on your . I could not have take you to Cuba . I have plan our life in a fairer , more civilised world . I be rich enough to have surround you with all that make life worth live . I would have give you love as true and as deep as ever man give to woman . all that would have be want would have be the legality of the tie : and as law never yet make a marriage happy which lack the element of bliss , our lawless union need not have miss happiness . Lesbia , you say that you would hold by I , come what might . the bad have come , love ; but it leave I not the less your true lover . ' 

 she look at he with wild despairing eye , and then , with a hoarse strange cry , rush from the cabin , and up the companion , with a desperate swiftness which seem like the flight of a bird . Montesma , Hartfield , Maulevrier , all follow she , heedless of everything except the dire necessity of arrest her flight . each in his own mind have divine her purpose . 

 they be not too late . it be Hartfield 's strong arm that catch she , hold she as in a vice , drag she away from the edge of the deck , just where there be a space open to the wave . another instant and she would have fling herself overboard . she fall back into Lord Hartfield 's arm , with a wild choking cry : ' let I go ! let I go ! ' another moment , and a flood of crimson stain his shirt - front , as she lie upon his breast , with closed eyelid and blood - bedabble lip , in blessed unconsciousness . 

 they carry she on to the steam - yacht , and down to the cabin , where there be ample accommodation and some luxury , although not the elegance of Bond Street upholstery . Rilboche , Lady Kirkbank , Kibble , luggage of all kind be transfer from one yacht to the other , even to the vellum bind Keats which lie face downwards on the deck , just where Lesbia have fling it when the Cayman be board . the crew of the steam - yacht Philomel help in the transfer : there be plenty of hand , and the work be do quickly ; while the Meztizoes , Yucatekes , Caribs , or whatever they be , look on and grin ; and while Montesma stand lean against the mast , with fold arm and sombre brow , a cigarette between his lip . 

 when the woman and all their belonging be on board the Philomel , Lord Hartfield address himself to Montesma . 

 ' if you consider yourself entitle to call I to account for this evening 's work you know where to find I , ' he say . 

 Montesma shrug his shoulder , and throw away his cigarette with a contemptuous gesture . 

 ' ce n'est pas la peine , ' he say ; ' I be a dead shot , and should be pretty sure to send a bullet through you if you give I the chance ; but I should not be any nearer win she if I kill you : and it be she and she only that I want . you may think I an adventurer swindler gambler slave - dealer what you will but I love she as I never think to love a woman , and I should have be true as steel , if she have be plucky enough to trust I . but , as I tell she an hour ago , woman have not lion heart . they can talk tall while the sky be clear and the sun shin , but at the first crack of thunder va te promener . ' 

 ' if you have kill she ' begin Hartfield . 

 ' kill she ! no . some small bloodvessel burst in the agitation of that terrible scene . she will be well in a week , and she will forget I . but I shall not forget she . she be the one flower that have spring on the barren plain of my life . she be my Picciola . ' 

 he turn his back on Lord Hartfield and walk to the other end of the deck . something in his face , in the vibration of his deep voice , convince Hartfield of his truth . a bad man undoubtedly steep to the lip in evil and yet so far true that he have passionately , deeply , devotedly love this one woman . 

 it be the dead of night when Lesbia recover consciousness , and even then she lie silent , take no heed of those around she , in a state of utter prostration . Kibble nurse she carefully , tenderly , all through the night ; Maulevrier hardly leave the cabin , and Lady Kirkbank , always more or less a victim to the agony of sea - sickness , still find time to utter lamentation and wailing over the ruin of her protťgťe 's fortune . 

 ' never have a girl such a chance , ' she moan . ' quite the good match in society . the house in Park Lane alone cost a fortune . her diamond would have be the fine in London . ' 

 ' they would have be stain with the blood of the nigger he trade in out yonder , ' answer Maulevrier . ' do you think I would have let my sister marry a slave - dealer ? ' 

 ' I do not   believe a syllable of it , ' protest Lady Kirkbank , dab her brow with a handkerchief steep in eau de Cologne . ' a vile fabrication of Montesma 's , who want to blacken poor Smithson 's character in order to extenuate his own crime . ' 

 ' well , we will not   go into that question , ' say Maulevrier wearily . ' the Smithson match be off , anyhow ; and it matter very little to we whether he make most money out of nigger or bubble company , or lottery or gaming hell . ' 

 ' I be convinced that Smithson make his fortune in a thoroughly gentlemanlike manner , ' argue Lady Kirkbank . ' look at the people who visit he , and the house he go to . and I do not   see why the match need be off . I be sure , if Lesbia play her card properly , he will look over this this little escapade . ' 

 Maulevrier contemplate the worldly old face with infinite scorn . 

 ' do she look like a girl who will play her card in your fashion ? ' he ask , point to his sister , whose white face upon the pillow seem like a mask cut out of marble . ' upon my soul , Lady Kirkbank , I consider my sister 's elopement with this spanish adventurer , with whom she be over head and ear in love , a far more respectable act than her engagement to Smithson , for whom she care not a straw . ' 

 ' well , I hope if you so approve of her conduct you will help she to pay her dressmaker , and the rest of they , ' retort Lady Kirkbank . ' she have be plunge rather deeply , I believe , under the impression that Smithson would pay all her bill when she be marry . your grandmother may not quite like the budget . ' 

 ' I will do all I can for she , ' answer Maulevrier . ' I would do a great deal to save she from the degradation to which your teaching have bring she . ' 

 Lady Kirkbank look at he for a moment or so with reproachful eye , and then shrug her shoulder contemptuously . 

 ' if I ever expect gratitude from people I might feel the injustice the insolence of your last remark , ' she say ; ' but as I never do expect gratitude , I be not disappoint in this case . and now I think if there be a cabin which I can have to myself I should like to retire to it , ' she add . ' my care be throw away here . ' 

 there be a cabin at Lady Kirkbank 's disposal . it have be already appropriate by Rilboche , and smell of cognac ; but Rilboche resign her berth to her mistress , and lay herself meekly on the floor for the rest of the voyage . 

 they be in Cowes road at eight o'clock next morning , and Lord Hartfield go on shore for a doctor , whom he bring back before nine , and who pronounce Lady Lesbia to be in a very weak and prostrate condition , and forbid she be move within the next two day . happily Lord Hartfield have borrow the Philomel and her crew from a friend who have give he carte blanche as to the use he make of she , and who freely leave she at his disposal so long as he and his party should need the accommodation . Lesbia could nowhere be well off than on the yacht , where she be away from the gossip and tittle - tattle of the town . 

 the roadstead be quiet enough now . all the racing yacht have melt away like a dream , and most of the pleasure yacht be off to Ryde . Lady Lesbia lie in her curtained cabin , with Kibble keeping watch beside her bed , while Maulevrier come in every half - hour to see how she be sit by she a little now and then , and talk of indifferent thing in a low kind voice , which be full of comfort . 

 she seem grateful for his kindness , and smile at he once in a way , with a piteous little smile ; but she have the air of one in whom the mainspring of life be break . the pallid face and heavy violet eye , the semi - transparent hand which lie so listlessly upon the crimson coverlet , convey an impression of supreme despair . Hartfield , look down at she for the last time when he come to say good - bye before leave for London , be remind of the story of one whose life have be thus rudely break , who have love as foolishly and even more fondly , and for whom the world hold nothing when that tie be sever . 

 ' she look on many a face with vacant eye , 
 on many a token without know what ; 
 she see they watch she , without ask why , 
 and recke not who around her pillow sit . ' 
 but Lesbia Haselden belong to a wide and more sophisticated world than that of the daughter of the Grecian Isle , and for her existence offer wide horizon . it might be prophesy that for she the dark ending of a girlish dream would not be a life - long despair . the passionate love have be at fever point ; the passionate grief must have its fever too , and burn itself out . 

 ' do all you can to cheer she , ' say Lord Hartfield to Maulevrier , ' and bring she to Fellside as soon as ever she be strong enough to bear the journey . you and Kibble , with your own man , will be able to do all that be necessary . ' 

 ' quite able . ' 

 ' that be right . I must be in the House for the expect division to - night , and I shall go back to Grasmere to - morrow morning . poor Mary be horribly lonely . ' 

 Lord Hartfield go off in the boat to catch the Southampton steamer ; and Maulevrier be now sole custodian of the yacht and of his sister . he and the doctor have agree to keep she on board , in the fresh sea air , till she be equal to the fatigue of the journey to Grasmere . there be nothing to be gain by take she on to the island or by carry she to London . the yacht be well find , provide with all thing needful for comfort , and Lesbia could be nowhere well off until she be safe in her old home : that home she have leave so gaily , in the freshness of her youthful inexperience , nearly a year ago , and to which she would return so battered and break , so deeply degrade by the knowledge of evil . 

 Lady Kirkbank have start for London on the previous day . 

 ' I be evidently not want here , ' she say , with an offend air ; ' and I must have everything at Kirkbank ready for a house full of people before the twelfth of August , so the soon I get to Scotland the well . I shall make a dťtour in order to go and see Lady Maulevrier on my way down . it be due to myself that I should let she know that I be entirely blameless in this most uncomfortable business . ' 

 ' you can tell her ladyship what you please , ' answer Maulevrier , bluntly . ' I shall not gainsay you , so long as you do not slander my sister ; but as long as I live I shall regret that I , know something of London society , do not interfere to prevent Lesbia be give over to your keeping . ' 

 ' if I have know the kind of girl she be I would have have nothing to do with she , ' retort Lady Kirkbank with exasperation ; and so they part . 

 the Philomel have be lie off Cowes three day before Mr. Smithson appear upon the scene . he have get wind somehow from a sailor , who have talk with one of the foreign crew , of the destination of the Cayman , and he have cross from Southampton to Havre on the steamer Wolf during that night in which Lesbia have be carry back to Cowes on the Philomel . 

 he be at Havre when the Cayman arrive , with Montesma and his tawny - visage crew on board , no one else . 

 ' you may examine every corner of your ship , ' Montesma cry , scornfully , when Smithson come on board and swear that Lesbia must be hide somewhere in the vessel . ' the bird have fly : she will shelter in neither your nest nor in mine , Smithson . you have lose she and so have I. we may as well be friend in misfortune . ' 

 he be haggard , livid with grief and anger . he look ten year old than he have look the other night at the ball , when his dash and swagger , and handsome spanish head have be the admiration of the room . 

 Smithson be very angry , but he be not a fight man . he have enjoy various opportunity for distinguish himself in that line in the island of Cuba ; but he have always avoid such opportunity . so now , after a good deal of bluster and violent language , which Montesma take as lightly as if it have be the whistling of the wind in the shroud , poor Smithson calm down , and allow Gomez de Montesma to leave the yacht , with his portmanteaux , unharmed . he mean to take the first steamer for the spanish Main , he tell Smithson . he have have quite enough of Europe . 

 ' I daresay it will end in your marry she , ' he say , at the last moment . ' if you do , be kind to she . ' 

 his voice falter , choke by a sob , at those last word . after all , it be possible for a man without principle , without morality , to begin to make love to a woman in a mere spirit of adventure , in sheer devilry , and to be rather hard hit at the last . 

 Horace Smithson sail his yacht back to Cowes without loss of time , and send his card to Lord Maulevrier on board the Philomel . his lordship reply that he would wait upon Mr. Smithson that afternoon at four o'clock , and at that hour Maulevrier again board the Cayman ; but this time very quietly , as an expect guest . 

 the interview that follow be very painful . Mr. Smithson be willing that this unhappy episode in the life of his betrothed , this folly into which she have be beguile by a man of infinite treachery , a man of all other man fatal to woman , should be forget , should be as if it have never be . 

 ' it be her very innocence which make she a victim to that scoundrel , ' say Smithson , ' her girlish simplicity and Lady Kirkbank 's folly . but I love your sister too well to sacrifice she lightly , Lord Maulevrier ; and if she can forget this midsummer madness , why , so can i. ' 

 ' she can not forget , Mr. Smithson , ' answer Maulevrier , gravely . ' she have do you a great wrong by listen to your false friend 's address ; but she do you a still great wrong when she accept you as her husband without one spark of love for you . she and you be both happy in have escape the degradation , the deep misery of a loveless union . I be glad yes , glad even of this shameful escapade with Montesma though it have drag her good name through the gutter , glad of the catastrophe that have save she from such a marriage . you be very generous in your willingness to forget my sister 's folly . let your forgetfulness go a step far , and forget that you ever meet she . ' 

 ' that can not be , Lord Maulevrier . she have ruin my life . ' 

 ' not at all . an affair of a season , ' answer Maulevrier , lightly . ' next year I shall hear of you as the accepted husband of some new beauty . a man of Mr. Smithson 's wealth and good nature need not languish in single blessedness . ' 

 with this civil speech Lord Maulevrier go back to the Philomel 's gig , and this be his last meeting with Mr. Smithson , until they meet a year later in the beat track of society . 

 CHAPTER XLV . 

 ' that FELL ARREST , without all BAIL . ' 


 it be the beginning of August before Lesbia be pronounce equal to the fatigue of a long journey ; and even then it be but the shadow of her former self which return to Fellside , the pale spectre of joy depart , of trust deceive . 

 Maulevrier have be very good to she , patient , unselfish as a woman , in his ministering to the break - hearted girl . that broken heart would be whole again , no doubt , in the future , as many other break heart have be ; but the grief , the despair , the sense of hopelessness and aimlessness in life be very real in the present . if the picturesque seclusion of Fellside have seem dull and joyless to Lesbia in day go by , it be much dull to she now . she be shock at the change in her grandmother , and she show a good deal of feeling and affection in her intercourse with the invalid ; but once out of her presence Lady Maulevrier be forget , and Lesbia 's thought drift back into the old current . they dwelt obstinately , unceasingly upon Montesma , the man whose influence have awaken the slumber soul from its torpor , have stir the deep of a passionate nature . 

 slave - dealer , gambler , adventurer , liar his name blacken by the suspicion of a still dark crime . she shudder at the thought of the villain from whose snare she have be rescue : and yet , his image as he have be to she in the brief golden time when she believe he noble , and chivalrous , and true , haunt her lonely day , mix itself with her troubled dream , come between she and every other thought . 

 everybody be good to she . that pale and joyless face , that look of patient , hopeless suffering which she try to disguise every now and then with a faint forced smile , and silvery little ripple of society laughter , seem unconsciously to implore pity and pardon . Lady Maulevrier utter no word of reproach . ' my dearest , Fate have not be kind to you , ' she say , gently , after tell Lesbia of Lady Kirkbank 's visit . ' the handsome woman be seldom the happy . destiny seem to have a grudge against they . and if thing have go amiss it be I who be most to blame . I ought never to have entrust you with such a woman as Georgina Kirkbank . but you will be happy next season , I hope , dearest . you can live with Mary and Hartfield . they will take care of you . ' 

 Lesbia shudder . 

 ' do you think I be go back to the society treadmill ? ' she exclaim . ' no , I have do with the world . I shall end my day here , or in a convent . ' 

 ' you think so now , dear , but you will change your mind by - and - by . a fancy that have last only a few week can not alter your life . it will pass as other dream have pass . at your age you have the future before you . ' 

 ' no , it be the past that be always before I , ' answer Lesbia . ' my future be a blank . ' 

 the bill come pour in ; dressmaker , milliner , glover , bootmaker , tailor , stationer , perfumer ; awful bill which make Lady Maulevrier 's blood run cold , so degrading be their story of selfish self - indulgence , of senseless extravagance . but she pay they all without a word . she take upon her shoulder the chief burden of Lesbia 's wrongdoing . it be her indulgence , her weak preference which have foster her granddaughter 's selfishness , train she to vanity and worldly pride . the result be ignominious , humiliating , bitter beyond all common bitterness ; but the cup be of her own brewing , and she drink it without a murmur . 

 Parliament be prorogue ; the season be over ; and Lord Hartfield be establish at Fellside for the autumn he and his wife utterly happy in their affection for each other , but not without care as to their surrounding , which be full of trouble . first there be Lesbia 's sorrow . grant that it be a grief which would inevitably wear itself out , as other such grief have do from time immemorial ; but still the sorrow be there , at their door . next , there be the state of Lady Maulevrier 's health , which give her old medical adviser the grave fear . at Lord Hartfield 's earnest desire a famous doctor be summon from London ; but the great man could only confirm Mr. Horton 's verdict . the thread of life be wear thin every day . it might snap at any hour . in the meantime the only regime be repose of body and mind , an all - pervade calm , the avoidance of all exciting topic . one moment of violent agitation might prove fatal . 

 know this , how could Lord Hartfield call her ladyship to account for the presence of that mysterious old man under Steadman 's charge ? how venture to touch upon a topic which , by Mary 's showing , have exercise a most disturbing influence upon her ladyship 's mind on that solitary occasion when the girl venture to approach the subject ? 

 he feel that any attempt at an explanation be impossible . it be not for he to precipitate Lady Maulevrier 's end by pry into her secret . grant that shame and dishonour of some kind be involve in the existence of that strange old man , he , Lord Hartfield , must endure his portion in that shame must be content to leave the dark riddle unsolve . 

 he resign himself to this state of thing , and try to forget the cloud that hang over the house of Haselden ; but the sense of a mystery , a fatal family secret , which must come to light soon or later since all such secret be know at last know , sift , and bandy about from lip to lip , and publish in a thousand different newspaper , and cry aloud in the street the sense of such a secret , the dread of such a revelation weigh upon he heavily . 

 Maulevrier , the restless , be off to Argyleshire for the grouse shoot as soon as he have deposit Lady Lesbia comfortably at Fellside . 

 ' I should only be in your way if I stop , ' he say , ' for you and Molly have hardly get over the honeymoon stage yet , though you put on the air of Darby and Joan . I shall be back in a week or ten day . ' 

 ' in Lady Maulevrier 's state of health I do not   think you ought to stay away very long , ' say Hartfield . 

 ' Poor Lady Maulevrier ! she never care much for I , do not   you know . but I suppose it would seem unkind if I be to be out of the way when the end come . the end ! good heaven ! how coolly I talk of it ; and a year ago I think she be as immortal as Fairfield yonder . ' 

 he go away , his spirit dash by that awful thought of death , and Lord and Lady Hartfield have the house to themselves , since Lesbia hardly count . she seldom leave her own room , except to sit with her grandmother for an hour . she lie on her sofa or sit in a low arm - chair by the window , read Keats or Shelley or only dream dream over the brief golden time of her life , with its fond delusion , its false brightness . Mr. Horton go to see she every day feel the feeble little pulse which seem hardly to have force enough to beat urge she to struggle against apathy and inertia , to walk a little , to go for a long drive every day , to live in the open air to which instruction she pay not the slight attention . the desire for life be go . disappointed in her ambition , betray in her love , humiliate , dupe , degrade a social failure . what have she to live for ? she feel as if it would have be a good thing , quite the good thing that could happen , if she could turn her face to the wall and die . all that past season , its triumph , its pleasure , its variety , be like a garish dream , a horror to look back upon , hateful to remember . 

 in vain do Mary and Hartfield urge Lesbia to join in their simple pleasure , their walk and ride and drive , and boat excursion . she always refuse . 

 ' you know I never care much for roam about these everlaste hill , ' she tell Mary . ' I never have your passion for Lakeland . it be very good of you to wish to have I , but it be quite impossible . I have hardly strength enough for a little walk in the garden . ' 

 ' you would have more strength if you go out more , ' plead Mary , almost with tear . ' Mr. Horton say sun and wind be the good doctor for you . Lesbia , you frighten I sometimes . you be just let yourself fade away . ' 

 ' if you know how I hate the world and the sky , Mary , you would not   urge I to go out of door , ' Lesbia answer , moodily . ' indoor I can read , and get away from my own thought somehow , for a little while . but out yonder , face to face with the hill and the lake the scene I have know all my life I feel a heart - sickness that be bad than death . it madden I to see that old , old picture of mountain and water , the same for ever and ever , no matter what heart be break . ' 

 Mary creep close beside her sister 's couch , put her arm round her neck , lay she cheek rich in the ruddy bloom of health against Lesbia 's pallid and sink cheek , and comfort she as much as she could with tender murmur and love kiss . other comfort , she could give none . all the wisdom in the world will not cure a girl 's heart - sickness when she have fling away the treasure of her love upon a worthless object . 

 and so the day go by , peacefully , but sadly ; for the shadow of doom hang heavily over the house upon the Fell . nobody who look upon Lady Maulevrier could doubt that her day be number , that the oil be wax low in the lamp of life . the end , the awful , mysterious end , be draw near ; and she who be call be make no such preparation as the Christian make to answer the dread summon . as she have live , she mean to die an avow unbeliever . More than once Mary have take courage , and have talk to her grandmother of the world beyond , the blessed hope of re - union with the friend we have lose , in a new and bright life , only to be meet by the sceptic 's cynical smile , the materialist 's barren creed . 

 ' my dearest , we know nothing except the immutable law of material life . all the rest be a dream a beautiful dream , if you like a consolation to that kind of temperament which can take comfort from dream ; but for anyone who have read much , and think much , and keep as far as possible on a level with the scientific intellect of the age for such an one , Mary , these old fable be too idle . I shall die as I have live , the victim of an inscrutable destiny , work blindly , evil to some , good to other . ah ! love , life have begin very fairly for you . may the fate be kind always to my gentle and love girl ! ' 

 there be more talk between they on this dark mystery of life and death . Mary bring out her poor little argument , glorify by the light of perfect faith ; but they be of no avail against opinion which have be the gradual growth of a long and joyless life . Time have attune Lady Maulevrier 's mind to the gospel of Schopenhauer and the Pessimists , and she be content to see the mystery of life as they have see it . she have no fear , but she have some anxiety as to the thing that be to happen after she be go . she have take upon herself a heavy burden , and she have not yet come to the end of the road where her burden might be lay quietly down , her task accomplish . if she fall by the wayside under her load the consequence for the survivor might be full of trouble . 

 her anxiety be increase by the fact that her faithful servant and adviser , James Steadman , be no long the man he have be . the change in he be painfully evident memory failing , energy go . he come to his mistress 's room every morning , receive her order , answer her question ; but Lady Maulevrier feel that he go through the old duty in a mechanical way , and that his dull brain but half understand their importance . 

 one evening at dusk , just as Hartfield and Mary be leave Lady Maulevrier 's room , after dinner , an appalling shriek run through the house a cry almost as terrible as that which Lord Hartfield hear in the summer midnight just a year ago . but this time the sound come from the old part of the house . 

 ' something have happen , ' exclaim Hartfield , rush to the door of communication . 

 it be bolt inside . he knock vehemently ; but there be no answer . he run downstairs , follow by Mary , breathless , in an agony of fear . just as they approach the low door , lead to the old house , it be fling open , and Steadman 's wife stand before they pale with terror . 

 ' the doctor , ' she cry ; ' send for Mr. Horton , somebody , for God 's sake . oh , my lord , ' with a sudden burst of sobbing , ' I be afraid he be dead . ' 

 ' Mary , despatch some one for Horton , ' say Lord Hartfield . keep his wife back with one hand , he close the door against she , and then follow Mrs. Steadman through the long low corridor to her husband 's sitting - room . 

 James Steadman be lie upon his back upon the hearth , near the spot be Lord Hartfield have see he sleep in his arm - chair a month ago . 

 one look at the distorted face , dark with inject blood , the dreadful glassy glare of the eye , the foam - stain lip , tell that all be over . the faithful servant have die at his post . whatever his charge have be , his term of service be end . there be a vacancy in Lady Maulevrier 's household . 

 CHAPTER XLVI . 

 the day of reckoning . 


 Lord Hartfield stay with the frightened wife while she kneel beside that awful figure on the hearth , wring her hand with piteous bewailing and lamentation over the unconscious clay . he have always be a good husband to she , she murmur ; hard and stern perhaps , but a good man . and she have obey he without a question . whatever he do or say she have count right . 

 ' we have not have a happy life , though there be many who have envy we her ladyship 's favour , ' she say in the midst of her lamentation . ' no one know where the shoe pinch but those who have to wear it . poor James ! early and late , early and late , study her ladyship 's interest , caring and think , in order to keep trouble away from she . always on the watch , always on the listen . that be what wear he out , poor fellow ! ' 

 ' my good soul , your husband be an old man , ' argue Lord Hartfield , in a consolatory tone , ' and the end must come to all of we somehow . ' 

 ' he might have live to be a much old man if he have have less worry , ' say the wife , bend her face to kiss the cold dead brow . ' his day be full of care . we should have be happy in the poor cottage in Grasmere than we ever be in this big grand house . ' 

 thus , in broken fragment of speech , Mrs. Steadman lament over her dead , while the heavy pendulum of the eight - day clock in the hall sound the slowly - pass moment , until the coming of the doctor break upon the quiet of the house , with the noise of open door and approach footstep . 

 James Steadman be dead . medicine could do nothing for that lifeless clay , lie on the hearth by which he have sit on so many winter night , for so many year of faithful unquestione service . there be nothing to be do for that stiffening form , save the last office for the dead ; and Lord Hartfield leave Mr. Horton to arrange with the weep woman as to the doing of these . he be anxious to go to Lady Maulevrier , to break to she , as gently as might be , the news of her servant 's death . 

 and what of that strange old man in the upper room ? who be to attend upon he , now that the caretaker be lay low ? 

 while Lord Hartfield linger on the threshold of the door that lead from the old house to the new , ponder this question , there come the sound of wheel on the carriage drive , and then a loud ring at the hall door . 

 it be Maulevrier , just arrive from Scotland , smell of autumn rain and cool fresh air . 

 ' dreadfully bored on the moor , ' he say , as they shake hand . ' no bird nobody to talk to could not   stand it any long . how be the sister ? Lesbia well ? why , man alive , how queer you look ! nothing amiss , I hope ? ' 

 ' yes , there be something very much amiss . Steadman be dead . ' 

 ' Steadman ! her ladyship 's right hand . that be rather bad . but you will drop into his stewardship . she will trust your long head , I know . much well that she should look to her granddaughter 's husband for advice in all business matter than to a servant . when do it happen ? ' 

 ' half an hour ago . I be just go to Lady Maulevrier 's room when you rang the bell . take off your inverness , and come with I . ' 

 ' the poor grandmother , ' mutter Maulevrier . ' I be afraid it will be a blow . ' 

 he have much less cause for fear than Lord Hartfield , who know of deep and secret reason why Steadman 's death should be a calamity of dire import for his mistress . Maulevrier have be tell nothing of that scene with the strange old man the hidden treasure the anglo - indian phrase which have fill Lord Hartfield 's mind with the dark doubt . 

 if that half - lunatic old man , describe by Lady Maulevrier as a kinsman of Steadman 's , be verily the person Lord Hartfield believe , his presence under that roof , unguarde by a trust - worthy attendant , be fraught with danger . it would be for Lady Maulevrier , helpless , a prisoner to her sofa , at death 's door , to face that danger . the very thought of it might kill she . and yet it be imperative that the truth should be tell she without delay . 

 the two young man go to her ladyship 's sit room . she be alone , a volume of her favourite Schopenhauer open before she , under the light of the shade reading - lamp . sorry comfort in the hour of trouble ! 

 Maulevrier go over to she and kiss she ; and then drop silently into a chair near at hand , his face in shadow . Hartfield seat himself nearer the sofa , and nearer the lamp . 

 ' Dear Lady Maulevrier , I have come to tell you some very bad news ' 

 ' Lesbia ? ' exclaim her ladyship , with a frightened look . 

 ' no , there be nothing wrong with Lesbia . it be about your old servant Steadman . ' 

 ' dead ? ' falter Lady Maulevrier , ashy pale , as she look at he in the lamplight . 

 he bend his head affirmatively . 

 ' yes . he be seize with apoplexy fall from his chair to the hearth , and never speak or stir again . ' 

 Lady Maulevrier utter no word of sorrow or surprise . she lay , look straight before she into vacancy , the pale attenuate feature rigid as if they have be marble . what be to be do what must be tell whom could she trust ? those be the question repeat themselves in her mind as she star into space . and no answer come to they . 

 no answer come , except the opening of the door opposite her couch . the handle turn slowly , hesitatingly , as if move by feeble finger ; and then the door be push slowly open , and an old man come with shuffle footstep towards the one light spot in the middle of the room . 

 it be the old man Lord Hartfield have last see gloating over his treasury of gold and jewel the man whom Maulevrier have never see whose existence for forty year have be hide from every creature in that house , except Lady Maulevrier and the Steadmans , until Mary find her way into the old garden . 

 he come close up to the little table in front of Lady Maulevrier 's couch , and look down at she , a strange , uncanny being , wither and bent , with pale , fade eye in which there be a glimmer of unholy light . 

 ' good - evening to you , Lady Maulevrier , ' he say in a mocking voice . ' I should not   have know you if we have meet anywhere else . I think , of the two of we , you be more changed than I. ' 

 she look up at he , her feature quiver , her haughty head draw back ; as a bird shrink from the gaze of a snake , recoiling , but too fascinated to fly . her eye meet his with a look of unutterable horror . for some moment she be speechless , and then , look at Lord Hartfield , she say , piteously 

 ' why do you let he come here ? he ought to be take care of shut up . it be Steadman 's old uncle a lunatic I shelter . why be he allow to come to my room ? ' 

 ' I be Lord Maulevrier , ' say the old man , draw himself up and plant his crutch stick upon the floor ; ' I be Lord Maulevrier , and this woman be my wife . yes , I be mad sometimes , but not always . I have my bad fit , but not often . but I never forget who and what I be , Algernon , Earl of Maulevrier , Governor of Madras . ' 

 ' Lady Maulevrier , be this horrible thing true ? ' cry her grandson , vehemently . 

 ' he be mad , Maulevrier . do not   you see that he be mad ? ' she exclaim , look from Hartfield to her grandson , and then with a look of loathing and horror at her accuser . 

 ' I tell you , young man , I be Maulevrier , ' say the accuser ; ' there be no one else who have a right to be call by that name , while I live . they have shut I up she and her accomplice deny my name hide I from the world . he be dead , and she lie there stricken for her sin . ' 

 ' my grandfather die at the inn at Great Langdale , falter Maulevrier . 

 ' your grandfather be bring to this house ill out of his wit . all cloud and darkness here , ' say the old man , touch his forehead . ' how long have it be ? who can tell ? a weary time long , dark night , full of ghost . yes , I have see he the Rajah , that copper - face scoundrel , see he as she tell I he look when she give the signal to her slave to strangle he , there in the hall , where the grave be dig ready for the traitor 's carcass . she too yes , she have haunt I , call upon I to give up her treasure , to restore her son . ' 

 ' yes , ' cry the paralytic woman , suddenly lift out of herself , as it be , in a paroxysm of fury , every feature convulse , every nerve strain to its utmost tension ; ' yes , this be Lord Maulevrier . you have hear the truth , and from his own lip . you , his only son 's only son . you his granddaughter 's husband . you hear he avow himself the instigator of a diabolical murder ; you hear he confess how his paramour 's husband be strangle at his false wife 's bidding , in his own palace , bury under the Moorish pavement in the hall of many arch . you hear how he inherit the Rajah 's treasure from a mistress who die strangely , swiftly , conveniently , as soon so he have weary of she , and a new favourite have begin to exercise her influence . such thing be do in the East dynasty annihilate , kingdom overthrown , poison or bowstring use at will , to gratify a profligate 's passion , or pay for a spendthrift 's extravagance . such thing be do when that man be Governor of Madras as be never do by an Englishman in India before his time . he go there fetter by no prejudice he be more Mussulman than the Mussulmen themselves a deep , dark traitor . and it be to hide such crime as these to interpose the great peacemaker death between he and the government which be resolve upon punish he to save the honour , the fortune of my son , and the child who be to come after he , the name of a noble race , a name that be ever stainless until he defile it it be for this great end I take step to hide that feeble , useless life of his from the world he have offend ; it be for this end that I cause a peasant to be bury in the vault of the Maulevriers , with all the pomp and ceremony that befit the funeral of one of England 's old earl . I screen he from his enemy I save he from the ignominy of a public trial from the execration of his countryman . his only punishment be to eat his heart under this roof , in luxurious seclusion , his comfort study , his whim gratify so far as they could be by the most faithful of servant . a light penance for the dark infamy of his life in India , I think . his mind be all but go when he come here , but he have his rational interval , and in these the burden of his lonely life may have weigh heavily upon he . but it be not such a heavy burden as I have bear I , his gaoler , I who have devote my existence to the one task of guard the family honour . ' 

 he , whom she thus acknowledge as her husband , have sink exhaust into a chair near she . he take out his gold snuff - box , and refresh himself with a leisurely pinch of snuff , look about he curiously all the while , with a senile grin . that flash of passion which for a few minute have restore he to the full possession of his reason have burn itself out , and his mind have relapse into the condition in which it have be when he talk to Mary in the garden . 

 ' my pipe , Steadman , ' he say , look towards the door ; ' bring I my pipe , ' and then , impatiently , ' what have become of Steadman ? he have be get inattentive very inattentive . ' 

 he get up , and move slowly to the door , lean on his crutch - stick , his head sink upon his breast , mutter to himself as he go ; and thus he vanish from they , like the spectre of some terrible ancestor which have return from the grave to announce the coming of calamity to a doomed race . his grandson look after he , with an expression of intense displeasure . 

 ' and so , Lady Maulevrier , ' he exclaim , turn to his grandmother , ' I have bear a title that never belong to I , and enjoy the possession of another man 's estate all this time , thank to your pretty little plot . a very respectable position for your grandson to occupy , upon my life ! ' 

 Lord Hartfield lift his hand with a warning gesture . 

 ' spare she , ' he say . ' she be in no condition to endure your reproach . ' 

 spare she yes . Fate have not spar she . the beautiful face beautiful even in age and decay change suddenly as she look at they the mouth become distort , the eye fix : and then the heavy head fall back upon the pillow the paralysed form , wholly paralysed now , lie like a thing of stone . it never move again . Consciousness be blot out for ever in that moment . the feeble pulse of heart and brain throb with gradually diminish power for a night and a day ; and in the twilight of that dreadful day of nothingness the last glimmer of the light die in the lamp , and Lady Maulevrier and the burden of her sin be beyond the veil . 

 Viscount Haselden , alias Lord Maulevrier , hold a long consultation with Lord Hartfield on the night of his grandmother 's death , as to what step ought to be take in relation to the real Earl of Maulevrier : and it be only at the end of a serious and earnest discussion that both young man come to the decision that Lady Maulevrier 's secret ought to be keep faithfully to the end . assuredly no good purpose could be achieve by let the world know of old Lord Maulevrier 's existence . a half - lunatic octogenarian could gain nothing by be restore to right and possession which he have most justly forfeit . all that justice demand be that the closing year of his life should be make as comfortable as care and wealth could make they ; and Hartfield and Haselden take immediate step to this end . but their first act be to send the old earl 's treasure chest under safe convoy to the India House , with a letter explain how this long - hide wealth , bring from India by Lord Maulevrier , have be discover among other effect in a lumber - room at Lady Maulevrier 's country house . the money so deliver up might possibly have form part of his lordship 's private fortune ; but , in the absence of any knowledge as to its origin , his grandson , the present Lord Maulevrier , prefer to deliver it up to the authority of the India House , to be deal with as they might think fit . 

 the old earl make no further attempt to assert himself . he seem content to remain in his own room as of old , to potter about the garden , where his solitude be as complete as that of a hermit 's cell . the only moan be make be for James Steadman , whose service he miss sorely . Lord Hartfield replace that devoted servant by a clever austrian valet , a new importation from Vienna , who understand very little English , a train attendant upon mental invalid , and who be quite capable of deal with old Lord Maulevrier . 

 Lord Hartfield go a step far ; and within a week of those two funeral of servant and mistress , which cast a gloom over the peaceful valley of Grasmere , he bring down a famous mad - doctor to diagnose his lordship 's case . there be but little risk in so do , he argue with his friend , and it be their duty so to do . if the old man should assert himself to the doctor as Lord Maulevrier , the declaration would pass as a symptom of his lunacy . but it happen that the physician arrive at Fellside on one of Lord Maulevrier 's bad day , and the patient never emerge from the feeble phase of imbecility . 

 ' brain quite go , ' pronounce the doctor , ' bodily health very poor . take he to the South of France for the winter Hyeres , or any quiet place . he can not   last long . ' 

 to Hyeres the old man be take , with Mrs. Steadman as nurse , and the austrian valet as body - servant and keeper . Mary , for whom , in his bright hour he show a warm affection , go with he under her husband 's wing . 

 Lord Hartfield rent a chateau on the slope of an olive - clothe hill , where he and his young wife , whose health be somewhat delicate at this time , spend a winter in peaceful seclusion ; while Lesbia and her brother travel together in Italy . the old man 's strength improve in that lovely climate . he live to see the rose and orange blossom of the early spring , and die in his arm - chair suddenly , without a pang , while Mary sit at his foot read to he : a quiet end of an evil and troubled life . and now he whom the world have know as Lord Maulevrier be verily the earl , and could hear himself call by his title once more without a touch of shame . 

 the secret of Lady Maulevrier 's sin have be so faithfully keep by the two young man that neither of her granddaughter know the true story of that mysterious person whom Mary have first hear of as James Steadman 's uncle . she and Lesbia both know that there be painful circumstance of some kind connect with this man 's existence , his hidden life in the old house at Fellside ; but they be both content to learn no more . respect for their grandmother 's memory , sorrowful affection for the dead , prevail over natural curiosity . 

 early in February Maulevrier send decorator and upholsterer into the old house in Curzon Street , which be ready before the middle of May to receive his lordship and his young wife , the girlish daughter of a Florentine nobleman , a gazelle - eyed Italian , with a voice whose every tone be music , and with the gentle , shyest , most engaging manner of any girl in Florence . Lady Lesbia , strangely subdue and change by the grief and humiliation of her last campaign , have be her brother 's counsellor and confidante throughout his wooing of his fair italian bride . she be to spend the season under her brother 's roof , to help to initiate young Lady Maulevrier in the mysterious rite of London society , and to warn she of those rock and shoal which have wreck her own fortune . 

 the month of May bring a son and heir to Lord Hartfield ; and it be not till after his birth that Mary , Countess of Hartfield , be present to her sovereign , and begin her career as a matron of rank and standing , very much overpower by the weight of her honour , and look forward with delight to the end of the season and a flight to argyleshire with her husband and baby . 


 the END .
